They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics.
25.Rationale: Although some patients with neutropenia had fever and chills, microbiologic cultures did not show an infectious etiology, and management did not include routine immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics. Thus fever/chills in this context were not shown to be a reliable indicator of bacterial infection requiring immediate broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy in this cohort.

25.Option: Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was well tolerated in the cohort of patients with neurosyphilis, with rapid normalization of neutrophil counts and no life-threatening complications, even in cases of severe neutropenia, after discontinuation of the drug.
Evidence: The total incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was 2.42% (95% CI: 1.38–4.13%, 14/578) among this cohort of patients with NS.

Another two patients (case 1 with general paresis and case 13 with syphilitic meningitis) did well until they had fever, and repeat CBC revealed thrombocytopenia and severe neutropenia (ANC of case 1: 0.04×109/L; ANC of case 13: 0.21×109/L) near the end of therapy. Benzylpenicillin was discontinued.

They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics. Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.

The other 11 patients with mild or moderate neutropenia finished the 14-day therapy with close monitoring of CBC and observation for sequelae of neutropenia. None experienced any severe complication of therapy and all had recovery of a normal ANC within seven days.
25.Rationale: The study documents that neutropenia occurred infrequently and that patients, including those with severe neutropenia, recovered their ANC rapidly after benzylpenicillin withdrawal without life-threatening complications. These findings support the inference that, in this cohort, benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was generally well tolerated with prompt normalization after stopping the drug.

25.Option: The incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia among neurosyphilis patients is significantly higher than in patients receiving benzylpenicillin for other conditions, suggesting a unique susceptibility in this population.
Evidence: The total incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was 2.42% (95% CI: 1.38–4.13%, 14/578) among this cohort of patients with NS.

Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia, a complication of prolonged therapy with high doses, has been well documented when treating infective endocarditis, leading some patients to withdraw necessary treatment and even undergo insidious life-threatening sepsis [4–7].
25.Rationale: The passage provides the incidence within this neurosyphilis cohort but contains no comparative incidence data for other patient populations to support a claim of significantly higher incidence or unique susceptibility. The text also notes benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia has been documented in other conditions (e.g., infective endocarditis), which does not support the asserted uniqueness.

25.Option: The severity of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia in neurosyphilis patients is strongly associated with older age and the presence of neurologic complications, requiring more intensive monitoring.
Evidence: The severity of neutropenia had no association with age, gender or type of neurosyphilis (p>0.05).
The multivariate logistic regression was not carried out since no significant factors were found by chi-square test.
25.Rationale: The study explicitly reports no association between severity of neutropenia and age, gender, or type of neurosyphilis, and therefore does not support the claim that severity is strongly associated with older age or neurologic complications. The data do not justify the stated inference.

27.Option: WASH improvements in Kalemie had a greater impact on cholera transmission than vaccination, as they directly reduced environmental contamination and exposure over time.
Evidence: we estimated: 3,702 (mean: 3,702.3, 95% CrI: 1,302.5–7,542.0) additional cases, a 2.56% increase (mean: 2.56%, 95% CrI: 1.79%-3.30%), when removing vaccination alone (scenario with WASH only),
1,585 (mean: 1,585.5, 95% CrI: 1,321.9–5,108.8) cases avoided, 1.03% (mean: 1.03%, 95% CrI: 0.01%-2.85%), by WASH alone (scenario with vaccination only),

The WASH component of the intervention focused on improving access to clean water. ... MSF extended access to tap water in the northern part of the city by laying pipes, building water reservoirs, distributing water filters, and setting up public drinking fountains in collaboration with Solidarites International. In addition, sand filters were installed on paths where people draw water from the lake, and chlorination activities were performed during outbreaks.
27.Rationale: The passage provides model-estimated effects of removing each intervention: removing vaccination (i.e., having WASH only) led to 3,702 additional cases, whereas WASH alone avoided only 1,585 cases. These numbers indicate that, in this setting and for the implemented WASH package, vaccination had a larger modeled impact than the WASH improvements actually implemented. The description of the WASH activities shows they were focused and limited in scope, which does not support the stronger claim that WASH had a greater impact than vaccination.

27.Option: The high immunity observed in Kalemie is primarily due to repeated vaccination campaigns rather than environmental exposure, indicating that vaccination has been a key driver of population-level protection.
Evidence: This high immunity would be the likely consequence of annual outbreaks and persistent environmental exposure, which we explain further below.

Based on our model, the targeted vaccinations occurred when population immunity was high: 97.8% (95% CrI: 96.7–98.6) in November 2013, 89. 0% (95% CrI: 76.7–96.7) in July 2014, and 89.1% (95% CrI: 77.2–96.6) during the catch-up in August 2014.
27.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes the inferred high local immunity to annual outbreaks and persistent environmental exposure, not to vaccination. It also notes that the vaccination campaigns occurred when population immunity was already high. Thus the evidence contradicts the claim that repeated vaccination campaigns were the primary driver of the high immunity.

27.Option: The impact of vaccination in endemic cholera settings may be limited when high population immunity is maintained through constant environmental exposure, even with high vaccine coverage.
Evidence: Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity (Fig 3C).
However, the timing of a pulse of vaccination could substantially influence the impact of vaccination campaigns.

The best performing vaccination scenario ... avoided 12,777 cases ... for 200,000 vaccinated people. However, the high level of local immunity would result in vaccinating a large proportion of immune individuals, reducing the impact of the vaccination.
The high immunity inferred by the model was maintained through annual flare-ups and constant environmental exposure.
27.Rationale: The passage directly states that in populations with high immunity vaccination campaigns (particularly with small targets) have limited impact, and that much of the high immunity in Kalemie is maintained by repeated environmental exposure. It also explains that vaccinating many already-immune individuals reduces the marginal impact of vaccination. This supports the inference that vaccination impact may be limited in such endemic settings.

27.Option: Vaccination campaigns in endemic cholera settings are most effective when timed to coincide with the peak of environmental bacterial growth, as this maximizes the number of susceptible individuals exposed to the vaccine.
Evidence: Our model suggested that vaccination campaigns with small target population sizes would have a limited impact in populations with high immunity (Fig 3C). However, the timing of a pulse of vaccination could substantially influence the impact of vaccination campaigns.
Specifically, timing the vaccination to occur at the lowest point of population immunity and before an outbreak began increased its impact.
27.Rationale: The passage recommends timing vaccination at the lowest point of population immunity and before an outbreak begins to increase impact, not at the peak of environmental bacterial growth. Peak environmental growth would typically coincide with higher transmission/outbreak periods; the authors instead emphasize vaccinating prior to outbreaks when susceptibility is greatest. Therefore the provided option is not supported by the passage.

27.Option: The modest impact of the intervention in Kalemie was primarily due to the limited scale and incremental implementation of the WASH improvements, which prevented a significant reduction in environmental exposure.
Evidence: The WASH component of the intervention focused on improving access to clean water. Although it was not acting on every dimension of WASH, we simply refer to it as “WASH intervention” below.

The WASH intervention incurred delays in the aftermath of the security issue that delayed the OCV intervention. Its first milestone, extending access to tap water was achieved in October 2014 and the remaining components were completed incrementally until early 2016.
1,585 (mean: 1,585.5, 95% CrI: 1,321.9–5,108.8) cases avoided, 1.03% (mean: 1.03%, 95% CrI: 0.01%-2.85%), by WASH alone (scenario with vaccination only),
We estimated that removing environmental exposure or contamination would have a critical impact on cholera dynamics. These strategies avoided 142,518 cases ... and 134,373 cases ... respectively.
The model did not allow the environmental contamination to vary because the intervention did not target waste management.
27.Rationale: The passage describes the WASH package as focused on access to clean water, incomplete across WASH dimensions, implemented incrementally and with delays. Model results show WASH alone yielded a modest number of cases avoided relative to what would be achieved by fully removing environmental exposure or contamination. The authors also note the model could not vary environmental contamination because the intervention did not target waste management—supporting the inference that the limited scale and incremental WASH implementation constrained reductions in environmental exposure and hence contributed to the intervention's modest impact.

28.Option: The environmental niche models predicted that regions in the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and Southern Florida Coastal Plain are least suitable for human-tick encounters, which aligns with the observed absence of ticks in these areas.
Evidence: the area of potential suitability for I. scapularis included all ecoregions in the southeastern US; however, there was a reduced risk to encounter I. scapularis ticks in southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi, and western Arkansas (Fig 4).
Eco regions included Piedmont (45), Blue ridge (66), Ridge and Valley (67), Southwestern Appalachians (68), and Central Appalachians (69).

Our models correctly predicted sites where dry ice traps were located to be environmentally unsuitable for all three tick species; these absences and unsuitable sites were found in a landscape of predicted suitability by ENMs (Fig 5).
28.Rationale: The passage explicitly states a reduced risk in southern Florida and in parts of Mississippi for I. scapularis, which partially supports the statement for Southern Florida and Mississippi. However, the passage does not state that the Blue Ridge ecoregion, nor specifically the 'Mississippi Alluvial Plain' or 'Southern Florida Coastal Plain' ecoregions, were predicted to be the least suitable overall. The Blue Ridge is only listed among ecoregions used in figures, not identified as least suitable. The authors also report observed absences at specific trap sites but those sites were in western North Carolina, not the named plains. Therefore the option overstates and generalizes beyond the passage evidence and cannot be fully derived from the provided text.

28.Option: The Southeastern Plains region is unsuitable for tick encounters due to its high elevation and cold climate, which prevent tick survival and activity.
Evidence: elevation, landcover, maximum temperature, minimum temperature, and vapor pressure were the best predictors of habitat suitability (Table 3).
High contribution variables to all three models included elevation, landcover, and maximum temperature.

area of potential suitability for I. scapularis included all ecoregions in the southeastern US; however, there was a reduced risk to encounter I. scapularis ticks in southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi, and western Arkansas (Fig 4).
28.Rationale: While elevation and temperature are indicated as important predictors in the models, the passage does not state that the Southeastern Plains region is unsuitable, nor that it is characterized by high elevation and cold climate that prevent tick survival. The Southeastern Plains are not discussed by name, and no evidence links that specific region to high elevation or cold climate in the passage. Thus the specific causal claim about the Southeastern Plains cannot be derived from the provided information.

28.Option: The observed absence of ticks in the Mississippi Alluvial Plain and Southern Florida Coastal Plain is primarily due to insufficient sampling by forestry crews, indicating that these regions are not truly unsuitable for tick encounters.
Evidence: The coordinates used for niche modeling are within 1.6 km (1 mile) of the actual encounter sites because (1) many of the plots are located on private lands and these exact locations are kept confidential ... and (2) ticks may have been encountered traveling to or from plots or while working at the plot.

Although the true distribution for each tick species may not be fully represented due to differences in collection, the CDC datasets represent the most comprehensive comparison for our models because they include surveillance by ArboNET or literature published each year throughout the Southeast.
there was a reduced risk to encounter I. scapularis ticks in southern Florida, eastern Tennessee and Mississippi, and western Arkansas (Fig 4).
28.Rationale: The passage acknowledges limitations in spatial precision and that true distributions may not be fully represented, which indicates potential sampling limitations. However, it does not assert that observed absences in the Mississippi Alluvial Plain or Southern Florida Coastal Plain are primarily due to insufficient sampling, nor does it provide evidence that these regions are truly suitable despite absences. The authors present model-predicted reduced suitability for some areas (southern Florida and parts of Mississippi) rather than attributing absences chiefly to sampling insufficiency. Therefore the statement claiming insufficient sampling as the primary reason is not supported by the passage.

28.Option: Human-tick encounters in the southeastern U.S. are most likely to occur in areas with high soil moisture and dense forest cover, as these conditions support tick survival and host availability.
Evidence: soil properties such as percent litter coverage and soil moisture are associated with A. americanum abundance [22,23].
Landscape variables associated with forests (e.g., land cover, primary productivity) are important for host populations and likely regulate infesting tick populations [e.g., 29,30].
normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), a measure of greenness, has been associated with the abundance of A. americanum [31].
Variables soil organic matter, vegetation indices, maximum temperature, gross primary productivity, and evapotranspiration contributed the most to predicting this tick’s geographic suitability.
28.Rationale: The passage directly links soil moisture and litter coverage to A. americanum abundance, and discusses that forest-associated landscape variables and vegetation indices (NDVI, vegetation indices, gross primary productivity) are important for host populations and likely regulate tick populations. These statements support the epidemiological inference that environments with higher soil moisture and dense forest/vegetative cover are associated with higher likelihood of human-tick encounters in the southeastern U.S.

28.Option: The removal of landcover as a predictor in the I. scapularis model indicates that land cover has no influence on tick encounters in the southeastern U.S.
Evidence: categorical variables (land cover) were removed from the environmental niche model for I. scapularis because the variable contribution was not balanced.
Landscape variables associated with forests (e.g., land cover, primary productivity) are important for host populations and likely regulate infesting tick populations [e.g., 29,30].
Values for each variable represent that variables percent contribution to each model, bolded values were those identified as significant contributing variables to the model (greater than 0.1 or 10%).
28.Rationale: The passage states landcover was removed from the I. scapularis model for technical reasons ('variable contribution was not balanced'), not because land cover lacks biological influence. Elsewhere the passage explicitly describes landscape and landcover variables as important for host populations and tick regulation. Thus removal from one species' model reflects modeling choices or data limitations, not evidence that land cover has no influence on tick encounters.

29.Option: Children with Giardia infection who received multi-nutrient supplementation had higher rates of diarrhea compared to uninfected children receiving the same supplementation.
Evidence: Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients (Figure 3; top panels, and Tables 2 and 3).
29.Rationale: The passage states that the protective association of Giardia against diarrhea was present in children receiving zinc or placebo but that there was no association in children receiving multi-nutrients. 'No association' means rates were not higher or lower in infected versus uninfected children in the multi-nutrient group; the passage does not report higher diarrhea rates among infected children receiving multi-nutrients. Therefore the claim that infected children receiving multi-nutrients had higher rates than uninfected peers is not supported.

29.Option: The observed reduction in diarrhea incidence among Giardia-infected children is likely due to improved sanitation and health-care seeking behavior in this population.
Evidence: Children with Giardia infection were on average 3.3 months older than their uninfected peers, resided somewhat closer to the dispensary, had a lower prevalence of inflammation, marginally higher hemoglobin concentrations, as well as marginally lower plasma concentrations of soluble transferrin receptor and folate.

Adjustment for age and distance to the dispensary led to smaller but still substantial associations between baseline Giardia infection and diarrhea (all events), whilst interaction effects between infection and the multi-nutrient intervention remained virtually unchanged (Figure 4).
Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).
29.Rationale: The option posits that improved sanitation or health-care seeking explains the reduced diarrhea incidence. While the passage notes that infected children 'resided somewhat closer to the dispensary', the investigators adjusted for age and distance to the dispensary and the protective association remained 'smaller but still substantial.' Thus the data and reported adjustments argue against attributing the reduction solely to differences in sanitation or health-care seeking behavior, and the passage does not present evidence that improved sanitation or care-seeking is the causal explanation.

29.Option: The protective association between Giardia infection and reduced diarrhea incidence may be due to the parasite's ability to suppress other enteric pathogens through competitive exclusion or immune modulation.
Evidence: Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).
Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.

Because the role of Giardia as diarrhea-causing agent is controversial and re-infection can occur rapidly in developing areas where it is highly endemic, it has been recommended that children with asymptomatic infection should not be treated in such settings [15], [16].
29.Rationale: Although the passage documents an association in which Giardia infection was linked to reduced diarrhea incidence in some groups, it does not provide mechanistic evidence that this is due to competitive exclusion or immune modulation of other enteric pathogens. The authors describe the epidemiologic findings and note controversy about Giardia's role, but they do not present data on pathogen interactions or immune effects. Therefore the mechanistic assertion is speculative and not derivable from the presented data.

29.Option: Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a marked reduction in the rates of subsequent diarrhea, but this protective effect was absent in children receiving multi-nutrient supplementation.
Evidence: Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).
Giardia was associated with protection against diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not among children receiving multi-nutrients.

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients (Figure 3; top panels, and Tables 2 and 3).
29.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that baseline Giardia infection was linked to substantially lower incidence of diarrhea (about 50% lower incidence of first episodes) and increased time to first diarrhea episode. It further states that this protective association was observed in children receiving zinc or placebo but was not found in children receiving multi-nutrient supplementation. This option accurately restates those findings and is directly supported by the quoted text.

30.Option: The positive associations between helminth infections and anemia observed in previous studies suggest that current control programs are failing to reduce anemia in Zanzibar.
Evidence: There is growing evidence that, besides malaria and nutritional deficiencies, heavy helminth infections as well as multiple helminth species infections of light intensities, are associated with anemia [6]–[8].
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Large-scale deworming programs in Zanzibar have reduced helminth-related morbidity and are likely to have lowered overall transmission [17], [21].
Participants with an A. lumbricoides infection had a decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji (OR = 0.55, 95% CI 0.31–0.98).
30.Rationale: The passage cites prior evidence that helminths can be associated with anemia and reports high anemia prevalence in the study communities. However, the study's own analyses do not show a consistent positive association between soil-transmitted helminths and anemia (for example, A. lumbricoides was associated with decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji). The passage also explicitly states that large-scale deworming has reduced morbidity. From the presented data one cannot conclude that current control programs are definitively failing to reduce anemia — the relationship between helminth control and anemia is not established in the study, and anemia likely has multiple causes. Therefore the claimed inference is not supported by the passage alone.

30.Option: The high prevalence of anemia in both communities, particularly in peri-urban Dole, suggests that anemia is primarily driven by nutritional deficiencies rather than by helminth infections.
Evidence: There is growing evidence that, besides malaria and nutritional deficiencies, heavy helminth infections as well as multiple helminth species infections of light intensities, are associated with anemia [6]–[8].
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Participants with an A. lumbricoides infection had a decreased risk of anemia in Bandamaji (OR = 0.55, 95% CI 0.31–0.98).
In Dole, no significant risk factors for anemia were found.
30.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that nutritional deficiencies (and malaria) can contribute to anemia, it does not present data measuring nutritional status or demonstrating that nutritional deficiencies are the primary driver of the high anemia prevalence. The study's results do not show a consistent positive association between helminth infections and anemia (and in one instance showed an inverse association with A. lumbricoides). Because the data needed to attribute a primary cause (e.g., nutritional assessments, malaria testing) are not provided, the statement that anemia is primarily driven by nutritional deficiencies cannot be derived from the passage.

30.Option: The significant differences in seroprevalence between Bandamaji and Dole indicate that the GPELF program was more effective in Dole than in Bandamaji.
Evidence: within the global program to eliminate lymphatic filariasis (GPELF), ivermectin plus albendazole were distributed to the entire at-risk population (excluding children younger than 5 years and pregnant women) in Zanzibar from 2001 to 2006, and a mean annual coverage rate of 80% was reached [17]–[19].

The seroprevalence of anti-S. haematobium antibodies was significantly higher in Bandamaji than in Dole (46.1% versus 24.8%; χ2 = 14.1, p<0.001). The seroprevalence of S. stercoralis infections was 32.9% in Bandamaji and 12.8% in Dole, which revealed a highly significant difference (χ2 = 15.3, p<0.001).

Both sites received single annual treatments of albendazole and ivermectin in the frame of the GPELF from 2001–2006, and albendazole or mebendazole plus praziquantel via the school-based deworming program till 2006.
Of note, the school in Bandamaji was only opened in 2005.
30.Rationale: Although seroprevalence and infection prevalence differ between Bandamaji and Dole, the passage does not provide site-specific data on GPELF implementation or coverage that would allow attributing these differences to differential program effectiveness. Other contextual factors noted in the passage (rural versus peri-urban setting, timing of school opening, demographic and socioeconomic differences) could explain seroprevalence differences. Therefore, inferring that GPELF was more effective in Dole than in Bandamaji is not supported by the presented data and would require additional information.

30.Option: Despite considerable progress made in the control of helminthiases in Zanzibar, the 'worm-problem' and anemia in Zanzibar remains a formidable challenge and cannot be overcome by preventive chemotherapy alone.
Evidence: Large-scale deworming programs in Zanzibar have reduced helminth-related morbidity and are likely to have lowered overall transmission [17], [21].
The overall prevalence of infection with any helminth species, according to different tests examined under a microscope was 73.7% in Bandamaji and 48.9% in Dole.

Among the infected individuals, 87.0% (120/138) and 13.0% (18/138) had a light and moderate infection with A. lumbricoides... With the exception of one moderate hookworm infection, all others 99.1% (105/106) were of light intensity. No heavy infections were found for any soil-transmitted helminth.
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.
Combined, this information will provide an important insight into the impact of ongoing interventions and hopefully evidence-based realignment of disease-control priorities.
30.Rationale: The passage documents that large-scale deworming has reduced morbidity and likely transmission, yet substantial prevalences of soil-transmitted helminths and high anemia rates remain in the studied communities. The coexistence of persistent infections (including polyparasitism in children) and significant anemia despite years of preventive chemotherapy supports the epidemiological inference that preventive chemotherapy alone has not eliminated the problem. The authors explicitly frame the need to realign control priorities based on the contemporary patterns, which is consistent with concluding that additional or integrated measures beyond preventive chemotherapy are required. Thus this inference is supported by the presented evidence.

30.Option: The age-related decline in S. haematobium infection prevalence suggests that the current control strategies have successfully interrupted transmission in older age groups.
Evidence: In both communities an incremental increase of age by 1 year was associated with a lower risk of a S. haematobium infection (Bandamaji: OR = 0.93, 95% CI 0.90–0.95; Dole: OR = 0.97, 95% CI 0.95–1.00).
S. haematobium infections were most prevalent in the 12–14-year-old age group (17.1% in Bandamaji and 26.3% in Dole). No S. haematobium infections were found in the elderly (≥60 years).

Both sites received single annual treatments of albendazole and ivermectin in the frame of the GPELF from 2001–2006, and albendazole or mebendazole plus praziquantel via the school-based deworming program till 2006.
30.Rationale: The observed age-related decline in S. haematobium prevalence (higher in adolescents, lower in older adults) is described in the passage, but multiple non-mutually exclusive explanations (age-related exposure differences, acquired immunity, cohort effects, behavioral factors, or selective survival) could account for this pattern. The passage does not provide direct evidence that control strategies specifically interrupted transmission in older age groups (no age-stratified intervention coverage or transmission interruption measures are reported). Therefore, attributing the age-specific decline to successful interruption of transmission by current control strategies is not supported by the data presented.

31.Option: The recombinant yellow-related protein rSP03B and apyrases rSP01B and rSP01, when refolded, show strong immunogenicity in both mice and canine sera and are therefore ideal candidates for monitoring exposure to P. perniciosus in endemic regions.
Evidence: All recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of all three repeatedly exposed mice; control sera and the GST tag were negative (Fig. 1A). A similar reactivity of recombinant antigens was found with the sera of the three dogs repeatedly exposed to P. perniciosus (Fig. 1B): all recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed dogs.
In all these cases, protein refolding was performed using dialysis against PBS using SnakeSkin Dialysis Tubing (10 kDa MWCO, Thermo Scientific Goettiengen, Germany).

Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 (for both proteins in pET42b with 1 His tag: r = 0.91, p<0.0001; for rSP01B with 2 His tags r = 0.89, p<0.0001; and for rSP01 with 2 His tags r = 0.91, p<0.0001) and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3).

Bitten mice had a highly elevated antibody response to the following seven recombinant proteins: apyrase rSP01B in all three plasmids, both rSP01 apyrases, yellow protein rSP03B and ParSP25-like protein rSP08.
31.Rationale: The passage supports that rSP03B, rSP01B and rSP01 evoke strong antibody responses in both mice and dogs. It also states that protein refolding was performed. However, the key experimental result for dogs explicitly refers to 'denatured apyrases' showing highly positive correlations with SGH, indicating the reported strong reactivity pertains to denatured forms. The option's assertion that the proteins are strong "when refolded" (and the implication that refolded forms are responsible for the observed immunogenicity) is not supported by the passage, which emphasizes denatured forms in the canine ELISA results. The wording "ideal candidates" is stronger than the passage's cautious framing (it describes them as promising candidates). Therefore the statement as written cannot be fully derived from the passage.

31.Option: The use of recombinant apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 in their refolded form is optimal for monitoring canine exposure to P. perniciosus because they elicit a stronger immune response in dogs than denatured forms and are more stable in field conditions.
Evidence: In all these cases, protein refolding was performed using dialysis against PBS using SnakeSkin Dialysis Tubing (10 kDa MWCO, Thermo Scientific Goettiengen, Germany).

Highly positive correlations with the reaction against SGH were obtained for the two denatured apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 (for both proteins in pET42b with 1 His tag: r = 0.91, p<0.0001; for rSP01B with 2 His tags r = 0.89, p<0.0001; and for rSP01 with 2 His tags r = 0.91, p<0.0001) and yellow-related protein rSP03B (r = 0.89, p<0.0001) (Fig. 3).
The correlations were not significant for the other four proteins tested (Fig. 3, data not shown for antigen 5 rSP07).
31.Rationale: While the passage documents that apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 show highly positive correlations with anti-SGH responses in dogs, the experimental results explicitly refer to the denatured apyrase forms as those with strong reactivity. The passage does not present any comparison showing that refolded forms elicit stronger immune responses than denatured forms, nor does it provide any data about field stability. Thus the claim that refolded forms are 'optimal' and 'more stable in field conditions' is not supported by the provided text.

31.Option: The antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 are the most promising markers for detecting exposure to P. perniciosus in dogs due to their consistent reactivity across multiple experimental methods and their high immunogenicity in both mice and canine sera.
Evidence: All recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of all three repeatedly exposed mice; control sera and the GST tag were negative (Fig. 1A). A similar reactivity of recombinant antigens was found with the sera of the three dogs repeatedly exposed to P. perniciosus (Fig. 1B): all recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed dogs.
The correlations were not significant for the other four proteins tested (Fig. 3, data not shown for antigen 5 rSP07).
31.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were not recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed mice or dogs in immunoblots. Additionally, the ELISA results indicate that correlations were significant for other proteins (apyrases and yellow protein) but not for several of the remaining proteins, and data for rSP07 were not shown. Therefore the claim that rSP07 and rSP04 are the most promising markers with high immunogenicity is contradicted by the experimental findings.

31.Option: The ParSP25 protein rSP08 and D7 protein rSP04 are the most reliable markers for monitoring exposure to P. perniciosus in the western Mediterranean because they show strong cross-reactivity with other Larroussius sand fly species and are highly conserved across vector populations.
Evidence: Bitten mice had a highly elevated antibody response to the following seven recombinant proteins: apyrase rSP01B in all three plasmids, both rSP01 apyrases, yellow protein rSP03B and ParSP25-like protein rSP08.

All recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of all three repeatedly exposed mice; control sera and the GST tag were negative (Fig. 1A). A similar reactivity of recombinant antigens was found with the sera of the three dogs repeatedly exposed to P. perniciosus (Fig. 1B): all recombinant proteins except antigen 5 protein rSP07 and D7 protein rSP04 were recognized by the sera of repeatedly exposed dogs.
The correlations were not significant for the other four proteins tested (Fig. 3, data not shown for antigen 5 rSP07).

In fact, rSP04 (D7) was not recognized by exposed sera in immunoblots. The passage contains no data or analyses about cross-species reactivity or conservation across vector populations, so the specific claims about cross-reactivity, conservation, and particular suitability for the western Mediterranean cannot be derived from the provided text.

32.Option: The induction of autophagy by rTsCatL2 is a secondary consequence of cellular damage caused by the physical disruption of tight junctions and cell rounding.

After 30 min, the cells became smaller and separated from each other, claudin 1, occludin and zo-1 became smaller in outline and wrapped around each cell, and the cells became less connected to each other. ... After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster.

The rTsCatL2-treated group showed bright green fluorescence and cells underwent autophagy; the EBSS-treated positive control group underwent autophagy, while no fluorescence was observed in MBP and PBS groups, scale bar: 100 μm. B. Transmission electron microscopy. M: mitochondrion; Go: Golgi apparatus; PS: foot; ASS: autolysosome; AP: autophagosome.
32.Rationale: The passage documents both morphological damage (cell rounding, loss of tight junctions, internalization of junction proteins) and induction of autophagy after rTsCatL2 exposure. However, it does not provide evidence establishing a causal sequence in which autophagy is secondary to the physical junction disruption. The data show co-occurrence but do not demonstrate that autophagy results from the mechanical/structural damage rather than being a direct or independent response to rTsCatL2. Therefore the specific causal claim that autophagy is a secondary consequence of physical disruption is not supported by the presented evidence.

Therefore the specific claim that the downregulation and internalization are primarily driven by direct proteolytic cleavage of TJ proteins by rTsCatL2 is not supported by the presented evidence.

The recoveries of IIL and AW in the TsCatL siRNA-302 group were lower than those in the control siRNA and PBS groups (FIIL = 177.34, FAW = 246.67, P < 0.05), and the reduction rates of IIL and AW were 44.37% and 47.57%, respectively.

The siRNA-treated T. spiralis ML were used to infect mice, and worms at different developmental stages were collected for counting, observation and length measurement. Compared with the control siRNA and PBS groups, the recoveries of IIL and AW were reduced in the siRNA-302-treated group (*P < 0.05), the ML worm load was reduced (*P < 0.05), and the worm reduction rates were 44.37%, 47.57% and 57.06% for IIL, AW and ML, respectively.

Together these observations support the epidemiological/biological inference that TsCatL contributes to degradation of the extracellular matrix, a mechanism that would facilitate parasite migration and invasion through host tissues.

It was revealed that the expression of claudin 1 decreased by 43.23% after 30 min incubation and 58.65% after 60 min incubation (F = 81.66, P < 0.05).

According to the aforementioned findings, TsCatL facilitated T. spiralis to invade the host epithelial cells.
32.Rationale: The experimental observations directly document that rTsCatL2 exposure causes morphological disruption (cell rounding, loss of tight junctions), redistribution/internalization of tight junction proteins (claudin 1, occludin, zo-1, e-cadherin), and induction of autophagy in Caco-2 intestinal epithelial cells. 32.Option: The binding of rTsCatL2 to the cell periphery and its subsequent internalization into the cytoplasm and nucleus indicate that the protein functions as a transcriptional regulator in host cells.

Recombinant TsCatL2 has cysteine protease activity and can hydrolyze host hemoglobin, serum albumin, immunoglobulin and extracellular matrix proteins in vitro with host-specific hydrolytic activity, indicating that TsCatL is a crucial digestive enzyme in T. spiralis [14].
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.

Instead, the protein is characterized as a cysteine protease with enzymatic activity and interactions with extracellular matrix proteins. Thus the specific conclusion that its internalization indicates a role as a transcriptional regulator is not supported by the presented data.

33.Option: Patient-reported preferences for oral and topical formulations are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns related to scarring and social stigma, rather than by treatment convenience or burden.
Evidence: In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.

This conceptually interesting discussion was mentioned by different patients, who expressed that, since the infection is local, they would prefer the treatment to be also applied locally: a topical treatment for a local infection.
Patients discussed the preference for an effective treatment in healing the wound, regardless of scar appearance, potential side effects or treatment duration.

Patients emphasized the need for general information and dissemination activities, including information about the disease, such as the mode of transmission and treatment options, as well as campaigns to de-stigmatize the disease.
33.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that preferences for oral and topical formulations are driven by easier administration and the idea that a local infection should be treated locally (convenience and perceived fit), and also mentions fear of needles and timeliness of topical application. Although stigma and scarring are discussed elsewhere (need for de-stigmatization; but patients also said they prefer efficacy regardless of scar appearance), the passage does not support the claim that aesthetics or stigma are the primary drivers. Therefore the option overstates the cause of preferences and is not supported by the provided text.

33.Option: Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
Evidence: We present a methodological and analytical approach as to involve patients in the identification of preferred characteristics of a new drug, and how this could inform TPPs.

We present patient preferences along the top-level domains of the DNDi’s TPP, 1 Safety and tolerability, 2 Contraindications, 3 Efficacy, 4 Formulation, 5 Treatment regimen, 6 Target population, 7 Cost, and two emerging categories 8 Perceived barriers and 9 Other development needs.
In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.

Treatment regimen was found to be a TPP category rich in themes and trade-offs. Patients perceived long treatment durations as burdensome, particularly against the need to balance work obligations, and in connection with indirect costs (days of work lost).
Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.
33.Rationale: The study explicitly aims to involve patients to inform TPPs and maps patient preferences onto TPP domains including formulation, treatment regimen, and cost. The passage provides evidence that patients value formulation (oral/topical), are concerned about treatment duration and its indirect costs, and frequently mention costs and access barriers. From an epidemiological and product-development perspective, incorporating these patient-reported preferences into TPPs would plausibly improve alignment with user needs and thus enhance real-world uptake, effectiveness, and accessibility. This inference follows directly from the stated aims and findings in the passage.

33.Option: The lack of shared decision-making in case management was apparent, with interviewees seeing injections as the only option to get cured, suggesting that patient preferences are often overridden by clinical protocols.
Evidence: One Brazilian patient with contraindications described moments of shared decision making with the treating medical team, which was not observed in other groups.

In contrast, some patients perceived injections as working faster and more effective than alternative modes of administration. While others, such as two Colombian patients, reported injections as the only treatment option.
33.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of limited shared decision-making in at least one instance (shared decision-making occurred for one Brazilian patient but "was not observed in other groups") and notes that some patients reported injections as the only option. However, the leap to the general claim that patient preferences are "often overridden by clinical protocols" is not directly supported. The evidence shows examples of limited shared decision-making and that some patients viewed injections as the only option, but it does not demonstrate a systematic overriding of patient preferences by protocols across the study population. Therefore the conclusion is an overreach beyond the passage's evidence.

33.Option: The findings suggest that patients in high-income countries are more likely to prioritize treatment efficacy over cost, while those in low-income settings prioritize affordability, indicating a need for region-specific TPPs.
Evidence: Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.

In a high-income setting, an Austrian patient describes paying a 140 EUR (154 USD) fee for specialized care to shorten waiting times, while a Brazilian patient described the challenges trying to access specialized care in their town, both due to the costs of the appointment and limited of availability of specialists.
This contrasted with patients with formal employment, such as the Colombian soldiers or the Austrian patients.
33.Rationale: While the passage notes cost concerns (particularly transport, tests, and indirect costs) and gives isolated examples from a high-income and low-income context, it does not present systematic evidence that patients in high-income countries prioritize efficacy over cost or that patients in low-income settings prioritize affordability. The text does not compare prioritization patterns by country in the manner asserted. Thus the region-specific prioritization claim and the implied need for region-specific TPPs are not directly derivable from the passage.

33.Option: The study demonstrates that discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) are the most effective method for capturing patient preferences in NTD drug development, surpassing qualitative interviews.
Evidence: There is little methodological guidance on how to construct TPPs; actual methodologies vary.

PATH has constructed TPPs for diagnostics for three NTDs using comprehensive approaches involving literature reviews, surveys and interviews with experts and stakeholders, process maps and review of available diagnostic tools [13].

A TPP for a point-of-care diagnostic test for CL was developed by FIND and DNDi, based on a draft and discussions with experts at a meeting, followed by an online survey with a larger audience of stakeholders and experts [14].
Studies using exploratory approaches (e.g., using qualitative semi-structured interviews or focus group discussions) of patient involvement are scarce [20].
We report the methods and findings of a qualitative study assessing preferences for treatment of CL patients from Brazil, Colombia, and Austria...
33.Rationale: The passage describes that multiple methods have been used to develop TPPs (literature reviews, surveys, interviews) and emphasizes that qualitative, exploratory approaches are scarce and that this study used semi-structured interviews. It does not present any evidence that discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) are the most effective method nor does it compare effectiveness of methods. Therefore the claim that DCEs 'surpass' qualitative interviews is unsupported by the passage.

34.Option: The simulation of parasite development in the SMT suggests that P. vivax parasites spend an average of 25.3 hours as trophozoites and schizonts before termination of the assay, which may explain the variability in EC50 values across different isolates.
Evidence: In the simulations of the growth dynamics under ex vivo assay conditions, the λ parameter for the Poisson distributions represented the duration of each parasite life cycle stage in P. vivax. The mean λ was 19.1 hours (95% CI 11, 29) for rings, 23.1 hours (95% CI 11, 36) for trophozoites, and 2.2 hours (95% CI 0, 7) for the schizonts. The total duration of the P. vivax erythrocytic cycle was estimated as 44.5 hours (95% CI 29, 62).
The trophozoite stages of P. vivax are almost completely resistant to CQ, and continue to mature no matter how high the concentration of drug [15], [16].
There was a significant negative association between the CQ assay duration (hours) and the ln (EC50) values for P. vivax (r2 = 0.219, p<0.001; Figure 1a).
The proportion of rings at the start of the assay was also significantly negatively associated with the ln(EC50) values for ... P. vivax (r2 = 0.245, p<0.001).
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.
34.Rationale: The passage gives stage duration estimates: trophozoites (23.1 h) + schizonts (2.2 h) = 25.3 h, which is directly derived from the simulation results. The text also documents that trophozoites are nearly completely CQ-resistant and shows statistical associations between assay duration / initial stage composition and ln(EC50), and that samples with fewer rings have higher and more variable EC50. Together these statements support the inference that the prolonged period spent as trophozoites/schizonts (≈25.3 h) relates to variability in EC50 across isolates.

34.Option: Because P. falciparum isolates are more synchronous and predominantly contain ring stage parasites, the SMT is more reliable for P. falciparum than for P. vivax, and thus should be prioritized in drug susceptibility testing.
Evidence: The SMT was initially developed for testing drug susceptibility in P. falciparum [13], [14], where almost all parasites in the peripheral circulation are at the immature ring stage.

There was a significantly higher mean proportion of ring stage parasites in samples at the start of the assay (0 hours) for P. falciparum (0.922) compared to P. vivax (0.588) (Wilcoxon rank sum test, p<0.001). 87.5% (189/216) of P. falciparum isolates contained 100% ring stage parasites at 0 hours, compared to only 2.8% (4/141) of P. vivax isolates.
34.Rationale: While the passage documents that P. falciparum samples are more synchronous with a higher proportion of ring stages and that the SMT was developed for P. falciparum, it does not explicitly state that the SMT is categorically more reliable for P. falciparum nor that P. falciparum testing 'should be prioritized'. The recommendation to prioritize one species over another is prescriptive and not directly derived from the presented data. Therefore the claim as a whole (including the prioritization) is not supported solely by the passage.

34.Option: The emergence of highly chloroquine-resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia has created an urgent need for improved understanding of drug resistance mechanisms and the development of new antimalarial agents.
Evidence: Further, the emergence of highly chloroquine (CQ) resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia (CQ remains a front-line treatment for vivax malaria as it is affordable, well tolerated and safe, and its long half-life ensures protection from early relapses [7], [8]) has created an urgent need for an improved understanding of the mechanisms of drug resistance in these parasites, the development of robust tools for defining the spread of resistance, and the discovery of new antimalarial agents.
34.Rationale: This sentence is stated directly in the passage as background context. Although it is not a methodological consideration per se, the claim is explicitly presented in the text and therefore is derivable from the passage.

34.Option: The use of mixed-effects modelling in the dose-response analysis for CQ data ensures that inter-isolate variability is accounted for, which improves the accuracy of EC50 estimates in P. vivax isolates.
Evidence: The sigmoid Emax dose-response curve (equation 1) was fitted to all available data simultaneously, with mixed-effects modelling used for CQ data.

Inter-isolate variability was included for Emax, EC50 and γ. The drug plate batch was also incorporated as a random effect, to control for batch-to-batch variability. Predicted values for EC50 for each isolate were calculated from Empirical Bayes estimates.

EC50 values for parasite samples collected from April 2004 and May 2007 have previously been reported (Russell 2008). However the methodology used to calculate EC50 for CQ differs between the previous report and the current study due to the use of mixed-effects modelling.
34.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mixed-effects modelling was used and that inter-isolate variability and plate batch were modelled as random effects, which shows that the modelling accounts for inter-isolate variability. However, the passage does not explicitly claim or demonstrate that this 'ensures' improved accuracy of EC50 estimates (no direct validation or comparison is provided). The improvement in accuracy is plausible but not directly proven within the text, so the stronger claim that it 'ensures ... improves the accuracy' is not fully supported by the passage alone.

34.Option: Assay duration and the proportion of ring stage parasites in the initial sample are critical factors that influence the accuracy of chloroquine susceptibility testing in P. vivax, and insufficient exposure of ring stage parasites to the drug can lead to artificially inflated EC50 values.

It follows that, if a drug only acts on ring stage parasites, but the sample contains a majority of trophozoites and schizonts, the parasite is likely to be erroneously categorised as resistant simply because there were no susceptible life cycle stages present in the assay.
There was a significant negative association between the CQ assay duration (hours) and the ln (EC50) values for P. vivax (r2 = 0.219, p<0.001; Figure 1a).
The proportion of rings at the start of the assay was also significantly negatively associated with the ln(EC50) values for ... P. vivax (r2 = 0.245, p<0.001).
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.
34.Rationale: The passage directly links assay duration and initial parasite stage composition to EC50 estimates: valid SMT results require sufficient drug exposure; trophozoites are largely CQ-resistant; samples dominated by non-ring stages can be misclassified as resistant; and statistical analyses show significant negative associations between assay duration / proportion of rings and ln(EC50) for P. vivax, with samples <65% rings showing higher and more variable EC50. These statements together support the methodological consideration described in the option.

35.Option: Although the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is often associated with gene copy number variations, regulatory variations in upstream regions of these genes also contribute to their up-regulation, as evidenced by allele frequency correlations with transcription levels and predicted effects on transcription factor binding.
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).
Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table).

These included two variations likely to have a major effect on the binding of enhancers: the variation at position -642 (respective to ATG) affecting the gene CYP6P12-like AAEL014891 favoring the binding of the HNF-1A element and the variation at position -410 affecting the gene CYP6CB1-like AAEL009018 favoring the binding of the HNF-3 element.

Together these genomic and transcriptomic findings support the inference that both gene dosage (CNV) and regulatory upstream variations contribute to up-regulation of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations.

35.Option: The over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is primarily driven by non-synonymous mutations in the coding regions of detoxification genes, as these mutations are significantly enriched in resistant strains and may directly enhance insecticide metabolism.
Evidence: Nearly half of them (5,857) were located within annotated genes with 625 being non-synonymous (NS Diff SNPs) affecting 436 annotated genes.
Diff SNPs were significantly enriched among detoxification enzymes such as P450s (2.5 fold), CCEs (9.5 fold) and UGTs (1.9 fold).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

Among all NS Diff SNPs affecting detoxification enzymes, none were located in the potential substrate binding sites of CCEs, GSTs and UGTs while seven were located in P450 substrate recognition sites (SRS).

Comparing the transcription profiles of the 20 detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations revealed their frequent conservation within regions while more divergence was observed between continents (Fig 2).
35.Rationale: While the passage documents many non-synonymous differential SNPs and an enrichment of such variants among detoxification genes, it does not support the claim that NS coding mutations are the primary driver of over-expression. The text explicitly shows that CNVs correlate with expression for several genes and that regulatory (upstream) variations also correlate with transcription levels. Moreover, most NS Diff SNPs were not located in substrate binding sites for several enzyme families, suggesting they are not universally functional in enhancing metabolism. Therefore the passage does not justify that NS coding mutations are the primary mechanism driving over-expression.

35.Option: The presence of kdr mutations in resistant populations is sufficient to explain the observed over-expression of detoxification enzymes, as these mutations are consistently found in all resistant strains and are known to confer pyrethroid resistance.

Asian populations carried the S989P, 1016G and F1534C mutations at medium to high frequencies (14 to 68%; 21 to 58% and 22 to 75% respectively) [28].

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.
35.Rationale: The passage distinguishes kdr (target-site) resistance from metabolic resistance and treats them as separate mechanisms. It describes kdr mutation frequencies in populations but provides no evidence that kdr mutations cause over-expression of detoxification enzymes. In fact, the study focuses on genomic (CNV, upstream regulatory) and transcriptomic correlates of metabolic resistance, implying that kdr presence alone is insufficient to explain detoxification gene up-regulation. Thus the claim of sufficiency is not supported by the passage.

35.Option: The identification of copy number variations (CNVs) in detoxification gene clusters provides a reliable and universally applicable method for monitoring metabolic resistance in Aedes aegypti populations across all geographic regions.
Evidence: However, studies performed in different geographical areas often highlighted different candidate genes [20, 22], questionning the universality of genes associated with metabolic resistance.

Comparing the transcription profiles of the 20 detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations revealed their frequent conservation within regions while more divergence was observed between continents (Fig 2).

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.
35.Rationale: Although CNVs are shown to correlate with expression for several detoxification genes and many over-transcribed genes lie in CNV-affected clusters, the passage also emphasizes geographic variability: different studies and populations highlight different genes and transcription profiles diverge between continents. Moreover, CNV does not explain all over-expression, necessitating examination of upstream regulatory variants. Therefore CNV identification cannot be considered a universally applicable lone method for monitoring metabolic resistance across all regions based solely on the passage.

35.Option: The up-regulation of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is solely due to the selection of transcription factor binding sites in upstream regions, as the identified variations show strong correlations with gene expression and are predicted to enhance binding of known enhancer elements.
Evidence: Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).
Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table).
