Therefore the specific claim of complete conservation and the rescue demonstration cannot be derived from the passage.

139.Option: The use of siRNA in filarial nematodes has been shown to be effective in reducing microfilariae release and inducing embryonic abnormalities, suggesting that siRNA may be a more efficient RNAi tool than dsRNA for targeting filarial genes.

the siRNA appeared in these regions earlier and was clearly already seen after 24 h

Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms, these reductions were significantly different from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0006, siRNA; P<0.0001) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0033, siRNA; P<0.0001).

The Bm-cpl transcript levels were also reduced in Bm-cpl Pro treated worms; 66.5% and 37.4% in dsRNA and siRNA treated worms respectively, with significant differences from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0003, siRNA; P = 0.002) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0016, siRNA; P = 0.0082).

Thus while siRNA is effective, the passage does not demonstrate that it is more efficient than dsRNA, so the comparative claim is not supported.

adult female worms treated with Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA or siRNA showed significant reductions in pre-microfilariae (pre-Mf); Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA; 6.8% ... Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA; 9.0% ...

Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms ... The Bm-cpl transcript levels were also reduced in Bm-cpl Pro treated worms; 66.5% and 37.4% in dsRNA and siRNA treated worms respectively ...

139.Option: The observed reduction in microfilariae release following RNAi treatment is likely due to non-specific off-target effects of dsRNA, as control dsRNA also caused a measurable reduction in Mf secretion.

Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms, these reductions were significantly different from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0006, siRNA; P<0.0001) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0033, siRNA; P<0.0001).

In comparison, the Bm-cpl-3 (89% identical to Bm-cpl-2 and -7) and Bm-cpl-6 transcripts that belong to the Ic subgroup of the B. malayi cysteine protease protein family were not reduced in the Bm-cpl Pro treated worms (data not shown).

139.Rationale: The passage shows that the negative control dsRNA (Ov-cpz-int2) produced a reduction that was not statistically different from medium controls (quote 1), arguing against a general non-specific effect of dsRNA. In contrast, dsRNA/siRNA targeting Bm-cpl produced significant transcript reductions relative to both medium and negative controls (quote 2), and transcripts of unrelated subgroup Ic genes were not reduced (quote 3), supporting target-specific effects. The authors explicitly link the phenotypic effects to reductions in group Ia Bm-cpl transcripts (quote 4). Therefore the claim that effects are likely due to non-specific off-target dsRNA effects is not supported by the passage.

139.Option: The B. malayi cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes are primarily involved in yolk protein processing during embryogenesis, as suggested by their localization in yolk vesicles and the embryonic lethality observed after RNAi.

These data demonstrate that the inhibition of Mf secretion in vitro, death of Mf, and changes in intrauterine progeny following dsRNA-mediated silencing is likely associated with reductions in the Bm-cpl gene specific transcript levels belonging to group Ia.
139.Rationale: While the passage reports predicted functions from IEM and documents embryonic abnormalities and embryo/Mf lethality after RNAi (quotes 2–3), it does not provide direct evidence that the Bm-cpl genes are 'primarily involved in yolk protein processing' nor does it state localization in yolk vesicles. The passage explicitly notes that CPL functions during embryogenesis were only predicted indirectly by IEM (quote 1), indicating this mechanism was not established by the experiments reported. Therefore the specific inference that these genes primarily function in yolk protein processing is not supported by the presented experimental outcomes.

140.Option: The HTLV-1 proviral load in the CSF as well as the ratio of the proviral load in the CSF to that in PBMCs have been reported to be effective for discriminating HAM/TSP patients from ACs or multiple sclerosis patients infected with HTLV-1, and may also be associated with the rate of disease progression.

The comparative analyses and ROC results presented focus on CSF chemokines, neopterin, cell count, and PBMC proviral load; there is no evidence in the passage that CSF proviral load or a CSF/PBMC proviral load ratio was evaluated or reported as an effective discriminator or predictor of progression. Therefore the specific claim about CSF proviral load and its ratio to PBMC proviral load is not supported by the passage.

140.Option: The CSF cell count is the most sensitive and specific biomarker for predicting HAM/TSP disease progression due to its high AUC in ROC analysis.

It should be noted that the sensitivity of CSF cell count was very low (36.7%) when compared to the other three: CXCL10 (83.3%), CXCL9 (86.7%), and neopterin (76.7%) (Figure S5).

ROC analysis revealed that the levels of the CSF markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and cell count) ... distinguished clearly between patients with deteriorating HAM/TSP and stable HAM/TSP (AUC>0.8, Figure 3B).
140.Rationale: Although CSF cell count had an AUC>0.8 in some analyses, the passage explicitly states that CSF cell count had very low sensitivity (36.7%) compared with other CSF markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin). Thus CSF cell count is not the most sensitive marker and the passage does not support the claim that it is the single best (most sensitive and specific) predictor of progression.

The results revealed that all five CSF markers were significantly higher in the deteriorating group compared to the stable group (Figure 3A), but that none of the four blood markers, including proviral load, were significantly different between the two groups.

ROC analysis revealed that the levels of the CSF markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and cell count) ... distinguished clearly between patients with deteriorating HAM/TSP and stable HAM/TSP (AUC>0.8, Figure 3B).

To validate the results obtained using the Training Set, the same nine markers were compared between deteriorating and stable patients using the Test Set ... the results indicated that the levels of five CSF markers, proviral load in PBMCs, and serum sIL-2R were significantly higher in deteriorating cases than in stable cases. Among them, CSF levels of CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and CSF cell count exhibited particularly high sensitivities and specificities for detecting the deteriorating HAM/TSP cases in the Test set as well as Training set (AUC>0.8, Figures 4B and S1).

We confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups. The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that CSF markers (chemokines CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and cell count), which reflect inflammatory activity in the spinal cord, are significantly elevated in deteriorating versus stable patients and have good discriminative performance (AUC>0.8). By contrast, blood PBMC proviral load did not distinguish deteriorating from stable patients in the Training set and only showed significance in the Test set, whereas CSF markers consistently associated with progression including in analyses controlling for disease duration. The passage also explicitly notes neopterin in CSF as a marker of cell-mediated immune responses in the spinal cord. These facts support the inference that progression rate is more closely reflected by inflammatory activity in the spinal cord (CSF markers) than by PBMC proviral load.

140.Option: The lack of significant differences in blood markers between deteriorating and stable HAM/TSP patients suggests that systemic immune activation is not a key driver of disease progression.
Evidence: The results revealed that all five CSF markers were significantly higher in the deteriorating group compared to the stable group (Figure 3A), but that none of the four blood markers, including proviral load, were significantly different between the two groups.

To validate the results obtained using the Training Set, the same nine markers were compared between deteriorating and stable patients using the Test Set ... the results indicated that the levels of five CSF markers, proviral load in PBMCs, and serum sIL-2R were significantly higher in deteriorating cases than in stable cases.
140.Rationale: The passage contains inconsistent findings regarding blood markers: in the Training set there were no significant differences in blood markers (including proviral load) between deteriorating and stable patients, but in the Test set proviral load in PBMCs and serum sIL-2R were significantly higher in deteriorating cases. Given these mixed results, the passage does not support a definitive inference that systemic immune activation is not a key driver of progression. At best the data indicate that CSF markers more consistently reflect progression; they do not establish that systemic immune activation is unimportant.

140.Option: The clinical course of HAM/TSP has classically been described as insidious onset and continuous progression, but recent evidence suggests that the rate of disease progression is primarily determined by the presence of Th17 cells and their associated chemokines.
Evidence: The clinical course of HAM/TSP has classically been described very simply as insidious onset and continuous progression [12], but recent reports have hinted at a more complex, heterogeneous pool of patients with differing clinical needs.

CSF IL-17A was detectable (>3.0 pg/mL) in only one of eight HAM/TSP patients screened (including six deteriorating-type patients), and the level in this one patient (deteriorating-type) was negligible (4.0 pg/mL).

CSF IFN-γ was detectable (>1.8 pg/mL) in only 3 of 10 HAM/TSP patients screened (six deteriorating patients), and the levels in all three were negligible (range 3.3–4.2 pg/mL). Therefore, these cytokines were not measured in additional patients.
140.Rationale: While the passage notes that the classical description is 'insidious onset and continuous progression' and that more complex heterogeneous progression patterns exist, it does not provide evidence that Th17 cells determine progression. In fact, IL-17A (a Th17-associated cytokine) was detectable in only one screened patient at a negligible level, and the authors did not pursue IL-17A further. The biomarkers that did associate with progression were CXCL9, CXCL10, neopterin, and cell count—chemokines and markers more indicative of cell-mediated/Th1-type inflammation. Therefore the specific claim that Th17 cells and their chemokines primarily determine progression is not supported by the passage.

141.Option: Eosinophilia is a strong independent predictor of pneumothorax in patients with pulmonary paragonimiasis, as both eosinophil count and percentage were significantly higher in the pneumothorax group.
Evidence: There was a significant difference in the eosinophil count and eosinophil percent between the two groups, (3.5±2.6 vs. 1.8±3.6, p = 0.045; 32.1±15.1 vs. 16.7±18.0, p<0.001).
Age, height, dyspnea, pleural effusion, eosinophil (1,000/mm3), and eosinophil (%) were removed.

Therefore the study does not support eosinophilia as a strong independent predictor; it was not retained as an independent risk factor in the multivariate model.

141.Option: The presence of pleural effusion is a significant predictor of pneumothorax in patients with pulmonary paragonimiasis, as it was observed in 78.9% of cases with pneumothorax and was significantly more common than in the non-pneumothorax group.

Age, height, dyspnea, pleural effusion, eosinophil (1,000/mm3), and eosinophil (%) were removed.
141.Rationale: The passage shows a strong univariate association between pleural effusion and pneumothorax (significantly more common in the pneumothorax group). However, pleural effusion was removed before multivariate analysis and thus was not demonstrated as an independent predictor in the final model. The study supports association but not independent predictive status.

141.Option: Chest pain, asthma, and intrapulmonary lesions are clinically important predictive risk factors for pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis.
Evidence: A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).

141.Rationale: The passage reports multivariate analysis results explicitly identifying asthma, chest pain, and intrapulmonary lesions as independent risk factors (with corresponding ORs and 95% CIs). This directly supports the inference that these factors are clinically important predictive risk factors for pneumothorax in pulmonary paragonimiasis.

141.Option: The high frequency of pneumothorax in patients with pulmonary paragonimiasis—10.6%—suggests that the disease should be routinely screened for in all patients presenting with spontaneous pneumothorax in endemic regions.
Evidence: Patients aged 19 years or older diagnosed with paragonimiasis between May 2011 and December 2021 were included.
During the study period, a total of 179 consecutive patients with pulmonary paragonimiasis were identified.
Of these patients, 19 (10.6%) were classified as having pneumothorax associated with paragonimiasis, with the remaining 160 (89.4%) not having pneumothorax.
141.Rationale: The study provides the proportion of paragonimiasis patients who developed pneumothorax (10.6%) but does not assess the inverse question (the proportion of spontaneous pneumothorax patients who have paragonimiasis). Because the study population was patients already diagnosed with paragonimiasis, one cannot infer from these data alone that routine screening of all spontaneous pneumothorax patients in endemic regions is indicated.

141.Option: Previous pneumothorax is the strongest risk factor for pneumothorax in patients with pulmonary paragonimiasis, with an odds ratio of 9.29, indicating a high likelihood of recurrence.
Evidence: Previous pneumothorax (odds ratio [OR] 9.29, 95% confidence interval [CI] 1.23–70.26), asthma (OR 5.81, 95% CI 1.27–26.61), chest pain (OR 6.77, 95% CI 2.49–18.42), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 10.24, 95% CI 1.33–78.66) were statistically significant (Table 4).

141.Rationale: While previous pneumothorax had a high univariate OR (9.29), it was not retained as an independent risk factor in the multivariate analysis; intrapulmonary lesions had a higher univariate OR (10.24). Therefore the passage does not support the claim that previous pneumothorax is the strongest independent risk factor or alone indicates a high likelihood of recurrence.

143.Option: The presence of non-typhoidal Salmonella in the feces of apparently healthy animals and humans, as well as in milk and environmental samples, indicates that asymptomatic carriers and contaminated food and environments are key reservoirs for potential transmission to the public.
Evidence: All humans and animals sampled were apparently healthy and were not taking antimicrobials at the time of sampling.

The overall proportion of NTS was 7.7% with a 95% confidence interval of 5.4–11.0%. The highest proportion of NTS was observed in cows’ feces (11.9%), whereas the lowest was in the milker’s hand swab samples (1.7%).
Among the human samples (n = 58) (stool and/or hand swabs), 3 (5.2%) NTS isolates were detected, and all were isolated from males, and ages ranged from 18 to 30 years.
The dominant serovar (S. Uganda) was isolated from pooled raw milk, milk containers, sewage, and cow’s fecal samples.
The majority of NTS infections in humans are associated with the consumption of contaminated food, unprotected contact with infected animals, and a contaminated environment.
Cattle are an important reservoir of NTS that plays a role in the contamination of foods from animal sources like meat and milk and the environment
143.Rationale: The passage documents NTS detection across apparently healthy animals and humans, in pooled raw milk, milk containers, sewage, and cow feces, and explicitly states cattle are an important reservoir and that human infections are associated with contaminated food, animal contact, and environment. These facts together support the epidemiological inference that asymptomatic carriage and contaminated food/environmental reservoirs can play key roles in transmission to the public.

143.Option: The presence of NTS in raw milk and sewage suggests that the primary route of transmission to humans is through contaminated water sources rather than direct contact with animals or consumption of raw milk.
Evidence: The dominant serovar (S. Uganda) was isolated from pooled raw milk, milk containers, sewage, and cow’s fecal samples.
The majority of NTS infections in humans are associated with the consumption of contaminated food, unprotected contact with infected animals, and a contaminated environment.
Most of the farms were small-sized (82.8%), with zero grazing (87.9%), used tap water as the primary water source (77.6%), and used plastic non-food grade containers (58.6%) for milking and storage.
The detection proportions were not statistically significant among herd size, feed sources, primary water sources, and commonly used milking container types of farms.
143.Rationale: While the passage shows NTS presence in raw milk and sewage, it does not provide evidence that contaminated water is the primary transmission route. The authors list multiple transmission pathways (contaminated food, animal contact, contaminated environment) and report NTS in milk, animals, humans, and sewage. There is no direct data or analysis in the passage demonstrating that waterborne transmission predominates over direct animal contact or milk consumption, so the specific claim that contaminated water is the primary route cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

143.Option: The high prevalence of multidrug-resistant non-typhoidal Salmonella in humans, animals, and the environment suggests that antimicrobial resistance is primarily driven by the overuse of antibiotics in human healthcare settings.
Evidence: Among the NTS isolates tested, 89.3% were resistant to one or more classes, and 46.4% were resistant to more than two classes of antimicrobial agents (multi-drug resistance (MDR)).
The result also showed that MDR isolates were isolated from animals, humans, milk, and the environment.
The development of AMR is highly related to the overuse and misuse of antimicrobials, which creates a selective pressure so that the susceptible group diminishes and resistant ones flourish.
143.Rationale: The passage documents substantial AMR and MDR across sources but does not provide evidence attributing the primary driver to overuse in human healthcare specifically. The text gives a general statement that AMR is related to overuse/misuse of antimicrobials (without specifying sector), and it demonstrates AMR in animals, milk, humans, and environment. Inferring that human healthcare settings are the primary driver requires additional data on antibiotic use patterns in humans versus animals or the environment, which the passage does not present. Therefore the option overreaches beyond the provided evidence.

143.Option: The lack of statistically significant association between NTS occurrence and farm size, water source, or milking container type indicates that hygiene practices in dairy farms have no impact on Salmonella transmission.
Evidence: The detection proportions were not statistically significant among herd size, feed sources, primary water sources, and commonly used milking container types of farms.

143.Rationale: The passage reports no statistically significant associations for certain measured farm variables in this study, but epidemiologically absence of evidence in a single study does not prove absence of effect. The passage supports absence of productive SHUV replication, but not the broader conclusion that Cx. p. pipiens cannot support any stage of arbovirus replication or life cycles in general.

The passage therefore does not support the conclusion that Cx. p. pipiens is a competent vector capable of sustaining and spreading SHUV in nature.

Therefore the claim that glucose increases during recovery and is directly responsible is not supported by the passage.

no significant difference was observed on the glucose levels between IHC5°C-0h and IHC5°C-3h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), while the glucose levels of males which have been chilled for over 3 h was significantly decreased (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, 6 h: P < 0.01; 12 h: P < 0.0001; 24 h: P < 0.0001).

The passage gives an observed discrepancy (many clinic dogs with laboratory abnormalities but few PCR positives), but it does not state that PCR is limited for subclinical or chronic infections or that blood PCR fails for chronic stages. Because the passage emphasizes PCR sensitivity and advantages for early detection rather than documenting a limitation for chronic/subclinical detection, the assertion that the low PCR prevalence is due to PCR’s inability to detect chronic/subclinical infections is not directly supported by the passage.

153.Option: Serological tests may have been more effective than PCR in detecting E. canis in this study, as they are more sensitive in identifying chronic or past infections, which could explain the discrepancy between clinical signs and PCR results.
Evidence: Currently serological tests are the most commonly practiced method for diagnosis of E. canis infection. These serological tests reflect the quantity of antibodies present in the serum and therefore indicate exposure but not the severity of disease and the duration of infection [11].
Furthermore, antibodies are usually absent during the first two weeks of onset [11].
False negative results are another common feature of serological tests and may occur due to the early stage of the disease and lack of antibody which may further impact the final diagnosis [13].

Conversely, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a sensitive method of detection of acute monocytic ehrlichiosis in dogs; in fact it is designed to aim for the organism itself which makes PCR an invaluable technique capable of detecting traces of pathogen even before the onset of clinical signs [14].
Among the clinic cases 27 (8.3%) dogs had both thrombocytopenia and anemia, but only one of them was positive for E. canis.
153.Rationale: The passage explains that serology indicates exposure (past or chronic infection) because it detects antibodies, whereas PCR targets the organism and is particularly useful for acute/early detection (even before antibodies develop). Given the observed mismatch (clinical/laboratory signs in multiple dogs but few PCR positives), an explanation consistent with the passage is that serology—by detecting past or chronic exposure—would identify infections that PCR (focused on current/acute bacteremia) might miss. The quoted text supports that serology and PCR detect different phases (exposure/antibody vs. organism), making this explanation derivable from the passage.

153.Option: The absence of E. canis DNA in dogs with typical clinical signs of CME may be due to the fact that these dogs were infected with other ehrlichial species, such as E. ewingii or A. phagocytophilum, which are known to cause similar hematological manifestations.
Evidence: Some closely related pathogens, including Ehrlichia ewingii, Ehrlichia chaffeensis, Anaplasma phagocytophilum and Neorickettsia risticii, are shown to cause similar clinical and hematological manifestations in dogs as well [2], [5].
These serological tests reflect the quantity of antibodies present in the serum and therefore indicate exposure but not the severity of disease and the duration of infection [11].
Additionally antibodies against several other ehrlichial organisms might cross-react with E. canis and complicate the serological diagnosis [12].
Among the clinic cases 27 (8.3%) dogs had both thrombocytopenia and anemia, but only one of them was positive for E. canis.
153.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that several closely related organisms cause similar clinical and hematological signs in dogs. The observed pattern—clinical signs consistent with CME but low PCR detection of E. canis—can therefore be plausibly explained (and is consistent with the passage) by infection with other ehrlichial species that produce similar findings. The passage also notes potential serological cross-reactivity among ehrlichial organisms, supporting the possibility of alternative etiologies.

153.Option: The low PCR detection rate may reflect a lack of transmission of E. canis in Malaysia, as the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus may not be present or infected in the region.
Evidence: E. canis is transmitted by the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus [3], [4].

Five hundred blood samples (323 clinic cases and 177 stray dogs) were evaluated using PCR in this study out of which ten were identified as E. canis positive, giving an overall prevalence rate of two (2.0%) percent.
This is the first confirmed detection of E. canis DNA from dogs in Malaysia.
153.Rationale: The passage confirms that E. canis DNA was detected in Malaysia (overall prevalence 2.0%) and explicitly calls this the first confirmed detection in Malaysian dogs. Detection of E. canis DNA demonstrates that transmission has occurred in the region and that the vector (brown dog tick) is at least functionally present/involved. The option's claim of lack of transmission or absence of infected ticks is therefore contradicted by the study's findings and not supported by the passage.

154.Option: The national health information system in Ethiopia is fully capable of capturing and tracking noma cases, as evidenced by the inclusion of noma-related data in the HMIS for all health facilities.
Evidence: The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor.
Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.
154.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the HMIS is still in scale-up and does not include indicators for acute noma cases or sequelae. Therefore the system is not fully capable of capturing and tracking noma cases across health facilities, and the option claiming full capability is not supported by the text.

154.Option: The declining trend in the number of noma cases receiving care from 2015 to 2020 reflects a genuine reduction in the incidence of noma in Ethiopia, likely due to improved public health interventions and increased awareness.
Evidence: In the last 10 years, I have seen about 300 cases, but since 2020, we are seeing fewer noma cases.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children.
The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020, with 97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission.
154.Rationale: Although a clinician noted fewer cases seen since 2020, the passage provides multiple reasons why reported case counts may not reflect true incidence (underdetection, limited diagnostic capacity, lack of routine surveillance, reliance on NGO reporting). The available data (mostly NGO-documented cases) and pervasive underreporting mean a decrease in reported cases cannot be reliably interpreted as a genuine reduction in incidence based solely on the passage.

154.Option: The high number of noma cases reported from Amhara and Somali regions indicates that these regions have the most advanced health systems and best access to specialized care for noma patients.
Evidence: The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020... The recorded patient address indicated that 31 patients are from Amhara region, 9 from Somalie...
The selection of hospitals was done based on convenience, with focus on institutions that provide plastic surgery, oral care, and dermatology services in each region.
97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission
Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors...
154.Rationale: The passage shows case counts are heavily influenced by where NGOs and convenience-sampled hospitals operate and by underdetection. There is no evidence that higher reported counts reflect better health system capacity or access; rather, reporting bias and NGO activity likely explain geographic patterns. Thus the inference that these regions have the most advanced health systems is not supported.

154.Option: The integration of noma into existing non-communicable disease and nutrition programs would be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management of acute cases across Ethiopia’s primary health care system.
Evidence: Eighty-five percent of the participant stated that there is no oral health program in the country’s health system.

All participant agreed on the limitation of on the availability of human resource for oral health and poor capacity of the health institution for integrating of oral health program in to the primary health care unit.
Currently there is no pre-service training or continuous professional development for oral/dental care in the country.
Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.
154.Rationale: While some respondents suggested potential integration options, the passage documents major gaps (no oral health program, limited human resources, lack of training, and absence of HMIS indicators). These deficits indicate that mere integration into existing NCD/nutrition programs would not, by itself, be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management without substantial investments in workforce, training, surveillance, and system capacity.

154.Option: The absence of acute noma case records in the national health information system underscores the urgent need for routine surveillance programs and improved data collection at the primary health care level.
Evidence: The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor.
Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children.
Comprehensive data on disease burden and epidemiology are important for planning and prioritizing service delivery, as well as for formulating disease prevention strategies.
154.Rationale: The passage directly states that HMIS lacks noma indicators and documents widespread underdetection and absence of routine surveillance. It also emphasizes the importance of comprehensive data for planning. Therefore it is reasonable and directly supported by the text to infer an urgent need for routine surveillance and improved primary-level data collection to monitor and respond to acute noma cases.

155.Option: The limited reactivity of antivenom with disintegrins and the DC fragments of SVMPs, which are recognized as inhibitors of platelet aggregation, suggests that these components are not critical in the pathophysiology of envenoming.
Evidence: SVMPs are multifunctional enzymes involved in the local and systemic symptoms of bites, such as the induction of local hemorrhage, inflammatory reaction, activation of coagulation factors and inhibition of platelet aggregation [35].

The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition. Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage does not state that antivenom has limited reactivity with disintegrins or DC fragments of SVMPs, nor does it conclude that these components are not critical to pathophysiology. Instead, the passage explicitly describes SVMPs as multifunctional and involved in inhibition of platelet aggregation (supporting their pathophysiological relevance) and states that antivenoms react equally with toxins from the same protein family. There is no evidence in the passage that reactivity with disintegrins/DC fragments is limited or that such limited reactivity implies lack of pathophysiological importance; therefore the option cannot be derived from the passage.

155.Option: The antivenom's efficacy in neutralizing B. jararacussu venom is lower than that for B. jararaca venom due to the absence of B. jararacussu venom in the immunization pool, despite similar toxin profiles.
Evidence: The antibothropic serum (SAB) was produced at the Instituto Butantan, São Paulo, Brazil in horses immunized with a mixture of the following venoms: B. jararaca (50%), B. neuwiedi (12.5%), R. alternatus (12.5%), B. moojeni (12.5%) and B. jararacussu (12.5%).

The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: This option asserts that B. jararacussu venom was absent from the immunization pool and that neutralization efficacy against it is lower. The passage explicitly lists B. jararacussu as included (12.5%) in the immunization mixture, so the premise is false. Additionally, the passage states antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of presence in the immunization pool, which undermines the claim that absence would explain lower efficacy. The passage contains no data showing lower neutralization of B. jararacussu versus B. jararaca; therefore the option is not supported.

155.Option: Despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB, the antivenom is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom.
Evidence: The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition. Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes that B. atrox venom (not present in the immunization pool) was included in neutralization assessments and states that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of phylogeny or whether the venom was included in the immunization pool. From these statements it is reasonable to infer that SAB can efficiently neutralize the principal effects of B. atrox venom despite its absence from the immunizing mixture. Thus this inference is directly supported by the passage.

155.Option: The cross-reactivity of the antivenom with P-III SVMPs across species is due to the high sequence homology of these toxins, which is confirmed by the structural similarity of their catalytic domains.
Evidence: The venom composition of many species of Bothrops complex is already known by venomics [13]–[27] or indirectly by transcriptomics [28]–[32].

The variability in venom composition is notable and can be correlated with phylogeny [36], [37], age [38], [39], sex [40], geographical distribution [13], [40], [41] and diet [42]–[44] of the snake. However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: While the passage documents cross-reactivity of antivenom with toxins from the same protein family across species, it does not provide molecular evidence (e.g., sequence homology data or structural analyses of catalytic domains) to attribute cross-reactivity specifically to high sequence homology or confirmed structural similarity of catalytic domains. The passage discusses venomics and variability in expression levels, but it does not present direct sequence or structural confirmation linking P-III SVMP cross-reactivity to catalytic domain similarity. Therefore the causal claim in this option is not supported by the provided text.

155.Option: The antivenom's neutralization of hemorrhagic activity is less effective in B. atrox venom than in B. jararaca venom due to differences in the expression levels of P-III SVMPs.
Evidence: The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

The variability in venom composition is notable and can be correlated with phylogeny [36], [37], age [38], [39], sex [40], geographical distribution [13], [40], [41] and diet [42]–[44] of the snake. However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage does not present experimental results demonstrating that hemorrhagic neutralization is less effective for B. atrox than for B. jararaca, nor does it link any observed difference in neutralization specifically to P-III SVMP expression levels. In fact, the passage states that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of phylogeny or inclusion in the immunization pool, which contradicts the assertion of reduced effectiveness for B. atrox. While the passage notes that venom variability is often due to expression level differences, it does not provide data connecting such differences to reduced hemorrhagic neutralization by SAB. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

156.Option: The experimental model demonstrates that ST313 infection in chickens leads to prolonged gastrointestinal inflammation, mirroring the chronic enteropathy seen in human iNTS patients.
Evidence: Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).

At 7 days post-infection, little intestinal inflammation was seen in the D23580 infected group and the inflammation-associated architectural disturbance had largely resolved, whereas the 4/74 infected group continued to show inflammation, flattening of the villi and crypt hyperplasia at significantly higher levels than in the D23580-infected group (P = 0.014) (Table 2).
156.Rationale: The passage shows ST313 (D23580) elicits an early, strong intestinal inflammatory response at day 3 but that this inflammation largely resolves by day 7. That pattern is transient rather than prolonged. The passage therefore does not support an inference that ST313 leads to prolonged gastrointestinal inflammation mirroring chronic enteropathy in humans.

156.Option: Chickens are the primary natural host of ST313, as evidenced by the high levels of systemic invasion and intestinal colonization observed in the experimental model.
Evidence: Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.

Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580), though both ST19 isolates gave similar levels of colonization.
Consequently, it has been suggested that ST313 is restricted to human infection, or at least adapted to a niche of immunosuppressed HIV or malaria-infected patients [1], [3].
156.Rationale: While the experimental data demonstrate that ST313 can infect chickens, colonize the ceca and rapidly invade systemic sites under experimental conditions, the passage explicitly notes lower intestinal colonization compared with ST19 and cites suggestions in the literature that ST313 may be human-restricted. Experimental infectivity in a laboratory model does not by itself demonstrate that chickens are the primary natural host in the field; the passage does not provide epidemiological evidence that chickens are the natural reservoir.

156.Option: The absence of epidemiological evidence for a zoonotic source of ST313 in Africa confirms that the pathotype is strictly human-adapted and incapable of sustained transmission through animal reservoirs.
Evidence: epidemiological investigation has been unable to determine an environmental or zoonotic source of iNTS, with transmission being considered likely to be through direct or indirect human-to-human routes and with asymptomatic carriage potentially playing a role [1], [3], [6].
Consequently, it has been suggested that ST313 is restricted to human infection, or at least adapted to a niche of immunosuppressed HIV or malaria-infected patients [1], [3].
However, potential domestic or wild animal sources of infection have been incompletely investigated.
156.Rationale: The passage states there is currently no epidemiological identification of an environmental or zoonotic source and that ST313 has been suggested to be human-restricted, but it also explicitly notes that potential animal sources have been incompletely investigated. Absence of evidence in the passage does not equate to confirmation that the pathotype is strictly human-adapted or incapable of animal transmission; that definitive conclusion is not supported by the presented data.

156.Option: Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chickens to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
Evidence: All ST19 and ST313 isolates colonized the ceca of the chicken by 3 days post-infection and remained colonized at 7 days post-infection (Figure 1A).

Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580), though both ST19 isolates gave similar levels of colonization.

Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
Although there is currently no epidemiological evidence that birds are a source of ST313 infection, they are perhaps the most likely zoonotic source of Salmonella.
156.Rationale: The passage documents that ST313 can colonize chicken intestines (albeit at lower levels than ST19) and rapidly invades systemic sites in experimentally infected birds. The authors also note that birds are plausible zoonotic sources and that animal sources have not been fully investigated. Therefore it is epidemiologically reasonable, based on the experimental findings presented, to infer that chickens could act as a potential zoonotic source despite reduced intestinal fitness compared with ST19. The statement is cautious ("potential source") and is supported by the experimental colonization and invasion data combined with the noted gap in field investigations.

156.Option: The rapid systemic invasion and intense inflammatory response observed in chickens infected with ST313 suggest that this pathotype is evolving toward a 'stealth infection' strategy similar to S. Gallinarum and S. Pullorum.
Evidence: Genomic analysis of D23580, an MDR ST313 isolated in Blantyre, Malawi, revealed that ST313 has undergone genomic degradation including pseudogene formation and chromosomal deletion that is a feature of host-adapted serovars such as S. Typhi and S. Gallinarum, that cause systemic typhoidal disease in their hosts [4], [5].

Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).

Histology revealed some inflammation in the liver and spleen following infection with both isolates (Table 2), but this was significantly greater in the D23580 infected group (P = 0.032), consistent with more rapid systemic infection.
156.Rationale: The passage notes genomic changes in ST313 that resemble features of host-adapted serovars, which might suggest evolutionary shifts. However, the experimental findings show rapid systemic invasion and an early, intense inflammatory response (particularly in liver and ileum), not a low-inflammatory or 'stealth' infection. The inference that ST313 is evolving toward a stealth strategy similar to S. Gallinarum/Pullorum is speculative and not directly supported by the data presented, which actually document overt inflammatory pathology during early infection.

157.Option: PORT is more likely to occur in patients with systemic immunosuppression, suggesting that immunosuppressed individuals should be prioritized for routine screening with multimodal imaging during posterior uveitis evaluations.
Evidence: Atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression.
PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
PORT cases were selected from the patients with other OT presentations.
157.Rationale: The passage identifies PORT as an atypical presentation of ocular toxoplasmosis and explicitly states that atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression. The authors evaluated cases using multimodal imaging (OCT, fundus photography, FAF) to identify PORT lesions. From these statements it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that immunosuppressed individuals are a higher-risk group for atypical manifestations like PORT and that multimodal imaging is the modality used to detect these lesions; therefore prioritizing such patients for careful imaging during posterior uveitis evaluation is a conclusion supported by the passage.

157.Option: Since PORT lesions are often located in the macula and can lead to legal blindness, all patients with posterior uveitis should receive immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy to prevent choroidal neovascularization.
Evidence: Most lesions have been reported in the macula, but this may be because macular lesions are visually the most symptomatic.
Complications encountered include choroidal neovascularization, serous retinal detachment, [4] and macular cysts[7] that may convert to what is known as huge outer retinal cystoid space (HORC).
Due to this, an anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (aflibercept) intravitreal injection was prescribed for her OD.
157.Rationale: The passage notes that PORT lesions are frequently macular and that choroidal neovascularization (CNV) can be a complication; it also gives one case in which anti-VEGF was used to treat a CNV. However, the passage does not state that all patients with posterior uveitis (or PORT) should receive immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF prophylactically. The evidence supports use of anti-VEGF to treat CNV complications when present, not routine immediate anti-VEGF for all posterior uveitis patients. Thus the universal prophylactic recommendation is not supported by the passage.

157.Option: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Most lesions have been reported in the macula, but this may be because macular lesions are visually the most symptomatic.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
157.Rationale: The passage describes PORT as lesions of the outer retina with minimal vitreous inflammation—features that can make recognition less straightforward than classical necrotizing retinochoroiditis. The study relied on clinical suspicion, serology, specialist assessment, and multimodal imaging (OCT, fundus photography, FAF) for diagnosis. These statements directly support the inference that PORT is diagnostically challenging and that clinical suspicion plus multimodal imaging are important for recognition.

157.Option: The high rate of recurrence in PORT (25%) compared to classical OT (up to 79%) indicates that patients with PORT require more aggressive long-term immunosuppressive therapy to prevent reactivation.
Evidence: Recurrences when they appear, usually involve the adjacent retina.
Treatment with pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and corticosteroids often results in full visual recovery, yet retinochoroidal scarring is often seen with optical coherence tomography (OCT).

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
157.Rationale: The passage does not provide any quantitative recurrence rates for PORT or classical OT (no 25% or 79% figures are given). It states that recurrences, when they occur, usually involve adjacent retina, and describes antimicrobial plus corticosteroid treatment leading to recovery. There is no evidence presented that PORT has a higher recurrence rate or that long-term immunosuppressive therapy is indicated to prevent reactivation. Therefore the specific numerical comparison and the therapeutic recommendation for aggressive long-term immunosuppression are not supported by the passage.

157.Option: The similarity between PORT and occult macular dystrophy (OMD) on OCT imaging suggests that patients with suspected posterior uveitis should undergo full-field and multifocal electroretinography to differentiate between the two conditions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
157.Rationale: While the passage documents OCT and other imaging modalities used to evaluate PORT, it does not mention occult macular dystrophy (OMD), similarities to OMD, or the use of full-field or multifocal electroretinography (ffERG/mfERG) for differentiation. The recommendation to perform ERG testing to distinguish PORT from OMD is not derived from any statement in the passage and therefore is not supported by the provided text.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
Evidence: Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A).
Quantitative data showed a significant increase of parasites with autophagic vesicles after PA treatment, as compared to control parasites (≈ 80% vs. ≈ 20%).
Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).

Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites. ... the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage supports that induction of autophagy promotes metacyclogenesis: rapamycin (Rap) increases MT generation and Rap increases infectivity, and spermidine (Spd) and spermine (Spm) induce autophagy (increased Atg8.1). However, the specific claim that spermidine (or spermine) leads to increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher Vero-cell infection is not directly shown. The experiments demonstrating increased MT production and higher infection used rapamycin (and TAU starvation) as autophagy inducers; spermidine was shown to induce autophagy markers but was not shown in the passage to increase MT counts or infection rates. Therefore the option overstates by attributing the MT/infection evidence to spermidine in addition to rapamycin, which is not directly supported by the presented data.

158.Option: Spermidine and spermine exert their proautophagic effects in T. cruzi by directly inhibiting the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes.
Evidence: The polyamine spermidine (Spd) has been recently described as a new modulator of autophagy since Spd inhibits the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to the upregulation of several ATG genes including ATG7, ATG11 and ATG15 [9].

When added to culture media, Spd is also able to directly induce autophagy in a transcription-independent manner. The mechanism has not been fully elucidated yet; however, this phenomenon could be due to the enhanced deacetylation of essential autophagy-related proteins such as ATG5 and ATG7 [10].
Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A).
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage describes mechanistic evidence for spermidine inhibiting histone acetyl transferase and upregulating ATG genes in other (mammalian/yeast/invertebrate) systems, and notes that spermidine can directly induce autophagy via transcription-independent mechanisms. For T. cruzi the data show that spermidine and spermine increase autophagy markers (Atg8.1), but no experimental evidence is presented that the mechanism in T. cruzi is direct inhibition of histone acetyl transferase resulting in transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes. The passage explicitly states the mechanism is not fully elucidated and does not demonstrate the HAT inhibition/transcriptional upregulation pathway in T. cruzi, so this specific mechanistic inference is not derivable from the presented data.

158.Option: The increased infection rate of Vero cells by T. cruzi parasites subjected to autophagy induction is primarily due to enhanced parasite motility rather than increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation.
Evidence: The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22].
Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.
the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage links increased Vero cell infection to increased numbers of metacyclic trypomastigotes (MT), explicitly stating infection was used as an indirect measure of MT present. There is no data or observation regarding parasite motility in the experiments, and no claim that motility explains the increased infection. Therefore attributing the increased infection primarily to enhanced motility is unsupported by the presented evidence.

158.Option: The presence of wortmannin during the first or total period of differentiation in TAU medium significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy inhibition leads to a complete block in parasite differentiation.
Evidence: Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage clearly states that wortmannin (Wort) significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes when present during differentiation, supporting that autophagy inhibition impairs differentiation. However, the data only report a significant reduction, not a complete block of differentiation. The claim that autophagy inhibition leads to a complete block is stronger than what is supported; the passage provides no evidence that differentiation was entirely abolished by Wort. Therefore the option overstates the experimental findings.

158.Option: Bafilomycin abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi due to its inhibition of the acidocalcisome acidification process, which is essential for PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis.
Evidence: Bafilomycin (Baf), which is another important autophagy inhibitor, is mainly used to study the autophagic flux [28]. The treatment of mammalian cells with Baf blocks the normal autolysosomal degradation, leading to accumulation of autophagic compartments at different maturation stages.
Unexpectedly, a significant reduction in the autophagosome number was observed when parasites were incubated in TAU in the presence of Baf, as compared to TAU medium alone (Fig 4A and 4B).
The number of autophagosomes was lower in parasites subjected to Baf in control medium than in non-treated parasites, indicating that Baf inhibits both basal and induced autophagy.
158.Rationale: The passage documents that bafilomycin (Baf) treatment in T. cruzi unexpectedly reduced autophagosome numbers and inhibited autophagy, whereas in mammalian cells Baf typically blocks autolysosomal degradation causing accumulation. However, the proposed mechanistic explanation involving inhibition of acidocalcisome acidification and consequent disruption of PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis is not mentioned or supported anywhere in the text. The specific mechanistic link to acidocalcisomes and PI3P synthesis is speculative and not derivable from the provided experimental data.

159.Option: The induction of both IFN-γ and IL-10 production in vaccinated mice suggests that the immune response is balanced and may be sufficient for long-term protection against visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: Immunization with Live Recombinant L. tarentolae-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE Induces a Mixed Production of IFN-γ and IL-10

As shown in Figure 3A, stimulation of splenocytes isolated from vaccinated groups G1 and G2 prior and after challenge with all three recall antigens ... elicited a significantly higher IFN-γ production than control groups G3, G4 and G5.
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5) (Fig. 3B).

Indeed, the Leishmania-specific IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge.
Thus, the G2 vaccination regimen protects against infection better than G5 (Live L. tar/Live L. tar).

In the spleen, the highest parasite burden in control groups (G3, G4 and G5) was observed at 12 weeks after challenge and the organ remained chronically infected (Fig. 2D and 2E). Interestingly, only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2 ... were able to control the infection ... at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls
159.Rationale: The passage documents that vaccination elicited both IFN-γ and measurable IL-10 (a "mixed" response), that vaccinated groups had higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios, and that vaccinated mice controlled infection through 12 weeks post-challenge. These facts support that vaccination produces a balanced response associated with protection in the study timeframe. However, the statement asserts that this balance "may be sufficient for long-term protection." The study's data extend to 12 weeks post-challenge and show protection over that period, but do not provide evidence of durability beyond this timeframe. Therefore the claim that it is sufficient for long-term protection is not directly derivable from the presented data and is speculative beyond the provided results.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen using recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE provides superior protection against visceral leishmaniasis compared to the DNA/Live regimen and to control groups, as evidenced by significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen and improved preservation of splenic architecture.
Evidence: Immunization with Live Recombinant L. tarentolae-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE Protects Mice against L. infantum Infectious Challenge

As shown in Figure 2, immunization using a DNA A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G1) and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G2) prime-boosting regiments drastically (p<0.01) reduced the infection levels in both the liver (Fig. 2A and 2B) and the spleen (Fig. 2D and 2E) at 4 weeks after L. infantum challenge in contrast to the control groups (G3 to G5, n = 4 at each point/organ).

Interestingly, only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5).
Thus, the G2 vaccination regimen protects against infection better than G5 (Live L. tar/Live L. tar).
We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
Normal follicle formation of the spleen was noted in G1 and G2 in 4th and 8th week but the normal follicles were disappeared in the other groups (G3, G4 and G5) (Fig. 5A).
159.Rationale: The passage presents direct experimental comparisons showing that both vaccine regimens reduced parasite burden versus controls, and it specifically highlights that the Live/Live regimen (G2) was particularly effective ('especially G2'), controlled infection at 12 weeks, and "protects against infection better than G5." Vaccinated mice (including G2) lacked detectable spleen parasites and had preserved splenic architecture compared with non-vaccinated controls. These results support the inference that the Live/Live recombinant L. tarentolae regimen provides superior protection relative to controls and is at least as good as (and described as especially effective compared with) the DNA/Live regimen.

159.Option: The use of cationic solid-lipid nanoparticles (cSLN) as a delivery system for the DNA vaccine enhances antigen expression and is critical for the observed protective immunity in the DNA/Live regimen.
Evidence: For DNA vaccination with rA2-CPA-CPB-CTE, the pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE was formulated into cationic lipid particles with nanometer range (∼240–250 nm). It was shown previously that this formulation facilitates the uptake by dendritic cells and macrophages, thereby enhancing antigen expression, processing and presentation and resulting in stronger immune effects [59].

Group 1 (DNA cSLN/Live) immunized with pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-cSLN (50 µg of pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE formulated by cSLN nanoparticles ... as a prime and with 2×107 recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE as a boost; group 2 (L. tarentolae Live A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tarentolae Live A2-CPA-CPB-CTE) vaccinated with 2×107 recombinant L. tarentolae-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE as prime and boost; group 4 [(empty vector pcDNA-cSLN (prime)/Live L. tarentolae wild type (boost) as a control)].
159.Rationale: The passage states that cSLN formulation has been shown previously to facilitate uptake and enhance antigen expression and immune effects, and the DNA/Live group used pcDNA formulated with cSLN. However, the study did not include a comparison of DNA vaccination with and without cSLN, nor an experiment isolating the effect of cSLN versus DNA alone. Because the necessity/criticality of cSLN for the observed protection was not experimentally tested within this study, the claim that cSLN "is critical for the observed protective immunity" cannot be directly derived from the presented data.

159.Option: The persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity, and this is achieved by the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine through controlled infection without disease progression.
Evidence: Furthermore, it has been reported that persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity [24], [25]. The only way to meet this requirement is by using attenuated live vaccines.
We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
Parasite in the parenchyma was visualized only in one out of 12 mice of vaccinated groups but it was seen in 11 out of 18 non-vaccinated mice.
No parasites were found in bone marrow macrophages and no significant changes in bone marrow of all examined mice were observed (data not shown).
159.Rationale: While the introduction cites literature that persistence of a small number of live parasites can be important for durable immunity, the experimental data in this study do not demonstrate persistence of vaccine parasites in vaccinated mice. On the contrary, vaccinated groups generally lacked detectable parasites in spleen and rarely in liver or bone marrow. The study therefore does not provide evidence that the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine achieves persistence of a small number of live organisms, nor does it establish that such persistence is the mechanism of protection in this model. The claim that persistence is both essential and achieved by this vaccine is not supported by the presented data.

159.Option: The combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE antigens is more protective than individual forms, indicating that the C-terminal extension enhances immunogenicity and is essential for vaccine efficacy.
Evidence: we have demonstrated that the combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE is more protective against visceral and cutaneous leishmanial infections than the individual forms [50], [51], [52], [53], [54].
Immunization with CTE also displayed both type 1 and 2 immune signatures in experimental murine model of L. infantum infection and therefore is not protective as a vaccine candidate [49].

Recombinant L. tarentolae ... expressing the L. donovani A2 antigen along with L. infantum cysteine proteinases CPA and CPB without its unusual C-terminal extension (CPB-CTE) as a tri-fusion gene (A2-CPA-CPB-CTE) together with the EGFP gene were generated ...
159.Rationale: The passage does state in prior work that combinations (CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE) were more protective than single antigens, which supports that combinations can improve protection. However, it also reports that the C-terminal extension (CTE) alone was "not protective as a vaccine candidate," and in this study the authors constructed a tri-fusion including CPA and CPB "without its unusual C-terminal extension (CPB-CTE)" (text indicates ambiguity in naming but shows the CTE role is not straightforward). The statement that the C-terminal extension "enhances immunogenicity and is essential for vaccine efficacy" is too strong and not directly supported: the passage does not demonstrate that CTE is essential (and includes data that CTE alone was not protective). Therefore the claim is not derivable from the presented data.

160.Option: The fact that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were more attracted to infected dogs in the field suggests that these sand fly species have evolved a specific preference for L. infantum-infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior.
Evidence: The numbers of female P. perniciosus attracted towards infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 205.30 ± 16.94 and 80.57 ± 7.82 respectively and 18.80 ± 6.25 did not respond (NR) (Fig 3).

The attractiveness of the dogs differed significantly according to their infection status (ANOVA: F = 66.709, DF = 2, P<0.001) and infected dogs attracted significantly more P. perniciosus females than uninfected dogs (EMMEANS: z = 5.989, P<0.001, Fig 3).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). ... Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).
To achieve this overlap, some parasites are known to manipulate the host animal by changing its odour or behaviour to improve their chances of transmission [22].

We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
160.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that infected dogs attract more P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi. However, it also explicitly frames the mechanism as parasite-mediated changes in host odour/behaviour (parasite manipulation) and states a hypothesis that infection makes dogs more attractive, enhancing transmission. The option asserts an evolutionary adaptation of the sand fly species themselves (i.e., the vectors having evolved a specific preference for infected hosts). That specific evolutionary claim is not supported by the data presented; the experiments show altered attractiveness of infected hosts and differential attraction, but do not provide evidence that the vectors evolved an innate preference for infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior (versus responding to changed host cues produced by infection). Therefore the evolutionary inference in the option cannot be derived from the passage.

160.Option: The significant attraction of both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi to infected dogs in the field suggests that the presence of infected dogs leads to a dilution of parasite transmission by increasing vector exposure to non-competent hosts.
Evidence: Domestic dogs are the main reservoir host for L. infantum in the Old World [8,9] and in the New World [10].
Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).
Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

A minimum of 36% to 100% of the lab-colonised P. perniciosus that were fed on infected dogs developed L. infantum infections when dissected and examined under dissecting microscope as described by Chelbi and Zhioua (2019) [28], whereas none of the sand flies fed on uninfected dogs were found to be infected with parasites.
160.Rationale: The dilution hypothesis proposed (increase vector exposure to non-competent hosts reduces transmission) is not supported by the passage. The passage states that domestic dogs are the main reservoir (i.e., competent hosts), infected dogs attracted more sand flies, and sand flies feeding on infected dogs became infected (36%–100%), whereas those fed on uninfected dogs did not. These data indicate increased contact between vectors and competent, infectious hosts—not dilution via non-competent hosts. Thus the suggested dilution effect is contradicted by the evidence.

160.Option: The higher number of male P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that male sand flies are more likely to transmit L. infantum to humans due to increased contact with infected reservoirs.
Evidence: Zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (ZVL) is a vector-borne zoonotic disease caused by the parasite Leishmania infantum, which is transmitted by the bite of female phlebotomine sand flies.

The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393).

The number of male P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (34.11±8.18) was significantly greater than the mean number of males trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (11.78 + 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).
Male sand flies were present to encourage female feeding [25,26] but we did not determine their preferences.
160.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that transmission is via the bite of female sand flies; males are not described as vectors. Although more males were trapped near infected dogs in the field, there is no evidence that male sand flies transmit L. infantum or that their increased presence increases human transmission. Moreover, the manuscript notes male presence was used to encourage female feeding in laboratory experiments, and male preferences were not determined. Therefore concluding that males are more likely to transmit to humans is not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The significantly higher number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that female sand flies are more likely to feed on infected dogs due to increased blood meal size or nutritional quality.
Evidence: The numbers of female P. perniciosus that fed on the infected and uninfected dogs were (186.16 ± 16.98), and (66.83 ± 7.58), respectively. The proportion of P. perniciosus females that fed on infected and uninfected dogs were 0.90 ± 0.02 and 0.81 ± 0.03 respectively.

Feeding success was significantly affected by the infection status of dogs (ANOVA: F = 7.5714, DF = 1, P = 0.0079). The proportion of P. perniciosus females that fed on infected dogs was significantly higher than the proportion that fed on uninfected dogs (EMMEANS: z = 2.805, P = 0.005; Fig 4).

The number of eggs laid by each female P. perniciosus fed on infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 36.46 ± 0.16, and 43.35 ± 0.15, respectively (Fig 5). Fecundity was highly affected by the infection status of dogs (ANOVA: F = 13.35, DF = 1, P<0.001). Females that had fed on infected dogs laid significantly fewer eggs than females that had fed on uninfected dogs (EMMEANS: t = -3.654, P<0.001).
The number of retained eggs in females that had fed on infected dogs was 2.35 ± 0.09 and in females fed on uninfected dogs was 1.27 ± 0.09 (Fig 6).
160.Rationale: The data do show significantly more female P. perniciosus feed on infected dogs (higher numbers and higher feeding proportion). However, the option attributes this to increased blood meal size or nutritional quality. The passage provides fecundity data that run counter to the inference of higher nutritional quality: females that fed on infected dogs laid significantly fewer eggs (36.46 vs. 43.35) and had more retained eggs, suggesting feeding on infected dogs did not improve reproductive output and thus does not support the claim of increased blood meal size or nutritional quality. The passage contains no direct measures of blood meal size or nutritional content, so causally attributing attraction to those factors is unsupported.

160.Option: The enhanced attractiveness of infected dogs to both female and male Phlebotomus perniciosus and Phlebotomus perfiliewi under natural conditions suggests a zoopotentiation effect that increases the transmission success of Leishmania infantum.
Evidence: The numbers of female P. perniciosus attracted towards infected dogs and uninfected dogs were 205.30 ± 16.94 and 80.57 ± 7.82 respectively and 18.80 ± 6.25 did not respond (NR) (Fig 3).
