This directly supports the option's inference that vertical transmission occurs naturally in mosquito populations and could contribute to environmental persistence. The additional note that R. felis nucleic acid has been detected in many hematophagous arthropods supports the plausibility of persistence driven in part by vertical transmission.

172.Option: The absence of R. felis in control ticks cofed with uninfected fleas confirms that the pathogen is not capable of spontaneous or environmental contamination in laboratory settings.
Evidence: None of the ticks that cofed with uninfected fleas were positive for R. felis (Table 1).
All flea, tick, and mosquito samples were washed with 10% bleach for 5 mins, 70% ethanol for 5 mins, and three times with sterile distilled H2O for 5 mins to remove environmental DNA.
A negative environmental control, a 1.7 ml microcentrifuge tube containing buffer(s) but no sample, was included in each DNA extraction procedure as a negative environmental control for qPCR.
172.Rationale: The passage reports that control ticks cofed with uninfected fleas tested negative and describes procedural steps (washing, negative extraction controls) to minimize and detect contamination, which supports that contamination was unlikely in these experiments. However, the statement asserts an absolute confirmation that the pathogen 'is not capable of spontaneous or environmental contamination in laboratory settings.' The experimental observations and controls reduce the likelihood of contamination in this study but do not 'confirm' the impossibility of contamination in all laboratory settings. Thus the stronger, universal claim is not supported by the passage.

172.Option: The high rickettsial load in cat fleas compared to mosquitoes during cofeeding indicates that mosquitoes are less competent vectors for R. felis than cat fleas, limiting their role in disease transmission.
Evidence: donor cat fleas exposed to R. felis prior to cofeeding consistently had significantly higher rickettsial loads than the mosquitoes with which they were cofed (Fig 2).
Combined, the data indicate that An. quadrimaculatus are susceptible to rickettsial infection via cofeeding with infected cat fleas.
Comparing the proportions of infected between the two feeding arthropod populations, no significance difference was identified between the two vectors on any of the time points assessed.
172.Rationale: The passage documents that donor cat fleas had higher rickettsial loads than cofed mosquitoes, but it also shows that mosquitoes nevertheless acquired infection (substantial proportions became positive) and that there was no significant difference in infection proportions between the two vectors at the assessed time points. Vector competence is multifactorial (infection, replication, dissemination, transmission) and cannot be concluded solely from a higher rickettsial load in one arthropod. The data do not demonstrate that mosquitoes are less competent in terms of transmitting R. felis in nature or that their role in transmission is limited; therefore the broad claim about reduced competence and limited role is not supported by the passage.

172.Option: R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit the agent to naïve vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host, suggesting a mechanism for inter-class and inter-order transmission that may contribute to the widespread distribution of R. felis in hematophagous arthropods.
Evidence: The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.

R. felis was detected in 75% (15/20) D. variabilis nymphs that were forcibly removed from the host 24 hrs after cofeeding. Comparably, 81.2% (13/16) of 4–5 days fed, engorged ticks were positive for R. felis; and, after molting, 56.2% (9/16) of adult ticks were positive.
Naïve An. quadrimaculatus were able to acquire infection through cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas.
Nucleic acid from R. felis and related R. felis-like organisms (RFLOs) has been detected in other hematophagous arthropods, including more than 40 species of fleas, ticks, and mosquitoes [4,10].
172.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit R. felis to naïve ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding on a vertebrate host, with substantial proportions of exposed ticks and mosquitoes becoming infected. It also notes widespread detection of R. felis nucleic acid in many hematophagous arthropods. Together these findings support the inference that cofeeding transmission can serve as a mechanism enabling inter-order (fleas to ticks; fleas to mosquitoes) transmission and may help explain the broad distribution of R. felis among blood-feeding arthropods. This conclusion follows from the presented experimental results and field observations described in the passage.

173.Option: Dengue fever is a major cause of severe pregnancy outcomes in pregnant women admitted to hospital in Vientiane, Laos, and its impact may be exacerbated by fluid balance changes during pregnancy.
Evidence: dengue was the most commonly found (76 (30%)), followed by pyelonephritis (23 (9%)), murine typhus (10 (4%)), scrub typhus (9 (3.6%)) and typhoid fever (6 (2%)).

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
Anatomical and physiological changes may predispose pregnant women to certain infections, such as those of the urinary tract.
173.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that dengue was the commonest laboratory-confirmed diagnosis among hospitalized febrile pregnant women and that a substantial fraction (21/76, 28%) of dengue cases had severe pregnancy outcomes, which supports the first clause that dengue is a major cause of severe pregnancy outcomes in this hospitalized population. However, the second clause—that its impact may be exacerbated by fluid balance changes during pregnancy—is not supported by any specific data or statements in the passage. The quoted sentence about anatomical and physiological changes is general and does not mention fluid balance or link it to worsening of dengue. Because the claim about exacerbation by fluid balance changes is not derivable from the study text, the combined statement cannot be accepted as fully supported.

173.Option: Dengue fever in pregnancy is primarily responsible for the high maternal mortality ratio in Laos, as evidenced by the 1 maternal death among 76 dengue cases.
Evidence: Although, Laos achieved 75% reductions in maternal mortality ratio (MMR) from 1990–2015, it still has the highest estimated MMR in mainland Southeast Asia (197/100,000 live births) [1,2].
Direct causes such as haemorrhage and eclampsia are the most common reasons [37].

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
173.Rationale: While the passage documents one maternal death among 76 dengue cases, it also states that direct causes such as haemorrhage and eclampsia are the most common reasons for maternal mortality in Laos. The study is limited to hospitalized febrile pregnant women and does not provide population-level attribution of maternal deaths to dengue or compare the contribution of dengue versus other causes to national MMR. Therefore, the assertion that dengue is primarily responsible for the high MMR in Laos cannot be derived from the study data.

173.Option: Congenital abnormalities in infants born to mothers with dengue are primarily due to the virus itself rather than maternal fever, based on the evidence from this study.
Evidence: Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.
173.Rationale: The study reports a single congenital abnormality (VSD) among 76 dengue cases but provides no data or analyses that distinguish whether congenital abnormalities were caused by the dengue virus itself, by maternal fever, by other co-factors, or by chance. The study design and data presented do not establish causation or mechanisms for congenital anomalies. Hence the claim that congenital abnormalities are primarily due to the virus rather than maternal fever is not supported by the passage.

173.Option: The study provides definitive evidence that dengue fever increases the risk of severe pregnancy outcomes in all pregnant women, regardless of gestational age or prior immunity.
Evidence: The hospital-based prospective study was conducted in Mahosot Hospital and the Mother and Child Hospital, Vientiane, Laos between February 2006 and November 2010. We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).
Of 76 patients, 28 (37%) had dengue with warning signs and 1 (1%) had severe dengue.
Of 76/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion; 16/57 (28%) had primary dengue and 41/57 (72%) had secondary dengue.
173.Rationale: The passage reports that a sizeable proportion of hospitalized pregnant dengue patients had severe outcomes, and provides data on primary versus secondary infections. However, the study population is a selected group (hospitalized pregnant women with fever), not a population-based sample of all pregnant women. The study does not provide stratified analyses demonstrating increased risk across all gestational ages or irrespective of prior immunity, nor does it provide definitive causal inference for all pregnant women. Therefore, the sweeping, definitive claim across all pregnant women and regardless of gestational age or immunity status is not supported by the study data.

173.Option: The high frequency of dengue in pregnant women admitted with fever suggests that dengue is the most common cause of fever in the general population of Vientiane, Laos.
Evidence: The hospital-based prospective study was conducted in Mahosot Hospital and the Mother and Child Hospital, Vientiane, Laos between February 2006 and November 2010. We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.
dengue was the most commonly found (76 (30%)), followed by pyelonephritis (23 (9%)), murine typhus (10 (4%)), scrub typhus (9 (3.6%)) and typhoid fever (6 (2%)).
Most (218, 87%) were from Vientiane City...
173.Rationale: The study demonstrates that among hospitalized pregnant women presenting with fever, dengue was the most frequently diagnosed single disease. However, this is a hospital-based sample of pregnant, febrile patients and is not representative of the general (non-pregnant) population of Vientiane. The passage does not provide community-based surveillance data to support extrapolation to the general population; therefore the inference that dengue is the most common cause of fever in the general population of Vientiane cannot be derived from the study.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of closely related genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single introduction of a new S. Typhi strain from a neighboring country during the period of hyperendemic transmission.
Evidence: a cluster of closely related genotype 1.1 S. Typhi isolates was detected in the years 2011 (1 isolate) and 2012 (19 isolates).
The smallest SNP distance between a 1980s 1.1 isolate and a 2010s 1.1 isolate was 79 SNPs.
The S. Typhi isolates from Santiago of genotypes 3.5, 2, 1.1 and 1.2.1 clustered together and were genetically distinct from those sequenced previously from other countries.
Only six other isolates, 0.3% of the global collection described by Wong et al [14], belonged to clade 1.1.
174.Rationale: The passage describes the 2011–2012 1.1 cluster as closely related to each other but genetically distinct from sequences from other countries and notes very few global 1.1 isolates. It also shows substantial SNP distance between the 1980s and 2010s 1.1 isolates. These data support local persistence or a local common ancestor rather than a single recent introduction from a neighboring country during the hyperendemic period. Therefore the specific claim of a single introduction from a neighboring country is not supported by the provided evidence.

174.Option: The persistence of ancient S. Typhi clades in Santiago, Chile, despite the interruption of hyperendemic transmission, suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers are likely responsible for ongoing sporadic cases.
Evidence: We show that the genotypic composition of the population structure is similar between these two periods, even though the local epidemiology has changed drastically.
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
we describe a potential carrier-related cluster of the “ancient” 1.1 clade,

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
174.Rationale: The passage documents that ancient clades present in the 1980s persist into the 2010s and explicitly describes a potential carrier-related cluster of the ancient 1.1 clade. It also states that contemporary sporadic cases include transmission from long-term Chilean chronic carriers infected decades earlier. Together, these statements support the epidemiological inference that asymptomatic/chronic carriers are a likely source of ongoing sporadic typhoid cases.

174.Option: The detection of a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two genotype 1.2.1 isolates from Santiago suggests that this plasmid may play a role in the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host.
Evidence: Two pHCM2 plasmids were found among our isolates from genotype 1.2.1; one in a 1984 isolate (ERR2710544) and the other from 2013 (ERR4289185).
pHCM2 plasmids are found in ~15% of S. Typhi and are generally highly conserved (Fig 4A).

These plasmids were highly similar to each other, but significantly different from the pHCM2 found on S. Typhi reference CT18 (accession no: AL513384)... The pHCM2 sequences described here differed by >2000 SNPs (98.4% nucleotide identity, 89% coverage) from the reference
174.Rationale: While the passage documents the presence of a novel pHCM2 variant in two isolates and describes its genetic divergence from the reference, it provides no evidence that this plasmid contributes to persistence in the human host. Inferring a functional role in persistence is speculative and not supported by the data presented (which are descriptive/genomic). Therefore the causal suggestion that pHCM2 may play a role in persistence cannot be derived solely from the passage.

174.Option: The global dominance of the H58 S. Typhi clade in other industrialized nations suggests that imported cases are the primary source of sporadic typhoid in Santiago, Chile.
Evidence: The majority of publicly available sequenced isolates come from hyperendemic regions in South Asia and East Africa where H58 (4.3.1) genotypes dominate [17].
These include three previously described importations of isolates belonging to H58 genotypes 4.3.1.1 and 4.3.1.2 [35].
the genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras (Fig 1).
most of the post-2010 sporadic S. Typhi isolates shared bootstrap-supported phylogenetic clusters with the 1980 hyperendemic isolates.
174.Rationale: Although H58 dominates in many hyperendemic regions and a few H58 importations into Santiago were observed, the passage emphasizes that most modern Santiago isolates cluster with historical local isolates, indicating local persistence rather than predominance of imported H58 cases. The passage also explicitly lists local chronic carriers as a source of sporadic cases. Thus the claim that imported cases are the primary source in Santiago is not supported by the presented data.

174.Option: The low genetic diversity observed in the 2011–2012 genotype 1.1 cluster indicates that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has undergone a recent bottleneck event, leading to the loss of most ancestral genotypes.
Evidence: These isolates also exhibit lower genetic variation (median SNP distance of 8) than those observed among the two 1980s 1.1 isolates (119 SNPs).
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
A diverse range of 14 genotypes were detected including 1.1, 1.2.1, 2, 2.0.2, 2.2, 2.3.2, 2.3.3, 2.3.4, 3.1, 3.3, 3.5, 4.1, 4.3.1.1, 4.3.1.2. Most of the isolates belonged to genotype 1.1, 2 and 3.5.
174.Rationale: Although the 2011–2012 1.1 cluster shows low within-cluster diversity, the passage also documents persistence of multiple genotypes across eras and intermingling of historical and recent isolates, arguing against a conclusion that a recent bottleneck eliminated most ancestral genotypes. The low diversity within one temporal cluster can be explained by a recent common ancestor (e.g., a carrier-related outbreak) without invoking a population-wide bottleneck and loss of ancestral diversity. Therefore the specific bottleneck conclusion is not supported by the evidence provided.

175.Option: Ergot alkaloids are efficacious modulators of planarian regeneration and motility, and their structural-activity relationships suggest potential for developing novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite muscle function.
Evidence: Intriguingly, serotonergic screens highlight ergot alkaloids as a class of compounds that potently and penetrantly miscued planarian regeneration and schistosomule muscle function, with structure activity insight from active compounds highlighting modifications of the ergot scaffold predictive for flatworm efficacy.
we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.

Numerous ergot compounds yielded regenerative phenotypes, either phenocopying PZQ to promote bipolar (‘two-head’) regeneration or inhibiting head regeneration (‘no-head’), all at doses lower than PZQ.

In schistosome contractility assays, the compounds that caused planarian bipolarity all inhibited schistosomule motility (just like PZQ), whereas the compounds that inhibited planarian head regeneration caused the opposite effect, stimulating contractile activity (Fig 3B). Therefore, ergot alkaloids possess efficacy against schistosomules, with an action predictable by planarian polarity outcomes.
175.Rationale: The passage directly reports that ergot alkaloids potently altered planarian regeneration and affected schistosomule muscle function, that structure-activity relationships were identified (modifications predictive for flatworm efficacy), and that the authors recommend further exploration of the ergot scaffold to develop chemotherapeutics against parasite musculature. These statements support the epidemiological/experimental inference in this option.

175.Option: The structural-activity relationships identified in planarian regenerative screens are sufficient to guarantee the development of parasite-specific drugs with minimal off-target effects in humans.
Evidence: structure activity insight from active compounds highlighting modifications of the ergot scaffold predictive for flatworm efficacy.
we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
175.Rationale: While the passage states that structure-activity insights are predictive for flatworm efficacy and that the ergot scaffold merits further exploration, it does not claim that these findings are sufficient to guarantee development of parasite-specific drugs or minimal off-target effects in humans. The authors call for further exploration, implying additional work is required; no data on human specificity or safety are provided in the passage. Therefore the strong guarantee in this option is not supported.

175.Option: Since PZQ’s mechanism of action is unresolved, the discovery that ergot alkaloids mimic its effects in planarians suggests that PZQ’s primary target is the serotonergic system in flatworms.
Evidence: the mechanism of action of PZQ remains unresolved and rationally designed derivatives of PZQ typically prove less efficacious [7,14,15].
Therefore, these data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
The bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
175.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that PZQ's mechanism is unresolved and shows that PZQ's effects are functionally antagonized by serotonergic activation and that PZQ mimics serotonergic blockade. However, these observations establish functional interaction or phenocopying, not definitive evidence that PZQ's primary molecular target is the serotonergic system. The passage does not provide direct biochemical or receptor-binding data proving PZQ directly targets 5-HT receptors, so the causal claim in this option is not supported by the presented data.

175.Option: Because ergot alkaloids are already used clinically for migraine, obstetrics, and Parkinson’s disease, they are likely to be safe and effective for treating schistosomiasis without requiring further development.
Evidence: Intriguingly, serotonergic screens highlight ergot alkaloids as a class of compounds that potently and penetrantly miscued planarian regeneration and schistosomule muscle function, with structure activity insight from active compounds highlighting modifications of the ergot scaffold predictive for flatworm efficacy.
we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
Ergot alkaloids are a historically important class of compounds that realize their effects because of the close structural similarity of the ergoline scaffold to bioaminergic transmitters.
175.Rationale: The passage does not claim ergot alkaloids are already appropriate as schistosomiasis treatments without further development; instead, it emphasizes potential and the need for further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics. The noted structural similarity to bioaminergic transmitters implies possible activity at human receptors, which could raise safety concerns rather than assure safety. Thus the assertion that they are likely safe and effective without further development is not supported by the passage.

175.Option: The functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action suggests that PZQ may directly target flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic, which could be confirmed through heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs.
Evidence: Therefore, these data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

A total of 17 predicted serotonergic G protein coupled receptor (GpCR) sequences were identified based upon homology to previously identified sequences [22–24].

We do note that both the planarian receptor (S7.4, DjSER-7 [22]) and a schistosome S7-like receptor have been successfully deorphanized following heterologous expression and shown to respond to 5-HT [25,26].
175.Rationale: The passage documents functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action and describes identified platyhelminth GpCRs, including examples deorphanized by heterologous expression that respond to 5-HT. However, it does not present direct evidence that PZQ itself directly targets bioaminergic GpCRs or acts as an ergomimetic. While heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs is a reasonable method to test receptor interactions (and the passage notes such deorphanization has been done for some receptors), the specific causal claim that PZQ directly targets those receptors is not supported by the data in the passage and remains speculative.

176.Option: The decision tree algorithm for prognostication achieved high positive predictive value in low-prevalence settings, making it ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods.
Evidence: The overall error rate using k-fold crossover validation analysis was 20.5%, with a sensitivity of 78.2% and specificity of 80.2%.

The use of data obtained from the 89 hospitalised cases alone resulted in a very similar decision algorithm, although the AUCs were substantially lower than the above analysis due largely to the smaller dataset.
176.Rationale: The passage reports sensitivity, specificity and error rate for the prognostic algorithm but does not provide positive predictive value (PPV) or any analysis stratified by disease prevalence. PPV depends on prevalence, and the authors do not present results specifically from low-prevalence (non-outbreak) settings or claim superiority there. The quoted performance metrics and the note about lower AUC with smaller hospitalized-only data do not support the specific claim about high PPV in low-prevalence periods; therefore this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

176.Option: The decision tree model for dengue diagnosis is more effective than traditional laboratory tests such as RT-PCR and ELISA in early detection of dengue infection.
Evidence: Definitive early dengue diagnosis thus requires laboratory tests and those suitable for use at this stage of illness are either costly, such as RT-PCR for dengue; not sufficiently rapid, such as virus isolation; or undergoing field trials, such as ELISA for NS1 protein of dengue virus [8],[9].

We show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.
The overall error rate estimated after k-fold cross validation was 15.7%, with a sensitivity and specificity of 71.2% and 90.1%, respectively (Figure 2B).
176.Rationale: While the passage notes limitations of laboratory tests (cost, speed, field-trial status) and demonstrates that a decision tree can distinguish dengue early with specified sensitivity and specificity, it does not present a head-to-head comparison showing that the decision tree is more effective than RT-PCR or ELISA. The quoted sentences show the existence of both approaches and the decision tree's performance metrics, but superiority over laboratory tests in early detection is not established in the passage and cannot be inferred.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach can differentiate dengue from non-dengue febrile illness and predict disease outcome using simple haematological and virological parameters obtained within the first 72 hours of illness.
Evidence: We show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

A. Decision algorithm for predicting dengue diagnosis calculated on 1200 patients with data obtained in the first 72 hours of illness. PLT = platelet count; WBC = white blood cell count; T = body temperature; HCT = hematocrit; Lymphocyte = absolute number of lymphocytes; Neutrophil = absolute number of neutrophils.
The overall error rate estimated after k-fold cross validation was 15.7%, with a sensitivity and specificity of 71.2% and 90.1%, respectively (Figure 2B).

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.
The overall error rate using k-fold crossover validation analysis was 20.5%, with a sensitivity of 78.2% and specificity of 80.2% (Figure 4B).
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that a decision tree using routinely obtainable clinical and haematological parameters can distinguish dengue from non-dengue within the first 72 hours. It also shows a prognostic decision tree that uses simple haematological (platelet counts) and virological (Ct from RT-PCR, anti-dengue IgG) parameters from the first visit to predict severe disease, with reported error rates, sensitivity and specificity. These statements directly support the inference.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach allows for the integration of dynamic laboratory parameters into a single predictive model, enabling real-time clinical decision-making during dengue outbreaks.
Evidence: The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3).

We show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

Evidence-based triage strategies that identify individuals likely to have dengue infection in the early stages of illness are needed to direct patient stratification in clinical investigations, management and healthcare resource planning.
176.Rationale: The passage supports the integration of laboratory and clinical parameters (platelet count, Ct, IgG) into decision tree models and argues these algorithms can be useful for early diagnosis and prognostication. However, it does not claim or provide evidence that these models enable 'real-time clinical decision-making during dengue outbreaks.' Terms such as 'real-time' and specific application during outbreaks are not presented or evaluated in the passage. Therefore, the latter part of the statement is not supported by the provided text and cannot be inferred.

176.Option: The lack of evidence-based diagnostic algorithm for early dengue diagnosis as well as prognostic triage strategies limits effective patient management, use of healthcare resources and disease surveillance efforts.
Evidence: Dengue illness is often confused with other viral febrile states, confounding both clinical management [4]–[6] and disease surveillance for viral transmission prevention [7].

Definitive early dengue diagnosis thus requires laboratory tests and those suitable for use at this stage of illness are either costly, such as RT-PCR for dengue; not sufficiently rapid, such as virus isolation; or undergoing field trials, such as ELISA for NS1 protein of dengue virus [8],[9].

Furthermore, many dengue endemic places lack the necessary laboratory infrastructure or support [7] and thus a scheme for reliable clinical diagnosis, using data that can be obtained routinely, would be useful for early recognition of dengue fever, not only for case management but also for dengue surveillance.

Evidence-based triage strategies that identify individuals likely to have dengue infection in the early stages of illness are needed to direct patient stratification in clinical investigations, management and healthcare resource planning.
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes problems caused by early clinical similarity to other febrile illnesses, limitations of laboratory diagnostics (cost, speed, availability), and lack of laboratory infrastructure in endemic areas. It states that a reliable clinical diagnostic scheme and evidence-based triage strategies are needed to support case management, healthcare resource planning and surveillance. These statements directly support the inference that the absence of such evidence-based algorithms limits effective patient management, resource use and surveillance.

177.Option: Surveillance methods with greater sensitivity and more targeted sampling strategies to better detect residual hotspots than the current TAS methodology will be required.
Evidence: It is acknowledged that the TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings[5, 7].

Consequently, there is increasing interest in the appropriateness and effectiveness of alternative methods of LF surveillance, and whether these can be integrated within health systems in post-validation settings.
Table 6 which shows that alternative surveillance methods can identify evidence to support ongoing transmission in areas which passed TAS.
For example, Sheel et al. report LF prevalence (using Filarial Test Strips) of 6.2% in a community survey in an area, which had recently passed TAS[14].
177.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS may lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings and that there is growing interest in alternative methods. It also provides concrete examples where alternative surveillance detected evidence of ongoing transmission in areas that passed TAS. From an epidemiological perspective, these statements and examples support the inference that surveillance with greater sensitivity and more targeted sampling to detect residual hotspots will be required compared with current TAS alone.

177.Option: Because mosquito surveillance provides a snapshot of current infection and is less affected by the persistence of markers in the host, it should replace human-based surveillance in all post-validation settings.
Evidence: Overall, there was great variability in survey methods and results which limited comparisons.
Interpretation is also limited by the fact that there are currently no recommended species-specific Mosquito Infectivity Rate (MIR) thresholds for LF[8, 53].

There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did. For example, the study by Ramaiah et al. reported a mosquito infection rate of 4.7% of mosquitoes when a community survey performed concurrently found no evidence of human infection on ICT testing.
177.Rationale: While the passage shows xenomonitoring can detect transmission in some situations (and gives examples), it also emphasizes large variability in results, limited comparability, and the absence of recommended MIR thresholds. The passage does not assert that mosquito surveillance is uniformly superior to human surveillance nor that it should replace human-based surveillance in all settings. Therefore the strong policy prescription in the option (replace human surveillance in all post-validation settings) is not supported by the evidence presented.

177.Option: The use of antibody tests such as Wb123 Ab may be particularly useful in post-validation settings due to their early positivity and faster decay after infection clearance, making them more suitable for detecting current transmission than antigen tests.
Evidence: Compared to Binax Now or Alere ICT (the most commonly used tests at the time of most of these surveys) as the index test, Table 3 shows that antibody tests produce a higher proportion of positive results. Bm14Ab and Wb123Ab values are, on average, 5.1 and 6.7 times higher respectively than the corresponding BinaxNOW values, based on the median value of this ratio across the selected studies.
177.Rationale: The passage documents that antibody tests (including Wb123 Ab) yield higher proportions of positive results than common antigen tests in the surveyed studies. However, it does not state that these antibody tests show 'early positivity' and 'faster decay after infection clearance' or that they are therefore more suitable for detecting current transmission. In fact, higher antibody positivity could reflect prior exposure and may not indicate recent transmission. Because the specific claims about timing and decay of antibodies are not supported by the passage, the conclusion cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

177.Option: The increased risk of disease recrudescence in post-validation settings means that countries should immediately resume mass drug administration (MDA) upon detection of any positive case in surveillance.
Evidence: Following validation of elimination of LF as a public health problem, the WHO recommend that countries continue surveillance for LF to detect any possible recrudescence of infection but there are no clear recommendations on specific surveillance methods and thresholds to be used[4, 6].

This has involved actions to interrupt transmission, through the systematic delivery of mass drug administration (MDA) at a population level, and to ensure that cases of morbidity linked to LF receive appropriate treatment[4].
177.Rationale: The passage notes WHO guidance to continue surveillance after validation and describes MDA as the intervention used to interrupt transmission historically. However, it explicitly states there are no clear recommendations on specific surveillance methods and thresholds. The passage does not provide evidence or guidance that any single detected positive case should trigger immediate resumption of MDA. Thus the strong operational recommendation in the option (immediate resumption of MDA upon any positive) is not supported by the text.

178.Option: The current WHO monitoring protocol for LF elimination is sufficient to support the detection of system-level changes in parasite dynamics, as it aligns with the time points at which significant reductions in model uncertainty occur.
Evidence: To be managerially relevant to current WHO-specified LF intervention surveillance efforts, we evaluated the usefulness of infection data collected in these sites at the time points proposed by the WHO monitoring framework in carrying out the present assessment [44].

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4). The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
178.Rationale: The passage states that the study was explicitly designed to evaluate data collected at the WHO-recommended time points and shows that scenarios including the post-MDA5 survey (which is part of the WHO scheme) produce the greatest reduction in model uncertainty. It also reports that adding still later surveys did not significantly improve uncertainty reduction. Together these statements support the inference that the WHO monitoring time points (including the post-MDA5 survey) are sufficient to detect the system-level changes that most reduce model uncertainty.

178.Option: The most accurate predictions of LF elimination timelines are achieved when models are calibrated using data collected at the time of the first post-MDA survey, as this early data point provides the strongest constraint on model parameters.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
178.Rationale: The passage explicitly indicates that inclusion of the post-MDA5 survey (later than the first post-MDA survey) produced larger reductions in prediction uncertainty than using only baseline plus the first post-MDA (post-MDA3) data. Therefore the claim that the first post-MDA survey alone provides the strongest constraint and yields the most accurate predictions is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

178.Option: Model predictions of LF elimination timelines are most sensitive to the initial baseline infection data, and additional longitudinal data have minimal impact on reducing prediction uncertainty.
Evidence: models constrained by data provided significantly more precise intervention predictions compared to model-only predictions (Table 2).

Thus, for all models and sites, model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty, highlighting the need for data to improve the predictive performance of the present models.
as much as 92% to 96% reductions in prediction variance were achieved by these scenarios in comparison to model-only predictions between the three models (Table 2).
178.Rationale: The passage shows that baseline-only or model-only predictions have high uncertainty and that adding longitudinal data markedly reduces that uncertainty (large reductions in prediction variance). This contradicts the option claiming baseline data alone suffice and that additional longitudinal data have minimal impact. The evidence supports the opposite: longitudinal data substantially improve predictive precision.

178.Option: The use of longitudinal data from at least five years post-MDA is sufficient to constrain model predictions of the impact of LF interventions and detect qualitative changes in system behavior, with additional data beyond this point not leading to significant further improvements in predictive performance.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4). The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
For all sites, either scenario 3 or 4 had the lowest entropies, and scenario 4 was not significantly different from scenario 3 for Kirare and Alagramam.
178.Rationale: The passage repeatedly finds that inclusion of the post-MDA5 survey (i.e., data up to five years post-MDA) provides the greatest reduction in model uncertainty and that adding still later surveys (beyond post-MDA5) does not produce statistically significant further improvement. Thus longitudinal data including at least the five-year post-MDA time point are sufficient to constrain model predictions and capture qualitative changes important for forecasting, consistent with this option.

178.Option: The optimal duration of longitudinal surveillance for parasite elimination models is determined by the length of the MDA program itself, with data collection required for the full duration of interventions to ensure accurate forecasts.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.

The results of increasing the assumed coverage of MDA to 80% for each site are shown in Fig 6 and indicate that the choice of MDA coverage in this study are unlikely to have significantly influenced the conclusion made above that the best performing data streams for reducing model uncertainty for predicting LF elimination pertains to data scenarios 3 and 4.
178.Rationale: The passage indicates that data collected up to the post-MDA5 time point (which is not necessarily the entire duration of an MDA program) are generally sufficient to constrain models and further data beyond that point do not significantly improve predictive precision. It also shows robustness to different assumed MDA coverages. Therefore the claim that surveillance must span the full duration of the MDA program to ensure accurate forecasts is not supported by the evidence provided.

179.Option: The under-diagnosis of zoonoses is mainly due to a lack of integration between animal and human healthcare sectors, which prevents the sharing of case information and limits cross-sectoral awareness.
Evidence: The recognition and successful clinical management of zoonoses depend on several factors, including individual healthcare seeking behavior; differential consideration of a zoonosis by patients and clinicians; availability, accuracy, and uptake of diagnostic testing; and treatment accessibility.
Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses.

It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses [12].

A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses. Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews:
179.Rationale: The passage lists multiple causes for under-diagnosis (nonspecific presentations, limited testing capacity, clinician and patient factors) and emphasizes lack of diagnostic tests and nonspecific clinical features as major contributors. Although the study maps animal and human healthcare sectors and notes differences in awareness, the passage does not present lack of cross-sectoral integration or information sharing as the main or primary explanation. Therefore the specific claim that under-diagnosis is mainly due to lack of integration between sectors is not supported by the provided text.

179.Option: The high visibility and dramatic clinical presentations of diseases like anthrax, rabies, and Ebola virus disease lead healthcare providers to prioritize these conditions over less conspicuous zoonoses such as brucellosis, leptospirosis, and Q fever.
Evidence: All (100%) respondents named rabies as a disease that could be transmitted by dogs.
Despite the high prevalence of bacterial zoonoses observed using study diagnostics, none of the study patients received a clinical diagnosis of a bacterial zoonosis.
Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses.

A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses. Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews:
179.Rationale: While the passage notes that respondents universally named rabies and mentions plague, it does not state that clinicians prioritize high-visibility dramatic zoonoses over less conspicuous endemic zoonoses as the primary reason for under-diagnosis. The text attributes under-diagnosis principally to nonspecific clinical presentations, limited testing capacity, and low clinician awareness, rather than explicit prioritization of dramatic diseases. Thus the provided passage does not support this specific explanatory claim.

179.Option: Persistent over-diagnosis of malaria is primarily driven by a lack of confidence in the accuracy of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), which leads healthcare providers to default to clinical diagnosis and overlook other causes of fever.
Evidence: Previous studies in northern Tanzania have indicated that clinicians’ perceptions of patient pressure play a role in malaria over-diagnosis and over-treatment, suggesting a need to raise awareness about alternative causes of febrile illness, including zoonoses, among both the healthcare provider and patient populations [13,14].

Twelve (20%) respondents indicated a lack of confidence in malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), and 11 (19%) insisted that sometimes mRDTs produced false negative malaria results when other tests had shown ‘true’ positives.

The doctors have developed a habit where they assume people with fever symptoms are suffering from malaria. That is why many clinicians do not remember to check zoonotic diseases" (BZQ-062, Human healthcare sector respondent).
179.Rationale: The passage reports lack of confidence in mRDTs among some respondents, but it also identifies other important drivers of malaria over-diagnosis — notably clinicians' perceptions of patient pressure, habitual assumptions that fever equals malaria, and low consideration of alternative causes. The evidence does not support the stronger claim that lack of confidence in mRDTs is the primary driver; rather it is one of several contributing factors documented in the text. Therefore this option overstates the role of mRDT confidence relative to the described evidence.

179.Option: The non-specific clinical presentation of many zoonoses, combined with limited diagnostic testing capacity, contributes to a failure to recognize and diagnose these diseases in clinical practice.

It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses [12].

A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses. Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews:

"People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
Despite the high prevalence of bacterial zoonoses observed using study diagnostics, none of the study patients received a clinical diagnosis of a bacterial zoonosis.
179.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that many zoonoses have nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other causes of febrile illness and that limited diagnostic testing capacity severely limits options for accurate laboratory diagnosis. The quantitative and qualitative data (78% of respondents citing lack of tests; interview quotations) directly support the inference that nonspecific symptoms plus lack of testing lead to failures to recognize and diagnose zoonoses in clinical settings. The documented finding that study patients with confirmed bacterial zoonoses were not clinically diagnosed further substantiates this causal chain.

179.Option: The low frequency of reported zoonoses cases in the past year is due to the fact that most patients with zoonotic infections do not seek healthcare, resulting in under-ascertainment of disease burden.
Evidence: The recognition and successful clinical management of zoonoses depend on several factors, including individual healthcare seeking behavior; differential consideration of a zoonosis by patients and clinicians; availability, accuracy, and uptake of diagnostic testing; and treatment accessibility.

A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses. Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews:

"People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
Despite the high prevalence of bacterial zoonoses observed using study diagnostics, none of the study patients received a clinical diagnosis of a bacterial zoonosis.
179.Rationale: Although the passage lists individual healthcare-seeking behavior as one factor influencing recognition, it does not present evidence that most patients with zoonotic infections fail to seek care and that this is the principal reason for low reported case frequencies. Instead, the text emphasizes clinician-related factors (nonspecific presentations, low awareness) and diagnostic constraints as key explanations, and provides direct evidence of missed clinical diagnoses despite confirmed cases. Therefore the specific claim that patient non-attendance is the main driver is not supported by the passage.

180.Option: Cx43 plays a key role in cell proliferation, potentially leading to cholangiocarcinoma development upon stimulation by excretory-secretory products of Clonorchis sinensis and the carcinogen NDMA.

ESP and NDMA synergistically increase H69 cell proliferation

These findings support the inference that Cx43 has a functional, permissive role in promoting cell proliferation under NDMA+ESP stimulation and therefore may contribute to cholangiocarcinoma-related cellular changes.

180.Option: The upregulation of Cx43 expression in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis is likely an adaptive protective response of the liver to enable better cell-to-cell communication.

Among gap junction proteins, Cx43 is involved in almost all steps of the inflammatory response of cells, cytokine production, and inflammatory cell migration [17, 20, 22]. ESP and NDMA synergistically increase H69 cell proliferation
We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.
180.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Cx43 upregulation is associated with increased proliferation and cancer-related marker expression, and that silencing Cx43 reduces these pro-tumorigenic changes. Prior statements in the passage link high Cx43 expression to increased tumor invasion in other cancers. There is no evidence in the passage that Cx43 upregulation is an adaptive protective response; rather the data indicate a pro-proliferative, potentially pro-carcinogenic role. Therefore the protective-response interpretation is not supported by the provided evidence.

180.Option: The inhibition of Cox-2 expression following Cx43 knockdown suggests that Cx43 functions as a direct suppressor of inflammatory signaling in cholangiocytes exposed to NDMA and ESP.
Evidence: Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2 (Fig 5C and 5D, respectively).

The expression levels of several cell proliferation- including E2F1, Ki-67 and Cy-19, and Cox-2 (an essential regulator of the G2/M transition) as cancer-related makers, were upregulated, especially in NDMA + ESP-treated cells (Fig 2).
We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.

If Cx43 were a suppressor of inflammatory signaling, its knockdown would be expected to increase Cox-2, not decrease it. Thus the data indicate that Cx43 promotes (rather than suppresses) Cox-2–associated inflammatory/cancer-related signaling; the option's claim is the opposite of what the experimental evidence shows.

After treatment with NDMA and ESP for 72 h, the concentration of intracellular connexin 26, 32, and 43 in H69 cells was measured by laser scanning microscopy (×4,000). Together, these observations permit the epidemiological/biological inference that loss/downregulation of Cx32 in this model is consistent with a tumor-suppressor role for Cx32 in the context of clonorchiasis-associated carcinogenic changes.

180.Option: The synergistic increase in cell proliferation and G2/M phase progression observed in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP is primarily driven by the upregulation of Cx26 rather than Cx43.
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).
We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.
Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2 (Fig 5C and 5D, respectively).
ESP and NDMA synergistically increase H69 cell proliferation
180.Rationale: Both Cx26 and Cx43 are upregulated with NDMA+ESP, but experimental perturbation (Cx43 silencing) reduced proliferation and also caused Cx26 to decrease. This indicates Cx43 has a functional upstream role in mediating proliferation and influences Cx26 levels. The passage provides no evidence that Cx26, independently of Cx43, is the primary driver; rather the data point to Cx43 as a key mediator. Therefore the claim that Cx26 is primarily responsible is not supported by the experimental evidence in the passage.

181.Option: The high compliance with repeated PZQ treatments and the low frequency of adverse events suggest that multiple doses of praziquantel are both safe and highly effective in eliminating Schistosoma mansoni infections in school-aged children.
Evidence: In the intense treatment group, compliance to each following treatment was unexpectedly high, from 100% in week 2 (second treatment) to 98% in week 6 (fourth and final treatment).
Most of the adverse events were mild and all of them resolved 24 hours after treatment. Adverse events decreased with subsequent treatments in the intense treatment group.
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group, (P<0.01; primary outcome, both CRs measured 10 weeks after first treatment).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
In both groups, most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point.
181.Rationale: While the passage provides clear evidence of very high compliance and mostly mild, transient adverse events (supporting safety/tolerability of repeated PZQ), it does not support the stronger claim that multiple doses are 'highly effective in eliminating' infections. KK-based cure rates are high in the intense group, but POC-CCA — which detects active worm infection — shows much lower cure rates and that most children remained POC-CCA positive. Thus elimination (true parasitological cure) is not demonstrated; the evidence supports safety but not unequivocal elimination of infection.

181.Option: Since the POC-CCA test detects active infections and the KK technique detects eggs, the higher cure rate observed with KK must be due to the test's greater specificity, indicating that PZQ is highly effective in killing adult worms.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods, such as urine filtration for S. haematobium and the stool-based Kato-Katz (KK) technique for S. japonicum and S. mansoni. However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group... (KK-based).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% ... and 36% ... in the intense treatment group.
181.Rationale: The passage states that POC-CCA detects active worm infections and that POC-CCA has higher sensitivity than KK. It also explicitly states that microscopy-based methods (KK) lack sensitivity and may overestimate cure rates. Therefore, the higher KK cure rate is not evidence of greater specificity of KK nor definitive proof that PZQ is highly effective at killing adult worms. The assertion that KK's higher CR 'must be due to the test's greater specificity' is not supported by the passage; the passage instead suggests KK under-detects infections (lower sensitivity), causing apparent higher CRs.

181.Option: The use of the POC-CCA test revealed a significantly lower cure rate compared to the KK technique, indicating that PZQ may be less efficacious than previously reported due to persistent active infections despite treatment.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group ... (KK-based).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
In both groups, most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point.

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods... However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
181.Rationale: The passage directly reports that POC-CCA (which detects circulating antigen from live worms) shows much lower cure rates than KK (which detects eggs). Because POC-CCA is more sensitive for detecting active infections, the lower POC-CCA CRs indicate persistent active infections despite treatment. The authors also note that microscopy-based CRs are likely overestimates because of low sensitivity. Therefore, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the provided data that PZQ may be less efficacious at eliminating active infections than suggested by KK-based estimates.

181.Option: The discrepancy between KK and POC-CCA results suggests that the POC-CCA test is unreliable for detecting Schistosoma mansoni infections in post-treatment settings, particularly in children with low-intensity infections.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].
Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods... However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18].

The proportion of POC-CCA negatives (including traces) increased over time, particularly in the intense treatment group. In both groups, most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point.
It is now being recommended for surveillance and mapping of prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis [10, 18, 21, 23].
181.Rationale: The passage characterizes POC-CCA as more sensitive than KK and recommended for surveillance, which argues against labeling it 'unreliable' post-treatment. The discrepancy is explained in the passage by KK's limited sensitivity and the fact that KK detects eggs while POC-CCA detects antigen from active worms. There is no evidence in the passage that POC-CCA performs poorly specifically in post-treatment or low-intensity settings; rather, the passage implies KK under-detects infections and thus overestimates cure rates.

181.Option: The increase in POC-CCA positivity after the fourth treatment in the intense group indicates that repeated praziquantel doses may stimulate worm fecundity or accelerate parasite maturation, leading to renewed egg production.
Evidence: The proportion of POC-CCA negatives (including traces) increased over time, particularly in the intense treatment group.
In both groups, most children remained POC-CCA positive at the final time point.

In addition to the possible overestimation of CRs due to insensitive diagnostic tools, the limited activity of PZQ on immature worms as well as continuing reinfection might have led to an underestimation of the efficacy [24–27].

181.Rationale: The passage does not report an increase in POC-CCA positivity after the fourth treatment; instead it notes POC-CCA negatives increased over time, particularly in the intense group. Moreover, there is no evidence provided that repeated PZQ stimulates worm fecundity or accelerates maturation. The passage offers alternative explanations for persistent antigen positivity such as limited activity of PZQ on immature worms and reinfection, but nothing that supports the causal mechanism proposed in the option. Thus this inference is not supported by the study data.

182.Option: The fact that H2B-derived peptides were the most immunogenic in inducing IFN-γ responses suggests that H2B is the dominant protective antigen in human leishmaniasis and should be prioritized in future vaccine development.
Evidence: However, among these peptides, H2BI and H2BII showed the highest IFN-γ levels, although not statistically significant when compared to unstimulated cultures. We further analyzed these 2 peptides in a larger group of healed CL and in healthy individuals. As shown in Fig 1, significant levels of IFN-γ were detected in healed CL, when compared to unstimulated cultures (Fig 1B). In addition, H2BI and H2BII were not able to induce a significant IFN-γ response in healthy individuals (Fig 1B).

These proteins are involved in the organization and function of DNA within the nucleus for histone, and in resistance to complement lysis and macrophage invasion for PSA, whereas a role in phagosome maturation and regulation of the secretory pathway was described for RAB proteins in Leishmania parasites. H2B, PSA and LmlRAB are highly conserved among Leishmania species while displaying low level of homology with mammalian homologues. They are present in both amastigote and promastigote stages and confer significant protection in mice and dogs [47–52].
182.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that H2BI and H2BII peptides elicited the highest IFN-γ among the tested individual peptides and that these peptides induced significant IFN-γ in healed CL subjects. This supports that H2B-derived peptides are immunogenic. However, the option asserts that H2B is the dominant protective antigen in human leishmaniasis and should be prioritized for vaccine development — a causal claim about protection and prioritization that is not supported by the Passage Body. The passage reports immunogenicity (IFN-γ responses) in humans and protection data only in animal models (mice and dogs), but it does not demonstrate that H2B provides protective efficacy in humans or that it is dominant over other antigens in conferring protection. Therefore the stronger conclusion about dominance as a protective antigen and recommendation for prioritization cannot be derived solely from the provided evidence.

182.Option: The ability of the multi-epitope peptide pools to induce high levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α, but not IL-10, indicates that they can effectively mimic the immune response seen in natural infection and may serve as a reliable surrogate for live antigen exposure in vaccine development.
Evidence: IFN-γ levels induced by P21 pool (including all multi-epitope peptides) in cured CL individuals (median/IQR: 1207/928) were similar to those induced by SLA in the same group (1182/1837), (p>0.05) (Fig 3).
Significant TNF-α levels were induced by P21 in cured (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: 10/13; 0/1.9) (p = 0.001), but not in healthy individuals (5.4/2; 1.9/10) (p>0.05) (Fig 6A).
No IL-10 was observed in response to peptides used either individually (except low but significant levels observed for H2BII) or as peptide pools (Figs 4 and 5).

No GrB response was observed after P21 stimulation in cured CL individuals (Fig 6B). High and significant levels of TNF-α and GrB were observed in cured CL but not in healthy individuals, in response to SLA stimulation (p<0.005) (Fig 6).
As shown in Fig 10, no significant increase was observed in the percentage of CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, IL-2 and TNF-α, in response to both peptide pools and SLA in cured CL individuals.
182.Rationale: The passage shows that some peptide pools (notably P21) induced IFN-γ at levels similar to SLA and induced TNF-α in cured individuals while peptides generally did not induce IL-10. These data support that the peptides can reproduce certain elements of the immune signature seen with SLA (a crude Leishmania antigen). However, the passage also shows important differences: peptides did not induce Granzyme B (GrB) in response to P21 whereas SLA did, and multifunctional triple-positive CD4+ T cells (IFN-γ+ IL-2+ TNF-α+) were not significantly increased by peptide pools or SLA. Because the peptide pools do not replicate all aspects of the live/whole-antigen response (e.g., GrB induction, multifunctionality), concluding they are a reliable surrogate for live antigen exposure and therefore suitable as direct surrogates for vaccine development is not fully supported by the Passage Body. The evidence supports partial mimicry of natural infection but not equivalence or reliable surrogate status.

182.Option: The selected multi-epitope peptide pools were able to induce both CD4+ and CD8+ IFN-γ-secreting T cell responses, as well as a significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ and CD8+ central memory T cells (TCM) in individuals with healed cutaneous leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a significantly higher percentage of CD4+T cells producing IFN-γ in response to all selected peptide pools, in comparison to unstimulated cultures (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: P8: 0.7/1.9; 0.2/0.7, P9: 1.6/2.5; 0.3/0.6, P13: 4/6; 0.1/0.6, P14: 1.1/1.1; 0.2/0.7, P16: 3/4; 1/1.3 and P21: 1.2/3.1; 0.1/0.6) (p≤0.009) (Fig 8A and S1 Fig).

As shown in Fig 9, a significant increase in the percentage of IFN-γ+ CD8+ T cells was observed after stimulation with P8 (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: 2.5/2.6; 2/1.4), P9 (2.6/2.8; 2/1.4), P13 (6.1/3.8; 2.1/0.9), P16 (3.3/4.8; 2/1.4) and P21 (4.6/7; 2/1.4) (p≤0.03).

A significant increase in the percentage of CD4+ TCM as well as CD8+ TCM cells was observed in response to P8 (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures for CD4+ and CD8+ TCM: 1.1/1.3; 0.3/0.1% and 0.3/0.3; 0.06/0.06), P13 (2/2; 0.4/0.8% and 0.5/1.3; 0.06/0.2), P14 (2/0.9; 0.3/0.1% and 0.3/0.4; 0.06/0.06) and P21 (2.3/1.4; 0.4/0.8% and 0.9/1.3; 0.06/0.2) (p≤0.04) (Fig 11A and 11C).
182.Rationale: The Passage Body documents statistically significant increases in IFN-γ-producing CD4+ T cells and IFN-γ-producing CD8+ T cells after stimulation with several selected multi-epitope peptide pools. It also reports significant increases in the frequencies of both CD4+ and CD8+ central memory T cells (CD45RO+CCR7+) in response to specific peptide pools (P8, P13, P14, P21). These observations directly support the inference that the peptide pools can induce both CD4+ and CD8+ IFN-γ responses and expand central memory T cell populations in individuals with healed cutaneous leishmaniasis.

182.Option: The suppressive effect of some peptide pools on IL-10 production suggests that these peptides may help prevent excessive inflammatory responses, thereby contributing to a balanced immune response in vaccinated individuals.
Evidence: However, a suppressive effect on IL-10 production was observed after stimulation with PII, P6, P8, P10, P13, P14 and P18 (Figs 4C and 5).
