Therefore the data do not provide evidence that microfilariae presence explains the increased activation; attributing the activation to microfilariae is not supported by the study data and cannot be derived from the passage.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is linked to systemic CD4 T cell activation, providing a possible mechanism for the increased HIV-susceptibility observed in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.
Evidence: Our data shows that W. bancrofti is an independent risk factor for HIV acquisition even when adjusting for sexual behavior, condom use, circumcision, marital status, age and gender, among other factors.
Activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for continuous HIV replication in vivo, as shown in several studies [17–20].

we studied expression of activation (HLA-DR, CD38) and maturation (CD27, CD45RO) markers in CD4 and CD8 T cell populations, as well as T regulatory CD4 T cells and HIV entry receptor (CCR5) in relation to W. bancrofti infection status.

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 Mann-Whitney test, univariable analysis, Fig 3A) or the combined group of individuals harboring other helminths ... (n = 151 median 7.38%, p = 0.009).
The significant association of W. bancrofti infection with HLA-DRpos CD4 T cell counts was confirmed in the multivariable analysis (Table 2).
224.Rationale: The passage documents (1) that W. bancrofti infection was previously associated with increased HIV acquisition, (2) that activated CD4 T cells are key targets for HIV, and (3) that W. bancrofti-infected individuals in this study had significantly higher frequencies of activated (HLA-DRpos, and HLA-DRposCD38pos) CD4 T cells, with the association confirmed in multivariable analysis. These points together support the epidemiological inference that W. bancrofti is linked to systemic CD4 T cell activation, which provides a plausible mechanism for increased HIV susceptibility described for these individuals.

224.Option: The lack of significant difference in CD8 T cell activation after multivariable adjustment implies that W. bancrofti infection does not influence systemic immune activation in the study population.
Evidence: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection ... (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 ... )
The significant association of W. bancrofti infection with HLA-DRpos CD4 T cell counts was confirmed in the multivariable analysis (Table 2).
224.Rationale: The option overgeneralizes from a non-significant CD8 finding to conclude no influence on systemic immune activation. The passage shows that although CD8 activation differences lost statistical significance after adjustment, CD4 T cell activation was significantly and robustly increased (confirmed in multivariable analysis). Systemic immune activation cannot be ruled out on the basis of CD8 results alone; the clear CD4 activation indicates W. bancrofti does influence systemic immune status. Therefore the option's conclusion is not supported by the data.

224.Option: Treatment of W. bancrofti infection may reduce immune activation and thereby decrease HIV transmission in areas where lymphatic filariasis is prevalent, as suggested by the observed immunological changes.
Evidence: Infections with W. bancrofti are chronic and persist over many years despite antihelminthic treatment.

Our data shows that W. bancrofti is an independent risk factor for HIV acquisition even when adjusting for sexual behavior, condom use, circumcision, marital status, age and gender, among other factors.

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 Mann-Whitney test, univariable analysis, Fig 3A) or the combined group of individuals harboring other helminths ... (n = 151 median 7.38%, p = 0.009).
In the study area, governmental treatment of W. bancrofti started in 2009. However, none of the 235 participants reported participation in the mass drug administration program.
224.Rationale: The passage documents increased immune activation in W. bancrofti-infected individuals and prior observation of increased HIV acquisition, but it does not present any data on the effects of treatment on immune activation or HIV transmission. In fact, the text states infections can persist despite antihelminthic treatment and that study participants did not participate in mass drug administration, so no treatment-outcome data are available. Therefore the causal policy inference that treating W. bancrofti will reduce activation and HIV transmission is not supported by the presented data and cannot be derived from the passage.

225.Option: The high incidence of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for dengue fever (DF) or dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) suggests that the clinical spectrum of symptomatic dengue infection is broader than previously recognized, and that current diagnostic criteria may miss a substantial proportion of cases.
Evidence: Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF (i.e. acute febrile illness with at least two of the following symptoms: headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, hemorrhagic manifestations, leucopenia) or DHF (i.e. fever with hemorrhagic tendency, thrombocytopenia and evidence of plasma leakage) but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.
There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF including 22 cases (7.0%), 5 cases (1.6%), and 7 cases (2.2%) of DHF grade 1, 2 and 3, respectively.
225.Rationale: The passage defines UF as symptomatic dengue infections that do not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF but are confirmed by serology. Nearly half (49.8%) of the confirmed symptomatic dengue episodes were UF. From these facts it is epidemiologically justified to infer that the symptomatic clinical spectrum includes a substantial portion of presentations that would not be captured by DF/DHF clinical criteria alone, and therefore existing clinical criteria (used without laboratory confirmation) can miss many cases.

225.Option: The clinical manifestations of dengue infection are highly variable across different serotypes, with DEN4 being associated with more severe disease despite its lower prevalence.
Evidence: Dengue serotypes were identified by either mosquito inoculation or RT-PCR in 259 cases (82.7%) including 115 (36.7%), 80 (25.6%), 41 (13.1%), and 23 (7.3%) cases of DEN1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively.

DEN4 was found to cause only UF, DF and DHF grade 1 and seemed to have less headache, anorexia, nausea/vomiting and rash compared to other DEN serotypes. However, these differences are not statistically significant.
225.Rationale: While the passage reports some variability in manifestations by serotype, it explicitly states DEN4 caused only UF, DF and only DHF grade 1 (i.e. not the most severe grades) and that observed differences across serotypes were not statistically significant. Thus the specific claim that DEN4 is associated with more severe disease is not supported by the study data.

225.Option: The high proportion of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases in this cohort suggests that dengue infection is more commonly asymptomatic than previously believed.
Evidence: In two studies, infected Thai children, ranging from 49 [3] to 87% [4], were asymptomatic.
There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF including 22 cases (7.0%), 5 cases (1.6%), and 7 cases (2.2%) of DHF grade 1, 2 and 3, respectively.
Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF ... or DHF ... but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.
225.Rationale: The option conflates UF (a symptomatic, serologically confirmed but clinically non-specific febrile presentation) with asymptomatic infection. The passage distinguishes asymptomatic infections (reported in prior studies) from UF. The reported high proportion of UF refers to symptomatic cases that lacked specific DF/DHF clinical criteria, not to asymptomatic infections. Therefore the study's finding about UF does not support the inference that dengue is more commonly asymptomatic.

225.Option: The presence of diarrhea in dengue infection may be related to plasma leakage that causes malabsorption, suggesting it could serve as an early predictor of severe dengue disease.
Evidence: It was found that nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, diarrhea and petechiae were statistically more common in DHF compared to DF and UF.

The exceptions are for anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea that tended to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 of illness. It was also revealed that most symptoms were more common in DHF and, specifically, DHF cases had significantly higher prevalence of anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain during day 3–6 and diarrhea during day 4–6 of illness (Chi-square test; p<0.05).
DHF (i.e. fever with hemorrhagic tendency, thrombocytopenia and evidence of plasma leakage)

Table 6 shows the occurrence of selected clinical manifestations and their predictive value for DHF among symptomatic dengue infected cases. It was found that all of these clinical manifestations had low positive predictive value but high negative predictive value for DHF.
225.Rationale: The passage shows diarrhea is more common in DHF and tends to increase later in the illness (day 4–6), and it defines DHF as involving plasma leakage. However, the study does not present evidence linking diarrhea mechanistically to plasma-leakage–related malabsorption, nor does it show diarrhea functions as an early predictor; in fact diarrhea peaks later in illness. Additionally the passage reports low positive predictive value for clinical manifestations for DHF, arguing against diarrhea being a reliable early predictor. Therefore the specific causal/mechanistic and predictive claims are not supported by the presented data.

225.Option: The WHO's new guidelines for dengue diagnosis may fail to identify UF cases because clinicians are unlikely to suspect dengue in patients presenting with mild, non-specific symptoms.
Evidence: Clinical diagnosis of DF, DHF and grading of DHF were made using WHO's criteria [2] after the subject recovered.

Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF (i.e. acute febrile illness with at least two of the following symptoms: headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, hemorrhagic manifestations, leucopenia) or DHF (i.e. fever with hemorrhagic tendency, thrombocytopenia and evidence of plasma leakage) but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.

The clinical diagnosis of these 71 and 85 UF cases are shown in table 1. Significantly more cases in the group whose data were obtained were diagnosed as acute febrile illness/suspected viral infection (p<0.001).
Blood samples for dengue diagnostic tests were collected from all febrile subjects irrespective of clinical diagnoses.
225.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that UF cases are serologically confirmed dengue infections that do not meet WHO clinical criteria and that many of these were clinically labeled as acute febrile illness or suspected viral infection. Because WHO clinical criteria were used for clinical diagnosis and UF cases by definition do not meet those criteria, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the study data that reliance on clinical guidelines alone—without laboratory testing—will miss UF cases and that clinicians may not suspect dengue when symptoms are mild and non-specific.

226.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis in dogs and their frequent movement between households and villages suggest that they are the most important reservoirs for the spread of T. penetrans in rural Uganda.
Evidence: Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs were significantly more affected than other species (pigs vs. dogs, p<0.0001; pigs vs. cats, p = 0.02; pigs vs. goats, p<0.0001.
Dogs, cats and poultry roam freely on compounds throughout the year.
226.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts and that pigs were significantly more affected than dogs (p<0.0001). Although dogs do roam freely, the data show lower prevalence and intensity in dogs compared with pigs. Therefore the claim that dogs are the most important reservoirs is not supported by the study findings.

226.Option: The fact that pigs are not confined during the crop-growing season suggests that their role in transmission is limited to the dry season, when sand flea activity peaks.
Evidence: the majority (154 out of 155; 99.4%) confined pigs on earthen floors during the crop growing season and released them to scavenge after harvest.
A cross sectional study was conducted between January 22 and March 28, 2014 which coincided with the middle and end of the dry season when the attack rate of T. penetrans usually peaks [20].
226.Rationale: The option's premise is incorrect: the passage reports that pigs were confined during the crop-growing season and released after harvest (i.e. they are not roaming during crop season). The study was performed during the dry season when attack rate peaks, but the passage does not state that pig involvement is limited to the dry season; in fact pigs are released to scavenge after harvest which could coincide with dry periods. The statement mischaracterizes the reported husbandry pattern and draws a temporal-limitation inference that is not supported by the data.

226.Option: The absence of tungiasis in cattle and chickens suggests that these species are not susceptible to T. penetrans infection, and therefore cannot contribute to transmission in the study area.
Evidence: Pigs, dogs, goats and a single cat were the only infected domestic mammalian species out of the seven examined (Table 2).

Rats (n = 65) and all poultry species examined were not infected even in households where other animal species and humans were heavily infected. However, many chickens were infested with the flea Echdinophaga gallinacea.
226.Rationale: While the study observed no infections in cattle and poultry in this sample, absence of detected infection in this cross-sectional survey does not prove species-level non-susceptibility. The passage notes chickens had a different flea species and that certain species vary in reservoir importance by area. Thus it is not epidemiologically justified to conclude cattle and chickens are not susceptible or cannot contribute to transmission based solely on these negative findings.

226.Option: Pigs were identified as the major hosts of T. penetrans in the study area, and the strong correlation between human and pig tungiasis prevalence and infection intensity supports their role as the primary reservoir for zoonotic transmission.
Evidence: Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs were significantly more affected than other species (pigs vs. dogs, p<0.0001; pigs vs. cats, p = 0.02; pigs vs. goats, p<0.0001.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).

Occurrence of infected animals in households was strongly associated with human infections (OR = 6.0, 95% CI 2.4–9.1; p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs in a household (OR = 5.8, 95% CI 2.5–13.5; p < 0.0001) among other factors.
The odds of households to have pigs infected with T. penetrans were significantly increased if there was also human tungiasis as illustrated in Fig 7B (OR = 2.1, p = 0.001).
226.Rationale: The passage directly identifies pigs as the most important domestic hosts and documents higher prevalence and much higher parasite loads in pigs than in other species. Strong, significant correlations between pig lesion counts and human prevalence at both household and village levels, plus multivariable results showing presence of pigs increases odds of animal tungiasis and pig infection odds increase with human tungiasis, support the epidemiological inference that pigs act as a primary reservoir contributing to zoonotic transmission in this setting.

226.Option: The strong correlation between human and animal tungiasis is likely due to shared environmental exposure rather than direct zoonotic transmission, indicating that pigs play a minimal role in the transmission cycle.
Evidence: In 40 (17%, 95% CI 12.7–22.3) of the sampled households, animal and human tungiasis coexisted. These constituted 50% and 64.5% of the households with human and animal tungiasis respectively.
Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.
Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).

Occurrence of infected animals in households was strongly associated with human infections (OR = 6.0, 95% CI 2.4–9.1; p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs in a household (OR = 5.8, 95% CI 2.5–13.5; p < 0.0001) among other factors.
226.Rationale: Although shared environmental exposure could contribute to the observed correlation, the passage provides multiple lines of evidence implicating pigs as important reservoirs: pigs had the highest parasite loads, pigs were identified as the most important hosts, and presence of pigs strongly increased odds of animal tungiasis and correlated with human prevalence. The data therefore support a substantive role for pigs rather than a minimal role, and the study does not provide evidence sufficient to attribute the correlation primarily to shared environmental exposure over zoonotic transmission.

227.Option: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the key environmental determinants of tsetse presence in the study area were forest cover and riverine vegetation, while savannah vegetation and elevation were negatively associated with tsetse presence.
Evidence: In the autologistic model, forest (p<0.05, OR = 1.105) and riverine vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 1.008) were positively correlated with tsetse presence.
Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) were negatively correlated.
These three land cover classes and elevation are, thus, considered to be important determinants of tsetse presence and absence in the study area.
227.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports the results of the autologistic (spatial) model: forest and riverine vegetation had statistically significant positive associations with tsetse presence, while savannah vegetation and elevation had statistically significant negative associations. The text also states these covariates are important determinants after accounting for spatial autocorrelation, so the option is directly supported by the passage.

227.Option: Temperature was the most influential factor in determining tsetse presence in the multivariate logistic regression model, with a strong positive association (p<0.05, OR = 2.63).
Evidence: In an initial univariate logistic regression stage, 44% of the land cover variables had a statistically significant association with tsetse presence-absence (p<0.05). Covariates; cropland, forest, riverine vegetation, woody vegetation, NDVI, elevation, temperature and rainfall were all positively correlated (p<0.05, Odds ratio (OR)>1),
Cropland, temperature and rainfall failed to retain their significant association with tsetse presence (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation.
227.Rationale: While temperature was positively associated in earlier univariate/multivariate analyses, the passage does not provide an OR of 2.63 and, critically, states that temperature "failed to retain" significance (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation. Therefore the specific claim about temperature being the most influential factor with OR=2.63 is not supported by the passage.

227.Option: Rainfall and cropland were found to be positively associated with tsetse presence in the final autologistic regression model, indicating their importance in supporting tsetse populations.
Evidence: The significant covariates were; rainfall, elevation, temperature, cropland, savannah vegetation, forest, and riverine vegetation. Parameter estimates are given in Table 2.
However, the covariates cropland, riverine vegetation, elevation and rainfall presented only very small positive associations, with wide confidence intervals.
Cropland, temperature and rainfall failed to retain their significant association with tsetse presence (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation.
227.Rationale: Although rainfall and cropland showed small positive associations in the multivariate (non-spatial) model, the passage explicitly states that cropland and rainfall did not remain significant (p>0.05) in the autologistic (spatial) model. Thus the assertion that they were positively associated in the final spatial model is not supported by the passage.

227.Option: Savannah vegetation and elevation were positively associated with tsetse presence, as they provide suitable microclimatic conditions for the survival of G. f. fuscipes.
Evidence: In an initial univariate logistic regression stage, ... savannah vegetation, herbaceous vegetation and built-up area were negatively correlated (p<0.05, OR<1) with tsetse presence.
In the autologistic model, ... Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) were negatively correlated.
227.Rationale: The passage reports that savannah vegetation and elevation were negatively correlated with tsetse presence (ORs slightly below 1) in both univariate and the autologistic spatial model. Therefore the claim that they were positively associated is contradicted by the reported results and is not derivable from the passage.

228.Option: Groundwater iron concentration is a predominant schistosomiasis risk factor, with high iron levels in improved water sources associated with increased surface water use and higher transmission risk.
Evidence: Two additional raster layers of interpolated groundwater iron and total dissolved solids (TDS) concentrations (mg/l) were also obtained from a prior study [32].

Groundwater quality variables were included because prior studies [29,32,33] suggested that elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors for schistosomiasis transmission.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03. However, iron concentration became by far the most important variable.

The importance of iron concentration was also evident in the predicted prevalence surfaces, with high values on the western side of the Atiwa Mountain Range (Fig 6) coinciding with high groundwater iron content (S21 Fig, Supporting Information).
228.Rationale: The passage reports that groundwater iron was included as a WASH predictor because prior studies suggested elevated iron in boreholes (BHs) may lead communities to rely more on contaminated surface water, an indirect risk mechanism. In the study's final model, iron concentration "became by far the most important variable" and predicted prevalence surfaces coincided with areas of high groundwater iron. Together these statements support the inference that groundwater iron concentration is a predominant risk factor and that high iron in improved sources can be associated with increased surface water use and higher transmission risk.

228.Option: The high predictive power of the final model, as indicated by R2 and RMSE values, suggests that environmental and WASH variables together can reliably forecast schistosomiasis prevalence at the community level.
Evidence: The R2 values for all models were relatively low (<0.20), indicating that environmental variables alone were not able to describe more than 15–20% of the variability in S. haematobium prevalence, regardless of RS data source or extraction mask.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
228.Rationale: The passage explicitly states model explanatory power was low (R2 < 0.20) and that adding WASH variables only marginally improved R2 (from 0.15 to 0.17) and modestly reduced RMSE. These reported metrics do not indicate high predictive power or reliable forecasting at the community level, so the option's claim is not supported by the passage.

228.Option: Improved water access, as measured by functional improved water sources (FIWS), is a significant predictor of reduced schistosomiasis risk in the study communities.
Evidence: SPs, BHs, and protected HDWs that were functional at the time of the study constituted functional improved water sources (FIWS) that are not capable of transmitting schistosomiasis.
FIWS and SWAP access indicators were not important in the final model.
228.Rationale: Although FIWS are defined as improved sources not capable of transmitting schistosomiasis, the passage states that FIWS access indicators "were not important in the final model." Therefore the data do not support the claim that improved water access (FIWS) was a significant predictor of reduced schistosomiasis risk in these analyses.

228.Option: The use of surface water access points (SWAPs) as a mask for environmental variable extraction produced the highest correlation between predicted and observed prevalence values, indicating that SWAPs are the most accurate proxy for schistosomiasis transmission risk.
Evidence: SWAPs {6}–defined as the single pixel GPS points of known surface water contact sites.
Models derived using the SWAP mask produced the highest correlation values using both Landsat 8 (r = 0.76, p < 0.01) and Sentinel-2 data (r = 0.67, p < 0.01) (Table 4).

From a visual assessment of the predicted prevalence surfaces produced using environmental variables (Fig 5; S24–S33 Figs, Supporting Information), it appears that the SWAP mask resulted in more precise prediction, including correct delineation of water bodies as high-risk locations (Fig 5, panel A6).
228.Rationale: The passage defines SWAPs as known surface water contact sites and reports that models using the SWAP mask achieved the highest correlations with observed prevalence (r = 0.76 and r = 0.67). The authors also note the SWAP mask visually produced more precise predictions and correctly delineated high-risk water bodies. These statements support the inference that using SWAPs was the most accurate proxy among the tested extraction methods for transmission-relevant environmental conditions.

228.Option: Elevated iron concentration in groundwater is a direct cause of schistosomiasis infection, as it increases the viability of Schistosoma cercariae in water bodies.
Evidence: Groundwater quality variables were included because prior studies [29,32,33] suggested that elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors for schistosomiasis transmission.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03. However, iron concentration became by far the most important variable.
228.Rationale: The passage describes elevated groundwater iron as a potential indirect risk factor because it may increase reliance on contaminated surface water, and it reports iron was the most important predictor in the model. However, it provides no evidence that iron directly increases cercarial viability or is a direct biological cause of infection. The mechanism presented is behavioral (increased surface water use), not a demonstrated direct effect on parasite viability, so the causal statement in the option is not supported by the passage.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are at higher risk of leptospirosis due to greater exposure to floodwater and standing water after hurricanes, which are known to harbor Leptospira spp.
Evidence: Leptospira spp. can survive in fresh water and wet soil for weeks to months in appropriate environmental conditions [7,8].
As demonstrated in 2017, outbreaks can occur after extreme weather events like hurricanes and floods [1,9–14].
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03),
229.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Leptospira can survive in fresh water and that outbreaks can follow hurricanes and floods, and it reports a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity. However, the specific causal claim that urban residents are at higher risk because they have greater exposure to floodwater/standing water after hurricanes is not supported by data in the passage. The survey did not present measurements of floodwater exposure by community type or link urban residence to greater floodwater contact. Therefore the specific causal mechanism asserted (greater post-hurricane floodwater exposure among urban residents) cannot be derived solely from the passage.

229.Option: The association between urban residence and leptospirosis seropositivity suggests that urban environments in USVI may have higher levels of environmental contamination with Leptospira spp. due to increased rodent populations and poor infrastructure.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03),

Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01),

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included ... seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02);
229.Rationale: The passage does link rodent presence/contact to seropositivity and shows an urban bivariate association, so the idea that rodents could explain urban associations is plausible. However, the passage does not provide evidence that urban environments in USVI have higher environmental contamination with Leptospira spp. or that urban areas have increased rodent populations or poor infrastructure relative to non-urban areas. The claim adds the unmeasured attribution of 'poor infrastructure' and a stated higher rodent prevalence in urban settings, which are not documented in the passage. Thus the specific explanatory statement cannot be directly derived from the provided data.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are more likely to have been exposed to Leptospira spp. through contact with domestic animals such as cows and rodents, which are more prevalent in urban settings.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01),
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002),
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001),

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02);
229.Rationale: The passage shows that contact with cows and rodents is strongly associated with seropositivity (both in bivariate and multivariate analyses). However, it does not provide any data that cows or rodents are more prevalent in urban settings in USVI, nor that urban residents had higher animal contact. The option asserts that domestic animals and rodents are more prevalent in urban areas, which is not supported by the text. Therefore the specific inference about urban residents being more likely exposed through those animals because they are more prevalent in urban settings cannot be derived from the passage.

229.Option: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03),

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers, which raises the potential for misclassification. It also shows that urban residence was significant in bivariate analysis but that urban residence is not listed among variables independently associated with seropositivity in the multivariate model. Together these facts justify cautious interpretation of the bivariate urban association because it may be confounded by other factors (e.g., animal contacts) or affected by subjective classification.

229.Option: The higher seropositivity among urban residents reflects a true geographic disparity in leptospirosis risk, indicating that urban areas in USVI should be prioritized for targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03),

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: While there was a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity, the passage indicates that neighborhood classification was subjective and that urban residence did not persist as an independent predictor in multivariate analysis. The multivariate results instead identify specific exposures (contact with cows and rodents, and increasing age) as independent factors. Because the urban association may reflect confounding or misclassification rather than a true independent geographic disparity, concluding that urban areas definitively should be prioritized for interventions is not justified by the data presented.

230.Option: The real-time surveillance system demonstrated in this study relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework that is highly efficient and error-prone, as evidenced by the high completion rates and remote monitoring of data.
Evidence: we have tested the feasibility of using software-coupled mobile phones operated with minimal training by village-based lay mHealth reporters
Paper forms were distributed as a backup alternative to mHealth data collection (e.g. in case of cell phone loss or failure).
the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).

Online software-integrated village-based survey forms, transmitted by smart phone to the Magpi data management website, were monitored weekly and analyzed by the program manager (RVA) located at Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU).

Any mistyping, duplicated reports, data inconsistencies or doubtful information were reported by phone or email to the field coordinator and from there to the corresponding field supervisor for double-checking, fixing, and resending if necessary.
The monitoring potential of Magpi also allowed for the rapid recognition of fabricated data
230.Rationale: The passage describes a digital, smartphone-based Magpi system as the primary reporting platform and explicitly notes paper forms only as a backup. Thus the claim that the system "relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework" is unsupported. While the passage documents high completion rates and remote monitoring, it also describes digital data transmission, automated monitoring, and correction of errors—not a paper-centered system. The statement's characterization as "highly efficient and error-prone" is a mixed assertion not supported as a single conclusion by the provided text.

230.Option: The use of GPS stamps and timestamped data in the Magpi system ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling perfect spatial and temporal mapping of disease outbreaks in real time.
Evidence: Magpi ... GPS stamping and display of data-points on an electronic map; and e) instantaneous data integration from multiple users.
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.

it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.
230.Rationale: Although the system includes GPS stamping and timestamp features and supports mapping, the passage explicitly documents failures and limitations: malfunctioning geotracers and weak/no cellular coverage that compromised acquisition of GPS stamps and mapping. Therefore the strong claim that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling "perfect" real-time mapping, is not supported by the text.

230.Option: The use of minimally trained lay mHealth reporters with basic smartphones enabled real-time monitoring of neurological health data in remote rural communities, allowing for near-real-time identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths with a delay of no more than 1–7 days.
Evidence: we have tested the feasibility of using software-coupled mobile phones operated with minimal training by village-based lay mHealth reporters charged with repeatedly assessing the status of children
Their weekly reports, transmitted by simple smartphones, were instantaneously aggregated, analyzed and mapped by data collection centers locally (Gulu, Uganda) and remotely (Oregon, USA).

The survey, which was applied once weekly, included information on coded household location, number of NS children/household, seizure frequency, availability of anti-seizure medication, and injuries and deaths.

mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.

The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data ... highlight those households with at least one child with NS that did not have anti-seizure medication (Fig 2E1, green color), was injured (Fig 2E2, green color) or died (Fig 2E3) that week.
the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week
230.Rationale: The passage documents that minimally trained lay reporters using smartphones collected data on household location, medication availability, injuries, and deaths; those reports were aggregated, analyzed and mapped instantaneously upon upload. Upload frequency depended on connectivity and was daily or weekly, supporting the assertion that identification could occur with delays up to about 1–7 days. The Magpi interface explicitly allowed visualization of households without medication, injured children, and deaths for a given week. Taken together, these facts support the inference that the system enabled near-real-time identification of hotspots and shortages with typical delays no greater than a week.

230.Option: The high survey completion rate of 97.3% indicates that the mHealth system is fully independent of network connectivity and can operate without any delays in data transmission, even in areas with poor infrastructure.
Evidence: the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).

mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.
some mHealth workers reported traveling to a specific hotspot or even climbing a particular tree to access satisfactory signal strength and network coverage.
On the first day of September, all mobile phones ran out of data because the network provider failed to renew the monthly phone bundle on time.

Although the surveys collected during this period were stored on the memory of the cell phone and could have been uploaded later, some reporters stopped working when they realized their data bundles were exhausted.
230.Rationale: While completion rates were high, the passage clearly indicates dependence on network connectivity and documents multiple connectivity-related delays (need to seek hotspots, phones running out of data, local storage with later upload). Therefore the completion rate does not imply full independence from network connectivity or absence of transmission delays; the system experienced infrastructure-dependent delays.

230.Option: The mHealth system's success in data collection and monitoring is primarily due to the high level of formal medical training and clinical expertise of the lay reporters, who were able to accurately diagnose and report neurological conditions without supervision.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained.

The training covered basic concepts of health and disease, the purpose and content of the program, clinical and social aspects of NS, use and maintenance of the equipment (cell phones, solar batteries, bicycles), questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission, home and community entry, informed consent administration and patient confidentiality.
A medical diagnostician (DLK) physically examined a sample of households reporting existing and newly reported NS cases, the large majority of which had longstanding but unregistered NS.

Other children that had not been registered with NS and who were identified as newly reported cases in the mHealth survey were referred to a primary health center for observation, NS confirmation and treatment.
230.Rationale: The passage describes the reporters as minimally trained lay persons whose instruction covered basic concepts and device use rather than advanced clinical training. Clinical confirmation and diagnosis were performed by a medical diagnostician and through referrals to health centers. Thus success is attributable to the data-collection system, supervision, and linkage to clinical follow-up—not to a high level of formal medical training or independent diagnostic capability among the lay reporters.

231.Option: The high proportion of multibacillary (MB) leprosy cases (up to 74.3% in 2019) suggests that the current leprosy elimination strategies in Yunnan are failing to prevent the progression of early-stage disease to advanced forms.
Evidence: Among the 11,052 newly detected cases, the majority (7,031, 63.6%) were classified as MB leprosy and 4,021 (36.4%) were classified as PB leprosy (Table 2).
the percentage of multibacillary (MB) cases was relatively high in most of the years investigated, ranging from 59.0% in 2008 to 74.3% in 2019 (Table 1).
early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P <0.01).
The percentage of detected cases involving G2D decreased slightly from 22.6% (n = 1550) in time period 1 to 19.2% (n = 812) in time period 2 (P <0.01) (Table 3).
With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.
231.Rationale: The passage documents a high proportion of MB cases but also shows improvements in other indicators (increased early detection, decreased G2D, and an overall decline in newly detected cases after interventions). From these data one cannot conclude that elimination strategies are 'failing' to prevent progression; the high MB proportion alone does not prove failure and may reflect case-mix, historical burden, or detection practices. The passage provides evidence of improvement rather than unequivocal failure, so the strong causal statement in the option is not supported by the text.

231.Option: The observed reduction in the number of newly detected leprosy cases after 2004 can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
Evidence: After many years of intensive effort, leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).
In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region. The amount given from 2004 to 2019 was 150 million CNY (US$25 million).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes reductions to governmental support, a surveillance system, MDT, a special fund, specific public health activities (contact examination, health education), and investments to improve transport and health services. However, it does not explicitly state that broader socioeconomic development or improved general education of the population were causes of the post-2004 decline. Because the option includes factors (socioeconomic development, improved education) that are not directly evidenced in the passage, the full statement cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

231.Option: The continued presence of leprosy in 17 highly endemic counties indicates that multidrug treatment (MDT) has been ineffective in preventing transmission in remote, mountainous regions of Yunnan.
Evidence: Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.

Despite the declaration of the elimination of leprosy at the national level in 1998 (<1/100,000), leprosy remains prevalent in some of the difficult-to-access mountain regions of Southwest China [2,4].
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3). Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
231.Rationale: While the passage documents that MDT has been used province-wide since 1990 and that 17 counties remain endemic—especially in difficult-to-access mountain regions—it does not provide evidence that MDT itself has been ineffective. The persistence of endemicity in remote areas could be due to access, surveillance, socioeconomic, or other programmatic barriers rather than failure of MDT as a treatment or transmission-blocking intervention. Therefore the strong causal inference that MDT has been ineffective cannot be derived from the passage.

231.Option: The increase in early detection rates from 53.9% to 60.3% between time periods 1 and 2 suggests that contact examination and health education have been the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.
Evidence: Interestingly, the percentage of newly detected patients belonging to an ethnic minority group increased from 51.4% (n = 3533) in time period 1 to 68.7% (n = 4181) in time period 2 (P <0.01). Additionally, early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P <0.01).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage shows that early detection increased and lists contact examination and health education among multiple interventions aimed at early detection. However, it also links declines in case numbers to other major interventions (MDT introduction, a special fund, surveillance and system-wide measures). The data do not isolate contact examination and health education as the 'primary' drivers; attributing primary causality to those two measures alone is not supported by the provided information.

231.Option: The substantial decline in leprosy case detection and the reduction in leprosy-endemic counties in Yunnan Province over the past 30 years can be attributed to the implementation of multidrug treatment (MDT), the establishment of a special fund for leprosy, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system.
Evidence: After many years of intensive effort, leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).
Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region. The amount given from 2004 to 2019 was 150 million CNY (US$25 million).
With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3).
231.Rationale: The passage directly links implementation of MDT to a significant decline in newly detected patients and states that the establishment of a special fund in 2004 was followed by further reductions. It also explicitly cites the establishment of an effective surveillance system and sustained government investment among the measures that have contributed to effective control. These statements support the inference that MDT, the special fund, and surveillance development were major contributors to the long-term decline.

232.Option: The high proportion of rabies cases in children under 15 years of age suggests that the current surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in younger populations.
Evidence: Almost half of cases occur in children under the age of 15 years [3, 5, 6].
Table 1 shows that, of the 14 cases of human rabies, 12 were children below 15 years of age and 8 were male.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage documents a high proportion of cases in children, but nowhere states that the surveillance system preferentially detects children. The observed age distribution can reflect true higher incidence or vulnerability in children (the passage even notes children are more affected), not surveillance sensitivity. Moreover, the passage also states that identified cases were not notified through routine surveillance, which argues against the idea that surveillance is effectively detecting any subgroup preferentially. Therefore the conclusion that surveillance is more effective at detecting younger patients is not supported by the passage.

232.Option: The absence of rabies cases in the routine surveillance system during the outbreak indicates that the surveillance system is functioning effectively by excluding false positives.
Evidence: Table 4 shows that in Maputo City, the number of cases of animal bites notified through the routine surveillance system between April and July 2014 was lower than the number identified during the outbreak investigation for the same period.
Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly indicates that routine surveillance under-reported cases relative to the investigation and that cases were missed because rabies was not suspected. The absence of notified cases therefore reflects failure to detect and report, not a successful exclusion of false positives. Nothing in the passage supports the inference that the lack of notifications represents correct exclusion; rather the text describes under-ascertainment.

232.Option: The poor quality of records at Prophylaxis Centers is the primary reason for the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Only 72 (8.7%) of animal bite victims had an animal bite investigation form filled out and the majority of these forms were incomplete.

Table 4 shows that in Maputo City, the number of cases of animal bites notified through the routine surveillance system between April and July 2014 was lower than the number identified during the outbreak investigation for the same period.
Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
232.Rationale: The passage documents poor record-keeping at Prophylaxis Centers, which likely contributes to under-reporting. However, it also provides other clear reasons for under-reporting—cases not being suspected clinically and therefore not recorded, failures in referral and post-exposure management—so the evidence does not support the stronger claim that poor records are the primary reason. The passage does not assign relative weights to causes, so concluding a single primary cause is not derivable from the provided information.

232.Option: The failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings, combined with a lack of clinical awareness among health professionals, contributes to the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
In Maputo City, of ten victims who attended health centers, nine were given only tetanus vaccine and parents were told that it was sufficient. They were not referred to the Prophylaxis Center.

The diagnosis of rabies was performed by experienced pediatricians and an experienced internal medicine specialist, for children and adults, respectively. Diagnosis was based on a combination of clinical findings and epidemiological history, without laboratory confirmation.
232.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that clinical suspicion was lacking ('Rabies was not suspected... they were not initially recorded as rabies') and that health centers often failed to refer bite victims for appropriate prophylaxis (most were given only tetanus vaccine and not referred). The reliance on clinical diagnosis without laboratory confirmation further increases the chance of missed or misclassified cases. Together these statements support the inference that failures in clinical recognition and awareness among health professionals contributed to under-reporting.

232.Option: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
Evidence: Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.

Table 4 shows that in Maputo City, the number of cases of animal bites notified through the routine surveillance system between April and July 2014 was lower than the number identified during the outbreak investigation for the same period.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that none of the identified rabies deaths during the key period were captured by routine surveillance and that routine notifications of animal bites were fewer than cases found by the active investigation. These direct statements support the conclusion that routine surveillance substantially under-reported rabies during this outbreak period.

233.Option: The failure to sustain a Th17 response beyond 14 days in the murine model indicates that this subset is not essential for long-term fungal control, as its decline coincides with disease remission.
Evidence: The number of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).
The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

These data suggest that Th17 cells might contribute to fungal elimination at the early stages of infection, whereas Th1 cells might be needed during the early and later stages of infection to eliminate the fungal pathogen.
in dectin-2 KO animals, Th17 contraction in the course of experimental CBM showed fungal burden impairment in the first 14 days of infection, although it did not affect disease resolution.
233.Rationale: The passage documents a strong Th17 response only during the first ~14 days and then a decline as Th1 responses rise, with that Th1 rise coinciding with remission. The authors explicitly state that Th17 likely contributes to early fungal elimination while Th1 is needed during early and later stages. Additionally, dectin-2 KO mice, which have reduced Th17, showed impaired control at early time points but did not have impaired overall disease resolution. Taken together, the experimental evidence supports the inference that Th17 is important for early control but is not essential for long-term clearance once Th1 responses develop, consistent with the option's statement.

233.Option: The initial Th17 response in the footpad is primarily responsible for the recruitment of neutrophils, which are essential for the early elimination of fungal conidia and hyphae, while Th1 cells become critical later for clearing muriform cells.
Evidence: The number of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).

These data suggest that Th17 cells might contribute to fungal elimination at the early stages of infection, whereas Th1 cells might be needed during the early and later stages of infection to eliminate the fungal pathogen.
B3Z / BWZ cells were stimulated in a 96-well plate with F. pedrosoi conidia, fragments of hyphae and muriform cells at multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 1.
233.Rationale: While the passage shows an early Th17 response that contributes to early fungal elimination and documents that different fungal morphotypes (conidia, hyphae, muriform cells) were used in assays, it does not state or provide experimental evidence that Th17 mediates neutrophil recruitment nor that neutrophils are 'essential' for early elimination. The text also does not link specific CD4 subsets explicitly to clearance of particular fungal morphotypes (for example, Th1 clearing muriform cells). Therefore the detailed mechanistic claims about neutrophil recruitment and morphotype-specific clearance cannot be derived from the presented data.

233.Option: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations, with initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement, is essential for effective fungal clearance and disease remission in experimental chromoblastomycosis.
Evidence: In this study, we show T lymphocytes with regulatory profile polarization in the popliteal lymph node of a self-healing animal model of CBM, while Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM.
The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

These results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection.
neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 ... allowing fungal persistence up to 28 days after infection.

The frequencies of Treg cells were significantly reduced in the course of infection, especially in dLN ... Footpad CFUs decreased significantly at day 14 post-infection. However, mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue.
233.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a temporal coordination: Th17 dominance early, followed by Th1 increase and concurrent Treg expansion, which the authors propose 'may represent the ideal balance' for remission. Functional perturbation experiments support the necessity of these subsets: IL-17 neutralization impairs early fungal control; IFN-γ neutralization or deficiency impairs clearance at early and later stages; and depletion of CD25+ (Treg) cells alters fungal dynamics and delays resolution. Together, these data justify the conclusion that coordinated CD4+ subset actions are essential for effective clearance and remission in this model.

233.Option: The chronic inflammatory process observed in human CBM patients may be due to an unbalanced immune response that fails to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance, resulting in persistent fungal burden and inadequate regulation by Treg cells.
Evidence: CBM is typically not lethal but often causes a chronic subcutaneous infection. Its complications can lead to the destruction of lymphatic organs, hyperplasia, and eventually, the amputation of affected limbs, increasing morbidity levels among patients [4].

In situ studies on CBM patients, as well as in experimental models, show a correlation between Th2 response and disease severity, together with a high fungal load. The milder form of the disease is related to a Th1 profile, with high IFN-γ production, lower levels of IL-10, and lower fungal burden.
An unbalanced response may favor the persistence of pathogens in infected tissues, worsening the patient’s clinical condition.
The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

These results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
However, mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue.
233.Rationale: The passage links chronic CBM in humans to high fungal load and unbalanced immune responses, and the experimental model demonstrates that timely transition from an early Th17 response to later Th1 dominance—along with appropriate Treg involvement—is associated with remission. Perturbations of these components (e.g., Treg depletion leading to delayed clearance) result in fungal persistence in the model. From these data, it is reasonable to infer that in human CBM chronic inflammation could reflect failure to make this transition and/or inadequate Treg regulation, leading to persistent fungal burden. The statement is an inference grounded in the presented human observations and experimental results.

234.Option: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
Evidence: Enterocytozoon bieneusi has a high genetic diversity and is capable of colonizing/infecting a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species.
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).

Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).
234.Rationale: The passage states that E. bieneusi infects a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species and that genotypes in Groups 1 and 2 are considered potentially zoonotic. The study identified genotypes (Type IV, S2, Peru11) previously reported in humans and a novel genotype that clusters in Group 1 alongside genotypes of human and animal origin. These facts support the epidemiological inference that cross-species (zoonotic) transmission is plausible in Mozambique; by implication, bidirectional transmission involving humans and animals (anthroponotic) is also possible given the shared genotypes across hosts.

234.Option: The detection of E. bieneusi genotypes in both rural and urban settings suggests that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact.
Evidence: Infections are acquired through ingestion of food and water contaminated with E. bieneusi spores, or through direct contact with faeces of infected persons and animals or with contaminated soils [2,12,13].
Three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas.
All these children lived in rural areas.
234.Rationale: While the passage describes environmental transmission routes (contaminated food, water, soil) and notes that most studied children lived in rural areas, it does not present data showing detection in urban settings nor evidence comparing the relative contribution of environmental contamination versus animal contact. The statement that transmission is 'primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact' is a causal claim not supported by the provided data. Therefore this specific inference cannot be derived solely from the passage.

234.Option: The high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in Mozambique, as evidenced by the identification of a novel genotype, suggests that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region.
Evidence: Enterocytozoon bieneusi has a high genetic diversity and is capable of colonizing/infecting a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species.
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
234.Rationale: The passage documents overall high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi and reports a newly identified genotype in this study. However, identifying a novel genotype in a region does not, by itself, demonstrate 'rapid evolution' there—alternative explanations include previously unsampled diversity or introduction from elsewhere. The passage provides no temporal genetic data or measures of evolutionary rate to support a claim of rapid local evolution, so this inference is not supported by the presented evidence.

234.Option: The presence of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children indicates that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes.
Evidence: Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
In the Zambézia province, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.

Among the 4 boys infected with the novel genotype, HhMzEb1, one was HIV positive, presented with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea, and was co-infected with Cryptosporidium meleagridis. Another boy with this genotype was co-infected with C. parvum, and a third one was co-infected with Rotavirus.
234.Rationale: The study identified multiple distinct genotypes and observed them in both asymptomatic and symptomatic children (e.g., genotypes present among asymptomatic schoolchildren and a mix of asymptomatic and one symptomatic case in Maputo). The occurrence of the same genotypes in children with and without symptoms (and frequent co-infections with other enteric pathogens) supports the epidemiological inference that clinical manifestations are not consistently linked to specific E. bieneusi genotypes in this dataset.

234.Option: The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 implies that E. bieneusi in Mozambique is primarily transmitted through direct human-to-human contact.
Evidence: Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
234.Rationale: The passage only explicitly states that the novel genotype clustered in Group 1; it does not assert that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1. Moreover, Group 1 genotypes are described as able to infect a broad range of mammalian species and are considered potentially zoonotic, which argues for cross-species transmission rather than implying primarily direct human-to-human spread. There is no evidence in the passage that would allow concluding that transmission is mainly via direct human-to-human contact, so the option is not supported.

235.Option: The successful completion of five rounds of MDA in Sri Lanka, combined with high MDA compliance rates, ensures that transmission assessment surveys are sufficient to confirm the interruption of lymphatic filariasis transmission.
Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.

The numbers of positive CFA tests were well below the TAS threshold level of 18 (critical cut-off value) in all EUs. Thus all EUs “passed” TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS cannot guarantee interruption of transmission. Although TAS 'passed' in all EUs after five rounds of MDA, the study found evidence of persistent infection (filarial DNA in mosquitoes) in numerous PHIs, including areas that passed TAS. Therefore the assertion that completion of MDA plus high compliance ensures TAS are sufficient to confirm interruption is not supported by the passage.

235.Option: The high rates of antifilarial antibodies in primary school children across most PHIs suggest that transmission assessment surveys are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing filariasis transmission in endemic areas.
Evidence: Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs. Antibody rates exceeded the target rate of 2% in 10 of 19 PHIs; five PHIs had borderline elevated antibody rates, and 5 others had higher rates with upper confidence limits >5%.

More than 17,000 primary grade school children were tested in TAS in 337 schools located in 11 EUs in 8 districts and in Colombo town (Table 5). The numbers of positive CFA tests were well below the TAS threshold level of 18 (critical cut-off value) in all EUs. Thus all EUs “passed” TAS
Only three of 137 children with positive antibody tests (out of 6198 children tested for antibody from all 19 PHI areas) had positive CFA tests, and all three of these children were Mf negative.
235.Rationale: The passage shows that antibody tests detected exposure in many PHIs while TAS (CFA testing) showed very few positives and all EUs passed. The low concordance between antibody positivity and CFA positivity in children (only 3 of 137 antibody-positive children had positive CFA) indicates that TAS (CFA in schoolchildren) did not detect the antibody signal. Thus the data do not support the claim that TAS are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing transmission; rather they suggest TAS may miss serological evidence of exposure.

235.Option: The fact that none of the children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia indicates that transmission assessment surveys are reliable indicators of the absence of active filarial transmission.
Evidence: None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia.

The numbers of positive CFA tests were well below the TAS threshold level of 18 (critical cut-off value) in all EUs. Thus all EUs “passed” TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
Sixteen of 65 CFA-positive subjects (age range 23–70 yr) were positive for Mf (mean count 14 per 60 µl range 1–51)
235.Rationale: Although none of the CFA-positive children had microfilaremia, the passage documents other evidence of ongoing transmission or residual infection: adult community CFA and some adult microfilaremia, and filarial DNA in mosquitoes in multiple PHIs. The presence of these findings despite TAS passage indicates that absence of microfilaremia in CFA-positive children in TAS does not reliably indicate absence of active transmission across the population. Therefore the inference that TAS reliably indicate absence of active transmission is not supported.

235.Option: The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.

Therefore we conducted more intensive surveillance activities in smaller areas (Public Health Inspector "PHI" areas) that were considered to be at high risk for persistent filariasis to complement the TAS program.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
235.Rationale: The passage reports that molecular xenomonitoring (MX) detected filarial DNA in mosquitoes and that the investigators used MX and intensive PHI surveys to complement TAS. However, the authors do not argue that TAS should be abandoned; rather they present complementary methods for detecting low-level persistence. Replacing TAS entirely with MX is not advocated or supported by the data presented. Thus the prescriptive claim that TAS should be abandoned in favor of MX for all post-MDA evaluations is not derivable from the passage.

235.Option: While transmission assessment surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping mass drug administration, they are not sufficient to show that lymphatic filariasis transmission has been interrupted, as evidenced by the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS.
