Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.

The numbers of positive CFA tests were well below the TAS threshold level of 18 (critical cut-off value) in all EUs. Thus all EUs “passed” TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.

Community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs. Microfilaremia rates were <1% in all PHI areas studied. Sixteen of 65 CFA-positive subjects (age range 23–70 yr) were positive for Mf (mean count 14 per 60 µl range 1–51), and 68% of Mf carriers were males.
Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs. Antibody rates exceeded the target rate of 2% in 10 of 19 PHIs;
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly cautions that TAS cannot guarantee interruption of transmission. Although all EUs passed TAS, the study found residual markers of infection: filarial DNA in mosquitoes in many PHIs, persistent antigenemia and some microfilaremia in adults, and elevated antibody rates in children in multiple PHIs. The investigators therefore performed more intensive, complementary surveillance in PHIs. Together, these findings support the inference that TAS are useful for program decisions (e.g., stopping MDA) but are not sufficient alone to demonstrate that transmission has been fully interrupted.

236.Option: The integration of mHealth tools with community health volunteers enables the collection of high-quality photographic data for therapeutic response assessment, which is as accurate as in-person evaluation and supports the scalability of digital health interventions in rural settings.
Evidence: The Guaral +ST app for Android is designed to monitor cutaneous leishmaniasis treatment and assess therapeutic response [11].

It has three main functions: i) promoting adherence to pharmacologic treatment, ii) measurement of adverse events associated with treatment, via periodic follow-up, and iii) assessment of therapeutic response via photographic evaluation.
This evaluation strategy has been found to be sensitive and specific, relative to in-person assessment [12].
The intended users are community health volunteers and other healthcare workers.
At each visit, i.e. prior to treatment, at the end of the treatment, and at weeks 7,13, and 26, a photograph of each lesion was taken with the mobile phone by CHVs.
236.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the app includes photographic assessment of therapeutic response and that this photographic evaluation "has been found to be sensitive and specific, relative to in-person assessment," supporting the claim that photographic data collected by CHV are high-quality and comparable to in-person evaluation. The text also identifies community health volunteers as the intended users who took lesion photographs, which supports the feasibility of integrating mHealth with CHV in rural settings. The passage's description of the app's design and functions supports an inference that this approach can be scaled to similar settings (i.e., supports scalability of digital health interventions).

236.Option: The use of a mobile health app by community health volunteers significantly improved the proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to standard institution-based follow-up, despite challenges related to connectivity and security.
Evidence: Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm who, according to the standard-of-care were instructed to return for evaluation at week 13 and 26 (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
The use of mHealth tools by health volunteers offers the opportunity to overcome these barriers.
travel to the town of Tumaco, where the health centers are located, may take up to 8 hours, which restricts access to health services.
Armed conflict has contributed to internal population displacement.
The app allowed CHV to monitor the clinical characteristics and evolution of the lesions, and to take and transmit photographs.
236.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that active follow-up using the app led to a markedly higher proportion of participants being evaluated at target week 26 (53.1% vs 0%), demonstrating a significant improvement in follow-up compared with standard institution-based care. The study setting is described as remote with long travel times (up to 8 hours) and mentions armed conflict contributing to population displacement, which constitute access and security challenges. The app's capability to enable CHV to take and transmit photographs and the authors' statement that mHealth tools "offer the opportunity to overcome these barriers" supports the inference that the improvement occurred despite such contextual challenges. While the passage does not use the word "connectivity" explicitly, it documents technical use of the app to transmit photos and mentions a technical error (see Evidence), which together with the described transportation and security barriers justifies the "despite challenges" clause as derived from the passage.

236.Option: The mHealth intervention significantly reduced the burden on health facilities by shifting the responsibility for treatment monitoring to community health volunteers, thereby improving access to care in remote areas.
Evidence: In the case of patients who received any part of their treatment at the CIDEIM health facility in the town of Tumaco, monitoring was carried out by CHV, liaising with the health facility.
The use of mHealth tools by health volunteers offers the opportunity to overcome these barriers.

The virtual absence of information on response to treatment for the two control groups after completion of treatment, precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment.
236.Rationale: While the passage documents that CHV performed monitoring (including for some patients who received treatment at the facility) and that mHealth tools can help overcome access barriers, it does not provide data demonstrating a reduction in burden on health facilities. The study measured follow-up completeness and photographic assessment, not health-facility workload or resource utilization. Moreover, the authors state that the lack of follow-up data in control groups "precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment," indicating that the trial did not assess or demonstrate effects on facility burden or on overall access to care beyond improved follow-up data capture. Therefore, the specific causal claim that the intervention "significantly reduced the burden on health facilities" and thereby improved access is not supported by the passage.

236.Option: Despite the challenges of poor connectivity and mobility, the mHealth intervention led to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm, enabling full therapeutic monitoring for all patients.
Evidence: There was no statistically significant difference in the compliance with the prescribed treatment between the randomized arms (Table 2).

The three study groups were similar in that the majority of patients were male and Afrocolombian, with median age between 20 and 30 years. More than 80% of each group were prescribed meglumine antimoniate, and the average number of ampoules administered, as a percentage of the number prescribed, was 88% or higher in each of the three groups.
Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm ...
Due to a technical error, the exact dates of visits were not retained in the app dataset, but rather only as the target study week.
travel to the town of Tumaco, where the health centers are located, may take up to 8 hours, which restricts access to health services.
236.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim of a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm or full therapeutic monitoring for all patients. It explicitly states there was no statistically significant difference in compliance with prescribed treatment between randomized arms and reports that the average proportion of doses received was high but not 100% (e.g., ~88% ampoules administered). Additionally, only approximately half (26/49) of intervention-arm participants were evaluated at week 26, so monitoring was not achieved for all patients. The passage also notes a technical error and substantial travel barriers, indicating ongoing operational challenges. Thus, the strong claim of complete resolution of non-adherence and full monitoring for all patients is not supported by the presented data.

237.Option: The spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES in coastal Kenya is primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
Evidence: The spatial patterning of prevalence of individual parasite infections and of poly-parasitism was quantified with the Getis' Gi*(d) local statistic ...
Results based on the Gi*(d) test showed that high density households and lower income were clustered in all villages (Figure 5).
Clusters of high household density were often located near the main road (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
An. funestus was significantly more abundant in Jego and Magodzoni, and An. gambiae was more abundant only in Jego (Table 2, Wilcoxon test, p<0.05; Fisher's LSD, p<0.05).
237.Rationale: The passage documents that high household density clusters were often near main roads and that low-income houses clustered closer to sites suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium, providing evidence of spatial associations. However, the option asserts a causal statement that these spatial clusterings are 'primarily driven by' proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles and snails. The passage does not claim primary causation nor link Anopheles aquatic habitats as a primary driver of household density or SES clustering. While associations are reported, causality and the designation of a single primary driver are not supported by the text. Therefore the stronger causal claim is not derivable from the passage.

237.Option: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections, ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought, and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.
Evidence: Because of their combined long-term effects, these infections appear to play a significant but, as yet, incompletely defined synergistic role in the causation of chronic clinical and sub-clinical human disease and poverty [6], [7], [8].

new advances in diagnostic technology have increased test sensitivity and specificity for these parasites, revealing that in endemic areas, chronic parasitic infection with Schistosoma spp. [24], Plasmodia spp. [25], and/or filaria [26] are much more common than previously thought.

In holoendemic areas such as coastal Kenya or Papua New Guinea, malaria prevalence, as detected by PCR is 60–75%, more than double the previous estimates of 20–33% by blood smear microscopy, with ≥10% carrying two or more malaria species [25], [27].
These findings indicate the need to carefully re-evaluate the risk of infection and parasite-related morbidity in exposed populations.
now in the era of integrated parasite control programs, it is important to define those factors that enhance risk of co-infection.
237.Rationale: Parts (i) and (ii) of the option are supported by the passage: the text explicitly states combined long-term effects contribute to chronic disease and poverty, and documents that more sensitive diagnostics reveal much higher prevalence of infections and concurrent infections than previously recognized. However, part (iii) asserts the existence of 'new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.' The passage mentions 'integrated parasite control programs' and that findings can 'inform the design and application of more effective, population-based control strategies,' but it does not provide evidence that new inexpensive treatment or transmission-control approaches are now available or increasingly accessible. Because the option asserts a specific claim (iii) not supported by the passage, the overall statement cannot be fully derived from the passage.

237.Option: The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults is primarily due to the prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003, which reduced hookworm and Trichuris infections in the 10–19 age group.
Evidence: Overall, children and young adults were more likely to be infected with malaria parasites, S. haematobium, and Trichuris, while adults were more likely to be infected with filariasis and hookworms.
Polyparasitism (two or more infections) was highest in the 10–19 age group.
237.Rationale: The passage documents that children and young adults have higher prevalence of certain infections and that polyparasitism peaks in the 10–19 age group. However, it contains no information about any prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003 or any causal attribution of the observed age-patterns to an MDA. Therefore the causal claim that the high co-infection prevalence in youth is 'primarily due to' a 2003 MDA campaign is not supported by the passage and cannot be derived from the provided evidence.

237.Option: The integration of socio-ecological data and spatial analysis techniques in this study demonstrates that environmental factors are the dominant drivers of polyparasitism in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Our study's aim was to detect spatial and demographic patterns of transmission and infection for schistosomiasis, malaria, filariasis, and soil-transmitted helminths (STH) in coastal Kenya through integration of parasitological data with landscape, land use, and socioeconomic risk factors.
Village and environmental factors were major independent correlates of infection.

For the aggregated data from all six villages, results from the GAMs and GAMMs (Table 3), showed that demographic factors (age, gender, and education), SES, and household characteristics (construction, use of bednets, water source, sanitation, and number of inhabitants) were significantly associated with parasite infections and co-infections.
237.Rationale: The passage shows that integrating socio-ecological data and spatial analyses revealed that village and environmental factors are important independent correlates of infection. However, it also reports that demographic factors, SES, and household characteristics were significantly associated with infections and co-infections. The option's assertion that environmental factors are the 'dominant drivers' is stronger than the evidence: the study demonstrates environmental factors are major contributors but does not establish they are the dominant or sole drivers over social and demographic determinants. Thus the stronger claim of dominance is not fully supported by the passage.

237.Option: The lack of significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases suggests that vector control measures are ineffective in reducing transmission in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Results from the GAM applied to the entomological data (Table 4) showed no significant correlation between presence or number of collected mosquitoes and malaria or filariasis cases.
However, not surprisingly, bednet use was protective against malaria.
237.Rationale: The passage reports a lack of significant correlation between mosquito counts from collections and the measured malaria or filariasis cases, but it also reports that bednet use was protective against malaria. A simple absence of correlation in these entomological sampling data does not logically imply that vector control measures are ineffective. Many alternative explanations (measurement limitations, sampling frequency, mosquito collection methods, temporal mismatches, or effectiveness of specific interventions like bednets) could explain the observed lack of correlation. The passage does not conclude that vector control is ineffective, and such a causal inference is not supported solely by the reported lack of correlation.

238.Option: Despite significant creatinine elevation in one patient, significant differences were not recorded in any of mean laboratory values at the end of the therapy, suggesting that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory during oral miltefosine treatment.
Evidence: Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
There were two dropouts (7.40%) due to creatinine level elevation, suspicious to drug induced nephrotoxicity in one patient and dissatisfaction to continue in one patient.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage states both that mean laboratory values showed no significant differences and that one patient experienced a substantial creatinine rise requiring exclusion and referral. From these data one can conclude that mean values did not change significantly overall but a clinically important adverse laboratory event occurred in an individual. The statement that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory is a management recommendation that is not supported by the passage data: the occurrence of a serious creatinine elevation actually argues for the need for monitoring in at least some patients. Therefore the inference that routine laboratory testing is not required cannot be derived from the passage.

238.Option: The high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up in this cohort suggests that oral miltefosine treatment leads to permanent sterilization of L. tropica infection in most patients.
Evidence: In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
Complete response (CR) for each lesion was defined as complete resolve of lesion induration and inflammation. All CR lesions were confirmed according to negative skin smear test.
238.Rationale: The passage documents substantial clinical improvement, defined complete responses with negative skin smears, and low observed recurrence over 12 months. However, claiming 'permanent sterilization' of L. tropica in most patients goes beyond the presented evidence. The study reports clinical cure and negative smear at follow-up but does not provide definitive parasitological eradication data over the long term or methods sensitive enough to prove permanent sterilization. Thus the stronger causal claim of permanent sterilization is not supported by the passage.

238.Option: The program to investigate miltefosine for new world cutaneous leishmaniasis (NWCL) was initiated in 2001 with an uncontrolled, open-label, dose-ranging study in Colombia, in which the per-protocol cure rate of 94% was reported by 2.5 mg/kg oral miltefosine per day after four weeks.
Evidence: Miltefosine—an alkylated phosphocholine- exerts its antimicrobial effects through affecting phospholipid membrane integrity and mitochondrial function of the microorganism [5]. Oral miltefosine has previously demonstrated a 95% cure rate in Indian visceral leishmaniasis [6–7].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
238.Rationale: The passage mentions prior miltefosine results for Indian visceral leishmaniasis and a study on zoonotic CL (ZCL) in Iran, but it does not mention a 2001 program, an uncontrolled dose-ranging study in Colombia, nor any 94% per-protocol cure for NWCL. The specific historical and numerical claims about Colombia/NWCL are not present in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

238.Option: The therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine on MA-resistant anthropontic cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica in Iran is effective and nearly safe, as evidenced by a high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up.
Evidence: It was an uncontrolled before-after study, conducted during 2018–2019 ... on 27 patients with MA resistant ACL.
Moreover, L.tropica species was confirmed by Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) method in all cases.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
Nausea, but not vomiting, was the most subjective complication during the study which detected in 9 (33.33%) patients.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage describes treatment of MA-resistant ACL due to L. tropica and documents substantial clinical response (81.48% complete or partial cure at 6 and 12 months; 68% per-protocol definite cure) with only one recurrence (3.7%) reported. Adverse events were mostly nausea and one notable creatinine elevation that led to dropout; mean laboratory parameters did not significantly change. These data support an epidemiological inference that oral miltefosine showed effectiveness and an acceptable ("nearly safe") safety profile in this cohort, as evidenced by high clinical improvement rates and low recurrence over 12 months.

238.Option: The higher cure rates observed in previous studies of miltefosine for zoonotic CL in Iran may be attributed to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections, which could explain the relatively lower cure rate in this cohort of patients with chronic L. tropica infections.
Evidence: ACL and ZCL caused by L. tropica and L. major respectively, are the most common types of CL in Iran [3].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
Mean duration of lesions was 3.45±0.76 years (ranges between 8 months and 20 years).
238.Rationale: The passage notes that different Leishmania species cause ACL (L. tropica) and ZCL (L. major) and cites prior studies of miltefosine in ZCL. It also documents chronic, long-standing lesions in the present L. tropica cohort. However, the specific causal claim that higher cure rates in prior ZCL studies are attributable to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections is not stated or demonstrated in the passage. That attribution is speculative and requires external biological or comparative data not provided here. Therefore this explanatory inference cannot be derived solely from the passage.

239.Option: The majority of V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA, indicating that transmission occurred either through a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact.
Evidence: When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
239.Rationale: The passage provides direct genetic evidence that isolates from the same household are more closely related than isolates from different households, and that in most households at least one water isolate matched clinical isolates. These findings support the inference that transmission within households occurred via either a shared contaminated environmental source (water) or person-to-person spread (or both). The statement does not overreach beyond the demonstrated relatedness and plausible transmission routes described in the study.

239.Option: The presence of V. cholerae in both source and stored water within households indicates that household water contamination is primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source had unimproved latrines.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
The findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks [17].
239.Rationale: While the passage links detectable V. cholerae in source water to unimproved latrines (supporting a sanitation-related pathway), it also presents evidence for both water-to-person and person-to-person transmission. The data do not establish that poor sanitation infrastructure is the primary cause over person-to-person transmission; MLVA results and the authors' statements indicate a combination of transmission modes. Therefore the claim of primary causation by poor sanitation infrastructure is not supported solely by the presented data.

239.Option: The fact that stored water samples had a higher proportion of V. cholerae than source water samples indicates that household-level contamination is the primary driver of V. cholerae transmission, independent of the index case.
Evidence: Thirteen percent of households (4) had V. cholerae in their source water during the surveillance period (all groundwater sources), and 27% (8) had V. cholerae in stored household drinking water.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.
239.Rationale: Although stored water was positive in a larger percentage of households than source water, the data do not support the strong inference that household-level contamination is the primary driver independent of the index case. Stored water contamination could derive from contaminated source water, handling by infected individuals, or both. The MLVA relatedness between water and clinical isolates indicates linkage between clinical cases and water isolates, so one cannot conclude transmission is independent of the index case from the presented evidence.

239.Option: The high diversity of MLVA genotypes observed in clinical and water samples suggests that V. cholerae infections in household contacts are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.

There were multiple MLVA genotypes among isolates collected from a single sample. This was similar for clinical samples (mean: 1.9 MLVA genotypes, range: 1–3) and water samples (mean: 2.1 genotypes, range: 1–4), p = 0.53.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: Although there is notable MLVA diversity overall, the within-household genetic data show close relatedness among isolates from the same household and identical genotypes among household members in most multi-case households. These findings are consistent with transmission within households (either via a shared source or person-to-person) rather than primarily multiple independent environmental exposures. Thus the claim that infections are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures is not supported by the combined genetic and household-level evidence.

239.Option: The significantly higher odds of symptomatic V. cholerae infection among household contacts with V. cholerae in their water source suggests that waterborne transmission is the dominant mode of infection, with person-to-person transmission playing a minor role.
Evidence: Contacts with V. cholerae in their source water had a significantly higher odds of a symptomatic V. cholerae infection (OR: 5.49, 95% CI: 1.07, 28.08) (25% (V. cholerae in water source) vs. 6% (no V. cholerae in water source)).

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
The findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks [17].
239.Rationale: The increased odds of symptomatic infection when the source water tested positive support an important role for waterborne transmission. However, the genetic data and the authors' statements indicate both waterborne and person-to-person transmission occur in households. The evidence does not justify the strong claim that waterborne transmission is dominant and person-to-person transmission is minor for these household outbreaks; the study demonstrates a combination of modes with varying contributions.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is due to the presence of multiple low-molecular-weight proteins that are selectively secreted during active infection.
Evidence: These results indicated that one or several intact parasite glycoproteins, but not separate protein and carbohydrate entities, mediate(s) DC suppression by McES.

Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D).
Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.
McES is markedly less complex than McH (in excess of 60 proteins) consistent with the observation that M. corti tetrathyridia differentially secrete a defined set of proteins (Fig 6B).
240.Rationale: The passage explicitly indicates the active immunomodulatory entities are intact glycoproteins and that active gel-filtration fractions clustered in the higher molecular-weight group, suggesting the active principle(s) are >20 kDa. Therefore the claim that activity is due to multiple low-molecular-weight proteins is contrary to the experimental fractionation data and cannot be derived from the passage.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa.
Evidence: Glycoproteins are the mediators of the immunosuppressive effects by McES
These results indicated that one or several intact parasite glycoproteins, but not separate protein and carbohydrate entities, mediate(s) DC suppression by McES.
Taken together, these findings demonstrated that both parasite proteins and carbohydrates are necessary for the immunosuppressive effect of McES.

Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D).
Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.
240.Rationale: Multiple lines of experimental evidence in the passage support this inference: biochemical depletion experiments show that both protein and carbohydrate moieties are required (implying glycoproteins), and chromatographic fractionation plus activity assays localize inhibitory activity to fractions with molecular weight >20 kDa. Thus it is directly derivable from the presented data.

240.Option: The ability of M. corti tetrathyridia to suppress dendritic cell activation is dependent on the metabolic activity of the parasite, as heat-killed larvae failed to recruit CD11c+ cells in vivo.
Evidence: We found that viable tetrathyridia significantly recruited cells within the peritoneum at day 7 p.i. when compared to dead tetrathyridia or to the mock control (3-fold more than the dead larvae and 15-fold more than the mock injections) (Fig 1C).

These results indicate that only live M. corti tetrathyridia massively attracted host CD11c+ cells up to day 7 post-infection whereas dead (ametabolic) larvae recruited CD11c+ cells just for the 3 days that followed injection.

Given the ability of live helminths to excrete-secrete molecules which directly interact with host immune cells [11,23] and considering the reported predominance of dendritic cells within murine peritoneal CD11c+ cells, our data suggest a central role for ES products of M. corti (McES) in the modulation of host DC responses in vivo.
As shown in Fig 2A, we found that all tested McES concentrations significantly inhibited LPS-driven release of IL-12p70 by BMDCs in a dose-dependent manner.

As expected, as little as 5 μg/ml of McES reduced LPS-driven IL-12p70 production by 50% in BMDC cultures whereas a similar amount of McH failed to show any suppressive effect on LPS-driven IL-12p70 release by BMDCs (Fig 5).
240.Rationale: The passage shows that only live (metabolically active) larvae strongly recruit CD11c+ cells and states that ES products (which require live secretion) play a central role in modulating DC responses. In vitro, McES (collected from live cultures) suppress DC cytokine production whereas larval homogenates do not. Together these findings support the inference that the suppressive ability depends on parasite metabolic activity (i.e., active secretion of ES products), and that heat-killed larvae—which cannot secrete ES—fail to recruit CD11c+ cells and thus would not mediate suppression in vivo.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is primarily due to the presence of cysteine-rich secretory proteins and annexins, which are known to inhibit proteases and modulate membrane dynamics.
Evidence: Among the 55 McES proteins identified were a selection of enzymes ... Protease inhibitors (cystatins, serpins) and annexins were also detected as were a high number of proteins with unknown functions with homologues in other cestodes (conserved cestode proteins) and novel proteins as yet unidentified in other helminths (S1 and S3 Tables).

Specifically and by descending order of magnitude, the 5 most represented McES protein entities identified were: a novel hypothetical protein (MCOS_959401, exclusively detected in McES), an annexin homologue (Number 1, MCOS_561301, exclusively detected in McES), a hypothetical protein conserved among parasitic cestodes (Number 2, MCOS_155201, ~3-fold enrichment in McES), a cystatin (MCOS_775601, exclusively detected in McES) and an endophilin (MCOS_968201, 2.4-fold enrichment in McES) (S4 Table).

Once functionally tested, this comprehensive library of metacestode-derived candidate immunomodulatory proteins should improve our understanding of how tissue-dwelling metacestodes subvert the host DC response.
240.Rationale: While cystatins (protease inhibitors) and annexins are present among McES proteins and are among represented candidates, the study does not demonstrate that these specific proteins are the primary mediators of DC suppression. The authors present a list of candidate proteins and state that functional testing is still needed. The option asserts a primary causative role and specific mechanisms ('inhibit proteases and modulate membrane dynamics') that are not experimentally established in the passage. Therefore this stronger causal claim is not supported by the presented data.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive activity of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is mediated by a combination of proteins and lipids that act synergistically to inhibit IL-12p70 production.
Evidence: As shown in Fig 4A, similarly to McES, lipid-free ES significantly diminished the production of LPS-driven IL-12p70 by BMDCs indicating that parasite released lipids are not required for McES-mediated BMDC suppression.
These results indicated that both parasite proteins and carbohydrates are necessary for the immunosuppressive effect of McES.

Next, we appraised the role of parasite proteins contained in McES by comparative testing of heat-inactivated McES (hiMcES) and mock-treated McES (Mock hiMcES). As expected, pre-exposure to Mock hiMcES significantly impaired BMDC ability to release IL-12p70 in response to LPS (Fig 4B). In contrast, hiMcES completely failed to show such an ability (Fig 4B) indicating that parasite proteins are important for McES-driven BMDC suppression.
240.Rationale: The passage directly tests and rejects a required role for lipids: lipid-depleted (ammonium sulfate precipitated) McES retained suppressive activity, indicating lipids are not required. Instead, the data show that intact proteins and carbohydrates (glycoproteins) are necessary. Thus the claim that proteins and lipids act synergistically is not supported by the presented experiments.

241.Option: Because the PLC primer set is more sensitive than TgsGP and SRA primers, it can detect infections even when the target subspecies DNA is present at extremely low levels.
Evidence: Identification of T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense is reliant on the detection of specific single-copy genes (TgsGP [18] and SRA [19,20] respectively) for each subspecies.

For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.

To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].

When the performance of the HRM was compared to TgsGP and SRA PCR on the same DNA dilution series, the HRM was as sensitive at detecting T. b. gambiense DNA and tenfold more sensitive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA (S2 and S3 Figs).
241.Rationale: The passage describes the PLC primer as a single-copy gene target intended to assess whether sufficient DNA is present for subspecies-specific single-copy assays; it does not state that the PLC primers are intrinsically more sensitive than the TgsGP or SRA primers. The sensitivity comparison given in the passage refers to the HRM assay overall versus TgsGP/SRA PCR, not to the PLC primer set being more sensitive than subspecies primers. Therefore the claim that PLC is more sensitive and can detect extremely low-level infections is not supported by the passage.

241.Option: The PLC primer set improves the overall sensitivity of the assay by amplifying a highly conserved region of the trypanosome genome, enabling detection of all T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
Evidence: The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].

For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.
241.Rationale: The passage does support that the PLC primers target a gene present in all members of Trypanozoon (i.e. they can indicate presence of T. brucei s. l.). However, it does not claim that PLC "improves overall sensitivity"; PLC is a single-copy target used to assess DNA quantity/quality rather than to increase assay sensitivity. Single-copy targets are not described as enhancing sensitivity in the text. Thus the statement's claim about improving sensitivity is not supported by the passage, so the option is incorrect.

241.Option: The inclusion of the PLC primer set enables the assay to detect both T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense in addition to confirming the presence of sufficient DNA, making it a comprehensive diagnostic tool.
Evidence: We aim to develop a qPCR HRM assay designed to distinguish between the two human infective sub-species of T. brucei s. l. To improve the reliability of the assay primers were included to determine if sufficient DNA is present for the sub-species primers to work.

The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].
HRM reactions were run in a total volume of 12.5μl ... 400nM of all forward and reverse primers were included in a multiplex.
Using the multiplexed HRM, PLC positive control peaks were produced by 43 samples (44.8%), indicating sufficient target DNA for identification of either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
Three flies (3.1%) were identified as positive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA both by HRM and PCR ... No flies were found to be positive for T. b. gambiense through either PCR or HRM.
241.Rationale: The passage describes a multiplex HRM assay that includes subspecies-specific primers for T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense together with PLC primers whose explicit purpose is to confirm sufficient DNA for subspecies detection. The field results demonstrate the multiplex detects subspecies and PLC signals together. Therefore the inclusion of PLC does enable confirmation of DNA adequacy alongside detection of the two subspecies, supporting the characterization of the assay as a comprehensive diagnostic tool for those purposes.

241.Option: The HRM assay's ability to detect sufficient DNA through the PLC primer set allows for more reliable identification of samples that are truly negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense, reducing the risk of false-negative results due to low DNA quantity.

247.Rationale: The passage does report prior associations (including TNFSF8) but explicitly states that few genes have been replicated or functionally characterized. While this study confirmed IL6 and NOD2 associations in Brazilians, the passage does not support the strong claim that a universal test could be developed with minimal cross-population validation. In fact, the text highlights the need for replication and functional characterization, contradicting the assertion that minimal validation across populations would suffice.

247.Option: The failure to detect associations with TLR1 SNPs in this study, despite prior reports, indicates that genetic markers for leprosy reactions are unreliable and should not be pursued further in future research.

From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians...
247.Rationale: The passage indicates that TLR1 SNPs were tested but only two SNPs (IL6 and NOD2) showed significant differences in this sample. However, the study also confirmed other genetic associations, demonstrating that genetic markers can be informative. The failure to replicate TLR1 in this study does not justify the broad conclusion that genetic markers are unreliable overall or that research should cease; rather, the passage implies variability and the need for further replication and functional work.

247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.

It does not analyze or report on additive, synergistic, or combined effects of multiple risk alleles. Therefore, the absence of evidence about interactions cannot be taken as evidence of absence of additive or synergistic effects. The conclusion that combining markers may not be feasible is not derivable from the presented data.

247.Option: The lack of association between IL6 rs2069845 and serum IL-6 levels in this study suggests that genetic markers are not useful for predicting leprosy reactions, as they do not correlate with measurable biological outcomes.
Evidence: AG genotype and AG/GG carriers of IL6 rs2069845 were associated with having an increased risk of developing a reaction (aHR = 1.88, p = 0.0008 and aHR = 1.73, p = 0.002, respectively).

individuals carrying NOD2 rs751271-GT showed a borderline difference (p = 0.05), suggesting lower IL-6 production (GT median: 0.008) as compared to GG individuals (GG median: 0.53) (Fig 3B).
247.Rationale: Although IL6 rs2069845 genotype did not correlate with measured serum IL-6 in this sample, the same SNP was nevertheless associated with increased reaction risk, and the NOD2 genotype showed a relationship with lower IL-6 levels. Thus, the lack of correlation for one SNP in this functional assay does not support the sweeping conclusion that genetic markers are not useful for prediction. The passage provides evidence both for genetic associations with outcome and for genotype–cytokine correlations for another locus, indicating that genetic markers can be informative even when genotype–biomarker correlations are not uniformly observed.

Evidence: As observed, the length of time before a leprosy reaction, as described by survival curves, shows a significant difference when considering the variables of gender (p = 0.002) and clinical form (p < 0.001).
men developed reactions earlier than women (MST = 86 weeks and 182 weeks, respectively).

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
247.Rationale: The passage documents non-genetic risk factors (gender and clinical form) and also demonstrates reproducible genetic associations (IL6 and NOD2) with reaction risk, with biological plausibility provided by an association between NOD2 genotype and IL-6 levels. From an epidemiological perspective, these findings support the inference that a predictive approach combining genetic markers with established non-genetic risk factors could improve risk stratification. While the passage does not provide a ready-to-deploy test, it provides direct evidence that developing a genetic test to complement existing clinical predictors is a reasonable and derivable implication.

248.Option: The high antibody titers observed in geese following DENV2 vaccination, reaching up to 1:6,400,000, suggest that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans due to its strong immunogenicity and long-lasting response.

248.Rationale: The passage documents very high anti-DENV2 titers in vaccinated geese (including the 1:6,400,000 value) and describes collection of IgY from egg yolks. However, the authors propose IgY as an alternative therapeutic antibody, not as a prophylactic vaccine for humans. There are no data presented on prophylactic efficacy in humans, on duration of immunity in humans, or on safety and immunogenicity of goose IgY in humans. Therefore the specific inference that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans (and that it provides long-lasting human immunity) is not supported by the passage.

neither the anti-DENV IgY nor control IgY sera were enhancing at any dilution tested (Fig 3).
100% therapeutic efficacy was also observed with 1 mg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 4) administered 24 hours post-infection (p = 0.0019)
248.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that IgY does not bind mammalian Fcγ receptors and links that property to the potential to avoid ADE. These statements and results directly support the inference that IgY's lack of FcγR binding is associated with absence of ADE and that IgY is a promising therapeutic candidate that does not increase ADE-mediated severe disease risk (at least in the presented in vitro and mouse model data).

There is no data on cross-reactivity or neutralization across other DENV serotypes (DENV1, 3, 4) or on conservation of those epitopes across serotypes. Therefore one cannot infer from the passage that IgY therapies will be broadly effective across all dengue serotypes due to conserved motifs; that conclusion would require cross-serotype binding/neutralization data not provided here.

IgY does not interact with other Fc-binding receptors, which limits its ability to elicit an inflammatory reaction in humans [19–24].
248.Rationale: While the passage reports that control (naïve) IgY provided 25% protection at 1 mg in the mouse model, the authors do not provide mechanistic data explaining this partial protection. The passage also emphasizes that IgY does not interact with mammalian Fc-binding receptors and does not activate human complement, which would argue against a generalized Fc-mediated pro-inflammatory mechanism in humans. The suggestion that the control IgY protection reflects non-specific innate immune activation in mice is speculative and not demonstrated by the presented data; therefore this mechanistic inference cannot be derived solely from the passage.

248.Option: Because IgY does not bind human complement, it is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement during secondary dengue infections, thereby reducing the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease.
Evidence: Although IgY is able to activate complement in the avian system, its unique structure prevents it from being able to activate human complement [23].
it has also been proposed that complement activation during secondary heterotypic DENV infection by non-neutralizing antibodies contributes to immune enhancement.
Therefore, the inability of anti-DENV IgY to induce complement activation could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state.
248.Rationale: The passage notes that IgY cannot activate human complement and that complement activation has been implicated in immune enhancement during secondary heterotypic DENV infection. However, the authors explicitly state that the inability of IgY to activate complement "could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state," indicating uncertainty about net effect. Thus one cannot conclude from the passage that lack of complement activation will necessarily reduce the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease; the passage itself presents this as potentially beneficial or detrimental and provides no direct data linking IgY's complement inability to reduced vascular leakage in secondary dengue.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).
A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
249.Rationale: The passage documents high reported coverage of school-based albendazole treatment targeting children since 2012, yet observed hookworm prevalence and a higher burden of moderate-to-high-intensity (MHI) infections are concentrated in adults, particularly males. This pattern—lower prevalence in treated age groups but persistent and relatively higher prevalence/intensity in adults—supports the epidemiological inference that school-based deworming alone does not eliminate community reservoirs of hookworm and is therefore insufficient to control community-wide transmission.

249.Option: The observed age-related increase in hookworm infection intensity among adults suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective because it does not account for the biological differences in susceptibility between age groups.
Evidence: Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).

Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

To understand how best to reduce STH transmission, it is essential to have age-stratified epidemiological data, as these can highlight age groups among whom infection remains common, most intense and therefore serve as a reservoir to re-infect the treated age groups.
249.Rationale: While the passage shows age-related increases in hookworm intensity among adults, it does not present any data or discussion about inherent biological differences in susceptibility by age, nor does it state that the deworming strategy was designed to account for such biological differences. Instead, the text emphasizes identifying age groups that serve as reservoirs to re-infect treated groups. Therefore, attributing the strategy’s ineffectiveness to failure to account for biological susceptibility is not supported by the passage and is speculative.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, particularly men, suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective in preventing reinfection due to poor compliance among adult populations.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

LF MDA rounds were implemented in Kwale County in 2003, 2005, 2008 and 2011. For the two most recent MDA rounds prior to this survey, 2008 and 2011, programmatic coverage levels of 62.7% and 58.3% were reported [11, 20, 21].
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).

To understand how best to reduce STH transmission, it is essential to have age-stratified epidemiological data, as these can highlight age groups among whom infection remains common, most intense and therefore serve as a reservoir to re-infect the treated age groups.
249.Rationale: The passage documents that deworming programs have primarily targeted children (school-based) and that community-wide MDAs occurred in earlier years with variable coverage, but it does not provide evidence on adult compliance with deworming. The observed high prevalence in adults could reflect that adults have not been recently targeted or reached by programs (or that they act as a reservoir), not necessarily poor compliance among adults. The specific claim that reinfection is due to poor compliance among adult populations is not supported by the data presented.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, despite school-based deworming, indicates that the treatment is ineffective against adult worms, and therefore, alternative drugs should be used in future interventions.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults; with over 90% (3,394/3,750) of infected individuals exhibiting light infections (< = 1,999 epg).
249.Rationale: The passage does not provide any data indicating that albendazole or mebendazole are biologically ineffective against adult hookworms. The observed persistence of hookworm in adults could result from adults not being targeted by school-based programs, reinfection from untreated reservoirs, or programmatic coverage gaps, rather than drug inefficacy. Additionally, the fact that over 90% of infections were light suggests the drugs (where given) may reduce intensity. Claiming intrinsic drug ineffectiveness against adult worms and recommending alternative drugs is not supported by the presented evidence.

249.Option: The persistence of moderate-to-heavy hookworm infections in adults indicates that the current deworming strategy has failed to reduce transmission, and therefore, the treatment regimen should be changed to target only the most vulnerable age groups.
Evidence: A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
In 23 clusters, prevalence of MHI infection exceeded the 1% threshold, despite the overall infection prevalence in these clusters lying below 20%.

To understand how best to reduce STH transmission, it is essential to have age-stratified epidemiological data, as these can highlight age groups among whom infection remains common, most intense and therefore serve as a reservoir to re-infect the treated age groups.

Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
249.Rationale: Although the passage shows persistence of MHI infections in adults, it also emphasizes that age groups with high infection intensity may serve as reservoirs to re-infect treated groups. Changing the regimen to target only the most vulnerable age groups (i.e., focusing more narrowly on children) would be inconsistent with the finding that adults carry a disproportionate share of MHI infections and likely act as a reservoir. Thus the recommendation to restrict targeting to only the most vulnerable is not supported; if anything, the evidence supports expanding treatment beyond school-based programmes. Therefore this option is not derivable from the passage.

250.Option: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
Evidence: the mean hepatic and splenic parasite burden (LDU) were lowered by ∼93% and ∼98% respectively, in animals immunized with cocktail antigens as compared to the adjuvant controls at 3 months post infection.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.
250.Rationale: The passage does state that the cocktail reduced hepatic and splenic parasite burden by ~93% and ~98% and that the highest protection in this study was achieved with the triple-antigen cocktail. However, the latter half of the option (“which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far”) is a sweeping comparison to all other CP-based vaccines in the literature. The Passage Body does not provide data or statements comparing this cocktail's protection to all other CP-based vaccines tested elsewhere, so that general claim cannot be derived from the provided text.

250.Option: Although direct comparison of our work with previous reports is difficult, it is noteworthy that rCPC individually induced better protection than promising vaccine candidates like gp63, ORFF, LelF-2 tested in murine VL against L. donovani.

Compared to an array of antigens that have been tested, very few are sufficiently promising to be carried out to Phase I clinical trials or advanced preclinical work against VL [12].

It also notes that relatively few antigens have advanced in VL vaccine development. However, the Passage Body does not present any data or specific comparisons between rCPC and the named vaccine candidates (gp63, ORFF, LelF-2) tested in murine models. Therefore the specific comparative claim to those other candidates is not derivable from the passage.

250.Option: The cocktail vaccine induced a synergistic enhancement of protection due to the cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner.
Evidence: Taken together, these finding suggest that all three CPs present in the cocktail vaccine act synergistically to counteract the impaired T cell response after challenge for improved protection.

Our aim in this study was therefore towards a comparative evaluation of prophylactic potential of CPA, CPB and CPC, individually, as well as a multi-antigen cocktail vaccine, maximizing the exposed antigenic epitopes to protect against VL.
250.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that the three CPs in the cocktail 'act synergistically' to improve protection and that the cocktail approach was intended to 'maximize the exposed antigenic epitopes.' However, the option asserts a specific mechanistic explanation — that the synergy is 'due to the cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner.' The passage does not present direct evidence mapping epitopes or otherwise demonstrating that the observed synergy is specifically caused by a cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes (nor does it demonstrate the absence of antagonism at the epitope level). Thus the general claim of synergy is supported, but the detailed mechanistic attribution is not directly derivable from the passage.

250.Option: The use of TLR agonists with cationic liposomes allows concurrent antigen presentation along with targeting pattern recognition receptor (PRR) pathways, for effective expansion of effector T cells.
Evidence: Monophosphoryl lipid A (MPLA) is a Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) agonist with more than 100,000 human doses safely administered as a part of licensed hepatitis B and Human papillomavirus vaccines [6].

Mycobacterial glycolipid trehalose-6,6′-dimycolate (TDM; cord factor) is a potent immunostimulant known for its macrophage activation properties and induction of proinflammatory cytokines... Recently, TDM has been shown to act via macrophage receptor with collagenous structure (MARCO), TLR2, CD14 and also macrophage-inducible C-type lectin (Mincle) receptors to exert its immunomodifying effects [8], [9].

Cationic liposomes protect the labile antigens from lysosomal degradation and take the advantage of electrostatic interactions with the cells' negative charge which makes them a natural target for antigen presenting cells (APCs), crucial for immune stimulation [4], [5].

CLSM of MΦ cells incubated for 2 hrs with both Rh123-labeled liposomes (green), LysoTracker Red (red) and DAPI(blue) simultaneously showed successful co-localization (yellow) of labeled liposomes in acidic compartments such as lysosomes (Fig. S3, B) which confirms antigen presentation by MΦ.

MPL-TDM alone activates MΦs from naïve hamsters resulting in increased NO and ROS production... These data indicated that combining MPL-TDM with cationic liposomes result in increased the adjuvant activity of the formulation on the APCs in vitro

All immunized groups receiving either of liposomal rCPA, rCPB and rCPC singly or as cocktail produced significant upregulation of IL-2 and IFN-γ mRNAs in comparison to controls, which progressively increased at 2 and 3 months post infection.
250.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that MPL-TDM contains TLR agonists (MPLA, TDM acting via TLR2 and others) and that cationic liposomes deliver antigen into lysosomal compartments of macrophages (facilitating antigen presentation). It further shows that combining liposomes with MPL-TDM enhances macrophage activation (NO/ROS) and that vaccinated animals develop strong T-cell associated responses (IL-2, IFN-γ, DTH, lymphoproliferation). These statements together support the epidemiological/ immunological inference that using TLR agonists with cationic liposomes can concurrently target PRR pathways and antigen presentation to expand effector T-cell responses.

250.Option: The liposomal formulation of rCPC alone provided the highest level of protection among all tested antigen formulations, surpassing even the cocktail vaccine.
Evidence: rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.
250.Rationale: The Passage Body indicates that while rCPC alone induced better protection than rCPA or rCPB individually, the triple-antigen cocktail produced the highest level of protection overall. Thus the statement that rCPC alone provided the highest protection and surpassed the cocktail contradicts the reported results and cannot be derived from the passage.

251.Option: The absence of differences in life cycle duration between infected and non-infected ticks indicates that R. conorii conorii infection does not affect the overall fitness of Rh. sanguineus in laboratory conditions.
Evidence: No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g (range, 0.2518–0.5389 g) compared to 0.4749 g (range, 0.2333–0.6203 g) for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g (range, 0.0998–0.2976 g, 31 samples) compared to 0.2919 g (range, 0.1321–0.4271 g, 6 samples) for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).

In the 3 experiments with engorged nymphs, 88–100% of the R. conorii conorii-infected nymphs that had been previously maintained at 4°C died after being transferred to 25°C ... compared to 41% (62/150) of non-infected nymphs ... (p = 0.0001).

In the first batch of adults, which was maintained at 4°C for one month and then transferred at 25°C for rabbit attachment, 10–40% infected ticks died compared to a lack of death for non-infected ticks (30/120 vs. 0/80, p = 0.0001).
251.Rationale: The passage does report no difference in life cycle duration at 25°C, but it also documents reduced engorged female and egg weights (statistically significant) and higher mortality of infected ticks under temperature stress. ‘‘Overall fitness’’ encompasses fecundity and survival as well as developmental timing. Because key fitness components (reproductive output and survival under temperature stress) are adversely affected in infected ticks, the statement that infection does not affect overall fitness in laboratory conditions is not supported by the passage.

251.Option: The lower fecundity of R. conorii conorii-infected female ticks, indicated by reduced engorged female and egg weights, suggests that the prevalence of infection in a tick population would gradually decline and disappear without periodic re-infection.
Evidence: the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g ... compared to 0.4749 g ... for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g ... compared to 0.2919 g ... for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).
Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

The filial transmission rate (FIR ... ) of R. conorii conorii was 99.07% (107/108), 94.3% (66/70) and 95.5% (21/22) in larvae from several infected females of the 3rd, 4th, and 11th generations, respectively.
251.Rationale: Although infected females produce lighter eggs and engorged females (indicating reduced fecundity), the passage provides strong evidence that R. conorii conorii is transmitted vertically with very high rates (near 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission, high FIR) and that an infected laboratory colony persisted for twelve successive generations and was still growing. These findings contradict the conclusion that infection prevalence would necessarily decline to disappearance without periodic re-infection; high-efficiency vertical transmission can sustain the infection despite reduced fecundity, at least under the laboratory conditions reported.

251.Option: The 100% transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus over twelve generations implies that the infection can be maintained indefinitely in tick populations without the need for vertebrate reservoirs.
Evidence: Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2).

The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection. The filial transmission rate (FIR ... ) of R. conorii conorii was 99.07% (107/108), 94.3% (66/70) and 95.5% (21/22) in larvae from several infected females of the 3rd, 4th, and 11th generations, respectively.
However, the prevalence in the wild of ticks infected by R. conorii is low (usually less than 1%).
251.Rationale: The laboratory data show sustained vertical transmission over many generations and a growing infected colony, which supports the idea that the bacterium can be maintained across tick generations in laboratory populations without vertebrate hosts. However, the option makes a broader claim about indefinite maintenance in 'tick populations' generally (implicitly including natural populations). The passage also notes low prevalence in wild ticks and documents adverse effects on tick survival under temperature stress, indicating environmental and fitness constraints in nature. Therefore, one cannot infer from the passage that indefinite maintenance without vertebrate reservoirs will occur in natural tick populations; the evidence supports maintenance in laboratory conditions but does not logically imply indefinite persistence in the field.

251.Option: The high mortality rate of R. conorii conorii-infected engorged nymphs and adults at 37°C compared to non-infected ticks suggests that temperature extremes may prevent the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions.
Evidence: Significantly more engorged, infected nymphs maintained at 37°C died than non-infected nymphs maintained at the same temperature (17–67%, 101/300 and 4–7%, 15/300, respectively, p = 0.0001).
In the third batch, which was maintained at 37°C for one month, 37.5–85% of infected ticks and 15–40% of non-infected ticks died (75/120 vs. 29/120, p = 0.0001).

251.Rationale: The passage documents substantially higher mortality of infected engorged nymphs and adults at 37°C versus non-infected ticks, and explicitly states that temperature influences tick–microorganism relationships and vectorial capacity. From these observations, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that extreme high temperatures that increase mortality of infected ticks could reduce the ability of the bacterium to persist and thereby hinder establishment of new local foci in regions where such high temperatures are encountered. The option is appropriately cautious ('may prevent') and follows from the provided experimental evidence linking temperature-driven mortality to potential impacts on maintenance of infection.

251.Option: The reactivation phenomenon observed in R. conorii conorii-infected ticks at elevated temperatures explains why infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions in temperate climates.
Evidence: In the 3 experiments with engorged nymphs, 88–100% of the R. conorii conorii-infected nymphs that had been previously maintained at 4°C died after being transferred to 25°C (53–80% died without molting) compared to 41% (62/150) of non-infected nymphs after one month (288/300 vs. 62/150, p = 0.0001).

In the first batch of adults, which was maintained at 4°C for one month and then transferred at 25°C for rabbit attachment, 10–40% infected ticks died compared to a lack of death for non-infected ticks (30/120 vs. 0/80, p = 0.0001).
Significantly more engorged, infected nymphs maintained at 37°C died than non-infected nymphs ... (p = 0.0001).
251.Rationale: The passage does not describe any 'reactivation phenomenon' at elevated temperatures that would lead to improved cold-weather survival. On the contrary, infected ticks showed high mortality after exposure to low temperature (4°C) and also higher mortality at high temperature (37°C). These data indicate that infected ticks are less likely, not more likely, to survive cold/winter conditions. Therefore the proposed explanatory mechanism and the conclusion are not supported by the passage.

252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
252.Rationale: Both sentences in the option are verbatim statements from the Passage Body. The passage explicitly reports the Quil A effect on IgG2c (and not IgG1) and summarizes that a single 1 µg VLP dose induced potent neutralizing antibody and IgG titers. Therefore this statement is directly supported by the passage.

252.Option: The complete CHIKV structural cassette was cloned downstream the strong polyhedrin promoter of the AcMNPV baculovirus (Ac-S27). Western analysis on cell and medium fractions of Sf21-cells that were infected with Ac-S27 indicated that glycoprotein processing (glycosylation, furin cleavage and surface localization) is efficient and that the functionality of E1 as fusion protein is retained.
Evidence: The coding sequence of the structural polyprotein was cloned downstream the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone, after which Sf21 cells were infected with a MOI = 10 TCID50/ml.

Western analysis of the cell fraction yielded protein bands of ∼50 kDa for α-E1 (Fig. 1B, lane 2) and two bands of ∼50 kDa and ∼57 kDa for α-E2 (Fig. 1C, lane 2). These sizes correspond to predicted molecular masses of E1 (47.5 kDa), E2 (47.3 kDa) and its precursor E3E2 (54.6 kDa), respectively.
Treated cells displayed ring-like structures (Fig. 2, middle and bottom), indicating that the glycoproteins are indeed exposed at the surface of the infected cells.
Therefore, it can be concluded that E1 retains its fusogenic properties when expressed in Sf-cells.
252.Rationale: The passage describes cloning the structural cassette downstream of the polyhedrin promoter to create Ac-S27, presents Western blot evidence consistent with correct processing (E1, E2 and precursor sizes), shows surface localization by immunofluorescence, and explicitly concludes that E1 retains fusogenic activity. Thus the statement is directly supported by the Passage Body.

252.Option: The 1 µg non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine elicited a similar neutralizing antibody response as compared to 10 µg of inactivated virus (Fig. 5A), yet induced lower IgG2c titers (Fig. 5C). This might suggest that chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity, although further experimentation would be required to confirm this.
Evidence: The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
252.Rationale: The first two clauses of the option accurately reflect the Passage Body: 1 µg VLPs elicited neutralizing titers comparable to 10 µg inactivated virus, and induced lower IgG (including IgG2c) titers. However, the additional inference that chemical inactivation "might suggest... generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity" is not supported by the passage. In fact, neutralizing activity was comparable between 1 µg VLPs and 10 µg inactivated virus, so the suggested implication about inactivation reducing neutralizing activity contradicts the provided data. The causal speculation therefore cannot be derived from the Passage Body.
