Evidence: The boy was born in Austria and had never left the country, and abnormalities at birth or previous illnesses were denied by the parents.

The mother recalled traveling to the Romanian Black Sea coast, a region endemic for VL [9], in 2015 and 2016, before the birth of the patient and around the birth of his older brother, but denied having had any symptoms indicative of leishmaniasis.
Based on the patient´s history and findings, a diagnosis of vertically transmitted concurrent VL and diffuse CL was made.
In Austria, which is considered non-endemic for leishmaniasis [9], this disease is not notifiable, thus, the availability of data is limited.
279.Rationale: The passage describes a congenital (vertically transmitted) case in which the mother had travel-related exposure; the authors explicitly diagnose vertical transmission. There is no evidence presented that local sandfly vectors in Austria transmitted the infection or that autochthonous vector-borne transmission is occurring. Austria is described as non-endemic. Therefore the conclusion that local sandflies are now capable of transmitting L. donovani/infantum and causing autochthonous transmission is not supported by the passage.

279.Option: The high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in Austrian military personnel indicates that leishmaniasis is now endemic in Austria due to widespread asymptomatic transmission.
Evidence: In Austria, which is considered non-endemic for leishmaniasis [9], this disease is not notifiable, thus, the availability of data is limited.
a relatively high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies was shown in several serological surveillance studies conducted in Austrian military personnel [30–33].

reports of serological surveillance studies conducted in healthy, asymptomatic Austrian military personnel [30–33] were excluded, as these individuals were healthy and asymptomatic and could not be conflated with the study cohort, which consists of patients who had presented at their respective physicians with symptoms...
A total of 154 patients had a borderline-positive serological result, the value of which remains debatable.
279.Rationale: While the passage notes seroprevalence findings in military personnel, it also explicitly states that Austria is considered non-endemic and that the military serology studies were excluded from the symptomatic case cohort because participants were asymptomatic. The passage does not provide evidence of widespread asymptomatic transmission across the general population or of endemic, sustained local transmission. Borderline serology results and the debated value of such findings further undermine concluding endemicity from these seroprevalence studies alone. Thus the claim is not supported by the presented data.

279.Option: The increasing detection of L. infantum in cutaneous leishmaniasis cases in Austria suggests that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility in human hosts over time.
Evidence: L. infantum was identified in 4 cases (4.0%) and two of these had occurred in 2021 (Fig 2D).
particularly since 2016, increasing numbers of members of the L. donovani/infantum complex (Fig 2E) as well as of the group L. tropica/major/mexicana (Fig 2F) were identified as the causative agents.
The course and severity of infection depend on the virulence of the causative parasite strain and the immune status of the human host.
As in many cases the discrimination into these clinically relevant groups sufficed for medical treatment decisions, further speciation to the species level had not been performed.
279.Rationale: The passage documents increased detection of members of the L. donovani/infantum complex among CL cases, but provides no data demonstrating changes in the intrinsic virulence or transmissibility of L. infantum over time. Observed increases could reflect travel patterns, diagnostic changes, or introduction of infected travelers rather than evolutionary changes in the parasite. The passage even notes limits in species-level speciation. Therefore the specific inference that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility cannot be derived from the provided information.

279.Option: The increasing detection of the L. donovani/infantum complex as a cause of cutaneous leishmaniasis in Austria reflects a shift in the epidemiological pattern of the disease, with this species complex now being recognized as a significant cause of CL in non-endemic regions.
Evidence: In 100 of the 146 CL cases information on the causative Leishmania species was available (Fig 2A and S1 Table). The most frequent species identified was L. major in 15 (15.0%) cases ...
L. infantum was identified in 4 cases (4.0%) and two of these had occurred in 2021 (Fig 2D).
particularly since 2016, increasing numbers of members of the L. donovani/infantum complex (Fig 2E) as well as of the group L. tropica/major/mexicana (Fig 2F) were identified as the causative agents.
Members belonging to the L. donovani/infantum complex were the cause of 29 (29.0%) ...
In Austria, which is considered non-endemic for leishmaniasis [9], this disease is not notifiable, thus, the availability of data is limited.
279.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that members of the L. donovani/infantum complex have increasingly been identified as causative agents of CL in the Austrian case series, particularly since 2016, and that they account for a substantial proportion (29%) of species-typed CL cases. Given that Austria is non-endemic, these observations indicate a changing epidemiological pattern with the L. donovani/infantum complex emerging as a recognized cause of CL in this non-endemic setting. This inference follows from the documented change in species distribution among cases in Austria.

280.Option: The attenuated virulence of GFP-PA2G4 transgenic parasites is primarily due to impaired differentiation into amastigotes, as shown by the failure of L. donovani transgenic lines to differentiate under axenic conditions.
Evidence: Interestingly GFP-PA2G4 are highly attenuated and lesions were only apparent after 40 days after inoculation.

As judged by the expression of the amastigote-specific A2 protein family [31], transgenic parasites carrying GFP-PA2G4 are bona fide amastigotes at 48 h and no differences are observed when compared with WT. This result suggests that the virulence attenuation is potentially due to defects in cell proliferation.
280.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that GFP-PA2G4 transgenic L. donovani lines do differentiate into bona fide amastigotes (A2 protein expression at 48 h) with "no differences" compared to WT. Thus the data contradict the claim that attenuated virulence is primarily due to impaired differentiation; the authors instead suggest the attenuation is potentially due to defects in cell proliferation. Therefore this option is not supported by the Passage Body.

280.Option: LmaPA2G4 functions as a transcriptional repressor in Leishmania, as suggested by its ability to bind double-stranded RNA and the observed defects in gene expression in overexpressing lines.
Evidence: Human homolog Ebp1 interacts with double stranded RNA [34] and thus it was tempting to study whether GFP-PA2G4 is able to bind a synthetic double stranded RNA Poly (I∶C).
These data suggest than indeed de novo protein synthesis is greatly reduced in pro- and amastigotes over-expressing LmaPA2G4.
Although the genome of Leishmania seems to be constitutively expressed [27], between 6% and 9% of the genes display significant expression profiles.
280.Rationale: While the passage shows that GFP-PA2G4 can bind double-stranded RNA (EMSA) and that overexpression reduces de novo protein synthesis, there is no evidence presented that LmaPA2G4 acts as a transcriptional repressor. In fact, the passage notes Leishmania's genome is largely constitutively expressed, and the reported defects are at the level of translation/protein synthesis rather than transcriptional repression. Therefore the specific claim that LmaPA2G4 functions as a transcriptional repressor is not supported by the Passage Body.

280.Option: LmaPA2G4 is a phosphoprotein that is phosphorylated by MPK7, as suggested by its isolation through a phospho-enrichment procedure (IMAC) and its role in regulating translation initiation and elongation.
Evidence: Phosphoproteins were isolated by immobilized metal affinity chromatography (IMAC) and differentially labeled with CyDye fluors (GE Healthcare) as detailed in Material and Methods.
Spot ID 207 was over-represented in GFPK7 (2.97 fold change and p = 0.00056) and excised from the gel and analyzed by MS/MS (Fig. 1).
Recombinant LmjF19.0160 was not phosphorylated in vitro by active GFPK7 using an in vitro kinase assay (Fig. S1).

Given the fact that LmjF19.0160 was enriched with an IMAC (phospho-specific) column, we are attempting to characterize the putative phosphorylation sites, with the objective of performing site-directed mutagenesis.
280.Rationale: Although LmaPA2G4 (LmjF19.0160) was enriched in a phospho-enrichment (IMAC) fraction and was over-represented in GFPK7 samples, the authors explicitly report that recombinant LmjF19.0160 "was not phosphorylated in vitro by active GFPK7" and that they are still attempting to characterize putative phosphorylation sites. There is no conclusive evidence in the Passage Body that LmaPA2G4 is phosphorylated by MPK7; the in vitro assay was negative. Thus the specific inference that it is a phosphoprotein phosphorylated by MPK7 cannot be derived from the presented data.

280.Option: The overexpression of LmaPA2G4 leads to a significant reduction in de novo protein synthesis, which is likely responsible for the observed growth and virulence defects in Leishmania parasites.
Evidence: The overexpression of LmaPA2G4 results in a significant growth delay (Fig. 4D).
Interestingly GFP-PA2G4 are highly attenuated and lesions were only apparent after 40 days after inoculation.
These data suggest than indeed de novo protein synthesis is greatly reduced in pro- and amastigotes over-expressing LmaPA2G4.
Defects in biosynthesis will likely impact cell proliferation and ultimately may be responsible for the phenotype observed.
280.Rationale: The Passage Body directly shows that overexpression of LmaPA2G4 causes a significant growth delay and attenuated virulence in mice, and provides experimental metabolic-labeling data stating that "de novo protein synthesis is greatly reduced" in parasites over-expressing LmaPA2G4. The authors explicitly connect defects in biosynthesis to impaired cell proliferation and the observed virulence phenotype. Therefore the inference that reduced de novo protein synthesis caused by LmaPA2G4 overexpression is likely responsible for the growth and virulence defects is justified by the data.

280.Option: The essentiality of LmaPA2G4 in L. major is due to its role in ribosome biogenesis, as evidenced by the over-representation of ribosomal proteins and translation initiation factors in the proteomic analysis of GFP-PA2G4 overexpressing parasites.
Evidence: These results suggest that LmaPA2G4 is an essential gene in L. major.

Interestingly, the homologs of identified eukaryotic translation initiation factor 5 (LmjF25.0720, 6.1-fold change), 60S ribosomal protein (LmjF29.2460, 5.9-fold change) and 40S ribosomal protein (LmjF28.0960, 5.3-fold change) are implicated in translation initiation and elongation [32].
The protein binds structured RNAs and was suggested to be involved in linking ribosome biosynthesis and cell proliferation [14].
Defects in biosynthesis will likely impact cell proliferation and ultimately may be responsible for the phenotype observed.
280.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes LmaPA2G4 as essential and presents proteomic results from GFP-PA2G4 overexpressing parasites showing marked changes (over-representation) in translation initiation factor and ribosomal proteins, which are directly "implicated in translation initiation and elongation." Additionally, the human homolog (Ebp1) is noted to bind structured RNAs and to be involved in linking ribosome biosynthesis and cell proliferation. Together, these statements support the epidemiological/biological inference that LmaPA2G4's essential function is tied to ribosome-related processes (ribosome biogenesis/translation), providing a plausible mechanistic explanation for its essentiality. While the study does not perform a direct assay of ribosome biogenesis, the combination of essentiality, proteomic perturbation of ribosomal/translation components, RNA-binding activity, and the known homologous function justifies this inference from the Passage Body.

281.Option: The lack of association between malaria infection and low birth weight in this study suggests that placental histopathological changes are not clinically relevant in this setting.
Evidence: The babies from both groups were also similar in terms of gestational age at delivery and birth weight (Table 1).

However, when the women who experienced more than one infection during their pregnancy were compared to uninfected women, there was a small but significant increase in both the thickness of the placental barrier (median [IQR]) 4.52 [4.25, 5.12] vs 4.17 [3.56, 6.61], p = 0.023 and the percentage of intervillous monocytes 3.13 [2.19, 4.22] vs 2.02 [1.20, 3.12], p = 0.025 (Table 2).
281.Rationale: The passage does report no difference in birth weight between infected and uninfected women, and it also documents small but significant histopathological changes (increased placental barrier thickness and intervillous monocytes) among women with multiple infections. However, concluding that those histopathological changes are not clinically relevant overreaches the data: the study documents tissue changes but does not claim or establish their lack of clinical relevance. The absence of an observed difference in birth weight in this sample does not prove the histopathology is clinically irrelevant (sample size, effect size, timing, or other outcomes might obscure clinical effects). Therefore the inference is not supported by the passage.

281.Option: The increase in placental barrier thickness observed in women with multiple malaria infections may be primarily driven by P. vivax due to its higher prevalence in the region, despite the lack of parasite sequestration in the placenta.
Evidence: The region where our study was conducted has been described elsewhere. This region presents a higher prevalence of P. vivax infections than P. falciparum infections [27].

However, when the women who experienced more than one infection during their pregnancy were compared to uninfected women, there was a small but significant increase in both the thickness of the placental barrier ... p = 0.023 ...

Despite the small numbers, it is possible to realise that the increase in the thickness of the placental barrier observed is at least partially driven by P. vivax, while the increase in the percentage of intervillous monocytes seems independent of it (S1 Fig).
Twenty-one percent of those women presented with infections at delivery (Table 1), with no detectable parasites in the placenta.
281.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the study region has higher P. vivax prevalence, documents a small but significant increase in placental barrier thickness among women with multiple infections, and explicitly notes that this increase is at least partially driven by P. vivax despite absence of detectable parasites in the placenta. Therefore the option is directly supported by the passage.

281.Option: The higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in women with active malaria infection indicates a systemic shift toward angiogenic activation that may counteract placental barrier thickening.
Evidence: we observed a decrease of peripheral Ang-1 in women with a current infection and a significantly higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in both the total women who had MiP or only the women who had a current infection, relative to the uninfected women (Table 3).

Multilinear regression models... revealed similar strength but opposite associations between placental barrier thickness and increased levels of Ang-2 (coeficient [95% CI], p-value) 0.30 [0.10, 0.51], p = 0.004 and Ang-1–0.30 [-0.60, -0.01], p = 0.045.
281.Rationale: While the passage reports a higher Tie-2:Ang-1 ratio in women with active infection, it does not state that this reflects an angiogenic activation that counteracts placental barrier thickening. In fact, the regression results show Ang-2 is positively associated with barrier thickness and Ang-1 is negatively associated (i.e. lower Ang-1 is associated with thicker barrier). The passage provides no evidence that a higher Tie-2:Ang-1 ratio counteracts thickening; the data do not support the causal interpretation offered. Thus this inference is not supported by the passage.

281.Option: The elevated levels of IL-10 in the placenta of women with malaria during pregnancy suggest a compensatory anti-inflammatory response that may protect against placental barrier thickening.
Evidence: The women with evidence of a current malaria infection had increased levels of cytokines in the periphery and of IL-10 in the placenta, which was also seen in vivax-only infected women (Table 3 and S2 and S3 Figs).
Multilinear regression models... revealed similar strength but opposite associations between placental barrier thickness and increased levels of Ang-2 ... and Ang-1–0.30 ... p = 0.045.
281.Rationale: The passage documents elevated placental IL-10 in women with current infection, but it does not provide evidence that this represents a compensatory anti-inflammatory response that protects against placental barrier thickening. The statistical analyses reported link barrier thickness to angiopoietins (Ang-1 and Ang-2), not to IL-10. No data or models in the passage demonstrate that IL-10 mitigates placental thickening, so the protective inference is unsupported by the presented results.

281.Option: In this area where diagnosis and treatment of malaria are readily available and where the impact of malaria during pregnancy is mild, we were able to detect a link between decreased levels of Ang-1 and an increase in the placental barrier thickness of Plasmodium-infected women.
Evidence: The region where our study was conducted has been described elsewhere. This region presents a higher prevalence of P. vivax infections than P. falciparum infections [27].

we observed a decrease of peripheral Ang-1 in women with a current infection and a significantly higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in both the total women who had MiP or only the women who had a current infection, relative to the uninfected women (Table 3).

Multilinear regression models... revealed similar strength but opposite associations between placental barrier thickness and increased levels of Ang-2 ... 0.30 [0.10, 0.51], p = 0.004 and Ang-1–0.30 [-0.60, -0.01], p = 0.045.
there was some evidence that malaria during pregnancy altered the effect of Ang-1 on placental barrier thickness (Fig 4).
281.Rationale: The core claim that a link was detected between decreased Ang-1 and increased placental barrier thickness is supported by the passage: peripheral Ang-1 is decreased with current infection and regression analysis shows Ang-1 is negatively associated with barrier thickness (p = 0.045), and there is evidence malaria modifies the Ang-1–thickness relationship. However, the option includes additional assertions that are not stated in the passage — specifically that diagnosis and treatment are "readily available" and that the impact of malaria during pregnancy is "mild" in this area. Those contextual claims are not provided in the Passage Body. Because the option contains unsupported contextual statements, it is not fully derivable from the passage and is therefore judged Incorrect.

282.Option: The high cure rates observed in the complete case analysis, particularly for the Milt+PM regimen, suggest that the treatment regimens are more effective in patients with less severe baseline clinical characteristics.
Evidence: In the complete case analysis, cure rates were SDA 95.5% (95% CI 93.9–96.8), AmB+Milt 95.5% (95% CI 92.7–97.5) and Milt+PM 99.6% (95% CI 98.6–99.9) (Table 2).

Although not significantly consistent, patients treated with SDA and Milt+PM (a majority of whom were treated at district hospitals) were younger, more likely to be female, and to present with severe wasting than those treated with AmB+Milt.
282.Rationale: The passage reports high complete-case cure rates and specifically a very high rate for Milt+PM, but it also states that patients receiving Milt+PM tended to present with more severe wasting, not less severe characteristics. The passage does not provide evidence that higher cure rates are confined to or caused by less severe baseline clinical characteristics. Therefore the inference that the regimens are more effective in patients with less severe baseline characteristics is not supported by the presented data.

282.Option: The use of combination regimens such as AmB+Milt and Milt+PM is associated with a higher risk of treatment failure in patients with prolonged illness duration, as indicated by the higher relapse rates in those with illness lasting 8 weeks or more.
Evidence: Relapse rates varied by drug regimen and were higher for children than for those older than 12 years (Tables 3 and 4).
Those with illness that had lasted 8 weeks or less were also more likely to have relapse at 6 months.
282.Rationale: The passage states the opposite of the option's claim regarding illness duration: patients with illness lasting 8 weeks or less were more likely to relapse, not those with prolonged illness. While relapse rates varied by regimen, no evidence in the passage links combination regimens to higher risk of failure specifically in patients with prolonged illness duration. Thus the proposed inference is contradicted by the provided data.

282.Option: The high rate of treatment compliance observed in this study, particularly in the Milt+PM and AmB+Milt arms, indicates that the regimens are more feasible in primary health care settings despite the logistical burden of daily visits.
Evidence: Patients treated at the district hospital were admitted to a VL ward for the 10 days of treatment, whereas patients enrolled at PHC level were managed on an outpatient basis, returning each day for the injection.
Patients who failed to return for ambulatory treatment were actively traced by telephone and, if necessary, in person to ensure maximum compliance.

Overall, 1,684 patients (95.6%) completed the 6-month follow-up visit. Thirteen (0.7%) patients did not complete treatment or had their treatment stopped by a study physician, and 64 patients (3.6%) were lost to follow-up at 6 months.

Milt+PM was used predominantly in Chapra district hospital (Saran District) and Saran PHCs; AmB+Milt in Hajipur District Hospital (Vaishali District) and Vaishali PHCs and SDA almost exclusively in Hajipur District Hospital (Vaishali District).
282.Rationale: The passage shows high overall completion and describes how outpatient PHC patients returned daily for injections and were actively traced to maintain compliance. However, it does not provide regimen-specific compliance or feasibility metrics stratified by care setting sufficient to conclude that the regimens are inherently more feasible in primary health care settings despite logistical burdens. The presence of active tracing and differing site allocation confounds a direct inference of feasibility in PHCs; therefore the option overstates what can be derived from the data.

282.Option: The safety profile of the SDA regimen is compromised by the high incidence of anaphylactic reactions and serious adverse events related to AmBisome, particularly in patients with severe wasting and elevated liver enzymes.
Evidence: There were 5 serious adverse events (SAE) in the SDA arm. Anaphylactic reaction occurred during treatment in one patient and was related to the study drug AmBisome.

There were four SAEs after completion of treatment and discharge from hospital: one asymptomatic atrial ectopic possibly related to AmBisome and three other SAEs that were unrelated to the study drugs: TB empyema, hospitalization due to dehydration and elevated creatinine, and lower respiratory tract infection. All SAEs resolved completely with no sequela.
Severe anaemia was more common in the SDA treatment arm. ALT levels were higher in the AmB+Milt arm, whereas AST levels were higher in the SDA and AmB+Milt arms than in the Milt+PM arm (Table 1).
Adverse events leading to treatment interruption were rare (<1% for all three drug regimens).
282.Rationale: While one anaphylactic reaction related to AmBisome occurred in the SDA arm, the passage characterizes serious adverse events as infrequent overall and notes that SAEs resolved without sequela. The reported number of SAEs (five in the SDA arm) does not constitute a 'high incidence' given the size of the arm, and no evidence links these SAEs specifically to patients with severe wasting or elevated liver enzymes. Thus the claim that the SDA safety profile is compromised by a high incidence of AmBisome-related SAEs, particularly in those subgroups, is not supported by the passage.

282.Option: Despite a range of clinical severity, presentations, and patient demographics, all of the treatments showed excellent safety profiles.
Evidence: A total of 1,761 patients were recruited, 534 (30.3%) children (≤12 years) and 1,227 (69.7%) adults (>12 years).

Although not significantly consistent, patients treated with SDA and Milt+PM (a majority of whom were treated at district hospitals) were younger, more likely to be female, and to present with severe wasting than those treated with AmB+Milt.
Serious adverse events were infrequent in all study arms.
All SAEs resolved completely with no sequela.
Adverse events leading to treatment interruption were rare (<1% for all three drug regimens).
The most common adverse events were gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain) and back pain (Table 5).
282.Rationale: The passage describes a diverse study population with varying ages, nutritional and clinical status, and reports that serious adverse events were infrequent across arms, that adverse-event-related treatment interruptions were rare (<1%), and that all SAEs resolved without sequela. These statements support the inference that, across the spectrum of clinical severity and patient demographics represented, the evaluated treatments demonstrated an overall excellent safety profile.

283.Option: The guinea pig model of Lujo virus infection exhibits severe, rapidly progressive, and uniformly lethal disease characterized by high viral loads, disseminated organ damage, and hallmarks consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation, despite a lack of significant early pro-inflammatory cytokine upregulation.
Evidence: Here, we report successfully establishing a lethal model of LUJV HF in strain 13/N guinea pigs infected with either authentic wild-type or a recombinant full-length reverse genetics-derived LUJV.
All animals infected with either the wtLUJV ... or with recLUJV ... experienced an onset of progressive illness starting approximately 5 days PI.
Animals were either found dead (wtLUJV N = 1; recLUJV N = 2) or euthanized when moribund (wtLUJV N = 9; recLUJV N = 8) 11–16 days PI.
Within 48 h PI, a dramatic rise in viral load, measured by qRT-PCR ... was detected in all tissues and fluids analyzed ... in all terminal cases exceeding 1.0×106 eq.
Consistently, animals in the terminal stages of LUJV induced disease developed multiple gross anatomical signs of profound coagulopathy suggestive of HF and bleeding diatheses.
However, ... we observed no change or decrease (2 to 5-fold) in several pro-inflammatory mRNA levels (IL-1b, RANTES), and modest up-regulation of TNFa (2 to 7-fold), primarily in kidneys.
Most prominently, IFNg was elevated in all tissues, ranging from an approximately 15-fold increase on day 5 PI to >100 fold-increase at day 14 PI.
IL-8 (a potent activator of neutrophils and other granulocytes) was upregulated (>11-fold at day 5 PI; >21 fold in terminal cases) in the liver.
283.Rationale: The passage does support that the guinea pig model demonstrates severe, rapidly progressive, and uniformly lethal disease with high viral loads, disseminated organ damage, thrombocytopenia, and gross/coagulation findings consistent with hemorrhagic fever/DIC. However, the option's final clause—'despite a lack of significant early pro-inflammatory cytokine upregulation'—is not supported. The passage documents strong early and later upregulation of pro-inflammatory/immune mediators (notably IFNg and IL-8) even though some markers (IL-1b, RANTES) showed no change or decreases and TNFa was only modestly upregulated. Thus the assertion of a lack of significant early pro-inflammatory cytokine upregulation is contradicted by the reported IFNg and IL-8 elevations; therefore the overall option is not derivable as stated.

283.Option: The lack of lethality in newborn and weanling mice suggests that Lujo virus is inherently less virulent than other pathogenic arenaviruses, such as Lassa or Junin viruses.
Evidence: Surprisingly, wtLUJV did not cause any signs of clinical illness or lethality in either 2-day-old or 14-day-old mice regardless of the dose (up to 2.0×103 FFU) or inoculation route (intracranial, subcutaneous, or intraperitoneal).
As expected, wtJUNV-XJ13 caused uniform neurological signs and lethality by 15 days PI in 2-day-old, but not 14-day-old mice.
In contrast and as expected, wtLASV infection resulted in near uniform (90%) lethality in 14-day-old weanling mice, but was non-lethal in 2-day-old newborn mice.
Here, we report successfully establishing a lethal model of LUJV HF in strain 13/N guinea pigs ...
During this outbreak ... resulting in a total case fatality of 80% (4/5).
283.Rationale: The passage reports that LUJV was non-lethal in the tested mouse age groups while Lassa and Junin showed the expected age-specific lethality in mice. However, concluding LUJV is inherently less virulent than other pathogenic arenaviruses is not supported: the virus caused highly lethal disease in guinea pigs and produced high human case-fatality in the outbreak. Virulence in one animal model (mice) does not justify a blanket inference of inherently lower virulence across hosts. Therefore this option overgeneralizes beyond the passage evidence and is not derivable.

283.Option: The robust induction of IL-10 and IFNg in multiple tissues suggests that the guinea pig immune response is effectively controlling viral replication and preventing lethal disease progression.
Evidence: Most prominently, IFNg was elevated in all tissues, ranging from an approximately 15-fold increase on day 5 PI to >100 fold-increase at day 14 PI.

Generally, we noted an induction of the anti-inflammatory/immunoregulatory gene IL-10 in all tissues, including >15-fold induction in lungs of LUJV-infected animals compared to control animals at day 14 PI.
Within 48 h PI, a dramatic rise in viral load ... was detected in all tissues and fluids analyzed ... in all terminal cases exceeding 1.0×106 eq.
Animals were either found dead ... or euthanized when moribund ... 11–16 days PI.
Consistently, animals in the terminal stages of LUJV induced disease developed multiple gross anatomical signs of profound coagulopathy suggestive of HF and bleeding diatheses.
283.Rationale: While the passage documents robust induction of IFNg and IL-10 in multiple tissues, it also shows that animals developed very high viral loads, progressive disease, and high mortality. Therefore the cytokine induction cannot be interpreted as effectively controlling viral replication or preventing lethal progression. The evidence demonstrates that despite the immune gene induction, infection progressed to fatal disease in this model, so the option's inference is not supported.

283.Option: The absence of significant increases in TNFa, iNOS, and IL-1b mRNA levels in terminal cases suggests that Lujo virus infection does not trigger a dysregulated host immune response, unlike other hemorrhagic fever viruses such as Ebola or Marburg.
Evidence: we observed no change or decrease (2 to 5-fold) in several pro-inflammatory mRNA levels (IL-1b, RANTES), and modest up-regulation of TNFa (2 to 7-fold), primarily in kidneys.
Specific gene mRNA signatures for IL-2 and iNOS were not detected in any tissue at any time.
Most prominently, IFNg was elevated in all tissues, ranging from an approximately 15-fold increase on day 5 PI to >100 fold-increase at day 14 PI.
IL-8 ... was upregulated (>11-fold at day 5 PI; >21 fold in terminal cases) in the liver.
Consistently, animals in the terminal stages of LUJV induced disease developed multiple gross anatomical signs of profound coagulopathy suggestive of HF and bleeding diatheses.
283.Rationale: Although some pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-1b, RANTES) did not increase and TNFa was only modestly upregulated, other immune markers (notably IFNg and IL-8) showed strong induction. In addition, severe pathology, high viral loads, and terminal coagulopathy occurred. From the passage alone one cannot conclude the absence of a dysregulated immune response, nor can one validly compare the immune phenotype directly to Ebola/Marburg without additional data. Thus the stated inference is not supported by the provided evidence.

284.Option: The reduced parasite burden in ALD1 KO-infected hosts may be due to a failure in stomodeal valve colonization in sand flies, which is critical for transmission to mammalian hosts.
Evidence: Both strains established infection well in Lu. longipalpis.
Percentages of infected females were over 95% for the WT and about 80% for the ALD1 KO strain on day 7 p.i.

no differences were observed in the localization of parasites at late-stage infections (7 d.p.i.) and stomodeal valve colonization was observed in the majority (> 90%) of these infected females in both tested groups.
284.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that stomodeal valve colonization was observed in the majority (>90%) of infected females for both WT and ALD1 KO and that no differences in localization were observed at late-stage infections. Therefore failure to colonize the stomodeal valve cannot explain the reduced parasite burden; the data show colonization was preserved in the KO. This option asserts a mechanism contradicted by the passage.

284.Option: The reduced parasite load in ALD1 KO-infected hosts is likely due to enhanced immune activation in the host, as suggested by the lack of significant differences in NO and urea production between infected macrophages.
Evidence: We found no significant differences between the ALD1 KO and WT infected non-stimulated or IFN-γ+LPS stimulated macrophages regarding the production of NO (F(1, 61) = 1.74, p = 0.19non-stimulated; F(1, 61) = 0.68, p = 0.41IFN-γ+LPS) or urea (F(1, 61) = 0.27, p = 0.61non-stimulated; F(1, 61) = 3.76, p = 0.07IFN-γ+LPS).
we noticed that at 4 h.p.i. similar numbers of macrophages were infected, suggesting that ALD1 KO harbored no defects in initial infection and transition to the amastigote stage.
284.Rationale: The option claims enhanced immune activation is the cause but then cites 'lack of significant differences in NO and urea' as supporting evidence; the passage shows no significant differences in these markers between WT and KO infections, which argues against enhanced host immune activation being responsible. Moreover, initial infection rates were similar (4 h.p.i.), further undermining the claim that enhanced immune activation explains the reduced burden. Thus this inference is not supported by the passage.

284.Option: The reduced parasite burden in both hosts is likely due to a loss of ALD1’s role in maintaining the integrity of the parasite’s mitochondrial membrane, as suggested by its cytoplasmic localization and ATP/GTP-binding potential.
Evidence: The transmembrane domain prediction programs indicated that ALD1 is unlikely to be a membrane protein.
In agreement with this, direct analysis of the tagged protein localization by confocal microscopy revealed it to be cytoplasmic (Fig 1B).
ALD1 is a cytosolic protein that most likely requires nucleotide binding for a function in Leishmania not shared with its orthologs in related monoxenous flagellates.

Skin flushing (including facial flushing, reddening of neck and upper chest), oliguria, arthralgia, periorbital pain and edema, and hypotension appeared in a relatively low rate in HFRS (15–49%), but not in SFTS.
Less than 10% of HFRS patients had icterus, and constipation, which were also not observed in SFTS patients (Table 1).
289.Rationale: While the passage notes that certain signs (skin flushing, oliguria, periorbital edema, etc.) appeared at low rates in HFRS and were not observed in the four SFTS cases, it also emphasizes that the overall clinical manifestations were hard to differentiate and that both diseases share common symptoms (fever, fatigue, headache, thrombocytopenia, proteinuria). The data do not demonstrate that those features reliably exclude SFTS in general clinical practice (small numbers, overlap in other symptoms), so the claim of reliable exclusion is not supported by the passage.

289.Option: The high sensitivity and specificity of the HFRS-IgM and SFTSV-IgM ELISA kits used in this study suggest that routine serological testing in clinical settings could eliminate misdiagnosis of SFTS as HFRS.
Evidence: Anti-hantavirus IgM was detected by using a HFRS-IgM ELISA Kit (Wantai Biological Pharmacy, Beijing, China) which coated with anti-Human IgM (μ-chain specific) antibodies to capture IgM antibodies in the serum and detected with HRP-conjugated Hantavirus antigen.

The hantavirus-negative patients’ sera were further analyzed for anti-SFTSV IgM antibodies by using a SFTSV-IgM ELISA Kit (Xinlianxin Biotech CO., LTD, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

A confirmed HFRS case is a clinically diagnosed patient plus one of the following conditions: serum IgM antibody positive to Hantavirus, IgG antibody seroconversion or 4-fold increases, or RT-PCR positive to Hantavirus RNA. IgM antibody test was used in this study to have a confirmed diagnosis.

ELISA results showed that 57.0% (73/128) of patients were seropositive to hantavirus; that among the 55 sera which were IgM negative to hantavirus IgM antibodies, 4 (7.3%) were positive to SFTSV IgM antibodies.
289.Rationale: The passage describes which ELISA kits were used and that IgM testing was used for confirmation, and it reports the serology results. However, it provides no data on the sensitivity or specificity of either ELISA kit, nor evidence that routine serological testing would eliminate misdiagnosis. Moreover, misdiagnosed SFTS cases occurred despite testing in the study context. Therefore the specific claim about high sensitivity/specificity and elimination of misdiagnosis is not supported by the passage.

289.Option: Because both SFTS and HFRS are caused by viruses in the Bunyaviridae family, they are likely to share similar transmission routes and should be managed with identical public health interventions.
Evidence: SFTSV is transmitted by tick bite and frequently spread from person-to-person [2–4]SFTSV is a negative-strand RNA virus belonging to the Phlebovirus genus of the family Bunyaviridae [1].

Hantaviruses are carried and transmitted by rodents. Humans acquire infection after direct contact with infected rodents or their excreta, which most likely occurs by inhaling virus-contaminated aerosols [18].
289.Rationale: Although both viruses belong to the Bunyaviridae family, the passage explicitly describes different transmission routes: SFTSV is tick-borne and can spread person-to-person, whereas hantaviruses are rodent-borne and transmitted via contact/inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Because transmission routes differ, the assertion that they share similar transmission and require identical public health interventions is not supported by the passage.

289.Option: The seasonal patterns of SFTS and HFRS differ, with SFTS primarily occurring in summer and HFRS having two peaks in spring and fall, which may help clinicians distinguish between the two diseases in endemic regions.
Evidence: Temporal distribution by month of disease onset was calculated and no seasonal pattern was observed for the confirmed HFRS patients (Fig 1) and the four SFTS patients occurred in May and June three of them were male and one was female.
289.Rationale: The passage reports that no seasonal pattern was observed for the confirmed HFRS patients and that the four identified SFTS cases occurred in May and June. It does not report that HFRS has two peaks in spring and fall; in fact it states the opposite (no seasonal pattern observed). Thus the specific claim about distinct seasonal peaks for HFRS and the utility of seasonality to distinguish the diseases is not supported by the provided data.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections compared to other soil-transmitted helminths, likely due to the combined reporting of two distinct species with different drug sensitivities.
Evidence: The latter two species are indistinguishable by microscopic examination of the eggs and are therefore frequently reported collectively as “hookworms”.

When ivermectin with albendazole is provided during the first round of treatment and albendazole or mebendazole is provided during the second round of treatment the impact is similar to TPMS1/TPMS2 with a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm infection [13].
In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: The passage does state that the two hookworm species are indistinguishable microscopically and therefore reported collectively, and it documents poorer model performance for hookworm. However, the option asserts the cause is that the two species have different drug sensitivities. The passage does not provide explicit evidence that the two hookworm species differ in drug sensitivity, nor that combined reporting of the two species with differing sensitivities explains the reduced model accuracy. The only related statement about drugs notes a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm when ivermectin is added, but this does not establish species-specific sensitivity differences nor link that mechanism to the poorer predictive performance. Therefore the specific causal claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the pharmacological interventions used in the control programmes were less effective against hookworms compared to other soil-transmitted helminths.
Evidence: Some 53.3% of control programmes used albendazole, 24.3% used mebendazole and the remainder used a combination of drugs which were being used for the control of lymphatic filariasis (albendazole and either ivermectin or diethylcarbamazine).

When ivermectin with albendazole is provided during the first round of treatment and albendazole or mebendazole is provided during the second round of treatment the impact is similar to TPMS1/TPMS2 with a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm infection [13].
In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: While the passage documents which drugs were used and notes a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm when ivermectin is added, it does not state that the pharmacological interventions were overall less effective against hookworm than against other STH species. There is no explicit evidence linking poorer intervention efficacy to the reduced predictive capacity for hookworm in the text. The passage reports poorer predictive performance for hookworm but does not provide data showing drug effectiveness differences as the cause. Thus this causal assertion cannot be derived from the passage.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the standard TPMS used in SM2 were derived from datasets with low baseline prevalence, leading to poor extrapolation at high prevalence levels.
Evidence: We developed 10 “standard” TPMS, one for each possible preventive chemotherapy intervention (Table 3) for use instead of TPMS from programme data.
At baseline the average prevalence of A. lumbricoides infection was 34% (range, 0–99%), of T. trichiura infection 27% (range, 0–100%) and of hookworm infection 45% (range, 0–99%).
In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: The option claims standard TPMS were derived from datasets with low baseline prevalence causing poor extrapolation at high prevalence. The passage describes that standard TPMS were developed and that baseline prevalences across datasets covered a wide range (including high values up to 99%). There is no statement in the passage that TPMS were derived predominantly from low-prevalence datasets or that this caused poor extrapolation for hookworm. Therefore the specific mechanism proposed by the option is not supported by the text.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the Kato-Katz method used in the studies has poor sensitivity for detecting low-intensity hookworm infections.
Evidence: In all the datasets used in the present study (i.e. those from the studies used to calculate the model parameters and those from control programmes used to evaluate the prediction capacity of the model), Kato-Katz was the only method used to collect parasitological data. We understand the poor precision of the diagnostic methods traditionally used to monitor STH control programmes (i.e. Kato-Katz) especially when intensities of infection are low [1].

We recognize that the prevalence would have been higher if measured with a more precise method than Kato-Katz, but we wanted to provide programme managers with a tool capable of interpreting the imperfect data collected during routine control activities.
In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Kato-Katz was the sole diagnostic method used and acknowledges its poor precision, particularly when infection intensities are low, and that prevalence would have been higher if measured with a more precise method. The passage also documents that model performance was poorest for hookworm. Poor sensitivity of Kato-Katz for low-intensity infections is a plausible and directly supported explanation for decreased predictive accuracy for hookworm in this dataset, because measurement error in observed prevalence and intensity will degrade model calibration and evaluation. Therefore this option is supported by the text.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the transition probabilities for hookworms were not updated annually, leading to a loss of stationarity over time.
Evidence: The Markov model assumes the “stationarity” of the TP, in the sense that if the intervention remains the same (e.g. albendazole is administered once a year) the TPMS will also remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme.

This assumption is based on: the remarkable capacity of STH to contaminate the environment: a soil survey conducted in 2013 in the Philippines in an area with STH prevalence of 44% found STH eggs in 85% of soil samples collected inside houses, in backyards or from the paths around the village [6]. the long survival time of STH eggs in the environment: several weeks for hookworms [7], a few years for T. trichiura [8] and more than 10 years for A. lumbricoides [9].
In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: The passage explains that the model assumes stationarity of transition probabilities (TP) when the intervention remains the same and describes the rationale for that assumption. It does not state that transition probabilities for hookworm were 'not updated annually' nor that this lack of updating caused loss of stationarity. The model by design uses TPMS assumed constant; the option asserts a procedural omission (not updating annually) and a causal link to reduced capacity for hookworm specifically, but the passage contains no evidence that TPs were expected to be updated annually or that a failure to do so explains the poorer hookworm performance. Therefore this option's specific causal claim cannot be derived from the passage.

291.Option: The use of visual analogue scores in the LeishCOM_LCL tool ensures that both clinician and patient perspectives are captured in a reliable and repeatable manner, which enhances the tool's validity for assessing treatment response in LCL.
Evidence: The CRF also includes visual analogue scores that capture clinician’s and patient’s perspectives.

To measure the impact of the treatment on the “skin problem” at the time of assessment a Visual Analogue Score (VAS) ranging from 0–10 (0 = “completely clear skin”, 10 = “severely affected skin”) was described for both investigator (VASi) and patient (VASp) (CRF Section 6.3).
Each line was 10 cm long. Each score is to be allocated according to the nearest whole cm.
Face and content validity of the core outcome instrument was assessed at the face-to-face meeting as well as through multiple virtual feedback engagement between stakeholders.
Further positive results of validity testing of the LeishCOM_LCL has been established including criterion validity. The robust methodology and results from this will be published separately.
291.Rationale: The passage documents that visual analogue scores (VASi and VASp) were included to capture both investigator and patient perspectives and describes how they are to be used. However, the statement claims that their use "ensures" reliability and repeatability and that this "enhances" validity for assessing treatment response. The passage does not provide direct evidence demonstrating that the VAS measures are reliable and repeatable (e.g., test-retest reliability, inter-rater reliability metrics) nor that their inclusion by itself ensures improved validity. While face/content validity and further validation are mentioned, these do not specifically prove that the VAS scores are reliable/repeatable. Therefore the stronger causal claim that VAS use ensures reliability/repeatability and enhances validity cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body.

291.Option: The development of the LeishCOM_LCL tool represents the first standardized and validated approach to assess core outcome domains in localized cutaneous leishmaniasis, enabling consistent evaluation of treatment response across clinical trials and routine care.
Evidence: The overall aim of this work was to adopt a standardized and validated approach to assess agreed clinical aspects of LCL and response to treatment in a measurable manner for use in clinical trials and routine patient care which were then reflected in a practical tool.

Once developed, the Leishmaniasis Core Outcome Measure Instrument for Localised Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (LeishCOM_LCL) was incorporated into a case report form (CRF) and the process of face and content validation was undertaken.
Further comprehensive validation of the final outcome measure instrument has been conducted through a clinical study.

There are currently only two studies that report a harmonized approach to assess LCL, one describing harmonized measurable clinical methodologies to assess the response to interventions in clinical trials [16] and a second follow-up study [25] assessing the capacity of implementation of the harmonized methodologies across several geographic regions. However, the proposed measurable outcomes in these studies have not been validated.
291.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states the aim to create a standardized and validated assessment approach for LCL for use in clinical trials and routine care and describes development, face/content validation, and further comprehensive validation of LeishCOM_LCL. It also notes that prior harmonized efforts existed but their measurable outcomes were not validated. From these statements one can infer that LeishCOM_LCL represents a standardized, validated instrument intended to enable consistent evaluation of treatment response across research and clinical practice. The claim that it "represents the first" such validated approach is supported by the passage's note that previous harmonized proposals were not validated.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool was developed through a multi-stakeholder consensus process that included representation from all major LCL-endemic regions, ensuring global applicability and cultural relevance.
Evidence: A multi-institutional, multi-disciplinary stakeholder panel of international experts from Sri Lanka, India, Brazil, and the United Kingdom comprising of dermatologists, general physicians and parasitologists were included along with a moderator (n = 20).

Patient perspectives including clinical and psychological aspects were captured by doing a field visit by the expert stakeholders one day before the NGT meeting to a hospital-based dermatology clinic in a LCL endemic area (Hambantota) in Sri Lanka and had face-to-face discussions with LCL patients.
to ensure that the most important patients’ perspectives encountered in different endemic areas were captured.
291.Rationale: The passage documents a multi-stakeholder consensus process with participants from Sri Lanka, India, Brazil and the United Kingdom and describes efforts to capture patient perspectives from an endemic area in Sri Lanka. However, the option asserts inclusion from "all major LCL-endemic regions" and concludes that this "ensures global applicability and cultural relevance." The Passage Body does not claim representation from all endemic regions, nor does it provide evidence that global applicability and cultural relevance are ensured. Thus the stronger claim of comprehensive global representation and ensured applicability cannot be derived from the provided text.

291.Option: The inclusion of open-ended questions on HRQoL in the LeishCOM_LCL tool allows for the systematic capture of patient-reported outcomes, which is essential for developing a patient-centered approach to LCL management.
Evidence: The CRF also includes two open-ended questions aimed at capturing some aspects of Health-related quality of life (HRQoL).
Therefore, two open-ended questions were included as a preliminary step to try and capture the most important aspects for the patient with a view to informing a novel tool at a later date.

It was decided to include open-ended questions to make it easy and straightforward for the patient to respond and to capture the most important aspect of thoughts originated by the patient. This would further ensure that clinicians recognize patient’s problems and consider these in patient management.

Information on the two open-ended questions was gathered from patients and thematic analysis was carried out during the validation process in the downstream application of the CRF in a dermatology clinic in Sri Lanka.

The psychological impact improved in line with treatment response over a 6 month period from baseline, however, 30% of patients expressed psychological concerns as a result of sequelae such as pigment changes and scarring (See S2 Table for Baseline raw data).
291.Rationale: The Passage Body states that two open-ended HRQoL questions were intentionally included to capture patient perspectives, were used in data collection and thematic analysis, and were intended to inform development of patient-focused measures. It also explicitly notes that capturing patient responses would help clinicians recognize patient problems and consider them in management. From these statements it is reasonable to infer that the inclusion of these questions permits systematic capture of patient-reported outcomes and supports a move toward patient-centered management of LCL.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool enables the assessment of both clinical efficacy and long-term sequelae, which allows for the evaluation of treatment impact on patient quality of life and psychological well-being.
Evidence: Reduction of lesion size (re-epithelialization in an ulcerated lesion) and reduced palpability defined as flattening / reduced induration of lesions were agreed as the parameters to measure the “Treatment efficacy” domain.
Pigmentary change (Hypo/hyper) and scarring (atrophic or hypertrophic) were recognized as the parameters to capture for the clinical sequelae assessment domain.

As sequelae including scarring and pigment changes are a common occurrence from LCL, a new Investigator Global Sequelae Assessment score was developed which consisted of “pigment change, atrophic scars, and hypertrophic/keloid scars”.
The aim was to establish the frequency of development of sequelae and also to try and assess whether earlier effective therapy might reduce the likelihood of sequelae.
The CRF also includes two open-ended questions aimed at capturing some aspects of Health-related quality of life (HRQoL).

The psychological impact improved in line with treatment response over a 6 month period from baseline, however, 30% of patients expressed psychological concerns as a result of sequelae such as pigment changes and scarring (See S2 Table for Baseline raw data).
291.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly defines separate domains and measures for treatment efficacy (size reduction, re-epithelialization, palpability) and for clinical sequelae (pigmentary change and scarring), and describes a sequelae assessment score. It also incorporated HRQoL questions and reports psychological outcomes during validation. Taken together, these statements support the inference that LeishCOM_LCL enables assessment of both clinical efficacy and longer-term sequelae, permitting evaluation of treatment impact on patient quality of life and psychological well-being.

292.Option: The national hotline 117 was widely used and trusted by Sierra Leoneans during the 2014–2015 outbreak, as evidenced by the high number of calls received and the rapid response times.
Evidence: In December 2014, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control (CDC) reported that Sierra Leone had more Ebola cases than Guinea and Liberia and that a significant number of Ebola-infected persons in Sierra Leone were being identified only after death, suggesting that these persons were either not captured by the surveillance system or were reluctant to use the response system [8].
In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Only three participants had ever called 117.
One person reported that he called six times, not receiving a response, and then seeking other health care resources for the suspected case.
292.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that few study participants called 117 ("Only three participants had ever called 117") and that some calls went unanswered ("called six times, not receiving a response"). It also notes many cases were identified only after death, suggesting reluctance or failures in the response system. There is no passage evidence of widespread use, trust, a high number of calls, or rapid response times; therefore the option's claim is not supported by the passage.

292.Option: The use of chlorine spray by ambulance workers was widely perceived as a necessary and effective public health measure, with most participants expressing confidence in its safety and efficacy.
Evidence: About one-third of participants expressed the belief that chlorine was harmful to people.
Most believed danger arose when patients were put inside ambulances with chlorine fumes:

“Chlorine suffocates patients. If a patient has been vomiting and toileting then in the end he or she is suffocated with chlorine, because when they spray the patient in the vehicle they usually close the door where the patient will be placed and no air enters the cabin.” —Community leader
Two respondents even believed it might be the chlorine spray that was killing people and not Ebola:
However, some respondents who believed chlorine was harmful also used chlorine as a part of their preventative hand-washing to avoid spread of the disease.
292.Rationale: The passage documents substantial concern and negative perceptions about chlorine spray (e.g., "About one-third... believed that chlorine was harmful" and vivid quotes describing suffocation). While some used chlorine for hand-washing, the evidence does not show widespread confidence in its safety or efficacy; instead it shows notable fear and misattribution of harm to chlorine. Thus the option's positive claim about widespread perceived necessity and safety is not supported.

292.Option: The high Ebola-related fatality rate in 2014 in Sierra Leone, combined with the delayed reporting of test results, likely led to widespread public skepticism about the accuracy and reliability of the Ebola laboratory test.
Evidence: As of January 2016, Ebola resulted in 3,956 cumulative confirmed deaths in Sierra Leone [3].
Due to inefficiencies in the transportation and reporting systems, returning results on an Ebola test took a minimum of 48 hours [22].
When asked what they knew about the laboratory Ebola test, nearly half of participants said they did not know anything.

Many respondents expressed concern or even disbelief that the testing process was valid: "I hear they take the blood specimen of people but even there it is not clear to me. They can take the blood of somebody under the pretext that he/she has Ebola but at the end of the day you will hear that that person has been discharged." —Business sector worker

Some also misunderstood the need to re-test for Ebola. Several participants discussed that they did not understand how a person who tested negative for Ebola could have subsequently died because their original Ebola "status was negative."
292.Rationale: The passage presents high mortality (3,956 deaths) and operational delays in test result reporting (minimum 48 hours) alongside multiple participant statements expressing lack of knowledge, disbelief, and confusion about tests (quotes above). These elements together support the epidemiological inference that the high fatality context plus delays contributed to public skepticism about test accuracy and reliability.

292.Option: Participants’ reluctance to call 117 was primarily due to financial barriers and lack of access to transportation, rather than fear or mistrust of the response system.
Evidence: In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
In about half of interviews, respondents said they would start by treating themselves with medicines normally used for other illnesses such as malaria, typhoid or cholera.
Three-quarters of participants said that if they got a fever they would go to a local health center before calling 117.
One participant said that he could not afford to go to a health center and so he would pray.
“With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people.” —Community leader
292.Rationale: The passage includes one example of financial barrier ("he could not afford to go to a health center"), but the dominant reasons reported are fear and mistrust (fear of ambulance taking people away, community anger, concerns about chlorine, confusion about testing). Given the multiple fear/mistrust statements and the small number of callers, the claim that reluctance was primarily due to finances or transport is not supported by the passage.

292.Option: Despite high reported intentions to use the Ebola response system, participants in this study expressed significant apprehension about calling the national hotline and using the response system, which may have contributed to low actual usage rates during the outbreak.
Evidence: In September 2014, over 90% of participants in a national household survey said they would go to a hospital or health facility if they thought they had Ebola [10]. In December 2014, 95% agreed that a person had a higher chance of survival if s/he immediately went to a health facility [11].
In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Only three participants had ever called 117.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call. One person said that he would not call personally, but he was confident someone else in the community would. Some expressed fears that by calling they would never see the person again:
“With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people.” —Community leader
One person reported calling six times, not receiving a response, and then seeking other health care resources for the suspected case.
292.Rationale: The passage contrasts high self-reported intentions in national surveys ("over 90%..." and "95% agreed...") with the study's qualitative findings that few participants would call 117 immediately, that only three had ever called, and that many expressed fear and apprehension (quotes above). This supports the inference that despite stated intentions, apprehension likely reduced actual use of the response system during the outbreak.

293.Option: Because ZIKV immunology is still in its infancy, we hypothesize that, similar to other flaviviruses (e.g., Yellow fever virus (YFV), Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV), and Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV)), nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response.
Evidence: The envelope (E) glycoprotein is a target for broadly protective neutralizing antibodies in ZIKV and other flaviviruses and is an attractive candidate immunogen for inclusion in ZIKV vaccines [20].
Understanding the breadth of immunity elicited by the envelope glycoprotein and the host selection of viral variants is therefore important for vaccine design.

Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
293.Rationale: The passage identifies the E glycoprotein as a known target of broadly protective neutralizing antibodies and emphasizes the importance of understanding immunity elicited by E for vaccine design. In the study, animals developed measurable neutralizing antibody titers (PRNT90 ≥20) after primary infection and were protected from detectable viremia on heterologous rechallenge. These co-occurring facts (E is the target of protective nAbs; measurable nAbs were present; animals were protected) support the epidemiologic inference that neutralizing antibodies are a critical component of the protective immune response in this model. While causality is not proven experimentally here (e.g., by passive transfer or depletion), the data and statements in the passage make the role of nAbs a well-supported inference.

293.Option: The continuing efficacy of YFV vaccines in reducing yellow fever disease in both Africa and South America suggests that the antigenic differences between individual YFV strains do not facilitate resistance to vaccine-induced immunity, i.e., diversity of YFV sequences appears to have little consequence for the use of currently formulated vaccines and/or the development of new YFV vaccines.
Evidence: ZIKV was first isolated from the blood of a sentinel rhesus monkey during yellow fever virus surveillance studies in the Zika forest of Uganda [6].
It is not known whether the differences between African and Asian lineage ZIKV have any phenotypic impact, e.g., increased transmissibility or pathogenicity.
293.Rationale: The passage mentions yellow fever only in the historical context of surveillance and does not present data or discussion about YFV vaccine performance, antigenic diversity among YFV strains, or vaccine-driven resistance. Nothing in the Passage Body provides evidence to support conclusions about continuing YFV vaccine efficacy or the impact of YFV sequence diversity on vaccines. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the provided text.

293.Option: The protection against detectable viremia from homologous and heterologous rechallenge suggests that the immunologic barrier for complete protection may be comparatively low, such that vaccines with acceptable safety may have desirable efficacy even if they are not highly immunogenic.
Evidence: All three animals were productively infected with MR766, with detectable plasma viremia one day post inoculation (dpi) in two of the three animals and in all three by two dpi ... By ten dpi, plasma viral loads were undetectable in all three animals.
vRNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.

Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

Including the in-frame four-amino-acid deletion, MR766 is ~96% amino acid identical in the E protein to Asian ZIKV isolates (data not shown). In contrast, Asian ZIKV isolates are more than 99% amino acid identical to one another in envelope, typically differing by only 0–2 amino acids.
293.Rationale: The passage documents that prior infection induced immune responses (including measurable nAb titers) that protected animals from detectable viremia on heterologous rechallenge. However, the claim that the "immunologic barrier" is "comparatively low" and that vaccines with modest immunogenicity will be effective is speculative and not directly supported by the experimental data. The study reports protection following natural infection and associated nAb titers, but it does not define minimal protective thresholds for antibodies, nor does it evaluate vaccine formulations with varying immunogenicity or safety profiles. Therefore the specific inference about vaccine efficacy at lower immunogenicity cannot be derived solely from the passage.

293.Option: The protection from detectable viremia observed in animals previously infected with African ZIKV suggests that immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against all Asian ZIKV strains, similar to what has been described for YFV, TBEV, and JEV.
Evidence: All of the ZIKV strains circulating in the Western hemisphere are Asian lineage. The E protein amino acid identity among all Asian lineage ZIKVs is >99%, and as a group, these are only ~96% and 97% amino acid identical to representative East African and West African viruses, respectively [21].

Therefore, we hypothesized that if immunity elicited by infection of macaques with MR766 protects against reinfection with Asian ZIKV infection, immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against subsequent Asian ZIKV reinfection.

To investigate the breadth of protective ZIKV immunity between heterologous lineages of the virus, we infected three macaques with the African prototype strain of ZIKV, MR766 [6]. ... Immune responses against MR766 provided protection against viremia in all three macaques when re-challenged with an Asian ZIKV strain and dose that productively infected 8/8 naïve macaques.
vRNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
293.Rationale: The passage shows that infection with the genetically divergent East African MR766 strain protected macaques from detectable viremia following heterologous challenge with an Asian-lineage strain that reliably infects naïve animals. The authors note that Asian-lineage E proteins are >99% identical to one another and explicitly state the hypothesis that immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should suffice to protect against subsequent Asian ZIKV reinfection. Taken together (cross-lineage protection demonstrated experimentally, high sequence identity among Asian strains, and the authors' stated hypothesis), it is a derivable epidemiological inference from the Passage Body that immunity elicited by an Asian ZIKV infection would likely confer protection across Asian ZIKV strains. (The comparison to other flaviviruses is presented as analogy in the option but the core inference about intra-Asian cross-protection is directly supported by the passage.)

294.Option: Mebendazole treatment administered during pregnancy may have masked the true effect of prenatal helminth infection on infant cognitive development due to high cure rates and reduced reinfection.
Evidence: Women were also given a total of 600 mg of mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) at the first ANC visit and again if they were tested positive for helminths at second ANC visit. Intake of mebendazole was not directly monitored.

Pregnant women who were infected with helminths at least, once during pregnancy had children with poorer ELC scores, thus-4.4 (95% CI: -7.2 to-1.5) compared to those of mothers who were never infected during pregnancy after adjustment (see Table 5).

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028). We observed that mothers who were infected with hookworms during the first ANC visit had children who scored less in the gross motor scale, -4.9 (95% CI: -8.6 to-1.1), compared to those whose mothers were never infected with hookworms at first ANC visit.
294.Rationale: The passage documents that mebendazole was administered during ANC and that intake was not directly monitored. However, the option asserts masking occurred because of "high cure rates and reduced reinfection," attributes (high cure rates, reduced reinfection) that are not stated or supported in the passage. Moreover, despite mebendazole being given, the study still observed statistically significant associations between prenatal helminth infection and poorer infant ELC and gross motor scores. The passage therefore does not provide evidence that treatment definitively masked the true effect; the claim about high cure rates and reduced reinfection is external and not derivable from the provided text.

While the study adjusted for several confounders and performed sensitivity analyses for some infant characteristics (preterm, LBW, weight-for-age), the specific claim of independence from infant helminth infection is not supported by the provided text. 295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region indicates that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks of Bunyamwera fever in East Africa.

295.Rationale: The passage documents detection of Bunyamwera virus in An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus reared from larvae in Homa Bay County, but it provides no data on human case attribution, vectorial capacity, biting behavior, infection rates in adult field-caught mosquitoes, or outbreak investigations linking these species to human outbreaks. Detecting the virus in these species does not justify the stronger claim that they are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks in East Africa; that inference is not supported by the presented evidence.

295.Option: The vertical transmission of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus indicates that these mosquito species are more likely to transmit the virus to humans than other known vectors in the region.
Evidence: Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: While the study shows evidence consistent with vertical transmission in these species (detection in adults reared from larvae), it does not compare transmission efficiency or human infection risk across vector species. The passage gives no data on relative vector competence, host biting rates, field infection prevalence in adults, or human exposure, so one cannot infer that these species are more likely than other regional vectors to transmit Bunyamwera virus to humans.

295.Option: The presence of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae suggests that the virus is primarily maintained in the environment through contamination of aquatic breeding sites rather than through vertical transmission.

To investigate how vertical transmission in different mosquito species in Homa Bay and Baringo counties of Kenya may be maintaining endemic arboviruses in circulation, we set out to identify arboviral infections in laboratory-reared adults of field-caught larvae and pupae.
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.
It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing ... from female Anopheles gambiae ... and Cx. univittatus ... that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The authors explicitly framed the study to detect vertical transmission and report Bunyamwera virus as a 'vertically transmitted arbovirus' found in adults reared from larvae. The data support vertical transmission (presence in adults that developed from field-collected immatures), not the alternative hypothesis that the signal primarily reflects contamination of aquatic breeding sites. The passage does not present evidence that breeding-site contamination, rather than vertical transmission, is the main maintenance mechanism; thus the option is not supported by the study findings.

295.Option: The absence of Bunyamwera virus in Baringo County samples suggests that the virus is not capable of persisting in mosquito populations outside the Lake Victoria region due to climatic or ecological differences.
Evidence: However, no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus was detected in Baringo County samples.
