Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The longitudinal data show that 16 of 18 patients (≈89%) had significant decreases in urine IgG by >180 days and that 16/18 were entirely negative on the dipstick by six months. This proportion (~90%) supports the statement that clearance occurs in ~90% within six months, and the authors explicitly state the potential of urine assays as a test of clinical cure. Therefore the inference is directly supported by the passage.

299.Option: Urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests are superior to serum-based rK39-RDTs for diagnosing VL in all endemic regions, including East Africa, due to their higher sensitivity and specificity.
Evidence: The test showed positivity in 95 of 97 VL cases, yielding a sensitivity of 97.94% for detection of Leishmania exposure.
Overall specificity was calculated using 75 non-VL urine samples of controls and found 100% specificity without any false positives.

Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
In comparison to urine dipstick test, serum-based rK39-RDT showed 95.87% sensitivity (93/97) and 97.33% specificity (73/75).
However, their diagnostic performance varies from moderate in Latin America to even low in East African region
299.Rationale: While the study shows urine assays had higher sensitivity and specificity than rK39-RDT in this study population, the passage does not present data from all endemic regions and explicitly notes rK39 performance varies geographically (including low performance in East Africa). The blanket claim of superiority 'in all endemic regions, including East Africa' is not supported by the presented data and overgeneralizes beyond the study sample. Therefore this option is not derivable from the passage.

299.Option: The dipstick test's high specificity and sensitivity in detecting VL and PKDL make it a reliable tool for diagnosing relapse in patients who remain positive after six months of treatment.
Evidence: Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
All 14 PKDL urine samples tested had positive results in dipstick assay thus showing 100% sensitivity of the test.

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
299.Rationale: The passage documents excellent diagnostic performance of the dipstick and notes two patients remained positive at six months. However, it does not provide evidence that persistent dipstick positivity at six months represents relapse (as opposed to delayed serologic decline), nor does it evaluate the test's ability to distinguish relapse from cured but antibody-persistent states. Therefore the specific inference that the dipstick is a reliable tool to diagnose relapse cannot be derived from the provided data.

299.Option: The delayed decline in urine antibody titres after treatment suggests that the immune response to Leishmania is slower to resolve in urine than in serum, indicating a longer duration of immune activity in the urinary system.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].

There is no significant decline in the titre just after completing the treatment (day 30). Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
299.Rationale: The passage states that serum antibodies can remain for years after treatment, whereas urine antibody titres in this study declined significantly by six months in most patients. The option's claim that urine antibodies resolve more slowly than serum is contradicted by the passage (serum persistence is described as lasting years). The passage provides no data showing urine antibodies persist longer than serum, so this inference is not supported.

299.Option: The persistence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in serum for several years after treatment makes serum-based tests unsuitable for monitoring treatment response, but urine-based tests overcome this limitation by detecting antibodies only during active infection.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].
Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180).
Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The passage supports the first clause: serum antibodies can persist for years and serum antibody tests are unsuitable as tests of cure. The passage also provides evidence that urine antibody levels fall by six months in most patients and that urine assays show potential as a test of cure. However, the stronger claim that urine-based tests 'detect antibodies only during active infection' is not supported: two patients remained positive at six months, and the study does not demonstrate that urine antibodies are present exclusively during active infection or absent for long-term post-treatment. Therefore the absolute claim in the second clause is not derivable from the passage.

300.Option: The fact that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs indicates that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs, suggesting that community-based dog adoption programs could be implemented without significant resistance.
Evidence: Dog owners who adopted their pets “off the street” were less likely to get their dogs vaccinated than those who either purchased them or were given them (OR 0.08, 95%CI 0.01–0.4, p = 0.001).
Dog-owners who had a negative perception of FRD were less likely to seek veterinary attention (OR 0.3, 95%CI 0.1–1, p = 0.047) for their pets.
300.Rationale: The passage does not provide any data stating that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs; that specific statistic is absent. Moreover, the quoted evidence shows that owners who adopted dogs 'off the street' were less likely to vaccinate them, and owners with negative perceptions were less likely to seek veterinary care. These findings do not support the strong conclusion that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs nor that community-based adoption programs would face no resistance. Therefore the option is not derivable from the passage.

300.Option: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level had more positive attitudes towards free-roaming dogs, including being more likely to feed them and take injured dogs to a veterinarian, which could be leveraged to promote responsible dog ownership and population management.
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
300.Rationale: The passage documents statistically significant, more positive attitudes and helping behaviors among high/middle socio-economic respondents (perceiving FRD as useful, feeding them, taking injured dogs to a vet). From an epidemiological and public-health planning perspective, these groups represent a strategic opportunity to engage and leverage existing positive attitudes to promote responsible ownership and FRD population management. This inference follows directly from the observed differences reported in the passage.

300.Option: Because younger respondents were less likely to view free-roaming dogs as a health threat, public health campaigns should prioritize youth education to reduce fear and promote coexistence with stray dogs.
Evidence: The younger respondents (≤34 years) did not consider FRD a threat to human health (OR 0.2, 95% CI 0.04–0.97, p = 0.05), and were more likely to feed them (OR 2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.5, p = 0.04) than older participants.
300.Rationale: The passage shows younger respondents are already less likely to view FRD as a threat and are more likely to feed them. The option's recommendation to prioritize youth education to reduce fear is inconsistent with the evidence (younger people already have lower fear). While youth-targeted education could be justified for other objectives (e.g., responsible feeding), the specific claim about reducing fear and promoting coexistence cannot be derived from the passage because youth do not exhibit the indicated problem (excessive fear).

300.Option: The lack of significant factors influencing favorable practices for rabies elimination suggests that community-wide interventions should focus on improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis rather than changing attitudes toward free-roaming dogs.
Evidence: Pet ownership (p = 0.11), dog ownership (p = 0.1), and poultry ownership (p = 0.19) were offered to the multivariable logistic regression model. A stable multivariate logistic regression model with significant p-values could not be generated.

there is a lack of awareness by the rural population about rabies control programmes [20]. Paucity of activities that can transfer knowledge about the disease to the rural population is a key concern for policy makers [21] and the importance of epidemiological studies to assess the awareness level and practices of people regarding aspects of rabies control is paramount in this context [12, 20, 22].
300.Rationale: The passage does report that a stable multivariable model with significant predictors for attitudes/practices could not be generated, indicating limited statistically significant associations in that analysis. However, the recommendation to prioritize improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) over changing attitudes toward FRD is not directly supported or tested by the study data. While supply and awareness issues are discussed elsewhere in the background, the specific strategic shift toward PEP access instead of attitude change is an interpretation beyond what the measured associations in this survey can justify. Therefore this conclusion is not directly derivable from the passage.

300.Option: Since poultry owners were more likely to perceive free-roaming dogs as a threat due to attacks on backyard poultry, targeted rabies awareness campaigns should focus on livestock-owning households to improve preventive practices.
Evidence: A significant number of poultry owners reported that FRD attacked their backyard poultry for food (OR 3.2, 95%CI 1.07–12.1, p = 0.02).
households that owned poultry were found less likely to report the presence of a rabid dog to the authorities (OR 0.3, 95%CI 0.13–0.7, p = 0.01).
300.Rationale: The passage documents that poultry owners both experienced attacks by FRD and were less likely to report rabid dogs. These observed differences identify livestock-owning households—particularly poultry owners—as a subgroup with direct exposure and potentially suboptimal preventive practices. From an epidemiological program-planning perspective, focusing targeted rabies awareness and prevention efforts on these households is a logical, evidence-based strategic opportunity that can be directly derived from the findings presented.

301.Option: The detection of Rickettsia felis DNA in the cerebrospinal fluid of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease, combined with the absence of other identifiable pathogens and the presence of rickettsial-like CSF findings, suggests that R. felis may be a causative agent of severe neurological disease in humans.
Evidence: we describe the identification of R. felis from the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease in North Sulawesi, Indonesia.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis (Table 1). Amplicons were confirmed to be R. felis by Sanger sequencing (Figs 1–3).
All CSF and serum samples were negative for Leptospira and O. tsutsugamushi by qPCR.
Retroactive testing for virus was negative for both [12].
Blood cultures were negative for both cases.
TB meningitis was initially suspected for both cases but neither CSF was positive by acid-fast bacilli stain and neither responded to presumptive TB treatment.
Case 1 ... he was found to have CSF mononuclear pleocytosis.
301.Rationale: The passage documents molecular identification of R. felis DNA in the CSF of two fatal cases and confirms species-level identification by sequencing, while routine testing for other bacterial, rickettsial (other species), leptospiral, and viral pathogens was negative and blood cultures were negative. TB meningitis was considered but AFB stains were negative and patients did not respond to TB therapy. One case had CSF mononuclear pleocytosis and meningeal enhancement, consistent with CNS infection. Taken together, these findings support the inference that R. felis may have been the causative agent of the severe neurological disease in these two patients (i.e., the data are consistent with R. felis as a potential cause), making the option a supported epidemiological conclusion. The statement is appropriately cautious (“may be a causative agent”) and does not claim definitive proof beyond the presented evidence.

301.Option: The seasonal co-circulation of Rickettsia felis with other rickettsial pathogens in Southeast Asia may compound healthcare system burden, particularly in pediatric intensive care units.
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
Since the description of the first human case in 1994 [5] R. felis has been increasingly identified worldwide as a cause of disease [6–8].
Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
301.Rationale: The passage describes R. felis ecology and increasing recognition but provides no data or statements about seasonality, co-circulation with other rickettsial pathogens, or impacts on healthcare system burden or pediatric intensive care units. The quoted text explicitly states that transmission mechanisms are unclear and does not address seasonal patterns or healthcare utilization. Therefore the specific claim about seasonal co-circulation compounding healthcare burden—particularly in pediatric ICUs—is not supported by the presented evidence and cannot be derived from the passage.

301.Option: The 100% sequence identity of the 17-kDa and ompB genes between the Indonesian cases and the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 confirms that R. felis is a zoonotic pathogen with a stable transmission cycle in human populations.
Evidence: Sequence homologies between Case 1 (patient E-059) and Case 2 (patient E-063) of 17-kDa, ompB, and gltA were, respectively, 100%, 100%, and 99.91%. The 17-kDa and ompB sequences were 100% identical to the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 [3], and gltA was 99.91% homologous.

It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
301.Rationale: While the molecular data show that the isolates from these two cases are genetically identical (or nearly so) to the R. felis reference strain, sequence identity alone does not establish broader epidemiological conclusions about zoonosis dynamics or a stable transmission cycle in human populations. The passage itself notes that transmission mechanisms and the role of vertebrate reservoirs are still unclear. Thus, confirmation of gene identity supports species identification but does not 'confirm' zoonotic status or stable human transmission cycles; those conclusions would require additional ecological and epidemiological evidence not provided here.

301.Option: The absence of skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy in both cases suggests that Rickettsia felis infection typically presents without classical rickettsial signs.
Evidence: Neither patient was noted on the charts to have skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy.
Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
301.Rationale: The passage records that these two fatal cases lacked skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy, but this is data from only two patients. The statement 'typically presents without classical rickettsial signs' generalizes beyond the small number of cases and contradicts the more cautious description that R. felis 'typically... presents as a mild, febrile illness' that can be confused with other fevers. The passage does not provide population-level clinical data to support the claim that absence of classical signs is the usual presentation, so the option overextends the evidence and is not supported.

301.Option: The failure of standard TB therapy in both cases, combined with negative acid-fast staining, provides definitive evidence that Rickettsia felis is the primary cause of death.
Evidence: TB meningitis was initially suspected for both cases but neither CSF was positive by acid-fast bacilli stain and neither responded to presumptive TB treatment.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis (Table 1). Amplicons were confirmed to be R. felis by Sanger sequencing (Figs 1–3).
301.Rationale: Although TB was suspected and empiric TB treatment did not produce a response, and R. felis DNA was detected in CSF, the passage does not provide definitive proof that R. felis was the primary cause of death. Definitive causation would require additional evidence (e.g., isolation of viable organisms, pathology consistent with rickettsial infection, exclusion of all other causes, temporal causality, or epidemiologic linkage). The passage itself notes limitations (e.g., culture capacity for TB was not available) and reports molecular detection consistent with R. felis, but this is insufficient to claim 'definitive evidence' of causation. Therefore the option's assertion of definitive proof is not supported by the data presented.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study, despite high-quality ICU care, suggests that age-related physiological decline and comorbidities are major contributors to poor outcomes.
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.

Old patients with DF are often associated with atypical presentations, longer hospitalization and more underlying diseases [14]. In particular, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and aging are associated with a higher risk of DHF/DSS and a worse outcome [6, 15–18].
The mean age of the 75 patients was 72.3±9.3 years (ranging from 50.7 to 88.5 years, Table 1).
The most common comorbidities included hypertension (72.0%), diabetes (43.7%), and chronic kidney disease (22.7%).
The rates of underlying diseases and conditions necessitating blood component transfusions were similar between the survivors and non-survivors.
302.Rationale: While the passage documents a high mortality (41.3%), advanced age, and a high prevalence of comorbidities in the cohort and cites literature linking aging and comorbidities to worse dengue outcomes, the study data do not demonstrate that age-related decline or comorbidities were the major contributors to mortality in this ICU cohort. Specifically, the authors report that "The rates of underlying diseases ... were similar between the survivors and non-survivors," and multivariate predictors of death did not include age or baseline comorbidities. The option also asserts "despite high-quality ICU care," but the passage does not explicitly state the quality of ICU care. Therefore this causal inference is not supported by the presented data.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in elderly patients with severe dengue is due to the high prevalence of bloodstream infections, particularly late-onset candidemia, which contributed significantly to mortality.
Evidence: Nineteen bacterial and three fungal pathogens were identified in the bloodstream of 18 (23.7%) patients.
Two patients experienced recurrent fever at the eighth and ninth day after the onset of DF, respectively, which was caused by candidemia.

The leading cause of fatality was multiple organ failure (14/31, 45.2%), and nine of these 14 patients had overt bleeding. Seven (22.6%) patients died of refractory shock and severe metabolic acidosis, six (19.4%) intracranial hemorrhage, three (9.6%) bloodstream infections, and one (3.2%) pneumonia with respiratory failure.
302.Rationale: Although bloodstream infections occurred in 23.7% of patients and late candidemia was observed in two patients, the passage shows that only 3 of 31 deaths (9.6%) were attributed to bloodstream infections. The majority of deaths were due to multiple organ failure, refractory shock, and intracranial hemorrhage. Thus, the data do not support the claim that bloodstream infections—particularly late candidemia—were the primary drivers of the high case fatality rate.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study is primarily due to delayed access to medical care and inadequate fluid resuscitation, as evidenced by the high rate of cardiac arrest before ICU admission.
Evidence: Seven (9.3%) patients experienced cardiac arrest before ICU admission.

cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]) ... were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s.
302.Rationale: The passage shows that cardiac arrest before ICU admission was an independent predictor of death (HR 6.26), but only 7 patients (9.3%) experienced pre-ICU cardiac arrest. The authors do not provide any data linking cardiac arrest to delayed access to care or inadequate fluid resuscitation, nor do they state that delayed care or inadequate resuscitation were common. Therefore, attributing the high overall case fatality primarily to delayed access or inadequate fluid resuscitation is not supported by the presented evidence.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this cohort is attributable to the limited availability of invasive life-supporting interventions such as hemodialysis and endotracheal intubation for elderly patients in Taiwan, which restricts their recovery potential.
Evidence: Approximately three-quarters (57, 76.0%) of all patients required ventilator support during the ICU stay.
At the ICU admission, 41 (54.7%) patients had AKI, according to the KDIGO criteria and 12 (29.3%) of whom needed renal replacement therapy during the ICU stay.
Four (5.3%) patients had end-stage renal disease and received maintenance dialysis.
302.Rationale: The passage documents that invasive life-supporting interventions were in fact used frequently: 76% required ventilator support and nearly 29.3% received renal replacement therapy during the ICU stay. There is no statement indicating limited availability of such interventions or that scarcity contributed to mortality. Thus the option's attribution to limited availability of interventions is contradicted by the presented data and is unsupported.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this elderly cohort is primarily driven by the high APACHE II and SOFA scores, which reflect the severity of organ failure and are the most significant predictors of mortality.
Evidence: Median APACHE II score and SOFA score at the ICU admission was 20 and 11, respectively.
In the univariate Cox model, ... a high APACHE II or SOFA score ... were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality ...

APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest.
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
302.Rationale: Although APACHE II and SOFA scores were associated with mortality in univariate analysis and had high AUCs, the multivariate analysis identified cardiac arrest before ICU admission, AKI at ICU admission, and prolonged APTT as independent predictors. The authors explicitly state APACHE II and SOFA were excluded from the final model due to collinearity and were not the independent predictors in this dataset. Therefore, claiming the high case fatality is primarily driven by APACHE II and SOFA scores overstates what can be inferred from the presented analyses.

303.Option: The availability of miltefosine in East Africa for visceral leishmaniasis treatment creates a strong foundation for immediate implementation of miltefosine as a first-line therapy for L aethiopica without further clinical evaluation.
Evidence: Miltefosine has been successfully used in more than 50 CL cases due to L aethiopica in Addis Ababa, but the findings have not been published (personal communication, Asrat Hailu).
The evidence base remains extremely limited.
randomized clinical trials are highly needed and should be undertaken.
303.Rationale: The passage notes that miltefosine has been used in a series of cases in Addis Ababa but explicitly states those findings are unpublished. More importantly, the passage repeatedly emphasizes that "the evidence base remains extremely limited" and that "randomized clinical trials are highly needed and should be undertaken." From these statements it does not follow that there is sufficient evidence to immediately implement miltefosine as first-line therapy without further clinical evaluation. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

303.Option: The national and WHO guidelines recommend intramuscular paromomycin as the preferential first-line treatment for LCL caused by L aethiopica, supported by strong clinical evidence from well-designed trials.
Evidence: Paromomycin ointment was highly effective in three Ethiopians treated in Israel.
The evidence base remains extremely limited.

Out of a total of 95 studies or reports identified and screened, only 27 provided information relevant to our topic ... This comprised 13 case series and seven case reports, generally clinical information collected as part of clinical practice.
Only two small clinical trials were identified
303.Rationale: The passage provides only small, observational reports about paromomycin (e.g., three treated in Israel) and emphasizes an overall very limited evidence base with few clinical trials. There is no mention of national or WHO guideline recommendations, nor of strong evidence from well-designed trials supporting intramuscular paromomycin as preferential first-line therapy. Thus this claim cannot be derived from the passage.

303.Option: The high genetic diversity of L aethiopica and its frequent association with mucocutaneous and diffuse forms suggest that treatment outcomes are inherently unpredictable and that no standardized therapy can be developed.
Evidence: Clinical manifestations of L aethiopica are particularly diverse and pleotropic, and a high genetic diversity has been documented as well [12].
Localized CL (LCL) is the most frequent manifestation, while mucocutaneous (MCL) and diffuse CL (DCL) is relatively common [7].
DCL is notorious for its chronic and progressive course and non-responsiveness to the common antileishmanial drugs.
303.Rationale: The passage documents high genetic diversity and the relatively common occurrence of MCL and DCL and notes that DCL is often non-responsive to common drugs. However, the stronger claim that "treatment outcomes are inherently unpredictable" and that "no standardized therapy can be developed" is not supported by the passage. The passage highlights limited evidence and treatment challenges, but it does not logically imply that standardized therapies are impossible to develop. Therefore the option overstates what can be inferred.

303.Option: Despite the lack of high-quality clinical trials, the consistent use of antimonials in clinical practice in Ethiopia indicates that their efficacy and safety have been well-established through long-term observational use.
Evidence: Most commonly used drugs were antimonials (n = 201), pentamidine (n = 150) and cryotherapy (n = 103).
In vitro data generally suggested a low susceptibility of L aethiopica to antimonials (Table 5).
Outcomes with systemic antimonials were reported in 38 cases, of which only nine demonstrated clinical response.
Antimonials were usually not found effective, but better outcomes were generally observed with pentamidine.
303.Rationale: While antimonials have been frequently used in practice, the passage provides evidence of poor in vitro susceptibility and limited clinical response (only nine of 38 cases responding), and explicitly notes antimonials were often not effective. The overall tone is that efficacy and safety are not well-established by quality evidence. Therefore it is not supported to infer that their efficacy and safety have been well-established through observational use.

303.Option: The evidence base for treatment of cutaneous leishmaniasis due to L aethiopica remains extremely limited, with most studies being small, non-randomized, and lacking rigorous methodology, despite the availability of certain drugs in clinical practice.
Evidence: In terms of treatment of L aethiopica, the evidence base remains extremely limited.

Out of a total of 95 studies or reports identified and screened, only 27 provided information relevant to our topic ... This comprised 13 case series and seven case reports, generally clinical information collected as part of clinical practice.
Only two small clinical trials were identified

Most studies had obvious limitations, beyond the descriptive nature. Definitions of treatment outcomes varied across studies and were often not clearly defined. Sample size was often small, follow-up short and information on patient characteristics often limited. In formal quality assessment, the non-randomized studies performed poorly, with a median score of three (maximum score is eight),
303.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the evidence base is extremely limited and describes the study corpus: mainly case series and case reports, only two small clinical trials, and widespread methodological limitations (variable outcome definitions, small sample sizes, short follow-up, poor quality scores). It also documents that certain drugs (antimonials, pentamidine, cryotherapy, miltefosine, paromomycin, amphotericin B) have been used in practice. Therefore the option accurately summarizes the state of clinical research as presented in the passage.

305.Option: The lack of association between IL18 haplotypes and infection risk suggests that the functional effects of the gene are not relevant in the immune response to T. cruzi.
Evidence: Five possible haplotypes were observed (rs2043055|rs187238|rs1946518|rs360719: TCAC, CGCT, TGCT, TGAT and CGAT), with the haplotypes TCAC and CGCT showing the higher frequencies (37.30% and 37.40%, respectively).

In relation to the seronegative vs seropositive analysis, the frequency of TCAC was increased in the former individuals, being this difference statistically significant (P = 1.50E-03, PFDR = 7.50E-03, OR = 0.76, CI = 0.65–0.90). On the other hand, the frequency of CGCT was significantly lower in seronegative individuals compared with seropositive individuals (P = 0.0116, PFDR = 0.0290, OR = 1.25, CI = 1.05–1.47), whereas the frequencies of TGCT, TGAT, and CGAT did not differed significantly between seropositive and seronegative individuals.

However, the haplotype model did not better explain the IL18 association to risk of infection than the model considering the SNPs independently (likelihood P-value = 0.1454), indicating no additive effects (that is, the associated haplotypes were a consequence of the independent associations of the considered variants).
305.Rationale: The premise of the option is that there is a lack of haplotype association and that this implies IL18 functional effects are irrelevant. The passage shows that certain haplotypes (TCAC and CGCT) do differ significantly between seronegative and seropositive individuals, so there is haplotype-level association. The authors further state that the haplotype model does not better explain the association than individual SNPs, meaning the association exists but is attributable to individual variant effects rather than additive haplotype effects. That does not justify concluding IL18 functional effects are irrelevant to immune response; rather, it indicates the association is driven by individual SNPs. Therefore the option overinterprets the haplotype-model result and is not supported by the passage.

305.Option: The promoter polymorphism rs360719 is likely the causal variant underlying the association between IL18 gene variation and susceptibility to infection by T. cruzi in the Colombian population.
Evidence: rs187238*C and rs360719*C were significantly increased in seronegative individuals compared to the seropositive subset (P = 2.18E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.77, CI = 0.65–0.91; and P = 1.49E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 0.76, CI = 0.64–0.90; respectively), thus suggesting that these variants are associated to protection against infection by T. cruzi.
an additional promoter variant (rs360719) that has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1 [28];

The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it, and a trend was maintained when conditioning rs360719 on these two latter variants (Table 2).
305.Rationale: The passage reports a significant protective association for rs360719 and explicitly states that conditional logistic regression pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant because the significance of other associated SNPs was lost after conditioning on rs360719. The variant is a promoter polymorphism with a reported interaction with transcription factor OCT-1, supporting a plausible functional role. Therefore the inference that rs360719 is likely the causal variant underlying the IL18 association with susceptibility to T. cruzi is directly supported by the data and analyses presented.

305.Option: The observed association between IL18 variants and infection susceptibility is likely due to population stratification in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: The population in this region of Colombia is a homogeneous mixture, with no specific concentration of any ethnicity.
The six IL18 SNPs were in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in all the analyzed subgroups (P>0.01), suggesting that a possible inbreed in Guanentina and Comunera provinces is not likely.

The genotyping success rate was over 95% and the allele frequencies in all cases were similar to those described for the Colombian population (CLM) of the 1000 genomes phase III project (http://www.1000genomes.org) [30].

The marked difference of the average age between the different subgroups of patients (i.e., seronegative and seropositive individuals) could represent a limitation in this study... To control for this possible confounding factor, we decided to perform a logistic regression analysis accordingly with the serological status using age as covariate. The statistical significance was maintained in this analysis thus supporting the consistency of our results (Table B in S1 Text).
305.Rationale: The option asserts that observed associations are likely due to population stratification. The passage argues against this: it describes the study population as a homogeneous mixture without concentrated ethnic groups, reports SNPs in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, allele frequencies similar to reference Colombian data, and shows adjustment for age did not change significance. The authors provide no evidence pointing to population stratification as the cause, so the claim is not supported by the passage.

305.Option: The functional role of rs360719 in IL-18 expression is likely to be independent of the transcription factor OCT-1, based on the observed genetic associations.
Evidence: an additional promoter variant (rs360719) that has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1 [28];

The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it, and a trend was maintained when conditioning rs360719 on these two latter variants (Table 2).
305.Rationale: The option claims rs360719's functional role is likely independent of OCT-1. The passage explicitly states rs360719 "has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1," so there is prior functional evidence of interaction. The genetic association and conditional analyses identify rs360719 as a likely causal variant, but they do not provide any evidence that its effect is independent of OCT-1; no mechanistic experiments or analyses addressing OCT-1 dependence are presented. Therefore the statement of independence is unsupported by the passage.

305.Option: The IL18 gene variants rs2043055 and rs1946518 are independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: rs2043055*C and rs1946518*C were reduced in the seronegative sample set in comparison with the seropositive one (P = 2.52E-03, PFDR = 5.04E-03, OR = 1.29, CI = 1.10–1.53; and P = 0.0162, PFDR = 0.0243, OR1.22, CI = 1.04–1.44; respectively), indicating that they are associated with a higher infection risk.

Subsequently, in order to investigate the possible association between IL18 and chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy, we compared the allelic and genotypic frequencies of the IL18 SNPs between seropositive asymptomatic individuals and chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy individuals (Table 3). No statistically significant differences were observed between asymptomatic individuals and chronic Chagasic cardiomyopathy patients for any of the analyzed polymorphisms.

In relation to the haplotype analysis according to the presence/absence of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy and/or to the progression of cardiomyopathy, no statistically significant differences among different subgroups of individuals were observed (Tables D and E in S1 Text).
305.Rationale: While rs2043055 and rs1946518 are associated with increased risk of T. cruzi infection, the passage explicitly reports that no statistically significant differences were observed between asymptomatic and chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy patients for any analyzed IL18 polymorphisms, and no haplotype associations with cardiomyopathy were found. Thus there is no evidence in the passage that these variants are independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy.

306.Option: Most of the few remaining infected individuals in Kyela district had been positive for circulating filarial antigen before the mass drug administration programme was conducted, indicating that the treatment was effective in reducing the adult worm burden but not fully eliminating persistent infections.
Evidence: Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).
The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.

In 2019, 1299 of previous EMINI subjects who participated in the RHINO study were screened for the presence of CFA as a marker of an active filarial infection. The overall LF prevalence among these participants was 1.7% (22/1299) which was significantly lower than the measurement pre- and two years post-introduction of MDA (p<0.0001).
306.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that 18 of the 22 individuals positive in 2019 had been positive in earlier (2009/2010–11) surveys, i.e., most remaining infections were persistent rather than newly acquired. At the same time, antigen levels (ODs) among positives fell substantially (mean OD fell from >1.0 range to 0.49 in 2019 and no high/medium positives remained), indicating reduced adult worm burden. Because some individuals remained CFA-positive despite lowered antigen levels and reduced prevalence, the correct inference is that MDA markedly reduced adult worm burden but did not completely eliminate all persistent infections.

306.Option: The four new infections observed in 2019 indicate that the mass drug administration programme in Kyela district was ineffective in preventing new Wuchereria bancrofti infections over the ten-year period.
Evidence: From 2011 to 2019, four new infections occurred among 947 previously uninfected individuals, leading to an incidence of 0.05 per 100 person years, showing a 27-fold reduction in new infections.

In 2019, 1299 of previous EMINI subjects who participated in the RHINO study were screened for the presence of CFA as a marker of an active filarial infection. The overall LF prevalence among these participants was 1.7% (22/1299) which was significantly lower than the measurement pre- and two years post-introduction of MDA (p<0.0001).
306.Rationale: The passage reports only four new infections over eight years among nearly 947 previously uninfected people, corresponding to a very low incidence (0.05 per 100 person-years) and explicitly states this represents a 27-fold reduction in new infections. These data indicate the programme greatly reduced new infections; the presence of four new cases does not support the conclusion that the programme was ineffective at preventing new infections.

306.Option: The higher prevalence of LF in males and older individuals in 2019 indicates that the mass drug administration programme disproportionately failed to reach these demographic groups.
Evidence: Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study (Table 3).
Of the 22 infected individuals in 2019, 5 were women and 17 were men... Of the 17 infected men, 15 were farmers (S2 Fig).

The LF prevalence defined by the level of circulating filarial antigens was measured by Trop Bio ELISA in the two EMINI surveys (2009 and 2010/11) and the RHINO study (2019) in individuals 14 to 65 years in Kyela district. LF prevalence is higher in male compared with female participants in all three surveys.
306.Rationale: The passage documents that infection in 2019 was associated with male sex and older age and gives counts showing more men infected. However, it does not provide evidence about differential program coverage, adherence, or reach by demographic group. The observed residual higher prevalence in males and older people could reflect baseline differences in exposure, pre-existing infection longevity, occupation (e.g., farming), or biological susceptibility rather than differential MDA delivery. Therefore the specific inference that MDA disproportionately failed to reach these groups is not supported by the passage.

306.Option: The significant reduction in the mean OD values of CFA-positive individuals over time suggests that the mass drug administration programme has led to a complete eradication of adult Wuchereria bancrofti worms in the Kyela district population.
Evidence: The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.
follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).

In 2019, 1299 of previous EMINI subjects who participated in the RHINO study were screened for the presence of CFA as a marker of an active filarial infection. The overall LF prevalence among these participants was 1.7% (22/1299) which was significantly lower than the measurement pre- and two years post-introduction of MDA (p<0.0001).

Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
306.Rationale: While the passage shows substantial reductions in antigen levels and prevalence, it also documents that 22 individuals remained CFA-positive in 2019 (including 18 persistent positives and 4 new infections). The presence of ongoing low-level positivity and new infections contradicts the claim of complete eradication of adult worms. Therefore the conclusion of complete eradication is not supported.

306.Option: The persistence of CFA positivity in 18 individuals who were previously infected suggests that the mass drug administration programme failed to reduce adult worm burden in the Kyela district population.
Evidence: Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
follow-up in 2019 revealed no high or medium positive values, but rather all 22 CFA positive individuals had low positive results (Fig 3).
Most filarial infected individuals in 2009 showed high positive results, reflecting a high adult worm burden. In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.
Despite being still positive a significant drop of the OD was noted.
306.Rationale: Although 18 individuals who were previously positive remained CFA-positive in 2019, the passage reports that their antigen (OD) levels decreased markedly and that no high- or medium-positive results remained—only low positives were detected. These data indicate that MDA reduced adult worm burden even among persistent infections. Therefore the statement that the programme failed to reduce adult worm burden is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas of the DRC suggests that leptospirosis is primarily an urban zoonotic disease driven by poor sanitation and overcrowding, with transmission linked to flooding and exposure to contaminated water.
Evidence: Fifty-two of the positive leptospiral IgM cases (59%) lived in an urban setting; and the odds of leptospiral IgM cases were higher in those living in urban compared to rural areas (Crude OR 3.39; 95% CI 2.18–5.28).
Of the 88 positive leptospiral IgM cases, 50 cases (57%) lived in Kinshasa province.
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).

The highest positivity rate of Leptospira IgM was found in the 20–29 years age group (42%) in the urban areas, while the highest positivity rate in the rural areas was found in the 0–9 years age group (36%).
307.Rationale: While the passage provides evidence that a majority of detected IgM-positive cases lived in urban areas and that urban residence had higher odds of IgM positivity, it does not establish that leptospirosis is primarily an urban disease driven specifically by poor sanitation and overcrowding. The passage mentions environmental factors (including flooding and rapid urbanization) that could facilitate transmission, but it also shows non-negligible rural cases (including a high positivity in 0–9 year olds in rural areas). The causal attribution to poor sanitation and overcrowding and the claim that the disease is primarily urban go beyond the data presented and are not directly supported by the quoted evidence.

307.Option: The seasonal distribution of leptospiral IgM positivity, with 79% of cases occurring during the rainy season, indicates that leptospirosis transmission in the DRC is strongly tied to climatic patterns and should be prioritized during rainy season surveillance campaigns.
Evidence: Seventy-nine percent of the IgM positive cases occurred during the rainy season.
In both urban and rural areas, most of the cases occurred during the rainy season.
the seasonal distribution of case group became stronger in effect size as well as statistically significant (Adjusted OR 1.94; 95% CI 1.03–3.65).
the odds of finding positive leptospiral IgM were in favor of the rainy season rather than the dry season only in the urban setting (Crude OR 2.96; 95% CI 1.22–7.716).
307.Rationale: The passage reports that 79% of IgM-positive cases occurred in the rainy season and provides analyses showing higher odds of positivity during the rainy season (including a significant adjusted OR). These findings support the epidemiological inference that transmission is associated with climatic (rainy) periods and that surveillance and control efforts could reasonably be prioritized during the rainy season when cases are more likely to occur.

307.Option: Leptospira spp. are a probable cause of fever with jaundice in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as evidenced by the detection of IgM antibodies in 7% of suspected yellow fever cases with negative serology, particularly among young adult males in urban areas.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of Leptospira IgM in this study was 7% (88 patients).
According to the WHO, a probable leptospirosis case is defined as a clinically compatible case with laboratory findings such as a positive result of an IgM ELISA or other rapid screening test[20].
30 leptospiral IgM positive cases (34%) were found among the 20–29 years age group, followed by the 0–9 years age groups with 20 (23%) of positive cases.
Of the 88 positive leptospiral IgM cases, 18 (20%) were female and 70 (80%) were male, with a crude odds ratio of 2.97 in favor of males (95% CI 1.74–5.06).

Male sex (Adjusted OR 2.70; 95% CI 1.57–4.65), 20–29 years age group (Adjusted OR 2.85; 95% CI 1.54–5.30) and living in an urban area, albeit less strongly, (Adjusted OR 1.91; 95% CI 1.04–3.48) remained statistically significant following adjustment for other factors
307.Rationale: The study detected anti-Leptospira IgM in 7% of suspected yellow fever cases that were negative for yellow fever, and the WHO definition cited classifies IgM positivity in a clinically compatible case as a 'probable' leptospirosis case. The demographic pattern showing higher prevalence in males and the 20–29 age group, together with higher odds in urban residence, supports the specific characterization that Leptospira spp. are a probable cause of fever with jaundice in this population and that young adult males in urban areas appear particularly affected.

307.Option: The finding that 7% of suspected yellow fever cases were seropositive for Leptospira IgM suggests that yellow fever surveillance systems in the DRC are effective at identifying co-infections with other pathogens, including leptospirosis.
Evidence: Since 2003, the DRC Ministry of Health, in collaboration with WHO, has set up a yellow fever surveillance system. In this system, case notification is based on a standard case definition and reporting by health districts.

Suspected yellow fever cases are defined as any person with acute fever with jaundice occurring within 14 days of the onset of symptoms and not responding to antimalarial drugs or who tests negative for malaria by thick blood smear[19]. Blood samples from suspected yellow fever cases are collected and sent from health districts to the Institut National de Recherche Biomedicale (INRB) (Kinshasa, DRC), the country reference laboratory for yellow fever serological analysis.

Between 2017 and 2018, 1,562 suspected yellow fever cases were reported to INRB. Of these 1,562 cases, 70 were confirmed as yellow fever cases by serological analyses and 1,492 cases had negative serological results.

We retrospectively analyzed data and serum samples notified to the INRB from January 2017 to December 2018 for IgM presence against Leptospira spp. We used samples and data of suspected cases of yellow fever that had negative yellow fever serology, and which had available data and sufficient sample volume for biological analysis.
307.Rationale: Although the surveillance system collected samples from suspected yellow fever cases and these available samples enabled a separate retrospective analysis that identified leptospiral IgM in 7% of cases, the passage does not state that the routine yellow fever surveillance system is designed to, or is effective at, identifying co-infections such as leptospirosis. The detection reported here was the result of a targeted retrospective study testing stored samples, not evidence that routine surveillance actively identifies co-infections. Therefore the claim that the surveillance system is effective at identifying co-infections is not supported by the data presented.

307.Option: The high proportion of positive cases among young children (0–9 years) in rural areas suggests that leptospirosis in the DRC is primarily a rural disease affecting children due to exposure to contaminated water sources during agricultural activities.
Evidence: The highest positivity rate of Leptospira IgM was found in the 20–29 years age group (42%) in the urban areas, while the highest positivity rate in the rural areas was found in the 0–9 years age group (36%).

Fifty-two of the positive leptospiral IgM cases (59%) lived in an urban setting; and the odds of leptospiral IgM cases were higher in those living in urban compared to rural areas (Crude OR 3.39; 95% CI 2.18–5.28).
Of the 88 positive leptospiral IgM cases, 50 cases (57%) lived in Kinshasa province.
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).
307.Rationale: While the passage notes that in rural areas the highest positivity rate was among 0–9 year olds, it also shows that overall more IgM-positive cases lived in urban areas and the highest overall positivity was in 20–29 year olds in urban settings. Thus the data do not support the assertion that leptospirosis is primarily a rural disease affecting children. Furthermore, the specific causal mechanism claimed (exposure during agricultural activities) is not described or evidenced in the passage. Therefore the strong claim that leptospirosis is primarily rural and due to agricultural exposures is not supported by the presented data.

308.Option: The widespread use of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) in the intervention arm led to a significant reduction in the number of adult female Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, which likely contributed to the lower seroconversion rates observed in the control arm.
Evidence: There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.

The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) (CI: 29.9–71.9) and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY (CI: 15.2–51.7).

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.
308.Rationale: The passage reports that entomological indices (including adult female Aedes measures) were similar across arms and that seroconversion was actually higher in the intervention arm. There is no evidence in the passage that the intervention arm experienced a significant reduction in adult female Ae. aegypti; therefore the claim that ITC use led to a reduction that somehow explains lower seroconversion in the control arm is not supported and contradicts the reported results.

308.Option: The lack of sustained insecticidal efficacy of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) and the emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local Aedes aegypti population likely contributed to the failure of ITCs to reduce dengue seroconversion in the intervention arm.
Evidence: Results were highly variable and ranged from 34%-100% mortality, with 8 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold, with no discernible pattern attributable to curtain color, sun exposure, or washing frequency.

After hanging for 8 months (July 2010), a further 18 ITCs were selected for testing using the same methodology. Results showed further declines in bioefficacy, with 13 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 14%-100%).

After hanging for 11 months (October 2010), the same process was repeated and all except for 1 curtain fell below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 32%-98%). Therefore, to ensure an effective intervention was present in the treated households for the remaining period of the study, curtains were re-treated with deltamethrin in the form of KO Tab 123 (Bayer) during November 2010, with a total of 3,886 curtains (91.9%) re-treated.

Cone bioassays after re-treatment with KO-Tab 123 showed that the curtains did not immediately recover to the 100% bioefficacy observed at baseline. At 1 month following re-treatment (January 2011), average mortality for 36 ITCs was 74.5% (range: 48%-94%). By 9-months following re-treatment (August 2011) average mortality from 9 tested ITCs was 97.2% (range: 87.5%-100%).

Insecticide susceptibility data from CDC bottle bioassays demonstrated that the local Ae. aegypti population was fully susceptible to deltamethrin at baseline and remained fully susceptible when tested during May 2010 and July 2010. Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.

There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates ... The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) ... and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY.
308.Rationale: The passage documents a marked decline in ITC bioefficacy over months after deployment (many curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold), the need for re-treatment, only partial and delayed recovery of bioefficacy after re-treatment, and the first detection and progressive decline in deltamethrin susceptibility in local Ae. aegypti during the study period. At the same time the intervention arm experienced higher seroconversion. These lines of evidence support the epidemiological inference that loss of sustained insecticidal activity of the fabric and the emergence of insecticide resistance likely undermined the ability of ITCs to reduce dengue seroconversion in the intervention clusters.

308.Option: The higher seroconversion rates in the intervention arm were primarily due to increased human mobility and cross-cluster transmission, which overwhelmed the protective effect of the insecticide-treated curtains.
Evidence: Clusters were geographically contiguous in the same region of the city.
There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.
No subgroup or adjusted analyses were done.
308.Rationale: The passage reports higher seroconversion in the intervention arm but does not provide any data on human mobility, movement patterns, or cross-cluster transmission dynamics. The fact that clusters were geographically contiguous is stated, but there is no evidence presented that increased human movement was measured or was the primary driver of higher seroconversion. Because the passage lacks data on mobility or transmission between clusters, the causal claim that mobility/cross-cluster transmission primarily caused the higher seroconversion cannot be derived from the provided information.

308.Option: The initial detection of deltamethrin resistance in the local Aedes aegypti population was likely due to the use of ULV spraying in surrounding areas, which created selection pressure on the mosquito population.
Evidence: In response to increases in reported dengue cases or elevated Ae. aegypti indices, emergency measures, including ULV spraying and city-wide cleanup campaigns (collection of water-holding containers), were employed [19, 21, 22, 26–30].

Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.
308.Rationale: While the passage notes that ULV spraying has been used historically as an emergency control measure and documents the timing of first detection of deltamethrin resistance during the study, it does not provide any direct evidence linking ULV spraying in surrounding areas to the emergence of resistance. No data are presented on the timing, location, intensity, or insecticide composition of ULV applications relative to the resistance detection, so attributing resistance causally to ULV spraying is not supported by the text.

308.Option: The perceived effectiveness of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) led to increased use of other protective measures in the intervention arm, which likely contributed to the higher seroconversion rates observed.
Evidence: They are also well accepted by communities [17], because their perceived efficacy is reinforced by the reduction in other biting insects, cockroaches, houseflies and other insect pests [11].
There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms.

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.
308.Rationale: The passage indicates ITCs were well accepted and perceived as effective by communities, but it provides no data that residents adopted additional protective measures as a result of perceived effectiveness, nor any mechanism by which increased use of other protective measures would result in higher seroconversion (if anything, increased protective measures would be expected to reduce infection). Therefore, the specific causal claim that perceived ITC effectiveness led to increased use of other measures that increased seroconversion is not supported by the passage.

309.Option: The short treatment duration and lower cost compared with other treatment options may make PMIM a preferred drug to include as part of a combination therapy regimen for VL patients in Bangladesh.
Evidence: Paromomycin is a low-cost drug that was demonstrated to be efficacious and generally safe and well tolerated for the treatment of VL in a Phase III trial conducted in endemic areas of India [4].

A clinical trial on different treatment regimens for VL completed in four East African countries showed that the combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone [6].
Thus, adding PMIM to the treatment regimen lowered both the cost and duration of treatment.

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.
The final clinical response rate 6 months after EOT was 94.2% (95% CI 90.0–98.4);
309.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that paromomycin is a low-cost drug and that adding PMIM to an SSG regimen reduced both cost and treatment duration (17 days vs 30 days). The Phase IIIb study was undertaken to confirm PMIM's effectiveness in Bangladesh, and the trial achieved a high final clinical response (94.2%), supporting PMIM's effectiveness in this setting. Together these statements support the epidemiologic inference that the short duration and lower cost make PMIM an attractive candidate to include in combination regimens for VL patients in Bangladesh (the option uses cautious language: "may make ... preferred").

309.Option: Paromomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside class of agents, which are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Patients with clinically significant renal dysfunction or history of hearing loss were excluded from this study. Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity were not formally studied in this trial.
Evidence: Exclusion criteria included pregnancy or lactation; active tuberculosis or taking antituberculosis medications; previous treatment with PMIM; clinically significant anemia; current or history of clinically significant renal or hepatic dysfunction; serum creatinine above the upper limit of normal range; proteinuria; history of hepatitis B or C or HIV positive; history of hearing loss; significant coexisting disease; any history of VL or treatment for VL; history of hypersensitivity to aminoglycosides or sulfite; and concomitant use of other aminoglycosides, nephrotoxic and ototoxic drugs, or immunosuppressive drugs.
tinnitus, hearing impairment, and renal impairment, all considered possibly related to study drug, were reported in one adult male after EOT.

Ear infection associated with slight to mild hearing loss considered possibly related to study drug was reported in an adult female after 4 doses of PMIM; the subject was withdrawn from study drug per protocol and improved after antibiotic treatment.
Safety.Safety was assessed by evaluation of all adverse events, regardless of causality, and vital signs.
309.Rationale: Parts of this option are supported by the passage: the trial excluded patients with clinically significant renal dysfunction and those with a history of hearing loss (quoted exclusion criteria), and the safety results report tinnitus, hearing impairment, and renal impairment that were considered possibly related to study drug. However, the claim that "Paromomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside class of agents, which are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity" and the assertion that "Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity were not formally studied in this trial" cannot be fully derived from the passage alone. The passage does not explicitly state that paromomycin is an aminoglycoside or that aminoglycosides are known to cause those toxicities (this is external pharmacologic knowledge). Moreover, while the study monitored adverse events and reported renal and auditory AEs, it does not state that nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity were or were not "formally studied" (the design descriptions indicate general safety assessment but do not support the definitive statement that these toxicities were not formally assessed). Because the option relies on external knowledge and makes a definitive claim about formal study of those toxicities that the passage does not support, the option is not derivable solely from the Passage Body.

309.Option: The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. This study was conducted with limited resources and was designed similar to a phase 4 study to generate evidence that PMIM works in Bangladesh similar to India, where the etiological agent and patient populations are thought to be very similar.
Evidence: Six months after EOT (approximately Study Day 202), patients were asked to return for an assessment of final clinical response.

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.

The planned sample size of 120 patients with VL was not based on statistical calculations but was considered a sufficient size to determine effectiveness and safety of PMIM in the Bangladeshi population based on high efficacy rates reported in large-scale studies of PMIM in Indian populations [4,5].
A subsequent Phase IV trial in India revealed a similarly high efficacy and safety profile coupled with excellent treatment compliance in a rural outpatient setting [5].
309.Rationale: The passage confirms that a 6-month clinical outcome assessment was part of the protocol and that the trial aimed to confirm effectiveness in Bangladesh to support registration, citing prior Indian studies. However, the option asserts several additional points that are not stated in the Passage Body: that the study was "conducted with limited resources," that it was "designed similar to a phase 4 study," and that the etiologic agents and patient populations are "thought to be very similar" to India. While the passage notes a Phase IV trial in India and that the sample size was pragmatic (not based on statistical calculations), it does not state the resource limitations, nor does it state that the design intentionally mimicked a phase 4 study or explicitly assert similarity of etiologic agents/populations. Those are inferences beyond the provided text. Therefore the full option is not derivable from the Passage Body.

309.Option: The combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone, suggesting that PMIM could be used as a standalone treatment for VL in rural Bangladesh.
Evidence: A clinical trial on different treatment regimens for VL completed in four East African countries showed that the combination of sodium stibogluconate (SSG) and PMIM administered over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable with a 30-day course of SSG alone [6].
Thus, adding PMIM to the treatment regimen lowered both the cost and duration of treatment.

This Phase IIIb study was designed to confirm the effectiveness of PMIM to treat VL in Bangladeshi patients in an outpatient setting to support registration of this low-cost treatment option in Bangladesh.
The initial clinical response rate at EOT in the ITT population was 98.3% (95% confidence interval [CI] 96.0–100.6)
The final clinical response rate 6 months after EOT was 94.2% (95% CI 90.0–98.4);
309.Rationale: The passage does state that the combination of SSG+PMIM over 17 days had efficacy and safety comparable to a 30-day SSG course, and it separately provides evidence that PMIM alone (21-day IM course) achieved high initial and final clinical response rates in this Bangladeshi trial. However, the option's specific causal leap—that the combination trial result implies PMIM could be used as a standalone treatment—is not supported by the cited combination trial (which compared combination therapy to SSG monotherapy) and therefore does not justify the inference that PMIM alone would be equivalent based solely on the combination finding. Although the current Phase IIIb trial shows promising results for PMIM monotherapy in Bangladesh, the combination-trial result by itself does not logically imply PMIM should be used as standalone; concluding so requires additional explicit comparative evidence. Therefore the statement, as phrased (linking the combination finding to support standalone use), is not fully derivable from the Passage Body.

310.Option: The use of speckle-tracking 2D echocardiography (STE) in pediatric Chagas disease patients reveals that subtle myocardial strain abnormalities are common, even in the absence of clinical symptoms.
Evidence: Myocardial contractility was evaluated by STE in 79/95 (83%) patients (16 patients were not available for evaluation due to missed appointments).

Values were found to be above (i.e., less negative) -16% strain in 8 patients. As regards strain rate, 3 patients presented values above -0.8 (seg-1). These cases were considered normal since the global echocardiographic contractility evaluation was normal.
In conclusion, no disturbances in myocardial contractility were observed by STE and three-dimensional echocardiography.
Furthermore, no clinical symptoms such as ventricular tachycardia, syncope, chest pain or palpitations were observed during the follow-up.
310.Rationale: The passage reports that STE was performed and that although a small number of patients had strain or strain-rate values less negative than certain cutoffs (8 and 3 patients respectively), these cases were explicitly considered normal in the context of global contractility evaluation. The authors conclude that no disturbances in myocardial contractility were observed by STE. Therefore the claim that subtle myocardial strain abnormalities are common is not supported by the reported results.

310.Option: The high prevalence of ECG abnormalities in pediatric Chagas disease patients, including cRBBB and LAFB, indicates that early treatment does not prevent cardiac conduction disturbances.
Evidence: During the follow-up period, alterations in routine ECG were observed in 11/234 (4.7%, 95% CI [2–7.4%]).

However, in 4/11 patients these ECG variations were considered normal variations for children. In the remaining 7/11 patients the ECG variations were considered pathological and only 4/7 patients with cRBBB and LAFB were considered to have ECG abnormalities probably related to CD.
310.Rationale: The data show a low proportion of routine ECG alterations (4.7% overall) and only a subset (4 patients) had ECG findings considered probably related to Chagas disease. This does not constitute a 'high prevalence' of ECG abnormalities. Moreover, without an untreated comparison group the passage does not establish that early treatment fails to prevent conduction disturbances. Thus the option overstates prevalence and draws a causal conclusion not supported by the presented data.

310.Option: The use of sensitive cardiological techniques such as speckle-tracking 2D echocardiography (STE) in pediatric Chagas disease patients treated in the early phase of infection shows no significant long-term regional wall motion abnormalities, suggesting that early treatment may prevent cardiac structural changes.
Evidence: Myocardial contractility was evaluated by STE in 79/95 (83%) patients (16 patients were not available for evaluation due to missed appointments).

Effective antiparasitic treatment of CD could, therefore, prevent CD cardiac complications.
310.Rationale: The passage reports comprehensive, sensitive cardiological testing (STE and 3D echocardiography) in early-treated pediatric patients with a median follow-up of many years and finds no disturbances in myocardial contractility or adverse ejection fraction results. The authors also state that effective antiparasitic treatment could prevent cardiac complications. From these facts it is reasonable to infer that early treatment is associated with absence of significant long-term regional wall motion abnormalities and therefore may prevent structural cardiac changes; the wording 'may prevent' appropriately reflects that this is an inference rather than definitive proof.

310.Option: The lack of an untreated control group due to ethical considerations limits the ability to definitively conclude that early treatment prevents cardiac complications in pediatric Chagas disease patients.
Evidence: Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.

Effective antiparasitic treatment of CD could, therefore, prevent CD cardiac complications. However, there is insufficient data to adequately evaluate the impact of early CD treatment on the prevention of cardiac lesions, since this requires long-term follow-up of treated pediatric patients.
310.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that no untreated control group could be included for ethical reasons and notes previously that evidence on prevention of cardiac lesions by early treatment has been limited by the need for long-term follow-up. These statements directly support the limitation that causally attributing prevention of cardiac complications to early treatment cannot be definitively established within this study design.

310.Option: The persistence of T. cruzi DNA in blood samples after treatment suggests that parasiticidal treatment may not fully eliminate the parasite, even in pediatric patients with long-term follow-up.
