331.Rationale: While the passage supports that potent inhibitors of S. mansoni TGR were identified and that the tested compounds did not inhibit GR in the Prx2-coupled assay, there is no evidence in the Passage Body that these compounds have activity against other parasites or would act as broad‑spectrum antiparasitics. The authors only report activity against S. mansoni TGR and provide no data on other pathogens or on cross-species efficacy; thus the broad-spectrum claim cannot be derived from the passage.

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.

we describe the miniaturization to 1536-well density of a cuvette-based assay for the TGR/Prx2 cascade which utilizes as a quantitative measure the decrease in fluorescence of the consumed NADPH substrate, the performance of a quantitative high-throughput screen (qHTS) against 71,028 discrete compounds, and the initial characterization of several novel series of inhibitors.
Analysis of the qHTS results revealed 39 actives characterized by full concentration-response curves and IC50 values of better than 10 µM.

the following series and singletons were selected for further studies after SAR analysis (Figure 4): oxadiazole 2-oxides (5 actives out of 29 analogues in collection, IC50 potency range between 8 µM and inactive), phosphinic amides (two compounds in the collection, one inactive and one active at 37 nM), phosphoramidite (singleton active, IC50 of 560 nM), and isoxazolone (singleton active, IC50 of 530 nM).

Gratifyingly, the previously-untested analogues of the phosphinic amides (compounds 1–4), phosphoramidite (13–16), and isoxazolone (10–12) actives all showed activity with various degrees of potency, supporting and expanding the qHTS findings.

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system.

The MLI provides academic investigators with the pharma-scale infrastructure and technologies necessary to discover both chemical probes of physiology, and starting points for development of novel therapeutics for the rare and neglected diseases that are of less interest to the pharmaceutical sector.
331.Rationale: The Passage Body documents discovery and initial characterization of multiple novel structural series with sub-micromolar to low-micromolar IC50s against the S. mansoni TGR target, confirms activity on isolated TGR, and describes follow-up SAR and analogue confirmation. It also frames the MLI goal of producing chemical probes and starting points for drug development. Together, these statements directly support the inference that the identified compounds can serve as mechanistic probes of parasite redox biology and as starting points for medicinal chemistry optimization toward new schistosomiasis treatments.

332.Option: The expansion of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in hookworm-infected individuals is primarily driven by the direct induction of FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells by excretory-secretory products of nematodes.
Evidence: To investigate Treg activity in human hookworm infection, we have evaluated Treg frequencies, function and immune responses to hookworm antigens in N. americanus-infected individuals from a rural area of Minas Gerais state (Brazil).
we described an augmentation of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in hookworm-infected individuals compared with healthy non-infected donors.
The parasite may promote its survival by excreting/secreting a panel of molecular immunosuppressive agents and, possibly, by stimulating the appearance of regulatory T-cell populations.

In order to determine the possible effect of hookworm antigens on the expression of cell surface markers (CTLA-4 and GITR) and cytokines (IL-17, TGF-β, and IL-10) by CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in N. americanus-infected donors, whole blood cultures were stimulated with either hookworm crude antigen or ES products.
Although there was a tendency in increase on the expression of IL-17 in ES stimulated blood it was not statistically significant (p = 0.3562) (Figure 3).
332.Rationale: The passage documents an increased frequency of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in infected individuals and states that parasites may secrete immunomodulatory products, but it does not provide direct experimental evidence that excretory-secretory (ES) products directly induce FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells nor that this is the primary driver of the observed expansion. The only antigen-stimulation data presented are short-term in vitro stimulations (crude antigen or ES) and these do not demonstrate direct induction of FOXP3 in naïve cells or establish primacy of this mechanism.

332.Option: The absence of changes in T cell numbers or Treg counts in human primo-infection with N. americanus suggests that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is not a primary mechanism of hookworm survival in early infection.
Evidence: This infection takes a particularly devastating toll on the most vulnerable of the world's population, including children, productive men, and women of childbearing age [5], [6], [7].

To investigate Treg activity in human hookworm infection, we have evaluated Treg frequencies, function and immune responses to hookworm antigens in N. americanus-infected individuals from a rural area of Minas Gerais state (Brazil).
we described an augmentation of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in hookworm-infected individuals compared with healthy non-infected donors.
Chronic human N. americanus infection is classically associated with a profound ablation of cell proliferation.
332.Rationale: The study and data presented concern individuals with established N. americanus infection (chronic/endemic exposure) and show increased Treg frequencies in these infected donors. The passage contains no data on primary (primo-) infection or on Treg dynamics during early infection. Therefore one cannot infer from the presented evidence that there is an absence of T cell/Treg changes in primo-infection or that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is not important early in infection.

332.Option: The reduction in CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells after hookworm antigen stimulation indicates that IL-17 production is directly suppressed by parasite antigens, leading to a permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype.
Evidence: When crude antigen was added to the in vitro cultures, the percentage and the absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells co-expressing IL-17 was significantly reduced (p<0.0030) (Figure 3).
Although there was a tendency in increase on the expression of IL-17 in ES stimulated blood it was not statistically significant (p = 0.3562) (Figure 3).
Whole blood was stimulated in vitro with ES and crude antigens (5 μg/well) in RPMI 1640 media ... for 24 hours at 37°C with 5% CO2.
332.Rationale: The passage reports a short-term in vitro reduction in the percentage and counts of FOXP3+ T cells co-expressing IL-17 after crude antigen stimulation. However, it does not demonstrate that parasite antigens directly suppress IL-17 production in vivo, nor does it provide evidence of a permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype. The observation is a short-term culture effect and cannot be extrapolated to permanent in vivo reprogramming without additional longitudinal or mechanistic data.

332.Option: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
Evidence: The discovery of regulatory T lymphocytes (Treg) that are actively involved in maintaining immune tolerance has recently led to new insights into mechanisms of tolerance breakdown and/or immunoregulation in human diseases, including those resulting from allergic, autoimmune, or infectious causes [14].

These cells have been shown to suppress cellular immune responses through direct contact with immune effector cells and by the production of regulatory cytokines, including TGF-β and IL-10 [15], [16].
Taken together, our results suggest that Treg cells may play an important role in hookworm-induced immunosuppression, contributing to the longevity of hookworm survival in infected people.

in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p = 0.0039) and ES (p = 0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A).
332.Rationale: While the passage documents that Treg cells suppress antigen-specific proliferation in hookworm infection and reviews that Tregs are important modulators in allergic and autoimmune contexts generally, the study provides no direct evidence that hookworm-induced Tregs confer control of or therapeutic diversion of immune responses in disorders such as intestinal inflammation, airway hyperreactivity, diabetes, or multiple sclerosis. The extrapolation to those specific clinical disorders is speculative and not supported by the data presented.

332.Option: The increased expression of GITR and CTLA-4 on Treg cells in hookworm-infected individuals is sufficient to fully account for the observed suppression of antigen-specific proliferation, independent of cytokine production.
Evidence: Infection of N. americanus significantly increased the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4 (p = 0.0002) and GITR (p<0.0001).

Flow cytometric analysis also showed a significant augmentation of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ cells producing IL-10 (p<0.0001), TGF-β (p<0.0001) and IL-17 (p = 0.0003) in hookworm infected individuals (Figure 2).

in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p = 0.0039) and ES (p = 0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A).

The depletion of Tregs elicited the increase of IL-2 and lower levels of IL-10 in supernatants of dPBMCs with or without antigenic stimulation although statistical significance was not achieved (Supplementary Figure S4).
332.Rationale: The passage shows that Treg populations in infected individuals have higher proportions expressing CTLA-4 and GITR and that Treg depletion increases antigen-specific proliferation. However, it also documents increased regulatory cytokine-producing Treg subsets (IL-10, TGF-β) and changes in cytokine levels after depletion, indicating cytokines likely contribute to suppression. The data do not establish that GITR/CTLA-4 expression alone is sufficient to fully account for suppression independent of cytokines; thus the claim of sufficiency and independence is not supported by the presented evidence.

333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: Since the implementation of the National Schistosomiasis Control Program (NSCP) in the 1970s and decades of consequent chemotherapeutic interventions, the Brazilian health authorities reported significant improvements in terms of transmission, prevalence, and parasite load in the country’s endemic regions, especially in the states of Minas Gerais and Bahia [10]. In this new epidemiological scenario, most of the infected individuals in endemic areas harbor low parasite loads and are very unlikely to be detected with the commonly used parasitological methods [11,12].

The inclusion of additional parasitological methods for schistosome diagnosis resulted in the detection of a much higher number of infected individuals within the study population (Table 3). The apparent prevalence rose from 20.4 to 29.9%, when the number of KK slides was increased from two to 14 slides, or to 38.3%, when we used two slides prepared from each of the three fecal samples.

Overall, and taking into consideration the results of all the parasitological methods (reference standard), the prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis reached 45.9%, which represents a 2.3 times increase in relation to the WHO’s recommended standard KK procedure.

Importantly, the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).
333.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that long-term control efforts have produced a new epidemiological scenario characterized by mostly low parasite loads, which standard parasitological methods are unlikely to detect. Empirical results from the study show that more exhaustive testing (the reference standard combining many KK slides and other methods) yielded substantially higher prevalence estimates—overall 45.9% (2.3× higher) and, by age group, between 1.7 and 4.7 times higher than the prevalence measured using the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample. Therefore, the statement that the recommended standard procedure can underestimate true prevalence by up to 4.7-fold is directly supported by the data and statements in the passage.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D, thereby enabling the toxin to exert its dermonecrotic effects.
Evidence: The molecular mechanism by which brown spider venom induces gravitational spreading of skin lesions and systemic involvement is not fully understood.

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
334.Rationale: The passage describes hyaluronidase as a spreading factor that degrades glycosaminoglycans and thereby increases tissue permeability to other venom components. It provides experimental evidence that hyaluronidase increases lesion size when co-injected with a dermonecrotic toxin. However, the passage does not state or provide evidence that hyaluronidase activates phospholipase-D (or any dermonecrotic toxin) at a biochemical level. It explicitly notes that the molecular mechanism is not fully understood. Therefore the specific claim that hyaluronidase is essential for activation of phospholipase-D is not supported by the passage.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom contributes to dermonecrotic injury by directly degrading collagen fibers and disrupting blood vessel walls in the dermis.
Evidence: Da Silveira and colleagues (2007) [20] identified the hyaluronidases in Loxosceles intermedia venom as endo-β-N-acetyl-D-hexosaminidases that degrade hyaluronic acid and chondroitin sulfate.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].

Histologically, the lesion is reported as a collection of inflammatory cells in and around the blood vessels and diffusely distributed in the dermis. It is also possible to observe degeneration of blood vessel walls, disorganization of collagen fibers with edema, hemorrhage into the dermis, necrosis of cells, and destruction of tissue structures.

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage documents that hyaluronidase enzymatically degrades glycosaminoglycans (hyaluronic acid and chondroitin sulfate) and that lesions show collagen disorganization and vessel wall degeneration. However, it does not state that hyaluronidase directly degrades collagen or directly disrupts blood vessel walls. Instead, the text supports an indirect role in facilitating spread by degrading glycosaminoglycans and increasing permeability, and notes that hyaluronidase alone does not produce dermonecrosis. Thus the claim of direct degradation of collagen and direct vascular disruption by hyaluronidase is not supported by the evidence provided.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom acts as a spreading factor that enhances the diffusion of other venom toxins, such as phospholipase-D, through the extracellular matrix, thereby increasing the area of dermonecrotic lesions and promoting gravitational spreading of skin injury.
Evidence: The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom (Fig. 6C).

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage explicitly presents the concept that hyaluronidase degrades glycosaminoglycans, decreasing matrix viscosity and increasing permeability to other venom toxins. Experimental in vivo data show that co-injection of hyaluronidase with a dermonecrotic toxin increases lesion size and reproduces gravitational spreading similar to whole venom, while hyaluronidase alone does not cause lesions. These statements directly support the inference that hyaluronidase functions as a spreading factor enhancing diffusion of other venom components and thereby increasing dermonecrosis and gravitational spread.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom functions as a major allergen that triggers anaphylactic reactions in susceptible individuals, contributing to the systemic symptoms observed in loxoscelism.
Evidence: Systemic involvement has also been reported including fever, malaise, weakness, nausea, vomiting and in severe cases, intravascular coagulation, hemolysis and acute renal disturbance [1], [2], [3], [4], [5].

Although hyaluronidase activity has been described in several venoms including that of snakes [10], [23], scorpions [24] spiders [22], bees [25], caterpillars [26], wasps [27], cone snails [28] and fish [29], the biochemical and biological characterization of these enzymes is restricted to a few examples [10], [21], [30].
334.Rationale: While the passage lists systemic manifestations of loxoscelism and documents the presence and enzymatic activity of hyaluronidases, it provides no evidence that hyaluronidase acts as an allergen or triggers anaphylaxis. There is no experimental or clinical data in the passage linking hyaluronidase to allergic or anaphylactic mechanisms. Therefore the claim that hyaluronidase is a major allergen causing anaphylaxis is not supported by the text.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is primarily responsible for the direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions and systemic toxicity in brown spider bites.
Evidence: On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom (Fig. 6C).
334.Rationale: The experimental results show that hyaluronidase alone does not produce dermonecrosis, whereas co-administration with a dermonecrotic toxin amplifies lesion size and spreading. The passage characterizes hyaluronidase as facilitating spread (a spreading factor) rather than being the primary direct cause of dermonecrotic lesions or systemic toxicity. Thus the statement that hyaluronidase is primarily responsible for direct induction of dermonecrosis and systemic effects is not supported by the evidence.

335.Option: The higher odds of cysticercosis in breeding female pigs compared to males indicates that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection.
Evidence: Data from a cross-sectional survey examining a range of zoonotic and non-zoonotic diseases (including Taenia spp.) in 416 households, carried out between July 2010 and July 2012, were used.

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage reports an association from a cross-sectional study that breeding sows had higher odds of antigen presence (OR = 10.35). However, an observed association in cross-sectional observational data does not establish a direct causal effect of reproductive status on susceptibility. The study design and the presented results do not provide evidence that reproductive status itself directly increases biological susceptibility (other explanations such as differences in husbandry, age, exposure, or confounding factors could explain the association). Therefore the causal statement that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility cannot be derived from the passage.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
Evidence: The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04).

The results from the multivariable human model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (Table 4) indicate ... The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of antigen in humans: lower odds of antigen presence were associated with very low or very high percentages of this land cover class, while the odds of antigen presence were highest in areas with intermediate percentages of the land cover class (OR = 1.09 and 0.998; p = 0.03 and 0.03 for the linear and squared terms respectively).
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage provides multivariable model results showing that land cover (percentage flooding agricultural and grassland) has a quadratic (non-linear) relationship with antigen presence in humans and a positive association with antigen presence in pigs. It also reports a statistically significant negative association between precipitation and human antigen presence. These results directly indicate that environmental covariates (land cover, precipitation) influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in the study population, and that flooding agricultural/grassland areas show non-linear associations with risk, so the option is supported by the passage.

335.Option: The spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk, overshadowing the influence of environmental factors.
Evidence: Spatial analyses have demonstrated significant clustering of taeniasis, porcine cysticercosis and human cysticercosis, with evidence of aggregation of human and porcine cysticercosis cases within close proximity to human tapeworm carriers [15–18].

This research aimed to assess the hypothesis that spatial clustering of cysticercosis is the result of a combination of (a) localised transmission cycles giving rise to spatial aggregations and (b) the impact of environmental conditions on egg survival and, thus, onward transmission.
The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04).
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).
The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs ... flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: While the passage notes prior evidence of spatial clustering near tapeworm carriers and frames localised transmission as one potential driver, the study explicitly tested both localized transmission and environmental effects. The results presented show statistically significant associations with environmental covariates (land cover, precipitation) for humans and pigs. The passage does not provide evidence that clustering near carriers is the primary driver nor that it overshadows environmental influences; rather it supports a role for environmental factors alongside possible localized transmission. Therefore the strong claim that clustering is the primary driver overshadowing environmental factors is not supported by the passage.

335.Option: The lack of correlation between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA results in this study suggests that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis, and thus, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.
Evidence: The correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis (microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA) was zero: four participants were positive for taeniasis via microscopy only, 397 were positive for taeniasis via copro-antigen detection only and none were positive using both methods.

Based on the lack of correlation between the two diagnostic methods, and unexpectedly high number of positive results, the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure.

Taeniasis detection was carried out for human participants using microscopy (sensitivity 28.6% to 52.5%; specificity 85.7% to 99.9% [20,21]) and copro-antigen ELISA (sensitivity and specificity of 98% and 99.1%, respectively [20]), which identify current Taenia spp. infections.

Detection of Taenia spp. HP10 antigen (which is suggestive of cysticercosis) was carried out for human participants and pigs utilising the HP10 antigen ELISA (sensitivity 44.4% to 84% for porcine sera and 75% to 84.8% for human sera; specificity 45% to 100% for porcine sera and 94% to 96.5% for human sera [22–25]).
335.Rationale: The passage documents a lack of concordance between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA for taeniasis in this dataset and states the taeniasis results were excluded from further analyses because of this discrepancy. However, the passage also cites high reported sensitivity and specificity for the copro-antigen ELISA (per referenced studies), and the cysticercosis spatial analyses are based on HP10 antigen detection in sera, a different assay. The observed lack of correlation in taeniasis tests in this study does not, by itself, justify concluding that the copro-antigen ELISA is generally unreliable, nor does it support the specific claim that cysticercosis spatial patterns are driven by diagnostic inaccuracies. Thus the broad inference in the option is not supported by the passage.

335.Option: The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment: the eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, regardless of temperature [11].
Egg survival is influenced by temperature and humidity, with tropical regions being particularly suitable for transmission [10].
335.Rationale: The passage reports a statistically significant negative association between precipitation and antigen presence in humans. However, it also states that egg survival is influenced by humidity and that eggs are vulnerable to desiccation (i.e. more moisture would be expected to favor egg survival). The option asserts a specific causal mechanism (rainfall reduces environmental contamination thereby decreasing transmission) that contradicts the earlier mechanistic statements in the passage and that is not directly established by the cross-sectional associations. The observational result (negative association) does not by itself demonstrate the proposed mechanism, and the passage provides no direct evidence that rainfall reduces contamination. Therefore this causal interpretation cannot be reliably derived from the passage.

336.Option: The high confidence in domestically-made vaccines and the preference for them over foreign-made vaccines indicate that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored after past scandals.
Evidence: Fig 4 shows the findings on the confidence of domestic and foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines. The vast majority of the study participants reported that they were confident (48.7%, 95%CI 47.0 to 50.3) or completely confident (46.1%, 95%CI 44.5 to 47.8) in domestically-made COVID-19 vaccines.

Findings on the preference of domestically-made over foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines revealed that 64.2% (95%CI 62.6 to 65.8) reported a preference for domestically-made and 11.9% (95%CI 10.8 to 13.0) preferred foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines.

In China, vaccine hesitancy is a complex public health issue. In the last decade, vaccine scandals and a series of reports about the serious side-effects of vaccination have increased vaccination hesitancy and distrust in the country’s immunization program [4].
336.Rationale: Although the passage documents high reported confidence in domestically-made vaccines and a majority preference for them, it also explicitly documents prior vaccine scandals that increased hesitancy and distrust. The claim that public trust has been "fully restored" is a stronger, system-level conclusion that the Passage Body does not assert or support. The passage provides current attitudes (confidence and preference) but does not provide evidence that trust in the entire regulatory system has been completely restored after past scandals. Therefore this causal, definitive inference is not supported by the provided data.

336.Option: The perception of susceptibility to COVID-19 was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention, indicating that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection to drive vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The participants had low perceptions of susceptibility. The majority disagreed that there was a great chance of getting COVID-19 in the next few months (68.7%), were also not worried about the likelihood of getting COVID-19 (56.8%), and disagreed that it was currently possible that they would get COVID-19 (72.0%).
Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.

For HBM constructs, similar to vaccination intention, a higher marginal WTP was significantly associated with the items in the perception of susceptibility and severity of COVID-19, barriers and cues to action constructs.
336.Rationale: The passage states that participants reported low perceived susceptibility, but it does not state that perceived susceptibility was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention. In fact, the passage indicates that most HBM constructs were significantly associated with definite intention in univariate analyses, and susceptibility items were related to WTP. The only explicitly identified strongest predictor was perceived benefit (not susceptibility). The recommendation to focus on "increasing fear of infection" (i.e., increasing perceived susceptibility) is a prescriptive step not directly derived from the reported associations and is not supported by the Passage Body. Thus the two-part claim (susceptibility not significant; therefore campaigns should focus on increasing fear) cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

336.Option: The significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP suggests that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.
Evidence: A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045).
Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).
There was a gradual increase in the odds of WTP for an amount of CNY¥500 [US$72] over CNY¥100/200 [US$14/28] with an increase in household income.
The higher marginal WTP for the vaccine was associated with higher socio-economic factors
336.Rationale: The Passage Body does provide evidence that income level positively moderates the association between perceived susceptibility/severity and willingness-to-pay (WTP), and that higher income is associated with higher WTP. However, these findings concern WTP (willingness to pay) rather than actual vaccine uptake or "firm decision" to vaccinate. Prioritizing higher-income groups to "maximize vaccine uptake" is a policy recommendation that does not follow directly from associations with WTP; the data do not show that higher-income groups are the primary drivers of overall coverage, nor that prioritizing them is the optimal public health strategy. Therefore the stated policy implication is not derivable solely from the Passage Body.

336.Option: The fact that only 28.7% of respondents reported a definite intention to vaccinate suggests that the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine, necessitating mandatory vaccination policies.
Evidence: On the whole, a total of 2,950 (83.5%; 95%CI 82.3–84.8) participants responded yes to COVID-19 vaccine intent, while only 582 (16.4%; 95%CI 15.2–17.7) responded no.
By a more specific breakdown, the majority responded probably yes (54.8%; 95% CI 53.2–56.5) followed by definitely yes (28.7%; 95% CI 27.3–30.3).
Only 4.5% (95% CI 3.9–5.3) responded definitely no and 11.9% (95% CI 10.8–13.9) reported probably no.
336.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that while only 28.7% answered "definitely yes," a much larger share (83.5%) responded affirmatively when including "probably yes." Thus the data indicate a generally high willingness to accept a vaccine (most respondents were willing, though many were not absolutely certain), and only small percentages were definite refusals. The assertion that the majority of the population is unwilling is contradicted by the reported overall affirmative intent. The further leap to assert necessity of mandatory vaccination policies is a policy prescription not supported by the survey data presented. Therefore this option is not supported by the Passage Body.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
Evidence: Under the perceived barriers construct, concerns about the COVID-19 vaccine being faulty/fake and affordability were reported by 82.0% and 75.1%, respectively. Concerns about safety (72.6%) and efficacy (71.2%) were also notable.

High perceptions of the benefits of COVID-19 vaccination were reported. The majority (87.2%) perceived the benefit of feeling less worry of contracting the coronavirus after getting the vaccine. A higher proportion (92.5%) perceived benefit of the COVID-19 vaccine in reducing the risk of infection and resultant complications.

Many of the participants (92.1%) reported that they would only receive the COVID-19 vaccine if given adequate information, and 82.3% reported that they will only take the vaccine if taken by many in the public.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
336.Rationale: The Passage Body documents notable concerns about vaccine safety and efficacy (barriers), yet also reports very high perceived benefits and that perception of benefit (belief that vaccination reduces infection risk) was the strongest predictor of definite intention (OR = 3.14). Additionally, a very large fraction (92.1%) said they would only receive the vaccine if given adequate information, indicating that provision of information is a key facilitator. These findings directly support the inference that despite safety/efficacy concerns, perceived benefits and adequate information provision are strong drivers of a firm decision to vaccinate.

337.Option: The multivariate regression model incorporating HI and socioeconomic/climatic variables is superior to simulation models in predicting DHF incidence, suggesting that real-world data can fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The predictability attained solely by HI was much inferior to that attained in any simulations (Figure 8). However, the predictability using the multivariate regression model was as good as that in simulations with cross-immunity, up to W=8.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that adding socioeconomic/climatic variables improved fit but still failed to reproduce the wide variation in observed incidence, so the multivariate model is not clearly superior in capturing all observed patterns. While the multivariate model matched the predictability of simulations with cross-immunity only up to a limited averaging window (W=8), the passage does not support the stronger claim that real-world data/models 'fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.' Therefore the option overstates what can be inferred from the passage.

337.Option: The observed non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI is primarily driven by age-dependent differences in the probability of manifesting severe illness, independent of cross-serotype immunity.
Evidence: This model hypothesized that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype protection, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities.

As a result, the incidence of DHF could be correlated negatively to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission, while the correlation is positive only at low levels of transmission. In the present study, such a complex correlation structure mixed with positive and negative correlations will be called "non-monotonic", hereafter.

GAM detected a non-monotonic relationship in the simulations with cross-immunity (Figure 7A), a negative relationship in those with age-dependency (Figure 7E), and a slightly positive relationship in the control simulations (Figure 7I).

Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E).
337.Rationale: The passage and simulations attribute the non-monotonic relationship specifically to transient cross-serotype (cross-)immunity in the model, not solely to age-dependency. Simulation results show that cross-immunity generates a non-monotonic pattern, whereas the age-dependency scenario produced a negative relationship. Thus the claim that the non-monotonic relationship is primarily driven by age-dependency independent of cross-immunity is not supported by the presented evidence.

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: This model hypothesized that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype protection, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities.
As a result, the incidence of DHF could be correlated negatively to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission, while the correlation is positive only at low levels of transmission.

The present study aims to provide an empirical example of this non-monotonic relationship between the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity, with transmission intensity represented by vector abundance.
Our findings have major implications for future epidemiological surveys and dengue control programs.
337.Rationale: The passage documents an observed (and model-predicted) negative correlation between DHF incidence and transmission intensity at high transmission levels and explicitly states that these findings have major implications for epidemiological surveys and dengue control programs. Therefore it is reasonable to infer that this negative response at high transmission has important public health implications, independent of exactly which mechanism (e.g., cross-immunity versus age effects) produces it.

337.Option: Since HI is strongly correlated with vector abundance, reducing HI to below 30 will inevitably lead to a significant reduction in DHF incidence across all transmission settings.
Evidence: Since HI and BI are strongly positively correlated [26], HI also may reflect transmission intensity to some extent.
Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI=30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
As HI decreases from 70 to 30, for example, the log incidence would increase by 0.35 (Figure 3), which is equivalent to an increase of 40% in incidence, since exp (0.35)=1.4.
In multivariate analysis, ... Multivariate analysis estimated that, as HI decreases from 70 to 30, log incidence would increase by 0.6 (Figure 4A), which corresponds to an increase of 80%.
337.Rationale: The passage shows that the relationship between HI and DHF incidence is non-monotonic: lowering HI into some ranges (e.g., from 70 to 30) was associated with increased incidence, not decreased. Therefore it is not correct to infer that reducing HI below 30 will inevitably reduce DHF incidence across all settings; the empirical data show context-dependent, non-monotonic effects.

337.Option: The high variability in DHF incidence across districts is primarily due to differences in the number of public large water wells, which serve as the main breeding sites for Aedes mosquitoes.
Evidence: Aedes mosquitoes [2]–[4], which breed predominantly in water-holding containers within human habitats.
In multivariate analysis, the following six variables remained in the final regression model (Table 1; Figure 4): HI, winter temperature, summer temperature, APET, public large wells, and birth rate.

Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).
337.Rationale: While 'public large wells' was one of several variables retained in the final model, the passage does not state that differences in public large wells are the primary cause of the high between-district variability. It also emphasizes that even the multivariate model (which included public large wells) could not reproduce the very wide variation in observed incidence. Moreover, the passage indicates Aedes breed in a variety of water-holding containers, not specifically or exclusively in public large wells. Thus the claim is not supported by the presented evidence.

338.Option: The 'valley of death' in diagnostic development is primarily caused by insufficient funding for large-scale prospective studies, which prevents promising early-phase candidates from being validated in real-world settings.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
Seven tests (6 antigens) in phase III are evaluated on less than 600 specimens, while 3 antigens/tests are evaluated on 601 to 5000 specimens and no antigen is evaluated on >5000 specimens.
338.Rationale: The passage documents that most tests remain in early phases and that few large-scale evaluations exist, but it does not state or provide evidence that insufficient funding is the primary cause. The claim about funding is an explanation not contained in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from the provided text. The quotes show the pattern (many early-phase studies and few large studies) but do not attribute cause to funding.

338.Option: The diagnostic accuracy of rK28-based immunochromatographic tests in East Africa is consistently high across multiple studies, suggesting that this antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment in the region.
Evidence: RDTs with rK28 antigen have been evaluated in four studies in Sudan and Ethiopia with promising results for the test manufactured by CTK Biotech (sensitivities between 94 and 98%, and specificities between 95 and 98%) while sensitivities and specificities based on RDTs by other manufacturers and in ELISA were more variable (sensitivities between 89 and 100%, and specificities between 81 and 99%) [13–17].

With rk28 having been evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens (VL suspected patients: 285), there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on.

Only Indirect Fluorescent Antibody Technique (IFAT) and the rK28 ICT were shown to be >94% sensitive and >97% specific when evaluated in phase III studies on respectively 104 and 285 East African persons [15,108].
338.Rationale: The passage reports promising, sometimes high, accuracy for rK28 ICT—notably for the CTK-manufactured test—and notes a phase III result on 285 East African persons. However it also documents variability by manufacturer and cautions that the rK28 evaluation involved a relatively small number of specimens, risking variable performance across populations. Therefore the stronger claim that rK28 accuracy is 'consistently high across multiple studies' and that it is ready for 'widespread deployment' in the region is not fully supported by the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Forty four tests representing 42 antigens are evaluated on ≤100 specimens in phase I studies, 57 tests representing 53 antigens were evaluated on >100-specimens in phase II studies, 10 tests representing 9 antigens, were evaluated in phase III studies.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
338.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a pipeline dominated by early-phase evaluations (89% in phase I/II) and shows that multiple tests demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity in those early phases, yet relatively few progressed to large-scale phase III studies. This pattern supports the inference that many promising candidates do not advance to large prospective validation despite favorable early-phase performance.

338.Option: The majority of serodiagnostic tests for visceral leishmaniasis are not advanced to phase III because they fail to meet minimum diagnostic accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
338.Rationale: Although the passage shows that most tests remain in early phases, it also reports that a substantial number of early-phase tests met high diagnostic accuracy thresholds (>94% sensitivity and >97% specificity). Thus the assertion that the majority fail to meet accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations is not supported; many high-performing candidates exist in early phases but nonetheless did not progress, indicating other reasons for lack of advancement rather than universal early failure on accuracy criteria.

338.Option: The lack of standardized production methods for native antigens with carbohydrate and lipid moieties contributes to high batch-to-batch variation, which limits their potential for commercialization and widespread use in low-resource settings.
Evidence: Fifty-three antigens (59%) were expressed in E. coli or on phages or were synthetic peptides and thus do not contain eukaryotic glycosylated or lipid moieties.

Fifteen antigens contain both a carbohydrate and a lipid moiety as they are made from whole parasites, whole parasite lysates, the entire secretome, the parasite membrane or lipophosphoglycan (LPG) [31,32,35,60,62,111,114,122].

All 20 antigens with a known carbohydrate and or lipid moiety are native antigens. Fourteen are antigen mixtures of undefined identity, such as the DAT [122] and soluble leishmanial antigen (SLA) (e.g. [62]) which consist of a mixture of parasite molecules including carbohydrates and lipids.
338.Rationale: The passage documents that many antigens containing carbohydrate and lipid moieties are native, often undefined mixtures, which implies potential challenges for standardization. However, the specific causal claim that lack of standardized production causes high batch-to-batch variation and thereby limits commercialization and use in low-resource settings is not explicitly stated or evidenced in the passage. That conclusion goes beyond the data presented and introduces a mechanistic/commercial assertion not directly supported by the text.

339.Option: Participants who reported trying to do as many physical and social activities as possible were more likely to experience a faster recovery and return to normal functioning, suggesting that active coping strategies are clinically effective.
Evidence: The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
As participants were aware that there was no cure for chikungunya, this left them wondering if their pain and other symptoms would ever fade away.

Using frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept. Frequent visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).
339.Rationale: The passage states that some participants who used emotional coping engaged in physical/social activities and perceived less impact, but it does not present any evidence that such behavior produced faster objective recovery or return to normal functioning. The text explicitly notes there is no cure and persistent symptoms may remain, and it documents unmet expectations and ongoing symptoms despite various coping approaches. Therefore the claim that active coping strategies are clinically effective (faster recovery) is not supported by the passage; the passage reports perceived lower impact for some, not demonstrated faster recovery or clinical effectiveness.

339.Option: Since effective long-term treatments are not available yet for chronic chikungunya, the high uptake of (alternative) medicines and frequent physician visits will normally not lead to success, resulting in dissatisfaction and further emotional stress.
Evidence: As participants were aware that there was no cure for chikungunya, this left them wondering if their pain and other symptoms would ever fade away.

Many participants had used medicines to treat their physical complaints. Those included the medicines bought over the counter or prescribed by the physician, amongst which were mainly paracetamol or NSAID’s. Furthermore, natural or other alternative medicines were very popular since they had been prominently presented in advertisements and media by alternative healers. Hence, people recommended these medicines to each other. Some individuals with persistent complaints reported to have used more than ten different medicines.

Although some decided not to go to see a physician, others decided to visit a physician many times (up to approximately 20 times in some cases) for the complaints caused by chikungunya. Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment.

Using frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept. Frequent visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there is no cure for chikungunya and documents widespread use of both conventional and alternative medicines and repeated physician consultations. It also directly links frequent physician visits and multiple treatments (problem-focused coping) to unmet expectations for cure and resultant stress/negative QoL. Thus the inference that high uptake of medicines and physician visits will often not produce success and can cause dissatisfaction and emotional stress is supported by the provided text.

339.Option: The universal health care program in Curaçao minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced the financial burden on individuals, which may have contributed to higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients.
Evidence: The financial impact due to chikungunya on participant’s personal situation was generally perceived as limited. In Curaçao, chikungunya patients could rely on an insurance system, which covered health care costs, which in turn minimized out-of-pocket expenditures. These insurances covered also parts of the income loss of chikungunya patients who could not work.

In general, chikungunya patients stayed in bed during the acute phase of chikungunya and depended on the care of family or friends. Their physical condition permitting, most of them went to see a physician, normally accompanied by someone to transfer them.

Although some decided not to go to see a physician, others decided to visit a physician many times (up to approximately 20 times in some cases) for the complaints caused by chikungunya. Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment. Frequent physician visits were also performed because people needed to prove disease persistence in order to be reimbursed for the absence from their work.
339.Rationale: The passage states that insurance in Curaçao covered health care costs and partial income loss, thereby minimizing out-of-pocket burden. The passage also documents high levels of physician consultation (including many repeated visits) and explicitly notes that frequent visits were sometimes performed to obtain reimbursement for work absence. Linking reduced financial barriers to increased utilization is a reasonable and supported inference in the passage (the text both documents low personal financial burden and high utilization, and gives a direct mechanism—reimbursement—encouraging physician visits). The option's cautious phrasing 'may have contributed' is consistent with the level of evidence provided.

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.

Many participants had used medicines to treat their physical complaints. Those included the medicines bought over the counter or prescribed by the physician, amongst which were mainly paracetamol or NSAID’s. Furthermore, natural or other alternative medicines were very popular ... Some individuals with persistent complaints reported to have used more than ten different medicines.

Using frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept.

In contrast, people with lingering complaints while reporting less impact of chikungunya also showed different coping strategies, i.e. emotional coping strategies. The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
339.Rationale: The passage directly describes problem-focused coping as associated with high use of medications (including alternative remedies) and repeated physician consultations. It also contrasts this with emotional coping (acceptance-oriented), which the passage links to lower reported impact on HRQoL and reduced healthcare-seeking and medication use. Therefore the statement accurately summarizes the relationships reported in the study.

340.Option: The increase in the median age of hospitalized dengue cases over time indicates that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred to hospitals.
Evidence: the median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 years in 2007 to 43 years in 2017 (p < 0.0005) (Fig 3).

the proportion of hospitalizations in those aged 25–44 years declined from 46.3% in 2003 to 36.4% in 2017, older adults (aged ≥45 years) accounted for an increasingly higher proportion over the 15-year period: from 21.0% to 29.9% in the age group 45–64 years, and from 4.9% to 16.8% in elderly patients ≥65 years of age (all p < 0.0005) (S3 Table).
340.Rationale: The passage documents an increase in the median age of hospitalized cases and a relative rise in the proportion of older adults among hospitalizations. However, it provides no data or statements about under-treatment or under-referral of younger patients. The observed shift in age distribution of hospitalized cases could reflect multiple causes (demographic shifts in cases, changes in admission criteria favoring admission of older patients, etc.), but the specific causal claim that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred is not supported by the presented data.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalization rates for dengue cases is primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care settings, which has led to fewer patients requiring hospital admission.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].
The hospitalization rates of persons suspected of dengue viral infection are high, as doctors tend to err on the side of caution and admit them for monitoring.
340.Rationale: While hospitalization rates fell markedly, the passage attributes changes to review and refinement of hospital admission criteria and MOH guidance, not to documented improvements in primary care diagnosis or management. The text does not provide evidence that improved early diagnosis/management in primary care was the primary driver of reduced hospitalizations. Therefore the specific causal attribution to primary care improvements is not derivable from the passage.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
Evidence: In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The CFR ranged from 0% in 2017 to 0.27% in 2006.
About 0.6% to 1.5% of dengue patients in hospital were admitted to the ICU during the study period (Table 1).
The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
340.Rationale: The passage links a major reduction in the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized with documented changes in admission criteria. Simultaneously, measured adverse outcome indicators (case fatality rate, ICU admission proportions, and length of stay) did not show an increase that would suggest worse short-term outcomes; CFR remained low (0% to 0.27%), ICU admission proportions were low (0.6% to 1.5%), and LOS remained stable. From these surveillance data, it is reasonable to infer that adopting more selective admission criteria was not associated with increased adverse in-hospital outcomes over the study period.

340.Option: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013, which may be attributed to both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.
Evidence: the highest proportions were in older adults aged 45–64 years from 2003 to 2012 (range: 50.8% to 100.0%) and elderly persons aged ≥65 years from 2013 to 2017 (range: 47.2% to 55.4%).

older adults (aged ≥45 years) accounted for an increasingly higher proportion over the 15-year period: from 21.0% to 29.9% in the age group 45–64 years, and from 4.9% to 16.8% in elderly patients ≥65 years of age (all p < 0.0005) (S3 Table).

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].
subjective criteria such as severe vomiting or diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, and elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell [15].
340.Rationale: The passage supports the factual component that hospitalized proportions shifted so that elderly persons (≥65 years) comprised the highest proportions from 2013–2017 and that there has been a demographic shift toward older age among hospitalized cases. However, the specific attribution to an MOH guideline to refer all persons aged ≥65 years for hospital evaluation is not stated in the passage. The passage only mentions guidance and criteria that include 'elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell' and other guideline changes, but does not state a blanket referral criterion based solely on age ≥65. Because the option asserts a causal attribution (MOH guideline to refer ≥65) that is not evidenced in the passage, the option is not fully supported.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics.
Evidence: In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 136 in 2005 to 69 in 2013 (Fig 4).
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized during these three epidemic periods was 93.2% in 2004–2005, 58.1% in 2007 and 28.9% in 2013–2014.
340.Rationale: The passage documents that hospitals revised admission criteria after the 2004–2005 epidemic, that the proportion of cases hospitalized fell dramatically thereafter, and that total hospital bed-years attributable to dengue halved between 2005 and 2013. The data also show much lower proportions hospitalized during the 2013–2014 epidemic compared with 2004–2005, supporting the inference that more selective admission practices reduced bed utilization even during large epidemics.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.
Evidence: MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
341.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that microfilaria (MF) prevalence fell very rapidly after MDA (from 26% to <1%) while antibody prevalence declined more slowly (from 80% to 6.4% by 2010). The authors explain that the antibody:MF ratio increased because MF rates fell faster than antibody rates, and they note that anti-filarial antibodies indicate past or present exposure. Together these statements support the inference that antibodies persist longer after clearance of microfilaremia and therefore may reflect past exposure rather than ongoing transmission.

341.Option: The increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio after MDA reflects a higher sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test in detecting past infections compared to the membrane filtration method used for microfilariae.
Evidence: The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
For this reason, it is not possible to use antibody rates to estimate MF prevalence rates following MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage describes that antibody detection (Brugia Rapid) persists after MF declines and explicitly frames the antibody test as a marker of ongoing or prior infection. The observed rise in the antibody:MF ratio reflects that antibody positivity remains when MF (detected by membrane filtration) has fallen. From the passage, one can reasonably derive that the Brugia Rapid antibody test is more likely to detect past infections (i.e., it remains positive when MF are no longer detectable), whereas membrane filtration detects current microfilaremia. Thus the increase in the ratio is consistent with the antibody test being more likely to detect past exposure than the MF assay detects residual infection.

341.Option: The persistence of anti-filarial antibodies in children after MDA cessation indicates that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted in the study population.
Evidence: While the positive test rate for Brugia Rapid in children aged between 3 and 10 years dropped rapidly following MDA, the prevalence and the upper 95% confidence limit dropped below 5% and 10%, respectively, for the first time in 2009, 22 months after the last round of MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
341.Rationale: The passage shows that antibody rates in children fell rapidly and reached low levels (below predefined thresholds) after MDA. It also emphasizes that antibodies mark past or present exposure and clear more slowly than MF, particularly in adults. Persistence of antibodies alone does not prove ongoing transmission because antibodies can reflect past exposure. The passage provides no direct evidence that residual antibodies in children necessarily indicate continued transmission; indeed the data on children suggest rapid declines in antibody prevalence. Therefore the statement that persistence of antibodies in children indicates transmission was not fully interrupted is not supported by the text.

341.Option: The rebound in STH infection rates after MDA cessation suggests that anti-filarial antibodies may also reappear in the population if transmission resumes.
Evidence: In 2010, 34 months after the last round of MDA, the prevalence (45%) was significantly higher than the baseline prevalence (34%) prior to MDA (p<0.01).
Hookworm rates rebounded to 7% at 34 months after the last MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
341.Rationale: The passage documents rebound of soil-transmitted helminth (STH) prevalence after cessation of MDA, and separately documents the decline in anti-filarial antibodies after MDA. However, there is no data or statement linking STH rebound to filarial transmission dynamics or to reappearance of anti-filarial antibodies. Anti-filarial antibodies are parasite-specific markers; STH trends do not provide direct evidence about filarial transmission. The suggestion that antibody levels would reappear if transmission resumed is plausible biologically but is not demonstrated or supported by the data in this passage, so it cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

341.Option: The rapid decline in microfilaria prevalence following MDA suggests that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure.
Evidence: MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.
For this reason, it is not possible to use antibody rates to estimate MF prevalence rates following MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage indicates the opposite of the option's claim: antibodies detected by the Brugia Rapid test persist longer and therefore are markers of past or present exposure, whereas MF detection falls rapidly and more closely reflects active microfilaremia. The authors explicitly state that MF rates fell more rapidly than antibody rates and that antibody rates cannot be used to estimate MF prevalence post-MDA. Thus it is incorrect to infer that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure.

342.Option: The high level of amino acid variation (36.4%) in mitochondrial protein-coding genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris is consistent with recent cross-infection events between the two host species.
Evidence: The amino acid sequence differences ranged from 13.6–62.5%, with COX1 being the most conserved protein, and ATP8 the least conserved.
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.

Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets ... reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris and also the grouping of these two members of Trichuris with T. spiralis (Trichocephalida), with absolute support, to the exclusion of members of the Dorylaimida and Mermithida (Fig. 2).
342.Rationale: The passage reports substantial amino acid and nucleotide divergence between human- and pig-derived Trichuris and describes 'clear genetic distinctiveness' with strong phylogenetic support. High genetic divergence is not evidence for recent cross-infection events (which would predict low divergence and evidence of gene flow). The option's numeric value (36.4% amino acid variation) is not an explicitly reported summary figure in the passage, and the interpretation that high divergence is 'consistent with recent cross-infection' contradicts the passage's presentation of distinctiveness. Thus this inference cannot be derived from the provided data.

342.Option: The genetic similarity in the rrnL gene between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris suggests that they are closely related and likely represent a single, host-generalist species.
Evidence: Comparison of the mt genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris showed that the rrnS and rrnL were the two most conserved genes (Table 5).
The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.

Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades, and the trees produced using the three different methods were essentially the same in topology (Fig. 3).
342.Rationale: Although rrnL and rrnS are described as the 'most conserved' genes, the passage also reports substantial rrnL differences (89 nucleotide positions) between human- and pig-derived samples and phylogenetic analyses that 'revealed strong support for the separation ... into two distinct clades.' These findings support distinctness rather than a single host-generalist species. Therefore the passage does not support the inference that rrnL similarity implies a single host-generalist species.

342.Option: The substantial genetic divergence observed in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris supports the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.
Evidence: Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
The amino acid sequence differences ranged from 13.6–62.5%, with COX1 being the most conserved protein, and ATP8 the least conserved.

Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets ... reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ... with absolute support, to the exclusion of members of the Dorylaimida and Mermithida (Fig. 2).
The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades...
342.Rationale: The passage documents substantial sequence divergence across the mitochondrial genome (32.87% nucleotide difference), wide-ranging amino acid differences in protein-coding genes, and multiple, concordant phylogenetic analyses (mt protein concatenates and rrnL) that 'reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness' and produced 'strong support' for separation into two clades. These lines of molecular evidence directly support the inference that the human- and pig-derived Trichuris represent distinct species.

342.Option: The high A+T content and AT-rich non-coding regions in the mitochondrial genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris indicate that these parasites have evolved a shared mechanism for mitochondrial DNA replication.
