387.Option: The publication of the ARRIVE guidelines has resulted in a statistically significant improvement in the reporting of animal model characteristics and experimental conditions.
Evidence: Before ARRIVE guidelines became widely available, approximately 51% of papers on animal models evaluating drugs for Chagas disease included a statement about compliance with local or international guidelines for experimentation with animals. This percentage increased to 66% (p = 0.26) after ARRIVE guidelines were published.

Reporting of protocol revision and approval by an Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) increased from 26% (10 / 39) to 41% (18 / 44) after ARRIVE guidelines publication (p = 0.22) (Table 7).

Macroenvironmental information such as room temperature was detailed in nearly the same proportion before and after ARRIVE guidelines publication (10 / 39 vs. 16 / 44), but reporting of light/dark cycle increased from 23% (9 / 39) to 43% (19 / 44) respectively, although not statistically significant.

In addition, more than half of the analyzed studies in both periods failed to mention any macroenvironmental parameters or included ambiguous information. However, the percentage decreased from 69% (27 / 39) to 57% (25 / 44) after publication of ARRIVE guidelines (Table 5), but this change was not enough to reach statistical significance.
387.Rationale: The passage provides specific comparisons and p-values showing that increases in several reporting items (ethical statements, IACUC approval, environmental reporting) did not reach statistical significance. Therefore the claim that ARRIVE publication resulted in a statistically significant improvement in reporting is not supported by the data presented.

387.Option: Despite the publication of the ARRIVE guidelines, there was no significant improvement in the reporting of key experimental details such as animal husbandry conditions, microbiological status, and environmental parameters in studies evaluating new chemotherapy for Chagas disease.
Evidence: Basic details about animal age, weight and microbiological status and source (Tables 3 and 4) were not provided in almost half of the reviewed publications, both pre- or post publication of ARRIVE guidelines.

Macroenvironmental information such as room temperature was detailed in nearly the same proportion before and after ARRIVE guidelines publication (10 / 39 vs. 16 / 44), but reporting of light/dark cycle increased from 23% (9 / 39) to 43% (19 / 44) respectively, although not statistically significant.

In addition, more than half of the analyzed studies in both periods failed to mention any macroenvironmental parameters or included ambiguous information. However, the percentage decreased from 69% (27 / 39) to 57% (25 / 44) after publication of ARRIVE guidelines (Table 5), but this change was not enough to reach statistical significance.

Concerning microenvironmental conditions, access to food and water (mostly ad libitum), was reported in same proportion (17 / 39 vs. 21 / 44) in papers published before or after ARRIVE guidelines appeared. Similarly to macroenvironmental details, more than half of the analyzed studies (22 / 39 and 27 / 44) failed to provide any microenvironmental information for both periods of time (Table 6).
387.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that substantial proportions of studies before and after ARRIVE failed to report critical details (age, weight, microbiological status, macro- and microenvironmental parameters), and where reporting increased the changes were not statistically significant. Thus it is reasonable to infer that publication of ARRIVE did not lead to significant improvement in reporting of these key experimental details.

387.Option: The use of standardized animal models and consistent reporting practices could significantly reduce the high failure rate of Chagas disease drug candidates in clinical trials.
Evidence: Variability of animal models for Chagas disease, and the heterogeneity in readout methods used to define drug response (e.g. parasitemia, PCR in blood, PCR in blood and in tissues) have led to highly variable results when evaluating new drug candidates.

This wide gap between results in preclinical research and high failure rate in clinical trials may be explained, in part, by the scarce information contained in most experiments that employ laboratory animals, in which crucial information related to species, strains, genetic background, microbiological status, husbandry conditions and procedures are not properly described or even missed in some occasions.

detailed reporting of animal use in scientific papers has a direct connection with the “3Rs’ Principles” of humane use of animals in scientific research (i.e. Replacement, Reduction and Refinement) since a complete and systematic description of what was done and what was found in the experiments may avoid unnecessary repetition [5], facilitate systematic revisions before new essays involving animals are carried out,[6] and simplify comparisons and data integration from different studies [7].

The main objectives of the ARRIVE guidelines are to improve the quality of animal use reporting in scientific publications to maximize the availability and utility of information gained from every animal in every experiment, preventing unnecessary animal use, and to allow an accurate critical review of animal experiments, making results easier to compare among different research groups to validate and contextualize results to promote translational research to patients’ benefit [8].
387.Rationale: The passage links variability in animal models and poor reporting to inconsistent preclinical results and a gap leading to clinical trial failures, and it states that detailed, standardized reporting facilitates comparison, integration, and translational research. From these premises, it is a logical epidemiological inference that adopting standardized models and reporting practices could reduce translational failure rates.

387.Option: The ARRIVE guidelines have led to a substantial increase in the use of refined procedures and anticipated endpoints in Chagas disease drug studies.
Evidence: The number of papers that mentioned refined strategies and procedures increased from 15% (6 / 39) to 23% (10 / 44) after ARRIVE guidelines publication.

Ten publications mentioned proceedings which would require anesthesia/analgesia (such as terminal bleeding. bioluminescence imaging techniques); seven of these reports (70%) described the procedures only as "under anesthesia" or "with anesthetized mouse" without further details, while two of them specifically reported isofluorane use.
387.Rationale: While there was a small increase in mention of refined strategies (from 15% to 23%), the passage characterizes the increase as modest and notes poor detail in descriptions (most just said 'under anesthesia' without specifics). The evidence does not support the claim of a substantial increase in refined procedures and anticipated endpoints.

387.Option: The lack of detailed reporting in preclinical studies is primarily due to researchers' unwillingness to comply with ethical standards, rather than systemic or logistical challenges.
Evidence: This wide gap between results in preclinical research and high failure rate in clinical trials may be explained, in part, by the scarce information contained in most experiments that employ laboratory animals, in which crucial information related to species, strains, genetic background, microbiological status, husbandry conditions and procedures are not properly described or even missed in some occasions.

The dates for the search period after ARRIVE guidelines publication were set one year after ARRIVE guidelines actual publication to allow scientific community (researchers, reviewers, editors and journals) to adopt them (Fig 1).

Before ARRIVE guidelines became widely available, approximately 51% of papers on animal models evaluating drugs for Chagas disease included a statement about compliance with local or international guidelines for experimentation with animals. This percentage increased to 66% (p = 0.26) after ARRIVE guidelines were published.
387.Rationale: The passage documents poor reporting but does not attribute this primarily to researchers' unwillingness to comply with ethics; it instead suggests scarce information and variability, and notes an allowance period for the scientific community to adopt guidelines. There is no evidence provided that unwillingness is the main cause; thus the causal claim is unsupported by the passage.

388.Option: Transmission of L. donovani is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts who are not eligible for treatment, and vector-related interventions are the intervention of choice from an epidemiological perspective because they can reduce the prevalence of asymptomatic infections.
Evidence: These results suggest that transmission of KA is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts who are not eligible for treatment.
Vector-based interventions reduce the prevalence of symptomatic and asymptomatic infections, which implies that the incidences of KA and PKDL were also reduced.

The reason for this minor contribution of symptomatically infected hosts to the overall transmission is simple: their prevalence is too low compared to the abundance of asymptomatically infected hosts.
388.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that transmission is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts and explains why symptomatic hosts contribute little to transmission (their prevalence is low relative to asymptomatics). It also shows that vector-based interventions reduce both symptomatic and asymptomatic infection prevalence and can even lead to elimination under plausible reductions in vector parameters. Therefore, the inference that transmission is driven by asymptomatic, untreatable hosts and that vector-related interventions are epidemiologically preferred (because they reduce asymptomatic infections) is directly supported by the text.

388.Option: The elimination of VL is only possible through a combination of early case detection and improved treatment regimens, as these interventions directly reduce the duration of infectiousness in symptomatic individuals.
Evidence: Fig. 2A shows that the different treatment regimens can only reduce the prevalence of symptomatic infections, with almost no effect on the prevalence of asymptomatic infections.
These results suggest that transmission of KA is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts who are not eligible for treatment.
Even under overoptimistic assumptions for all treatment parameters, the treatment does not seem to be capable of reducing the prevalence of symptomatic infection below 0.001% (scenario 2).
388.Rationale: While treatment and early detection reduce symptomatic disease, the passage shows these interventions have almost no effect on asymptomatic infections, which the model identifies as the main driver of transmission. The authors therefore conclude treatment-focused strategies alone are insufficient to eliminate transmission. The claim that elimination is only possible through treatment/early detection contradicts the model results presented in the passage.

388.Option: The high prevalence of LST-positive individuals in the population suggests that cellular immunity is long-lasting and that natural recovery from infection provides durable protection against reinfection.
Evidence: LST-positivity lasted on average 1/(ρHC+μH) = 300 days (approx. 95% CI: 255 to 348 days).
We assumed that LST-positivity could be lost because the consecutive PCR and DAT results of several KalaNet participants showed re-infection (first positive, then negative and then positive again).
LST-positivity (suggesting a state of cellular immunity) may remain over a period of 1/ρHC days, after which recovered humans (RHC) again become negative for all diagnostic markers (→SH).
388.Rationale: The passage quantifies LST-positivity duration at about 300 days and explicitly notes that LST-positivity can be lost and that re-infections were observed. Thus the passage does not support that cellular immunity is 'long-lasting' or that recovery confers durable protection against reinfection; instead it presents evidence of waning LST-positivity and re-infection. Therefore the option's inference is not supported by the passage.

388.Option: The use of treated bed nets may not provide this efficacy, as the majority of L. donovani transmission occurs outside the house, which limits the impact of indoor vector control measures.
Evidence: Indoor spraying, in particular, reduces sand fly densities, also long-lasting insecticide-treated bed nets and to a minor extent environmental management [22], [23].
However, the recent community intervention trial KalaNet failed to demonstrate an effect of long-lasting insecticide-treated nets on the incidences of L. donovani infection and VL [24].
388.Rationale: The passage reports that long-lasting insecticide-treated nets did not demonstrate an effect on incidence in the KalaNet trial, which supports that bed nets may be ineffective in that study. However, the specific causal claim that this is because the majority of transmission occurs outside the house is not stated or supported by the passage. The authors do not provide evidence attributing the nets' lack of effect to outdoor transmission; therefore the option adds an unsupported explanation and cannot be fully derived from the passage.

389.Option: The tissue-specific up-regulation of the PF07598 gene family in the liver and blood, but not consistently in the kidney, suggests that this gene family plays a role in organ-specific colonization during leptospirosis.
Evidence: All members of this gene family were up-regulated in blood and liver to varying degrees, with LA_3490 and LA_3388, both containing secretory signal peptide sequences, being most highly up-regulated (more than ∼1000-fold); all members of this gene family were up-regulated in the circulation and liver to varying degrees.
In contrast, up-regulation of other members this gene family significantly varied among experimental animals in kidney (Fig. 5C–E).
All full-length members of this family (PF07598/DUF1561) are predicted to have secretory signal peptides, although degenerate forms do occur.
389.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that PF07598 paralogs are strongly up-regulated in blood and liver during acute infection while showing inconsistent up-regulation in kidney. The fact that these proteins are predicted to be secreted (signal peptides) and show tissue-specific transcriptional patterns supports the epidemiological/biological inference that they may function in processes relevant to those tissues (e.g., organ-specific colonization or interaction). This inference follows logically from the observed tissue-specific expression pattern and predicted extracellular localization.

389.Option: The shared presence of the PF07598 gene family in pathogenic Leptospira and Bartonella species, combined with their similar expression patterns in host tissues, implies convergent evolution of virulence mechanisms in these distantly related pathogens.
Evidence: The PF07598 gene family has also been recently described in the unrelated α-proteobacteria species Bartonella bacilliformis and Bartonella australis.

All 12 members of the leptospiral PF07598 gene family were analyzed for in vivo expression in hamsters acutely infected with virulent, wild type L. interrogans Lai 55601. All members of this gene family were up-regulated in blood and liver to varying degrees, with LA_3490 and LA_3388 ... being most highly up-regulated (more than ∼1000-fold); all members of this gene family were up-regulated in the circulation and liver to varying degrees.

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: While the passage documents that PF07598 paralogs occur in both pathogenic Leptospira and Bartonella and that leptospiral paralogs are highly up-regulated in host blood and liver, it does not provide evidence that the similarity arose via convergent evolution. Convergent evolution is one possible explanation, but the passage does not present the phylogenetic or molecular evolutionary analyses needed to distinguish convergence from other mechanisms (e.g., horizontal gene transfer or shared ancestral retention). Therefore the specific inference of convergent evolution is not derivable solely from the passage.

389.Option: The presence of the PF07598 gene family in both pathogenic Leptospira and Bartonella species indicates that these bacteria share a recent common ancestor with a fully developed virulence mechanism.
Evidence: The PF07598 gene family has also been recently described in the unrelated α-proteobacteria species Bartonella bacilliformis and Bartonella australis.

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes 'great phylogenetic distances separating the genera' that contain the PF07598 family, which argues against the idea of a recent common ancestor shared by Leptospira and Bartonella. The data show a patchy distribution across distantly related taxa but do not support the conclusion of a recent common ancestor with a fully developed virulence mechanism. Thus the specific inference of a recent shared ancestor is not supported by the passage.

389.Option: The identification of a paralogous protein family shared by pathogenic Leptospira and the distantly related pathogens B. bacilliformis and B. australis suggests that horizontal gene transfer may have played a role in the evolution of virulence mechanisms in these bacteria.
Evidence: The PF07598 gene family has also been recently described in the unrelated α-proteobacteria species Bartonella bacilliformis and Bartonella australis.

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage documents a paralogous gene family present in distantly related bacterial genera (Leptospira and Bartonella) and emphasizes the great phylogenetic distances between those genera alongside a patchy distribution restricted to certain animal-infecting species. From genomic/evolutionary reasoning, such a pattern (presence in distant taxa but absence in many closer relatives) is consistent with horizontal gene transfer as a plausible explanation for the shared gene family. The option states this as a suggestion ('may have played a role'), which is a cautious inference that can be reasonably derived from the distributional genomic evidence provided in the passage (though the passage does not prove HGT).

389.Option: The high degree of transcriptional up-regulation of the PF07598 gene family in blood and liver during acute infection suggests that these genes are primarily involved in immune evasion within the host's circulatory system.
Evidence: All 12 members of the leptospiral PF07598 gene family were analyzed for in vivo expression in hamsters acutely infected with virulent, wild type L. interrogans Lai 55601. All members of this gene family were up-regulated in blood and liver to varying degrees, with LA_3490 and LA_3388 ... being most highly up-regulated (more than ∼1000-fold); all members of this gene family were up-regulated in the circulation and liver to varying degrees.
All full-length members of this family (PF07598/DUF1561) are predicted to have secretory signal peptides, although degenerate forms do occur.

There are great phylogenetic distances separating the genera that contain this gene family, but paralogs are restricted to select animal-infecting species within each lineage; suggesting that these proteins may be uniquely related to host adaptation.
389.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence of strong up-regulation of PF07598 genes in blood and liver and notes predicted secretion, which supports that these proteins interact with host environments. However, the specific functional claim that their primary role is 'immune evasion within the host's circulatory system' is not directly demonstrated or stated in the passage. Other functions related to host adaptation, colonization, or tissue-specific interactions could explain the observed expression. Because the passage does not present functional assays or direct evidence demonstrating immune evasion as the primary role, this specific causal conclusion cannot be derived solely from the provided data.

390.Option: The high frequency of HLA-A0201 expression in Latin American populations enables the identification of a broad range of T. cruzi-specific CD8+ T cell responses, making it the most effective strategy for studying immune responses in chronic Chagas disease.
Evidence: Contrasting with the mouse system, a lower and more diffuse pattern of CD8+ T cell responses specific for HLA-A0201-restricted ts peptides in human subjects was found [25].
The six most common HLA supertypes cover one or more HLA allele in >95 of a human population, irrespective of ethnicity [26],[27].
It is noteworthy that responses to HLA-A02 and HLA03 supertype-restricted epitopes covered 77% of the total responses detected, consistent with the high worldwide frequency of these alleles [26].

PBMCs from HLA-A201-positive T. cruzi-infected and uninfected individuals were tested for IFN-γ secretion in response to 28 individual HLA-A0201-binding peptides, showing 60% of individuals (9/15) in G0 responding and 22% of the patients (4/18) in G2–G3 responding to ts peptides (Table 1).
390.Rationale: The passage indicates that A02 (HLA-A02) supertypes account for a large portion of responses and that A2/A3 are common worldwide, and it shows results from HLA-A201+ subjects. However, it does not state that HLA-A0201 frequency is specifically high in Latin American populations nor does it claim that focusing on HLA-A0201 is "the most effective strategy". The text emphasizes coverage by multiple supertypes and uses a supertype-based approach to avoid individual HLA typing. Therefore the strong, region-specific and exclusive claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

390.Option: The broad and multispecific CD8+ T cell response to T. cruzi in humans, compared to the focused response in mice, is primarily due to the greater genetic diversity of HLA alleles in human populations.
Evidence: Contrasting with the mouse system, a lower and more diffuse pattern of CD8+ T cell responses specific for HLA-A0201-restricted ts peptides in human subjects was found [25].

An additional complication of translating the results of studies in mice to the assay of human T cell responses is the large degree of polymorphism of human HLA alleles and the requirement to HLA-type each individual subject prior to attempting to measure T cell responses.

A number of parameters have been implicated in determining which pathogen epitopes are primarily targeted by T cell responses, including the nature of the restricting MHC allele, efficiency of epitope processing and translocation into the endoplasmatic reticulum, the degree of sequence variability in epitopes derived from highly variable pathogens and antigen availability by either cross-presentation of exogenous antigen or processing of intracellular proteins [9]–[11].
390.Rationale: The passage identifies human HLA polymorphism as an important complicating factor and lists multiple parameters that influence immunodominance. While HLA diversity is plausibly a contributor to the broader, multispecific human CD8+ response (supported by the quoted lines), the passage does not provide evidence that HLA diversity is the primary or sole cause. Because the statement asserts a primary causal explanation, it goes beyond what the passage supports and therefore cannot be derived solely from the passage.

390.Option: The low prevalence of IL-2-secreting T cells specific for T. cruzi, despite the presence of functional IFN-γ-secreting T cells, suggests that chronic antigen persistence drives T cells toward a monofunctional, exhausted phenotype with impaired proliferative capacity.
Evidence: The simultaneous assessment of IFN-γ and IL-2 production after stimulation with ts-derived peptides, demonstrates that the CD8+ T cell compartment specific for T. cruzi displays a very low level of polyfunctional cytokine responses with a predominance of T cells secreting IFN-γ alone and very low prevalence of single IL-2-secreting or dual IFN-γ/IL-2 secreting T cells.
Less than 30% of the individuals evaluated displayed positive IL-2 and IFN-γ ELISPOT responses while IL-2 responses alone was not detected (Fig. 2A).
measurement of other T-cell functions, such as IL-2 secretion, proliferation capacity and cytotoxic activity is also informative [40].

This apparent impairment in CD8+ T cell responses was associated with an increased frequency of fully differentiated memory (CD27−CD28−CD45RA−) CD8+ T cells and a high rate of apoptosis, possibly reflecting exhaustion in the CD8+ T cell compartment [8].

This functional pattern of cytokine production in response to HLA-class I-restricted ts epitopes in conjunction with the negative correlation between disease severity and IFN-γ-secreting T cells, found in the present and former studies [7], as well the increased levels of fully differentiated T cells in these patients [8] supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: The passage documents a predominance of IFN-γ-only (monofunctional) T cells with very low IL-2 secretion, notes that IL-2 is informative about proliferative capacity, and links impaired responses to a phenotype (fully differentiated memory cells, high apoptosis) described as "possibly reflecting exhaustion." It also explicitly connects long-term persistence of T. cruzi with degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses. Together these statements support the inference that chronic antigen persistence is associated with a shift toward monofunctional, exhausted T cells with likely impaired proliferative capacity.

390.Option: The impaired IL-2 production in T. cruzi-specific T cells is a direct result of heart failure in advanced Chagas disease, rather than a consequence of chronic immune activation.
Evidence: Positive IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects, and was essentially undetectable in the G1 group upon stimulation with ts peptides (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 28±8, range = 15–55; mean SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in media alone = 6±5), [Fig. 2A].

Low IL-2 production was not confined to the CD8+ T cell compartment as stimulation with a T. cruzi lysate, which had been previously shown to induce both CD4+ T and CD8+ T cell responses [7],22 also failed to induce IL-2 secretion (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 65±50, range = 25–153) [Fig. 2A].
This functional pattern of cytokine production ... supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: The passage states that IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of clinical status, indicating impaired IL-2 production is not confined to those with advanced cardiac disease. The authors instead link functional degradation to long-term antigen persistence and features consistent with exhaustion. There is no evidence in the passage that heart failure directly causes the IL-2 impairment, so the option's causal claim is not supported.

390.Option: The presence of IFN-γ-secreting T cells in chronic Chagas disease patients indicates that the immune system is effectively controlling parasite replication, preventing further tissue damage.
Evidence: Even in cases in which such responses are stimulated sufficiently to control the acute infection, T. cruzi is not completely cleared but instead, persists in infected hosts for decades.

Supporting the hypothesis that disease is less severe when immune control is more efficient we have shown that chronic chagasic subjects with no or mild clinical disease have significantly higher frequency of interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) producing T cells specific for T. cruzi than do individuals with more severe disease [7],[8].
This functional pattern of cytokine production ... supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: While IFN-γ-producing T cells are detectable and are associated with less severe disease, the passage explicitly states that even when immune responses can control acute infection, the parasite persists for decades. Thus the mere presence of IFN-γ-secreting T cells does not imply effective control of replication sufficient to prevent ongoing persistence and potential tissue damage. The passage does not support the strong claim that IFN-γ presence equals effective control and prevention of further damage.

391.Option: The temporal pattern of dengue in Port Sudan showed a bi-cyclic trend, with two transmission peaks: a short peak in winter (November–December) and a longer peak in summer (June–August), both preceded by peaks in mosquito density.
Evidence: The M-shaped curves have two peaks. The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%).
The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%). It was preceded by a higher peak of P/P two months earlier in May (0.66) and by the highest peak of larval ration in April (1.81).
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
391.Rationale: The passage clearly documents two transmission peaks (a November P/P leading to a December IgM peak, and an earlier P/P peak in May preceding a July IgM peak), so the bi-cyclic element and the fact that peaks are preceded by increases in mosquito density are supported. However, the option additionally asserts that the summer peak is a "longer peak in summer (June–August)." The passage provides no evidence that the summer transmission peak extends across June–August; in fact it notes very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of the vector in September with zero IgM in September and October, which argues against a sustained long summer peak. Because the claim of a "longer peak in summer (June–August)" is not supported (and is contradicted by provided data), the overall option is not derivable from the passage.

391.Option: The low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods suggests that these areas have higher herd immunity due to earlier exposure to dengue.
Evidence: BI, HI and P/P indices were lower in the upper class neighborhoods (65, 51 and .24), as compared to the other two strata (91, 66 and .38, respectively).
A significant difference between the high stratum and the other two strata was shown on incidence rates (F = 70.97, d.f. = 2, p<0.001), 0.0031 versus 0.0036 each, respectively.
391.Rationale: The passage shows that entomological indices and incidence rates are LOWER in the upper (high) class neighborhoods and HIGHER in the middle and lower strata (incidence 0.0031 in high vs 0.0036 in the other two strata). Thus the premise of the option (low incidence in lower and middle class neighborhoods) is contradicted by the data. Furthermore, the proposed explanation (higher herd immunity from earlier exposure) is not supported by any direct evidence in the passage; the study did not present data on prior immunity levels or historical exposure sufficient to infer herd immunity. Therefore this causal inference is not derivable from the passage.

391.Option: The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
Evidence: The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
It is possible that Ae. aegypti dwells in different breeding containers during September and/or there are desiccated eggs of the mosquito in the breeding containers.
The global emergence of dengue is linked to increasing travel of viremic people as well as dispersal of its main vectors (i.e. Aedes aegypti and Ae. albopictus) into new locations [3]–[5].
391.Rationale: The passage documents an unexpected absence of detectable Ae. aegypti larvae/pupae in September and explicitly offers alternative explanations (hidden breeding or desiccated eggs), indicating uncertainty about whether the vector truly disappears. Given this uncertainty and the broader context noting vector dispersal as important for dengue emergence, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the passage that further research is warranted to determine if there is a true disappearance and, if so, the mechanisms and sources of reintroduction. The recommendation for additional investigation follows directly from the observations and stated uncertainties in the text.

391.Option: The bi-cyclic transmission pattern in Port Sudan is driven primarily by seasonal monsoon winds, which facilitate the dispersal of Ae. aegypti from neighboring countries.
Evidence: The M-shaped curves have two peaks. The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%).
The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%). It was preceded by a higher peak of P/P two months earlier in May (0.66) and by the highest peak of larval ration in April (1.81).

Average monthly records from November 2008 to October 2009 were obtained from the meteorological authority. Meteorological parameters (minimum and maximum temperature, relative humidity, precipitation and, evaporation rates, wind speed and direction) were recorded on daily basis in two stations in Port Sudan.
The global emergence of dengue is linked to increasing travel of viremic people as well as dispersal of its main vectors (i.e. Aedes aegypti and Ae. albopictus) into new locations [3]–[5].
391.Rationale: While the passage documents a bi-cyclic pattern and reports meteorological data (including wind), it provides no evidence that seasonal monsoon winds drive the bi-cyclic pattern or that Ae. aegypti dispersal into the city is mediated by such winds. The passage attributes global spread to travel and vector dispersal in general but does not link local seasonal wind patterns to introductions or to the observed temporal pattern. Therefore the specific causal claim that monsoon winds are the primary driver of the bi-cyclic pattern is not supported by the data presented and cannot be derived from the passage.

391.Option: The high seroprevalence of IgM in July (8.14%) was likely due to secondary dengue infection in adults aged 18–39, who are more vulnerable to severe disease.
Evidence: The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%).
Among these age groups, higher dengue attack rates were shown in adults aged 18–39 and above 40 years, 2.14% (17/791) and 1.77% (14/791), respectively.
A total of 791 individual blood specimens was tested using PanBio Capture/Indirect IgM ELISA (Table 3).
391.Rationale: The passage shows a July IgM peak and reports that adults 18–39 had higher overall age-group attack rates in the serosurvey, but it does not provide any data differentiating primary vs secondary infections (no IgG/IgM paired data or serotype history), nor does it present evidence that adults 18–39 experienced secondary infections or were specifically more vulnerable to severe disease in this outbreak. The assertion that the July IgM peak was "likely due to secondary dengue infection" and that adults 18–39 are "more vulnerable to severe disease" is not supported by the passage and therefore is not derivable from the presented data.

392.Option: Visiting workers in Al Ahsa exhibit a stronger immune response to sandfly saliva because they are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels and more lesions, and that IgG against Ph. papatasi saliva is associated with higher risk of L. major infection. However, it does not state that the stronger antibody response in visiting workers is because they were more likely to have been previously infected (i.e., infected prior to arrival). The data presented are cross-sectional among patients and residents and show higher antibody levels and lesion counts in non-locals, but no evidence is given about timing of infections or prior infection history before arrival. Therefore the causal claim that stronger response is due to prior infection cannot be derived from the passage.

392.Option: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites allows for the detection of recent exposure but does not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection.
Evidence: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites [6–10] allows for the study of temporal changes in transmission risk and the efficacy of vector control programmes [11].
Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the antibody response is transient and that this property permits study of temporal changes (supporting detection of recent exposure). That portion of the option is supported. However, the claim that it "does not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection" is not stated in the passage. In fact, the passage also reports an association between anti-PpSP32 (IgG) and higher risk of L. major infection, and compares antibody levels between infected and uninfected individuals. The passage does not provide evidence that the transient antibody measure fails to reflect long-term risk. Thus the latter half of the statement is not derivable from the provided text.

392.Option: The higher antibody response to sandfly saliva in visiting workers compared to long-term residents in Al Ahsa suggests that the migrant population is highly exposed to sandfly bites and less immune to cutaneous leishmaniasis, while residents may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).

Although such differences did not correlate with the levels of anti-PpSP32 (S3 Fig.), patients from the visiting labour group presented in general a higher number of lesions compared to the residents (S2 Table).
Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels and that they tended to present with more lesions. It also states that repeated exposure produces antibodies (so higher antibodies can indicate higher exposure). However, the specific claims that migrants are "less immune" to cutaneous leishmaniasis or that residents have "developed tolerance due to long-term exposure" are not supported by the data presented. The authors explicitly note that differences in lesion number did not correlate with anti-PpSP32 levels, and they do not present immunologic or longitudinal evidence of tolerance or reduced immunity in residents. Therefore the additional causal interpretation about immunity/tolerance is speculative and not derivable from the passage.

392.Option: The significantly higher number of lesions in visiting workers compared to local residents in Al Ahsa is primarily due to genetic susceptibility differences among the migrant population.
Evidence: Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).

Although such differences did not correlate with the levels of anti-PpSP32 (S3 Fig.), patients from the visiting labour group presented in general a higher number of lesions compared to the residents (S2 Table).
392.Rationale: While the passage documents a higher lesion count among visiting workers, it contains no data or statements about genetic susceptibility or any genetic analyses. The attribution of the observed difference "primarily" to genetic differences is not supported by the evidence provided. Other explanations (e.g., differences in exposure history, access to care, timing of infection) are possible but were not established; genetics is not addressed and therefore cannot be inferred from the passage.

393.Option: Elevated levels of IL-6 in severe visceral leishmaniasis are primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections, as indicated by high serum levels of LPS and FABP2.
Evidence: Recent reports have indicated a link between bacterial co-infection and VL disease severity [20,23,24]. To examine if bacterial infections could be influencing the release of the inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines, we measured levels of the LPS, FABP2, MIF and sCD14 molecules.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).

Further stratification of the cytokine profiles to allow comparison between classical VL and severe VL presentations revealed that IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
393.Rationale: The option asserts that IL-6 elevation in severe VL is primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections indicated by high LPS and FABP2. The study measured LPS and FABP2 specifically to assess bacterial involvement and found that "LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment," which contradicts the claim that high LPS/FABP2 indicate bacterial-driven IL-6. While IL-6 is elevated in severe VL, the passage does not provide evidence that this elevation is primarily driven by bacterial co-infection or by elevated LPS/FABP2; in fact, the LPS/FABP2 data argue against that mechanism.

393.Option: IL-6 levels are elevated in severe visceral leishmaniasis due to a failure in the regulation of IFN-γ production, which leads to uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: classical VL patients present an impairment of IL-2, IFN-γ and IL-12 production from T cells specific to leishmania antigens in vitro, that is restored after successful treatment [6–14]. Interestingly, patients with active VL have high levels of IFN-γ and IL-12 in their serum, which theoretically could help to control the infection.
High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents both elevated IFN-γ in serum and elevated IL-6 in severe disease, but it does not provide evidence that IL-6 elevation is caused by a failure to regulate IFN-γ. The authors explicitly state cytokine elevations "could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity," indicating uncertainty about causality. No direct data link dysregulated IFN-γ production as the causal mechanism driving IL-6 elevation, tissue damage, or severity in the presented results. Therefore the causal statement in the option is not supported by the passage.

393.Option: The presence of sCD14 in serum is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection in visceral leishmaniasis patients, as it is elevated during active disease and decreases after treatment.
Evidence: Sepsis caused by bacteria infection is characterized by the elevation of serum levels of lipolysaccharide (LPS) and sCD14 as a consequence of bacterial spread to the blood [19].

However, sCD14 and MIF, molecules associated with TLR recognition, were higher in the active disease group (2,882 ng/ml and 39 ng/ml, respectively) than DTH+ group (1,348 ng/ml and 19 ng/ml, respectively). Furthermore, circulating levels of sCD14 and MIF decreased with treatment (1,820 ng/ml and 17 ng/ml, respectively) (Fig 4C and 4D).
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: While sCD14 is elevated in active VL and decreases after treatment, the passage provides mixed evidence about its interpretation as a marker of bacterial co-infection. The authors note that sepsis is characterized by elevations of both LPS and sCD14, but in this study "LPS and FABP2 levels were similar" across groups, arguing against widespread measurable endotoxemia. The authors also caution by reporting that "sCD14 levels were not...associated with disease severity/ mortality" and suggest sCD14 "could directly participate in modulating the immune response against L. infantum." Thus, the elevation of sCD14 may reflect VL-associated immune activation rather than reliably indicating bacterial co-infection; the passage does not support the stronger claim that sCD14 is a reliable biomarker of bacterial co-infection in VL patients.

393.Option: The systemic release of cytokines, particularly IL-6, is associated with disease severity and mortality in visceral leishmaniasis, independent of bacterial co-infection.
Evidence: Further stratification of the cytokine profiles to allow comparison between classical VL and severe VL presentations revealed that IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
Remarkably, severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01).
All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: The passage provides clear data linking higher systemic IL-6 levels with severe VL and with death: IL-6 was significantly higher in severe cases (p=0.03), and fatal cases had markedly higher IL-6 (p=0.01), with all fatalities having IL-6 ≥200 pg/ml. At the same time, direct markers of bacterial translocation/endotoxemia (LPS, FABP2) were not elevated, and sCD14—though increased—was not associated with mortality. Together, these findings support the inference that cytokine release, particularly IL-6, is associated with severity and mortality in VL and that this association is not explained by the measured markers of bacterial co-infection in this cohort.

393.Option: The strong correlation between IL-27, IL-10, IL-6, and TNF-α suggests that these cytokines act in a synergistic cascade to drive the progression of visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).
Spleen and liver enlargement, the classical manifestation of VL, both strongly and positively correlated with the circulating levels of IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α (all p≤0.0005).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents strong correlations among IL-27, IL-10, IL-6 and TNF-α and correlations between these cytokines and clinical severity markers, which indicates co-occurrence and association. However, correlation alone does not demonstrate a causal "synergistic cascade to drive progression." The authors explicitly acknowledge uncertainty about directionality: cytokine levels "could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity." Therefore the stronger causal claim of a synergistic cascade driving progression is not directly supported by the presented data.

394.Option: The reproduction number in Chile fluctuated around 1.0 from early August 2020 onward, indicating that the epidemic was in a state of equilibrium with no net growth or decline in transmission.
Evidence: This was followed by a decline in the reproduction number to less than 1.0 between June 19th-August 9th, 2020.
Since then, the reproduction number has fluctuated around 1.0 with the most recent estimate of R ~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) (Fig 5).
The most recent estimate of R~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) as of November 2nd, 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that R declined to <1.0 between June 19 and August 9 and thereafter 'has fluctuated around 1.0' with the most recent estimate ~0.96. Epidemiologically, a reproduction number fluctuating around 1.0 indicates approximate equilibrium (no sustained net growth or decline). These statements together justify the inference that from early August onward transmission was approximately in balance.

394.Option: The reopening of society in late April 2020 under the 'Safe Return' plan led to a sustained increase in case incidence, which was not mitigated by subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso.
Evidence: the government started to ease the COVID-19 restrictions in late April by reopening the economy under the “Safe Return” plan, including the televised opening of some businesses and stores, as new infections had reduced between 350–500 per day by the end of April, implying an only apparent flattening of the COVID-19 curve [33–35].

This strategy resulted in a new wave of infections; with the virus spreading from more affluent areas of Chile to more impoverished, crowded communities, forcing the government to reimpose lockdown measures in Santiago in mid-May (Fig 1) [23,37,38].
imposing and lifting lockdowns in small geographical areas (municipalities) proved unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies such as the Greater Santiago [36].
We also estimate the reproduction number from May 9th- July 7th, 2020... The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage supports that reopening under 'Safe Return' was followed by a new wave of infections and that localized measures were sometimes unsuccessful. However, it also provides modeled evidence that by July 7 the reproduction number after reimposition of measures was 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84–0.89), i.e. below 1.0. That indicates subsequent measures did reduce transmission. The option asserts that localized lockdowns did not mitigate the sustained increase, which contradicts the reported decline in R to <1.0 by July 7. Therefore the stronger claim that they 'were not mitigated' is not supported by the passage.

394.Option: The initial sub-exponential growth pattern in Chile (p ~0.8) was primarily due to the country’s high population density and urbanization, which limited the effectiveness of early interventions.
Evidence: The incidence curve displays sub-exponential growth dynamics with the scaling of growth parameter (deceleration of growth parameter), p, estimated at 0.77 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.81) and the intrinsic growth rate, r, estimated at 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67, 1.00).

The 28-day calibration period of the GGM model yields an estimated growth rate, r, at 0.80 (95% CI: 0.60, 1.00) and a deceleration of growth parameter, p, at 0.80 (95% CI: 0.70, 0.80), indicating early sub-exponential growth dynamics.
Several socioeconomic, demographic, and political factors make control of the pandemic in Latin America particularly challenging [22–25].
imposing and lifting lockdowns in small geographical areas (municipalities) proved unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies such as the Greater Santiago [36].
394.Rationale: While the passage documents sub-exponential growth (p ≈ 0.77–0.80) and mentions socioeconomic/demographic factors and challenges in municipal-level lockdowns, it does not state that high population density and urbanization were the primary cause of the observed sub-exponential pattern or that these factors 'limited the effectiveness of early interventions' as the main explanation. The attribution in the option is a specific causal claim not supported by the passage text; it cannot be derived solely from the provided information.

394.Option: The implementation of lockdowns and apt social distancing interventions have indeed slowed the spread of the virus.
Evidence: The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.
This was followed by a decline in the reproduction number to less than 1.0 between June 19th-August 9th, 2020.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that early social distancing measures 'significantly slowed down the early spread' with an effect noticeable about two weeks after interventions. Modeled and empirical R estimates show declines to below 1.0 and R=0.87 by July 7, consistent with reduced transmission following interventions. Thus the epidemiological inference that the interventions slowed spread is directly supported by the text.

394.Option: The high positivity rate at the beginning of the epidemic indicates that the government failed to cast a wide enough net to test the masses early in the pandemic, and there were probably many more active cases than those detected by epidemiological surveillance, underestimating the epidemic growth curve.
Evidence: Daily testing and positivity rates for the time period April 9th–November 2nd, 2020, by the reporting date are shown in Fig 8.
The total number of tests performed for this time period were 4,325,617, amongst which 10.9% (47,597) had positive results.
The average number of daily tests was estimated at ~5,460 for April 2020 and ~12,959 for May 2020, a 137% increase.

The testing rate in Chile further increased in June 2020, testing on average ~17,578 individuals per day, followed by a slight decline in July 2020... testing rates continued to increase in August (average ~26,079 tests per day), September (average ~29,663 tests per day), and October (average ~31,821 tests per day), indicating an expanding testing capacity of the country.

The positivity rate (percentage of positive tests among the total number of tests) has fluctuated from a monthly average of ~9.07% (SD: 2.3) in April 2020 to a monthly average of ~4.87% (SD: 0.65) in October 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage provides testing volumes and positivity rates (e.g., ~9.07% in April) and documents expanding testing capacity over time. However, it does not explicitly state that the government 'failed' to test widely early on or quantify the magnitude of undetected cases. While a high positivity rate can suggest limited testing and under-ascertainment, that inference is not explicitly made or quantified in the passage. Therefore, the stronger claim that there were 'probably many more active cases' and that the epidemic growth curve was underestimated cannot be definitively derived from the provided text alone.

395.Option: The level of knowledge about rabies and its control measures is directly associated with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign, indicating that educational campaigns should be prioritized to improve vaccination rates.
Evidence: The vaccination uptake was significantly associated with the knowledge of the dog owners about rabies (p<0.001) and its control measures (p<0.05) (Table 6).

Of the 13 independent variables that had an association (p-value less than 0.25) with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign in the univariable analyses (Table 1 and Table 6), only five variables were retained in the final multivariable model.
Regency, having female dogs for breeding, economic value of dogs, income of dog owners, and accessibility of the village were significantly associated with the uptake of vaccination (Table 7).
395.Rationale: While the passage reports a univariable association between owners' knowledge and vaccination uptake (supporting that knowledge correlates with uptake), the multivariable analysis retained other factors (regency, female breeding dogs, dog economic value, owner income, and village accessibility) as the independent predictors of 2012 vaccination uptake. The passage therefore does not support the stronger causal claim that knowledge is the primary determinant or that educational campaigns alone should be prioritized above the other identified predictors. The implication that education should be prioritized is not directly supported by the multivariable results presented.

395.Option: Geographical accessibility is one of the important predictors of vaccination uptake among dog owners, and targeted interventions in villages with poor accessibility may increase vaccination uptake in the future.
Evidence: Villages that can only be reached by foot or motorcycle, which have no public transportation facility, were categorized as villages with ‘poor accessibility’.

The odds of having a high level of knowledge of rabies control measures was higher in the following situations: for dog owners who lived in villages with good accessibility (OR = 2.14; 95% CI = 1.07–4.27) compared to poor accessibility...

the uptake of vaccination was higher if the dog owners had a monthly income of more than 1 million Rupiah (OR = 2.39; 95%CI = 1.10–5.20) and lived in a village with a good accessibility (OR = 3.84; 95%CI = 1.92–7.67) compared with those having a yearly income less than Rp500,000 and who lived in a village with poor accessibility.
395.Rationale: The multivariable model identifies village accessibility as an independent predictor of vaccination uptake, with substantially higher odds of uptake in villages with good versus poor accessibility (OR = 3.84). This supports the epidemiological inference that geographical accessibility materially influences participation in the vaccination campaign. It is therefore reasonable, based on the presented data, to infer that targeting interventions to improve access in poorly accessible villages could increase vaccination uptake.

395.Option: The low vaccination coverage in Flores Island is primarily due to the lack of trust in the vaccine's efficacy, as many dog owners expressed skepticism about its effectiveness.
Evidence: For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%).
Other reasons, of minor importance, included the lack of belief in the vaccine efficacy (13%), and the young age of the dog at the time of the vaccination campaign (6%).
the uptake rate of the dog vaccination measure has been low, with an average vaccination coverage of around 53% of the registered dogs during the 2000–2011 vaccination campaigns.
395.Rationale: Although some owners cited lack of belief in vaccine efficacy (13%), the two most common reasons were lack of information (40%) and difficulty catching dogs (37%). Thus the data do not support the assertion that skepticism about vaccine efficacy is the primary cause of low coverage; it was a relatively minor reported reason. Therefore the option's claim is not supported by the survey results.

395.Option: The high uptake of post-exposure treatment among dog-bite victims indicates that the public health system in Flores Island is well-equipped to manage rabies exposure and prevent fatalities.
Evidence: Of the 450 dog owners interviewed, 89 (20%) reported that at least one of their family members had been bitten by a suspected rabid dog during the last fourteen years.
Of these 89 bite cases, 75 (84%) cleaned the wound, and 50 (56%) received vaccination.
Until 2012, 96 human cases of rabies were officially registered by the Public Health Department, with the highest number of cases in Manggarai regency (27 cases)...
395.Rationale: The passage shows that only 56% of reported bite cases received vaccination (and 84% cleaned the wound), which is not uniformly high for post-exposure prophylaxis. Moreover, the survey provides no direct data on health-system capacity, case fatality prevention, or outcomes; it also reports continued human rabies cases historically. Therefore one cannot conclude from these data alone that the public health system is well-equipped to manage exposures and prevent fatalities.

395.Option: The culling of roaming dogs in Manggarai Regency has been effective in reducing rabies transmission and should be expanded to other regencies as a primary control strategy.
Evidence: In response, the Flores Island government have implemented a comprehensive control campaign since 2000. This control campaign is based on a combination of control measures, including mass vaccination of dogs, culling of roaming dogs, placing imported dogs in quarantine, and giving pre- and post-exposure treatment to humans.
Despite the initial control measures applied, which entailed the culling of the entire dog population in and around the affected villages (1998–1999), rabies spread to other regencies of the island.
The proportion of dog owners that had culled their dogs was significantly higher in Manggarai (45%) than in Sikka (27%) (p<0.001).

Nevertheless, the uptake rate of the dog vaccination measure has been low, with an average vaccination coverage of around 53% ... This value is lower than the 70% coverage of the complete dog population, which is recommended to maintain the control of rabies between annual vaccination campaigns.
However, this campaign has not yet been successful in eliminating rabies from Flores Island.
395.Rationale: Although culling has been practiced (more commonly in Manggarai), the passage explicitly notes that initial culling did not prevent spread and that the overall campaign has not eliminated rabies. The data do not demonstrate that culling was effective in reducing transmission; no outcomes showing reduced human or dog rabies incidence attributable to culling are provided. Therefore expanding culling as a primary control strategy is not supported by the survey data.

396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue cases in Peru is primarily driven by human movement and population density, with weather patterns playing a secondary role in determining transmission intensity.
Evidence: With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
Human movement across the border may provide a dengue entry point into Peru.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts population centers (western coast) with the actual pattern of highest dengue incidence (northeastern Peru), and repeatedly emphasizes that a coupling of temperature and humidity is an important determinant of location, timing and intensity of transmission. While the passage acknowledges human movement can provide an entry point, it does not support the claim that human movement and population density are the primary drivers with weather secondary. Thus the option overstates the role of human movement/population and contradicts the passage's stated conclusion about temperature-humidity coupling.

396.Option: The coupling of temperature and humidity is a critical determinant of dengue transmission potential, with higher humidity levels amplifying transmission magnitude when temperature is optimal, and transmission intensity increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal weather-space.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.

High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity. Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.
For mean temperature >25°C, as humidity rises from 60% to 80%, incidence rates accelerate in highest transmission areas, p<0.00001.

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
396.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that temperature-humidity coupling regulates transmission potential and intensity, shows that high humidity (>75–80%) amplifies impact when mean temperature is in the 25–29°C range, and explicitly notes the exponential nature of incidence increase with longer duration in optimal weather conditions. Therefore the option accurately summarizes the overarching relationship supported by the analysis.

396.Option: The relationship between weather and dengue transmission is linear and predictable, with each degree increase in temperature directly proportional to a corresponding increase in case counts.
Evidence: Weather-dengue dynamics are complex, multi-factorial, and non-linear and traditional statistical methods have produced diverse and conflicting perspectives regarding the weather-disease connection [3–5,7,8,11–18].

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
396.Rationale: The passage describes the weather-dengue relationship as complex and explicitly non-linear, and it uses logarithmic scaling because incidence increases exponentially in optimal conditions. It does not support a simple linear, directly proportional relationship per degree increase in temperature.

396.Option: Rainfall is a more significant predictor of dengue transmission in Peru than temperature and humidity, as it directly influences mosquito breeding sites and seasonal onset of epidemics.
Evidence: Temperature and humidity are emerging as weather components with greatest impact on DENV transmission, yet the separate and combined effects of this coupling across a range of ecological conditions remain ill-defined [3,4,7,11].
High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
396.Rationale: The study focuses on temperature and humidity as the primary weather components affecting transmission and repeatedly demonstrates their central role in determining potential and magnitude. Rainfall is not presented in the passage as the primary predictor; thus the option is not supported by the passage.

396.Option: The largest dengue epidemics in Peru occurred in areas with the highest population density, indicating that human population size is the primary determinant of epidemic intensity regardless of weather conditions.
Evidence: With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
Together these areas represent 11% of the Peruvian population and 70% of cases reported during 1994–2012.
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

397.Option: The structural similarity of Ov-TSP-1 to Sm-TSP-2 from S. mansoni implies that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for tegument formation in both species due to conserved function.

397.Option: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 expression in adult O. viverrini results in tegument malformation, including increased vacuolation and a thinner tegument, indicating that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and structural integrity.

From an epidemiological/diagnostic perspective, antigens that are consistently recognized by sera from infected hosts are candidate targets for serodiagnosis. While the study does not present formal diagnostic performance data (sensitivity, specificity), the observed seroreactivity justifies the inference that Ov-TSP-1 is a promising serodiagnostic candidate.

397.Option: The expression of Ov-tsp-1 in all developmental stages of O. viverrini suggests that it plays a role in the initial excystation of metacercariae from fish hosts.

Expression in a stage is necessary but not sufficient to infer a role in a particular biological event (excystation). No assays of excystation, functional disruption at that stage, or mechanistic data connecting Ov-TSP-1 to excystation are provided; therefore the specific functional inference about excystation cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

397.Option: The high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and C. sinensis TSP suggests that Ov-tsp-1 may function as a receptor for host bile components, facilitating parasite survival in the biliary tree.

398.Option: Since FZT infections involve multiple reservoir hosts including domestic animals and the transmission cycle includes environmental contamination, preventive chemotherapy targeting only humans may not be sufficient to reduce long-term transmission.

As seen in Figure 2, the prevalence of small trematode eggs in stool before preventive chemotherapy for the two sites combined were 40.2% (95% confidence interval (CI) of 35.3–45.0). The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.
76/118 (65%) of those people egg-positive after 60 weeks were also positive before treatment, suggesting a relative high degree of reinfection in these people.

These facts together support the epidemiological inference that interventions targeting humans alone are unlikely to sustainably reduce transmission when animal reservoirs and environmental contamination persist.

398.Option: The high reinfection rate observed within one year after preventive chemotherapy suggests that the current strategy of selective treatment based on self-reported raw fish consumption is ineffective and should be replaced with universal treatment in all endemic areas.
Evidence: Preventive chemotherapy is here given as selective chemotherapy to people confirming that they eat raw fish.

As seen in Figure 2, the prevalence of small trematode eggs in stool before preventive chemotherapy for the two sites combined were 40.2% (95% confidence interval (CI) of 35.3–45.0). The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.

Results from the two study sites differed when analyzed separately (Fig. 2). Nghia Hong commune had a more rapid increase in FZT prevalence after chemotherapy. The prevalence after 60 weeks in Nghia Hong commune was the same as before preventive chemotherapy. In Hai Hoa commune, the FZT prevalence at the end of the study was less than half of that before preventive chemotherapy, and significantly lower than in Nghia Hong commune, as seen by the confidence intervals.
398.Rationale: While the passage documents substantial reinfection within one year and states that Vietnam uses selective chemotherapy based on reported raw fish consumption, it does not provide direct evidence that selective treatment is uniformly ineffective across settings or that universal treatment would be superior in all endemic areas. The two communes showed different outcomes (one returned to baseline prevalence, the other had prevalence less than half baseline), so the data do not justify the blanket recommendation to replace selective treatment with universal treatment in all endemic areas without further evidence comparing strategies and taking local context into account. Therefore the stronger policy prescription in the option is not derivable solely from the passage.

398.Option: The fact that C. sinensis infections are less common in fish than in humans suggests that human-to-human transmission of this species is more likely than transmission via contaminated fish.
Evidence: FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].

398.Rationale: The passage describes the established transmission route for FZT as infection after consumption of raw or undercooked freshwater fish and does not present any data indicating lower prevalence of C. sinensis in fish than in humans. It also notes difficulties in species identification by microscopy, which undermines any claim about relative prevalences across hosts based on the provided data. There is no evidence of human-to-human transmission; the transmission cycle presented involves fish as the source. Therefore the option's inference is unsupported by the passage.

398.Option: Because MIF infections are less serious and not carcinogenic, preventive chemotherapy should be prioritized for liver fluke infections rather than for minute intestinal fluke infections.
Evidence: The most serious health hazard associated with liver fluke infection is the increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.

398.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that liver flukes carry the serious hazard of increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma and that MIF are not considered carcinogenic and generally have less serious manifestations. It also cites WHO guidance that preventive chemotherapy is the main intervention strategy against liver flukes and that morbidity from liver trematodes is the primary reason for control measures. From these statements, an epidemiological inference that preventive chemotherapy prioritization should focus on liver fluke infections (which have greater long-term morbidity risk) rather than MIF is directly supported by the passage.

398.Option: The use of praziquantel in dogs and cats has been shown to be highly effective in reducing FZT transmission, making animal treatment a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions.
Evidence: Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.

Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].
398.Rationale: The passage reports rapid reinfection of dogs after praziquantel treatment and explicitly states that anthelmintic treatment alone was not recommended to control FZT in dogs. There is no evidence presented that treating dogs and cats is highly effective at reducing overall transmission; instead, the described rapid reinfection argues against animal-only anthelmintic treatment being a sufficient or viable alternative to human-targeted interventions. Therefore the option's claim is not supported by the passage.

399.Option: The presence of specific refractory genes in Ae. aegypti populations from northern Cameroon may explain the lower transmission rates observed in these populations, suggesting that genetic resistance could limit ZIKV spread in certain regions.

TR varied significantly (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.0014) according to the population with lowest TR reported in two populations from northern part of Cameroon, Bénoué (56%) and Maroua (43%) (Fig 3B).
399.Rationale: The passage documents population-level variation in vector competence and specifically reports lower transmission rates in northern Cameroon populations (Bénoué and Maroua). However, it contains no data or evidence about genetic mechanisms (e.g., refractory genes) underlying these differences. The assertion that specific refractory genes "may explain" the observed lower transmission is a speculative causal explanation not supported by the presented experimental results. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the passage.

399.Option: The invasive species Ae. albopictus, which has replaced Ae. aegypti in many areas of Central Africa, is likely to be the primary driver of future ZIKV outbreaks due to its higher population density and broader geographic distribution.
Evidence: it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: While the passage states Ae. albopictus has invaded and tends to supplant Ae. aegypti in the region, the experimental data show that Ae. aegypti has higher transmission efficiency and higher viral titres than Ae. albopictus.
