Multivariate analysis using forward logistic regression model further confirmed that participant less than 12 years old were 2.2 times (95% CI = 1.45–3.45; p<0.001), low household income had 4.9 times (95% CI = 3.15–7.73; p<0.001), using untreated water supply had 2.1 times (95% CI = 1.36–3.21; p<0.001) and indiscriminate defecation were 5 times (95% CI = 3.30–7.62; p<0.001) more likely to suffer from an IPIs, respectively.
The establishment of such data will be beneficial for the public health service to justify and facilitate the reassessment of control strategies and policies.
435.Rationale: The passage identifies poverty, poor sanitation, lack of safe water, low education and hygiene-related behaviors as strong and significant predictors of IPIs, and it also notes that not receiving anthelminthic drugs is associated with infection. Multivariate analysis confirms low income, untreated water use and indiscriminate defecation as major independent risk factors. These findings support a comprehensive control approach that combines socioeconomic improvement, sanitation and hygiene interventions (including health education) with periodic deworming, rather than relying on any single intervention alone. The passage's focus on environmental/personal hygiene links and the stated need to reassess control strategies further support this multifaceted recommendation.

436.Option: The improvement in passive surveillance in 2015 likely led to a higher number of detected cases, which may have masked the true impact of vector control on reducing transmission.
Evidence: In 2015, passive surveillance was expanded by equipping health facilities with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and upgrading confirmation and treatment centres [17].
In 2015, the new passive strategy was predicted to result in slightly more case reporting than the basic medical strategy and so was not attributed to this particular decline.
To account for this step-change in passive strategy in the model, it was assumed that this would speed up the detection of both stage 1 and 2 cases, and lead to less underreporting.
The single (unstaged) data point in 2015 is unfortunately insufficient to generate robust estimates here.
436.Rationale: While the passage documents that passive surveillance was enhanced in 2015 and that the model assumed this would increase case reporting, it explicitly states that the new passive strategy was predicted to result in slightly more reporting and therefore was not attributed to the observed decline. The passage does not support the claim that improved passive surveillance masked the true impact of vector control; rather, the model attributes the majority of the observed decline to vector control. Furthermore, the authors note insufficient data to robustly estimate the quantitative impact of the passive improvement, which undermines the assertion that it masked vector-control effects.

436.Option: The addition of tsetse control to the 'screen and treat' strategy was responsible for the majority of the decline in new HAT infections in the Mandoul focus, with 70.4% of the two-year decline attributed to vector control.
Evidence: Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.

Mathematical modelling estimates that, without vector control, there would have been 129 (95% CI, 105–157) and 123 (95% CI, 97–150) cases per year in 2014 and 2015 respectively with the reduction due to active screening. In reality only 90 and 47 cases were observed (in 2014 and 2015) when a combined vector and enhanced medical strategy took place;
436.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports model-derived attribution of the observed two-year decline, quantifying 70.4% of the decline as due to vector control. The modelling comparison of predicted cases without vector control versus observed cases with vector control supports the inference that adding tsetse control to existing medical interventions produced the majority contribution to the observed decline.

436.Option: The rapid and drastic decrease in catch of tsetse suggests that tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting, and this success may be due to the small size of the area, well-defined habitat, and low reinvasion pressure.
Evidence: A total of 145 tsetse were caught by the 108 biconical traps during the T0 survey, all belonging to G. f. fuscipes, with 50 males (34.5%) and 95 females (65.5%).

For the first deployment in 2014, 2600 targets were deployed on both sides of the river and its tributaries. These targets were replaced one year later with new ones along with an extra 108 additional targets, in order to improve the area coverage (Fig 2), giving a total number of 2708 targets.
The actual area on which targets were deployed represents 45 km2, corresponding to the gallery surrounding the portion of the Mandoul River where tsetse were found.

At the first evaluation post target deployment (T1, 2 months later), only 2 tsetse were caught (0.02 tsetse/trap/day) ... thereafter no tsetse was caught during the next three successive surveys (T3-T6) ... No tsetse were caught during the three surveys conducted in 2016 (T7-T9).

The best model had only location specific baselines, the pre-target deployment indictor (p<10−18, coeff = 5.3 i.e. a predicted 99.5% reduction due to tiny target intervention) and an overdispersion effect.
Tsetse were detected over a limited range along the Mandoul but targets were deployed up to 4 km beyond where tsetse were caught.
436.Rationale: The passage provides strong entomological evidence that tiny targets produced a rapid and near-complete reduction in G. f. fuscipes catches (e.g., only 2 flies two months after deployment, then zero in multiple follow-up surveys, and a model-predicted ~99.5% reduction). The description that tsetse were confined to the gallery along the river and that targets covered a 45 km2 gallery area where tsetse were found supports the inference that the habitat was well-defined and the deployment area relatively small relative to the wider focus. Those features (restricted distribution, targeted deployment area, and absence of detected flies subsequently) make low reinvasion pressure a plausible explanation for sustained control in this setting; the option carefully phrases this as a possibility ('may be due to'), which is consistent with the evidence presented.

437.Option: The age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is likely due to the use of a non-specific ELISA assay that produces false positives in older animals.
Evidence: Bland-Altman analysis [20] indicated good repeatability of the assay.

All sera collected after the 14th day post immunization were highly positive indicating that the ELISA is able to detect a specific seroconversion in camelids (Table 2) without any or only minimal reactivity to E. coli antigens itself.
In addition, 12 camel sera originating from German zoos were tested negative (S1 Table).
Since no positive reference field sera existed, a serum of an immunized alpaca was used as reference.
437.Rationale: The passage provides validation data for the camel ELISA and reports good repeatability and appropriate negative controls; it also documents that experimentally induced seroconversion is detectable. These statements argue against the interpretation that the observed age-related increase is simply an artifact of a non-specific assay producing false positives in older animals. While the authors note limitations in field reference sera, they performed validation steps that reduce the likelihood that assay non-specificity explains the age pattern.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence in older animals is due to increased tick infestation rates in older livestock, which are more susceptible to vector-borne transmission.
Evidence: Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
437.Rationale: The passage identifies tick infestation and age as important risk factors and documents that older animals have higher seroprevalence. However, it does not provide direct evidence that older animals have higher tick infestation rates or that increased infestation (as opposed to cumulative exposure over time) is the mediator of the age effect. Inferring that older animals are 'more susceptible to vector-borne transmission' or have higher tick burdens is plausible but goes beyond the evidence presented, so this specific causal claim cannot be derived solely from the passage.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals across all species, combined with the lack of experimental data on antibody persistence, suggests that cumulative exposure to infected ticks over time is the most likely explanation for the observed age-related increase in seroprevalence.
Evidence: Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
In addition, an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence was already also observed in sheep [14] and cattle [13,15,16].
All other age categories were clearly different from group A (0–2 years).
437.Rationale: The passage repeatedly documents an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG seroprevalence across species and cites tick infestation as a known risk factor. In epidemiological terms, when seroprevalence rises with age for a pathogen transmitted by a vector, the most parsimonious explanation is cumulative exposure over time to that vector. Although the passage does not provide experimental data on duration of antibody persistence, the observed cross-sectional age-seroprevalence pattern across multiple species is consistent with accrual of exposures (infections) with increasing age. This inference follows directly from the data and statements presented.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals is primarily due to longer-lasting antibody persistence in cattle and camels compared to sheep and goats.
Evidence: When considering the species, there were no significant seroprevalence differences between goats and sheep nor between cattle and camels.
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
Since no positive reference field sera existed, a serum of an immunized alpaca was used as reference.
437.Rationale: The passage does not present any data on interspecies differences in antibody persistence or kinetics. It explicitly states there were no significant seroprevalence differences between goats and sheep nor between cattle and camels, which does not support the claim that longer-lasting antibodies in cattle and camels explain the age pattern. The authors also used experimental immunization only to validate the assay rather than to quantify persistence. Therefore, the proposition that species-specific longer antibody persistence is the primary driver is not supported by the passage.

437.Option: The observed age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is mainly driven by differences in slaughter age, as young animals are more likely to be slaughtered before reaching high seroprevalence levels.
Evidence: Samples from the capital region Nouakchott originated from a large abattoir that is connected with one of the most important livestock markets in this region.
Livestock from all over the country were driven up to this market/slaughter house.
Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
437.Rationale: While the passage notes that some samples came from an abattoir and that livestock from across the country were brought there, it does not provide information on slaughter-age practices or demonstrate that younger animals are preferentially slaughtered in a way that would bias seroprevalence by age. The observed age-related increase is reported across sampled populations and is analyzed statistically; there is no evidence presented that differential slaughter age among sampled animals is the main driver. Thus, this specific explanation is not supported by the passage.

438.Option: The political commitment demonstrated by the establishment of the National Program for Blindness Control in 1989 laid the foundation for the long-term success of trachoma elimination in Togo.
Evidence: The organization of trachoma control began within the National Program for Blindness control created in 1989 by the Ministry of Health.

This article reviews the history of trachoma control activities and summarizes the extensive interventions of the Ministry of Health, Public Hygiene and Universal Access to Health Care through the National Programme for Neglected Tropical Diseases to eliminate this neglected tropical disease (NTD) in Togo from its launch in 2006 until the validation of its elimination in 2022.

As of May 2022, 15 countries have been declared reaching their trachoma elimination targets, but only thirteen (13) of them including Togo had been certified by WHO as having eliminated the disease as a public health problem [5].
438.Rationale: The passage documents that trachoma control organization began within a program created in 1989, but it does not state that this 1989 program was the key development that enabled elimination. The passage instead emphasizes interventions and programmatic activity beginning with the National Programme for Neglected Tropical Diseases launched in 2006 and the surveys and interventions through 2017 leading to validation in 2022. There is no direct statement attributing the final achievement to the 1989 program, so the specific causal claim in this option is not supported by the provided text.

438.Option: The 2017 survey, conducted according to the WHO recommended methodology, revealed that the prevalence of key indicators was below the WHO elimination threshold for trachoma, which allowed Togo to be validated as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem.
Evidence: The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3).

This article reviews the history of trachoma control activities and summarizes the extensive interventions ... to eliminate this neglected tropical disease (NTD) in Togo from its launch in 2006 until the validation of its elimination in 2022.

As of May 2022, 15 countries have been declared reaching their trachoma elimination targets, but only thirteen (13) of them including Togo had been certified by WHO as having eliminated the disease as a public health problem [5].
438.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that a WHO-recommended Tropical Data survey in the selected districts showed TF prevalence <1% (well below the 10% WHO threshold) and very low TT prevalence. The passage also indicates that Togo was later certified by WHO as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. Therefore, it is reasonable from the passage to infer that the WHO-methodology survey results showing indicators below the elimination thresholds enabled validation.

438.Option: The use of the Tropical Data protocol in the 2017 survey allowed for the accurate identification of trichiasis cases, which led to the successful surgical management of all identified patients.
Evidence: The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3).
At the end of the survey, 27 patients were identified in need for surgery. 19 of them were surgered while 6 refused surgery and therefore benefited from epilation of eyelashes.
In Tchamba district, one (01) patient diagnosed had died before the surgery period and one (01) other person was absent in Anié district during the surgery period.
438.Rationale: While the passage documents that the Tropical Data protocol was used and that 27 patients were identified as needing surgery, it does not claim that all identified patients were successfully surgically managed. In fact the text states only 19 were operated on, 6 refused surgery and were treated by epilation, and one diagnosed patient died before surgery. Thus the specific claim that all identified patients were successfully surgically managed is contradicted by the passage.

438.Option: The integration of trachoma screening into annual door-to-door mass treatment campaigns for onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis, and geo-helminthiasis enabled the identification of suspected trichiasis cases across all regions of Togo, including previously unsurveyed districts.
Evidence: Since 2015, the NPNTD has been organizing the Integrated mass treatment with Ivermectin, Albendazole and Praziquantel against onchocerciasis, urinary bilharzia and geo-helminthiases respectively every year.

As part of the mass treatment activities conducted door-to-door in all regions of the country, Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis. The suspected cases identified were examined for diagnostic confirmation to estimate the prevalence of TT.

At the end of the 2017 mass treatment, Community Health Workers had notified 588 cases of eyelashes rubbing on the eyes including 13 confirmed cases of TT by senior ophthalmic technicians in 5 health regions (Savanes, Kara, Centrale, Plateaux and Maritime) resulting in a TT prevalence of 2.2%.
438.Rationale: The passage describes that annual, door-to-door integrated mass-treatment campaigns covered all regions and included training of Community Health Workers to identify suspected trichiasis cases, and that these activities yielded reports of suspected and confirmed TT across five regions (including regions not limited to those earlier surveyed). From these statements it is supported that integrating screening into mass campaigns enabled identification of suspected TT cases across the country, including regions beyond those earlier targeted by surveys.

438.Option: The correction of methodological weaknesses in earlier surveys, such as the overdiagnosis of trachomatous follicular inflammation in 2006 and 2009, enabled the 2017 survey to produce reliable data for elimination validation.
Evidence: 2006 Survey.In 2006, a prevalence of 60 to 80% of TF among children aged 6 to 9 years was found in the Binah district (Kara Region).

2009 Survey.In 2009, the survey revealed a higher prevalence of TF in the Central region (5.7%) compared to the other regions (3.5% in the Kara region and 2.6% in the Savane region). The highest prevalences of TF were recorded respectively in Sotouboua (11.3%) and Blitta (10.9%) districts.

2011 Survey.The 2011 survey in the three districts ... reported very low prevalences ranging from 0 (Sotouboua district) to 0.2% (Blitta district) in the Central region and 0.1% for Binah district in the Kara region (Table 2).

The results of surveys from 2006, 2009, 2011; data from mass treatment, routine data in health facilities from 2014 to 2016 and TT case data from the project from June 2015 to December 2016 were collated. Taking all these data into account, 07 health districts from three regions were selected.
438.Rationale: The passage documents varying TF prevalences across surveys (high in 2006/2009, low in 2011) and states that earlier survey results and other data were collated to select districts for the Tropical Data survey. However, it does not state that earlier surveys had identifiable methodological weaknesses or that such weaknesses were corrected. Nor does it explicitly claim that overdiagnosis occurred or that corrections were the reason the 2017 survey produced reliable data. Therefore, the specific causal claim about 'correction of methodological weaknesses' enabling reliable 2017 data is not supported by the text.

440.Option: The higher total provider costs for P. vivax malaria are due to the greater number of outpatient consultations required for its treatment compared to P. falciparum.
Evidence: For P. vivax cases, one extra outpatient consultation was considered for radical cure six months after delivery.

In 2010, 135 out of the total of 364 MiP episodes reported in the Manaus area presented at the FMT-HVD, with 120 of them treated as outpatients and 15 requiring admission. One hundred of the outpatient cases were due to P. vivax and 20 to P. falciparum.

The average cost of managing an outpatient case (multiple consultations) was US$ 100.19 (range US$ 90.13 to US$ 108.88), and discriminating by Plasmodium species, it was US$ 103.51 (range US$ 92.93 to US$ 112.65) for a P. vivax malaria episode and US$ 83.59 (range US$ 76.15 to US$ 90.07) for a P. falciparum malaria episode.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
440.Rationale: The passage shows that most outpatient MiP cases were P. vivax (100 vs 20 P. falciparum) and explicitly states that P. vivax cases include one extra outpatient consultation for radical cure. The per-episode outpatient cost is higher for P. vivax than for P. falciparum. These facts support the inference that higher total provider costs for P. vivax are attributable, at least in part, to a greater number of outpatient consultations required for its management.

440.Option: The economic burden of P. vivax malaria in pregnancy is higher than that of P. falciparum, particularly when multiple episodes are considered, despite P. vivax being the predominant species in the region.
Evidence: Of the 73 episodes, 96% (70/73) were due to P. vivax and the remaining 4% (3/73) due to P. falciparum.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.

Table 4 shows costs incurred by the nine women who experienced two episodes of MiP during the study period. Median costs incurred at the health facility were US$ 259.50 (IQR, 120.69, 398.31) when the first malaria episode required admission and the second one was an outpatient consultation; median costs were US$ 25.38 (IQR, 22.79, 68.58) when the two malaria episodes were outpatient consultations.
Interestingly, all women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.
440.Rationale: The passage documents that P. vivax is the overwhelmingly predominant species in this setting and that total provider costs are largely attributable to P. vivax. It also shows that women with multiple episodes (all P. vivax) incurred substantially higher median costs, demonstrating that multiple P. vivax infections magnify the economic burden. Therefore the inference that P. vivax imposes a higher economic burden in pregnancy — especially when multiple episodes occur — is supported by the data.

440.Option: The high cost of managing inpatient cases of malaria in pregnancy is primarily driven by the need for prolonged hospital stays and intensive care, rather than by the species of Plasmodium involved.
Evidence: The cost of one bed/day was US$ 118.51 (ranging from US$ 111.64 to US$ 124.57) and considering the whole length of stay the cost of management of an inpatient case was US$ 355.53 (ranging from US$ 334.92 to US$ 373.71).
An inpatient malaria episode requires hospital admission for 3 days plus two follow-up consultations as outpatients after discharge.

In 2010, 135 out of the total of 364 MiP episodes reported in the Manaus area presented at the FMT-HVD, with 120 of them treated as outpatients and 15 requiring admission. ... All the 15 malaria admissions were due to P. vivax.
440.Rationale: While the passage provides the per-bed/day cost and the total inpatient cost and specifies a standard 3-day admission, it does not present evidence that inpatient costs are driven by 'prolonged hospital stays' or by use of intensive care. The admission length is described as 3 days (not prolonged), and all admissions in the data were due to P. vivax, so the assertion that costs are driven by factors other than species is not supported. The passage does not provide data attributing inpatient costs primarily to prolonged stays or intensive care rather than to species prevalence or other factors.

440.Option: The economic burden of P. falciparum malaria in pregnancy is underestimated in this study due to its lower prevalence and the fact that it is more likely to be misdiagnosed as P. vivax.
Evidence: Of the 73 episodes, 96% (70/73) were due to P. vivax and the remaining 4% (3/73) due to P. falciparum.

In 2010, 135 out of the total of 364 MiP episodes reported in the Manaus area presented at the FMT-HVD, with 120 of them treated as outpatients and 15 requiring admission. One hundred of the outpatient cases were due to P. vivax and 20 to P. falciparum.
440.Rationale: The passage documents the lower prevalence of P. falciparum in this setting, but it provides no information or data about misdiagnosis of P. falciparum as P. vivax. There is no evidence presented that P. falciparum cases were systematically misdiagnosed; diagnosis methods and costing for microscopy are described but misclassification is not discussed. Therefore the specific claim that the economic burden of P. falciparum is underestimated because it is more likely to be misdiagnosed cannot be derived from the passage.

440.Option: The use of preventive measures such as bed nets and insecticides is associated with lower treatment costs for P. vivax malaria in pregnant women, suggesting that prevention is cost-effective.
Evidence: The reported use of bed nets by study women was low (6%; 4/73) and 12% (9/73) reported having taken weekly chloroquine as a prevention of malaria relapse. The use of insecticides and skin repellents was reported in 26% (19/73) and 19% (14/73) of interviews, respectively. No use of malaria preventative measures was reported in nearly 40% of interviews (27/73).
Mean prevention costs were US$ 13.50 for an outpatient malaria consultation and US$ 3.85 for an admission.
Total median costs including previous treatments sought were US$ 45.91 (IQR, 28.44, 80.41) and US$ 216.29 (IQR, 138.36, 327.07) for a consultation and an admission, respectively.
440.Rationale: Although the passage reports low uptake of preventive measures and provides prevention and treatment cost figures, it does not present any analysis linking individual use of preventive measures to lower subsequent treatment costs, nor any comparison of costs between users and non-users. No causal or associative data are provided to support the claim that prevention reduced treatment costs in this cohort. Therefore the assertion that prevention is shown to be cost-effective cannot be derived from the presented data.

441.Option: NF2839’s ability to impair egg maturation without reducing egg production indicates that it disrupts developmental signaling pathways independent of parasite viability.
Evidence: Fourteen HDAC inhibitors, already recognized to be active on mammalian cells and with a known profile of inhibitory activity on recombinant human HDAC1, HDAC6 and HDAC8, were selected to be tested in phenotypic screenings against S. mansoni parasites and SmHDAC8 recombinant protein activity.
The eggs produced by worm pairs in vitro were counted at day 3 upon treatment using an inverted Leica DM IL microscope (Leica Microsystems, Wetzlar, Germany).
Egg maturation morphological score was assigned based on the Vogel and Prata’s staging system of egg maturation previously reported [32–33].
441.Rationale: The passage describes methods for counting eggs and assigning egg maturation scores, and it lists some example compounds tested, but it does not present any experimental results for a compound named NF2839 or state that any compound impaired egg maturation without reducing egg production. Because the passage contains no outcome data about NF2839's effects on egg production or maturation, the specific causal inference about disruption of developmental signaling independent of viability cannot be derived from the provided text.

441.Option: The quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor NF2839 demonstrates schistosomicidal activity against both larval and adult stages of S. mansoni, impairs egg maturation, and increases acetylation of histones and tubulin in parasites, indicating it targets multiple aspects of parasite biology.
Evidence: Fourteen HDAC inhibitors, already recognized to be active on mammalian cells and with a known profile of inhibitory activity on recombinant human HDAC1, HDAC6 and HDAC8, were selected to be tested in phenotypic screenings against S. mansoni parasites and SmHDAC8 recombinant protein activity.
The NTS obtained by mechanical transformation of cercariae and Percoll gradient was used for the ATP-based luminescence viability assays with the CellTiter-GLO as previously described [29–30].

For studies on adult worm pairs, mice infected 7–8 weeks previously with double sex cercariae were euthanized ... Adult worms were cultured ... Viability assays on adult worm pairs were based on a phenotyping scoring assessment as previously described [29,31].

Ten adult pairs were treated with selected HDAC inhibitors for 48 h or 72 h at partially lethal and sublethal concentrations. ... immunoblotting was performed using α-tubulin DM1A (1:5000), α-acetylated-lysine (1:4000) and α-acetylated tubulin (1:3000) antibodies.
441.Rationale: While the passage describes that the study tested HDAC inhibitors on both newly transformed schistosomula (NTS) and adult worms, measured viability, counted eggs and assessed acetylated-lysine and acetylated-tubulin by Western blot, it does not report any experimental results for a compound named NF2839. The specific claims that NF2839 is quinolone-based, has schistosomicidal activity across life stages, impairs egg maturation, and increases histone/tubulin acetylation are not presented in the passage; therefore these conclusions cannot be derived from the provided text.

441.Option: The quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor NF2839 shows selective inhibition of SmHDAC8 over other HDAC isoforms, which explains its potent schistosomicidal activity and specific impact on egg maturation.
Evidence: SmHDAC8 is a class I zinc dependent HDAC, which was demonstrated to be abundantly expressed in all S. mansoni life cycle stages [10].
SmHDAC8 is less conserved compared to its human orthologue than other class I schistosome HDACs 1 and 3 [13]. Therefore, SmHDAC8 has been proposed as a promising target for drug discovery [10].

Fourteen HDAC inhibitors, already recognized to be active on mammalian cells and with a known profile of inhibitory activity on recombinant human HDAC1, HDAC6 and HDAC8, were selected to be tested in phenotypic screenings against S. mansoni parasites and SmHDAC8 recombinant protein activity.
SmHDAC8 protein was produced and purified by a method previously described by us [18]. Inhibition assays of SmHDAC8 were performed as already described in previous publications [18,21].
441.Rationale: The passage explains the rationale for targeting SmHDAC8 and describes that SmHDAC8 inhibition assays were performed, but it does not provide any data demonstrating that a specific compound named NF2839 selectively inhibits SmHDAC8 over other HDAC isoforms, nor that such selectivity explains schistosomicidal potency or effects on egg maturation. The claim of selective inhibition and causal explanation for effects on egg maturation is not supported by any results in the provided text.

441.Option: The failure of NF2838 to affect parasite viability despite its structural similarity to NF2839 suggests that the quinolone-based cap group is not essential for schistosomicidal activity.
Evidence: Here to search for novel valuable molecules, we tested structurally different HDAC inhibitors, chosen from the Siena in-house library [23–26]. Fourteen HDAC inhibitors, already recognized to be active on mammalian cells and with a known profile of inhibitory activity on recombinant human HDAC1, HDAC6 and HDAC8, were selected to be tested in phenotypic screenings against S. mansoni parasites and SmHDAC8 recombinant protein activity.
441.Rationale: The passage describes testing multiple structurally different HDAC inhibitors but does not mention compounds named NF2838 or NF2839, nor does it present comparative viability results between structurally related compounds. Therefore, the inference about NF2838's failure to affect viability and the conclusion about the quinolone cap group's non-essentiality cannot be drawn from the text.

441.Option: The observed increase in histone and tubulin acetylation in NF2839-treated parasites is primarily due to the compound’s ability to inhibit hHDAC6, which is highly expressed in S. mansoni.
Evidence: Both Class I (HDACs 1, 3, and 8) and Class II orthologues (HDACs 4, 5, 6/10, 9) have been identified in S. mansoni [10–12].
SmHDAC8 is a class I zinc dependent HDAC, which was demonstrated to be abundantly expressed in all S. mansoni life cycle stages [10].
SmHDAC8 is less conserved compared to its human orthologue than other class I schistosome HDACs 1 and 3 [13]. Therefore, SmHDAC8 has been proposed as a promising target for drug discovery [10].

Ten adult pairs were treated with selected HDAC inhibitors for 48 h or 72 h at partially lethal and sublethal concentrations. ... immunoblotting was performed using α-tubulin DM1A (1:5000), α-acetylated-lysine (1:4000) and α-acetylated tubulin (1:3000) antibodies.
441.Rationale: The passage indicates that S. mansoni has class II orthologues including HDAC6/10 and that SmHDAC8 (a class I enzyme) is abundant in parasite stages; it also describes methods to detect acetylated lysine and acetylated tubulin. However, there are no data in the passage attributing any observed increase in acetylation to inhibition of human HDAC6 (hHDAC6), nor is hHDAC6 described as being highly expressed in S. mansoni (hHDAC6 is a human enzyme). The causal statement that NF2839's effects are primarily due to hHDAC6 inhibition is not supported by any results or statements in the provided text.

442.Option: The failure to systematically diagnose T. cruzi infection is one of the main causes of persistent neglect towards this global public health problem, and most patients remain undiagnosed and untreated.
Evidence: Chagas disease, one of 20 conditions classified as neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1], affects more than 6 million people worldwide, mainly in endemic areas of Latin America, and is largely underdiagnosed.
According to estimates, less than 10% of those currently infected know their infection status [2], which represents a significant barrier to receive timely comprehensive care.
Less than 1% of the estimated population with the infection receives etiological treatment.
Awareness is a key issue, as most patients are asymptomatic and unaware of their infection, and healthcare providers are often unfamiliar with the disease and its risk factors.
442.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Chagas disease is largely underdiagnosed and quantifies the gap in diagnosis (<10% know their status) and treatment (<1% receive etiological therapy). It also identifies lack of awareness among patients and unfamiliarity among providers as barriers to care. These statements support the epidemiological inference that systematic failure to diagnose is a central contributor to persistent neglect, leaving most patients undiagnosed and untreated.

442.Option: The use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in combination could provide diagnostic confirmation of chronic infection at the point of care, potentially improving access to early diagnosis and treatment for vulnerable populations.
Evidence: Facilitating testing at point of care, in addition to amplifying access to information about the disease, could stimulate increased testing and access to treatment.
Currently, there are several commercially available rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) for chronic T. cruzi infection which detect specific antibodies using whole blood, plasma, or serum.

These tests are easy to perform, involve fewer technical procedures, require small sample volumes that can be obtained through capillary/digital puncture, and have a short processing time, providing rapid results (in 10–35 minutes) without the need for a dedicated laboratory, infrastructure, equipment, and skilled operators.
Furthermore, they can be performed at the primary care level, close to the community, thus increasing adherence to treatment.

Therefore, the INS, DNDi, and FIND (Foundation for Innovative New Diagnostics) conducted a study to assess the diagnostic performance of 11 rapid tests for Chagas disease in the Colombian population, in order to estimate their operational characteristics under controlled laboratory conditions using the Colombian algorithm for serological diagnosis as a reference standard, and to model potential combinations of RDTs for screening and confirmatory diagnosis of T. cruzi infection.
442.Rationale: The passage describes RDTs as simple, rapid, and suitable for primary care/POC use and explicitly states that the study aimed to model potential combinations of RDTs for screening and confirmatory diagnosis. It also notes that facilitating POC testing could increase testing and access to treatment. Together, these statements support the inference that combining RDTs could enable point-of-care confirmation and improve access to early diagnosis and treatment for vulnerable populations (a plausible, evidence-based public health strategy suggested by the authors' aims).

442.Option: Geographic variation in the performance of diagnostic tools, particularly due to differences in circulating DTUs, may limit the generalizability of rapid test results across Chagas disease-endemic regions.
Evidence: In the last two decades, several RDTs for Chagas disease have been developed, and several studies have assessed their performance in different populations and sample types, with variable results.
Such variability could be partially explained by genetic variation in T. cruzi, leading to differences in infection pathogenicity and transmission.

Seven genetic lineages of the pathogen (discrete typing units, DTUs) have been identified to date, with different geographical distributions [17]. TcI is most common in human infections in Colombia [18].
442.Rationale: The passage links variable RDT performance across studies to possible genetic variation in T. cruzi (DTUs) with different geographic distributions and notes that TcI predominates in Colombia. These statements support the epidemiological inference that geographic variation in circulating DTUs may limit the generalizability of RDT performance across endemic regions.

442.Option: The high rate of discordant results between rapid tests and the reference standard, particularly false negatives, suggests that current rapid tests are unreliable for confirming chronic T. cruzi infection in clinical practice.
Evidence: In total, 38.6% (226) of the samples had at least one discordant RDT result; 61.5% (139) were in samples previously characterized as positive and 38.5% (87) as negative, i.e., most of the discordant results were false negatives.
Of the 6,401 tests analyzed, 7.4% (471) disagreed with the reference standard, of which 75.8% were false negatives and 24.2% (114) were false positives.

Using the current diagnostic algorithm for chronic infection in Colombia as a reference standard, the sensitivity of the assessed RDTs ranged from 75.5% to 99.0% (95% CI 70.5–100), while specificity ranged from 70.9% to 100% (95% CI 65.3–100).
Most tests (7/11, 63.6%) had sensitivity above 90%, and almost all (10/11, 90.9%) had specificity above 90%.
442.Rationale: While the passage documents discordance and a predominance of false negatives among discordant results, it also shows substantial heterogeneity in test performance: some RDTs have high sensitivity and specificity (many >90%), while others perform less well (sensitivity as low as 75.5%). The blanket conclusion that 'current rapid tests are unreliable for confirming chronic T. cruzi infection in clinical practice' overgeneralizes beyond the evidence. The passage supports concern about false negatives and the need for careful evaluation and possibly combinations or further field validation, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to label all current RDTs as broadly unreliable for clinical confirmation.

443.Option: The high seropositivity among dogs and equids in certain countries suggests that these animals can serve as useful sentinels for West Nile virus surveillance.

Equids (Iran, total SS = 1839, prevalence = 0.8–70.3%; Jordan, total SS = 253, prevalence = 24.9%; Morocco, total SS = 1189, prevalence = 25–100%; Pakistan, total SS = 449, prevalence = 65%; Palestine, total SS = 585, prevalence = 75%; Qatar, total SS = 421, prevalence: 0–27%; Saudi Arabia, total SS = 63, prevalence = 33.5%; Tunisia, total SS = 1473, prevalence = 28.0–45.2%; the UAE, total SS = 750, prevalence = 5.4–28.6%),
Dogs (Djibouti, total SS = 91, prevalence = 56.5%; Morocco, total SS = 231, prevalence = 54–96%),
443.Rationale: The passage states that prevention and control rely on surveillance in animals and documents substantial seropositivity in dogs and equids across multiple EMRO countries. It also indicates these species are susceptible to WNV infection. From these facts, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that dogs and equids could function as sentinels (i.e., detectable indicators of local WNV transmission) for surveillance purposes. The passage provides direct evidence of high seroprevalence in these species and the explicit role of animal surveillance in control efforts, supporting the sentinel inference.

443.Option: The high seroprevalence of West Nile virus in dogs and equids across the EMRO region indicates that current surveillance systems are effectively detecting animal infections and informing public health responses.
Evidence: Understanding the epidemiology of WNV in the EMRO faces a number of challenges including inadequate knowledge of physicians about the nature of the disease, misdiagnosis of other common infectious diseases due to similarity in clinical presentations, poor diagnostic infrastructures and the absence of confirmatory assays for serological tests, and lack of a comprehensive and progressive monitoring and surveillance system in majority of countries.
The latter has resulted in a gap in knowledge regading the prevalence of WNV infection in the EMRO region.
In these studies, serological evidence of WNV infection was detected in a wide range of domestic and wild animals, including ... Dogs ... Equids ...
443.Rationale: Although the review documents seropositivity in dogs and equids, the passage explicitly describes major limitations in surveillance, diagnostics, and monitoring across the EMRO region and states these limitations have produced gaps in knowledge about WNV prevalence. 443.Option: The presence of West Nile virus in dogs and equids in multiple EMRO countries suggests that these animals are primary reservoirs responsible for maintaining the virus in the region.

Thus, despite seropositivity in dogs and equids, the passage contradicts the idea that these mammals are primary reservoirs maintaining the virus. The evidence supports that birds and mosquitoes are the maintenance hosts/vectors, not equids or dogs.

443.Option: The temporal pattern of West Nile virus seroconversion in dogs suggests that human infections in the EMRO region are primarily driven by direct contact with infected dogs.
Evidence: This mosquito-borne virus has been classified in the genus Flavivirus within the family Flaviviridae [2].
WNV is maintained in a zoonotic transmission cycle between birds and mosquitos, principally the Culex species.
Equines and humans are incidental “dead-end” hosts who do not play a role in the transmission cycle of the virus.
Dogs (Djibouti, total SS = 91, prevalence = 56.5%; Morocco, total SS = 231, prevalence = 54–96%),

It does not describe any temporal pattern of seroconversion in dogs, nor does it indicate transmission to humans via direct contact with dogs. Given the described transmission ecology (mosquito-borne), inferring that human infections are primarily driven by direct contact with infected dogs is not supported by the passage.

443.Option: The high seropositivity among dogs and equids in certain countries indicates that these animals are more susceptible to West Nile virus infection than other domestic animals in the EMRO region.

Equids (Iran, total SS = 1839, prevalence = 0.8–70.3%; ... Pakistan, total SS = 449, prevalence = 65%; Palestine, total SS = 585, prevalence = 75%; ...)

A total of 22 studies investigated the WNV seroprevalence in animals (Fig 2A and 2B). 443.Rationale: While the passage reports high seroprevalence in dogs and equids in some settings, it also documents WNV antibodies across a wide range of other domestic species with variable prevalences. Therefore one cannot conclude from these prevalence figures alone that dogs and equids are inherently more susceptible than other domestic animals.

444.Option: Rickettsia felis infection can cause severe and potentially life-threatening conditions, including acute respiratory distress syndrome and severe hepatitis, even in the absence of typical exposure history such as flea bites or animal contact.

the patient’s liver function continued to deteriorate, with elevated aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), bilirubin (total and direct), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) up to 362 IU/L, 253 IU/L, 7.47 mg/dL, 5.4 mg/dL, 769 IU/L and 386 IU/L, respectively.

It also describes severe liver dysfunction in another patient with serologic evidence of R. felis (patient 3) and shows that seroconversion/≥4-fold rises in IgG were used to identify active infection. The passage explicitly states that none of the patients reported animal contact or flea bites. Together, these facts support the inference that R. felis can cause severe, potentially life-threatening conditions including ARDS and severe hepatitis, and that such severe disease can occur even when a typical exposure history (flea bites or animal contact) is not reported.

444.Option: Rickettsia felis infection is likely to be more prevalent in urban areas of Taiwan due to the high density of domestic animals and cat fleas in cities, as evidenced by the study’s findings in Taipei.

co-migration of humans and domestic animals harboring cat fleas probably contributes to the widespread distribution of R. felis [17, 18].
cat flea’s nature of lacking host specificity, several mammals carrying cat fleas, such as opossums, raccoons, and rats have been implicated in the transmission cycle of R. felis, too [6, 22–26].
444.Rationale: While the study was performed at a hospital in Taipei and most identified patients came from northern Taiwan, the passage does not provide data comparing urban versus rural prevalence or demonstrating higher prevalence due to urban domestic animal density. The cited statements about co-migration and cat flea ecology describe mechanisms for widespread distribution but do not provide evidence that prevalence is higher in urban areas. Therefore the specific causal claim that R. felis is likely more prevalent in urban areas of Taiwan based on this study is not supported by the passage.

444.Option: The seasonal pattern of R. felis infection in Taiwan, with most cases occurring in winter and spring, suggests that transmission is linked to colder weather and increased indoor human-vector contact.
Evidence: The seasons of onset were mainly winter and spring.
444.Rationale: The passage reports that onset was mainly in winter and spring, which documents a seasonal pattern. However, it does not provide evidence that transmission is linked to colder weather specifically or to increased indoor human-vector contact. The connection to colder weather or indoor exposure is an explanatory hypothesis not established or supported by data in the passage. Thus the causal inference about colder-weather-driven transmission or increased indoor human-vector contact cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

444.Option: The high rate of seroconversion in patients with severe symptoms indicates that R. felis infection is more likely to cause acute, self-limited illness rather than chronic or persistent disease.
Evidence: Four of these patients (patient 1–4) exhibited seroconversion, indicating active R. felis infection, while the other 4 patients might be infected by R. felis currently or exposed to the pathogen in the past, which could not be determined from single blood samples.

444.Rationale: The passage shows that seroconversion (≥4-fold rise) was observed in some patients and that R. felis is often characterized as a mild, self-limited illness resolving within about a week in most reported patients. However, the study does not demonstrate a 'high rate of seroconversion in patients with severe symptoms'—only four patients showed seroconversion and one of those had a fatal severe course. Moreover, the passage explicitly documents that severe outcomes, neurological involvement, and deaths have occurred. Therefore it is not possible to conclude from the presented data that R. felis is more likely to cause acute, self-limited illness rather than chronic or persistent disease in all cases; the evidence supports that most infections are self-limited but also that severe or prolonged illness can occur. The specific inference in the option overstates what the data justify.

445.Option: The suppression of T cell responses during pre-patent schistosome infection is mediated by a soluble factor produced by infected CD11b+ cells that inhibits T cell proliferation in a contact-independent manner.

Together, these data suggest that T cell suppression by splenocytes from infected mice is not mediated by a soluble factor, but instead requires cell-to-cell contact.
445.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports transwell experiments showing no suppression when infected splenocytes were physically separated from responding T cells, and concludes the suppression is not mediated by a diffusible inhibitor but requires cell-to-cell contact. Thus the claim that suppression is mediated by a soluble, contact-independent factor is contradicted by the experimental evidence and is not derivable from the passage.

445.Option: Pre-patent schistosome infection leads to a systemic increase in IL-12 production, which contributes to the activation of CD4+ T cells and the development of Th1 immunity.

we found that pre-patent schistosome infection resulted in significant down-regulation of IL-12p35 mRNA expression in the spleen by 4 weeks post infection (Fig. 8A).

Adding exogenous IL-12 did not restore proliferation and in fact appeared to further impair proliferation of OT-II T cells, regardless of whether APCs from infected or non-infected mice were used (Fig. 8B).

When splenocytes from infected RAG-1 −/− mice were used as APCs, production of IFN-γ in response to either OVA peptide or anti-CD3 was significantly reduced compared to co-cultures containing splenocytes from non-infected RAG-1−/− mice (Fig. 2F). The authors explicitly state the impairment is due to altered accessory cell stimulation and not a cell-intrinsic change in T cells. Therefore the option claiming a primary cell-intrinsic T cell defect is not supported by the passage.

445.Option: Pre-patent schistosome infection impairs the ability of CD11b+ innate antigen-presenting cells to stimulate CD4+ T cell responses, contributing to systemic T cell hyporesponsiveness.
Evidence: We show that the response to pre-patent schistosome infection is associated with a loss of T cell responsiveness to other antigens, and that loss of T cell responsiveness is due to a specific diminution in the ability of innate APCs to stimulate T cells.

To determine whether the loss in T cell stimulatory capacity was associated specifically with either CD11c+ or CD11b+ cells, these populations were isolated from the spleens of infected and non-infected mice and their ability to stimulate OT-II CD4+ T cells in vitro was examined.
when CD11b+ cells were used as APCs, a significant reduction in the production of IFN-γ in response to anti-CD3 was observed when the CD11b+ cells were isolated from infected mice (Fig. 4B).

CD11b+ cells from infected mice stimulated significantly less proliferation of OT-II CD4+ T cells than CD11b+ cells from non-infected mice, whether the cells were stimulated with OVA peptide or anti-CD3 stimulation (Fig. 4C).

Evidence: Finally, schistosome lyso-phosphatidylserine (lyso-PS), another immunomodulatory glycoconjugate, stimulates dendritic cells (DC) to induce IL-10 secreting regulatory T cells (Treg), leading to regulation of the T cell response [24].

Evidence: Homozygous AA individuals for the variant rs2288876 A>G have 30% higher odds of developing CL (OR = 1.3 [95% CI 1.1–1.7]; P = 0.006) than heterozygous individuals. The allele A is associated with susceptibility to CL (OR = 1.2 [95%CI 1.03–1.4]; P = 0.01).
Variance analysis between genotypes of rs2288877 and rs2288876 and plasma circulating levels of IL-1β, TNFα, and IL-8 showed a significant correlation with only IL-8 S3 and S4 Figs, respectively.

446.Option: The rs2288876 A>G variant in CARD8 is linked to a loss of function in the NLRP3 inflammasome, leading to uncontrolled IL-1β and IL-18 release and increased disease severity in L. guyanensis infection.

447.Option: The use of pentavalent antimonials in mice versus liposomal amphotericin B in humans explains the superior efficacy of combined immune modulation in the mouse model compared to human patients.
Evidence: The pentavalent antimonial, sodium stibogluconate (Sbv; Albert David Ltd, Kolkata, India], was administered at 500mg/kg/day doses ...
Patients were treated either with Amphotericin B or Ambisome.

447.Option: Combining immune modulation with anti-parasitic drug treatment improved anti-parasitic immunity, but IL-10 signaling blockade alone was more effective than combining it with GITR activation.

all FST values were non-significant, suggesting lack of genetic differentiation between the studied populations (Table 5).

The passage provides direct evidence of the temporal pattern (high before antivenom, fall after) and documents the severe coagulopathy to which such a compensatory response could be related; it does not present alternative mechanistic data that would rule out this explanation, so this inference is supported by the Passage Body.
