Evidence: Of all patients, 6.7% had hypertension, 4.9% had diabetes mellitus, 1.6% had COPD, and 1.6% had ischemic heart disease. Total 8.2% of patients had at least one comorbidity.
The distribution was statistically similar among patients who were alive and who died (Table 1).
452.Rationale: The passage reports low prevalence of comorbidities and states their distribution was statistically similar between survivors and non-survivors, which indicates no observed association in this sample. However, concluding that these comorbidities 'do not contribute to poor outcomes in tetanus patients' is an overgeneralization beyond the study's evidence; the study did not perform detailed adjusted analyses for comorbidities (nor had large numbers of comorbid patients), so absence of an observed association here does not prove the conditions do not affect outcomes in general.

453.Option: The effectiveness of Wolbachia-based arbovirus control might be compromised in ecosystems that experience periods of extreme heat, but given that Wolbachia levels partially recover after temperatures return to normal, any effects may be temporary.
Evidence: We found significantly lower Wolbachia densities relative to wMel controls in 0–2 d-old females emerging from eight of the nine treatments (Fig 2), with only the mature instar treatment lasting three days (M3) showing no significant reduction.
Wolbachia levels in the 0–2 d-old females that were exposed to 30–40°C for seven days starting at the egg stage (E7) were less than 0.1% of wMel control densities (Fig 2).

Compared with the 0–2 d adult collection time point, 4–7 d-old adult females in all treatment groups except the wMel control group and the M3 group had higher Wolbachia levels, with adults from three-day treatments (E3, I3, and M3) showing Wolbachia densities that were not significantly different from wMel-infected controls (Fig 2).
the prevalence of wMel-infected A. aegypti and the wMel Wolbachia levels within individual mosquitoes will remain high enough to prevent DENV and ZIKV transmission in all environments.
453.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that simulated heatwave conditions (30–40°C) during development substantially reduce Wolbachia densities in adult females (compromising the biological mechanisms—maternal transmission, CI, pathogen blocking—that require high Wolbachia levels). It also shows that some recovery of Wolbachia density occurs by 4–7 days post-emergence in several treatment groups, and that short (three-day) heat exposures in particular may not produce lasting deficits. Together these observations support the inference that extreme heat can compromise Wolbachia-based control but that effects can be at least partially reversible once temperatures return to normal, making the adverse effects potentially temporary rather than universally permanent.

453.Option: The wMel strain of Wolbachia is more thermotolerant than its A. aegypti host, which allows it to maintain high infection levels even under extreme heat conditions during early development.
Evidence: We found significantly lower Wolbachia densities relative to wMel controls in 0–2 d-old females emerging from eight of the nine treatments (Fig 2), with only the mature instar treatment lasting three days (M3) showing no significant reduction.
Wolbachia levels in the 0–2 d-old females that were exposed to 30–40°C for seven days starting at the egg stage (E7) were less than 0.1% of wMel control densities (Fig 2).
In A. albopictus all life stages maintained at 37°C had a lower levels of Wolbachia than those reared at 25°C, indicating that high temperatures may reduce Wolbachia levels in mosquito hosts [58].
453.Rationale: The passage presents experimental results showing that wMel Wolbachia densities decline markedly after exposure to high temperatures during development (including near-complete reduction in the E7 group). These data contradict the claim that wMel is more thermotolerant than the host and can maintain high infection levels under extreme heat. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

453.Option: The reduced Wolbachia levels observed in adult females after heat exposure during development are primarily due to decreased host body size, which impairs the mosquito’s ability to maintain symbiont populations.
Evidence: We found a significant effect of treatment group on wing length (F(10,71) = 13.70, MSE = 0.32, P < 0.001) and of the treatment group–collection time point interaction (F(9,71) = 2.81, MSE = 0.07, P = 0.007).
Treatment groups E7, I5, I7, M3, M5, and M7 were all significantly smaller than wMel controls (S3 Fig).

We found significantly lower Wolbachia densities relative to wMel controls in 0–2 d-old females emerging from eight of the nine treatments (Fig 2), with only the mature instar treatment lasting three days (M3) showing no significant reduction.
453.Rationale: While the passage documents both reduced Wolbachia densities and smaller wing lengths in several heat-treated groups, it does not present evidence that decreased body size is the primary cause of Wolbachia reduction. No experimental analysis linking body size causally to Wolbachia density is provided. The observed associations could be correlated consequences of heat stress rather than a direct mechanistic pathway; therefore the claim that size is the primary cause is not supported by the passage.

453.Option: The partial recovery of Wolbachia density by 4–7 days of age suggests that adult mosquitoes exposed to high temperatures during development can fully restore pathogen-blocking capacity, making Wolbachia-based control strategies highly resilient to heat stress.
Evidence: Compared with the 0–2 d adult collection time point, 4–7 d-old adult females in all treatment groups except the wMel control group and the M3 group had higher Wolbachia levels, with adults from three-day treatments (E3, I3, and M3) showing Wolbachia densities that were not significantly different from wMel-infected controls (Fig 2).
The Wolbachia levels in 4–7 d-old adults from the six other treatments remained significantly lower than in wMel-infected controls.

We also investigated whether we could visualize reductions in Wolbachia levels in the ovaries of adult mosquitoes after exposure to high temperatures during development. Using FISH we visualized very low levels of Wolbachia in the ovaries of 0–2 d-old E7 females (Fig 3B)... In 4–7 d-old E7 females (Fig 3D), Wolbachia remained at very low levels compared with 4–7 d-old wMel-infected controls (Fig 3C).
453.Rationale: Although some groups (notably the three-day exposures) showed recovery to control-like Wolbachia densities by 4–7 days, most longer or differently timed heat treatments did not fully recover and remained significantly lower than controls. Additionally, direct visualization (FISH) showed persistently very low ovarian Wolbachia in the severe E7 treatment even at 4–7 days. The passage therefore does not support the stronger claim that pathogen-blocking capacity is fully restored across the board or that Wolbachia-based strategies are 'highly resilient' to heat stress.

453.Option: Because high temperatures during larval development reduce Wolbachia levels in A. aegypti, the wMel strain is unlikely to persist in urban environments with frequent heatwaves, rendering Wolbachia-based control ineffective in tropical cities.
Evidence: Reduced Wolbachia levels in response to high temperatures during larval development could represent a barrier to the spread of wMel Wolbachia in A. aegypti populations if fundamental drive mechanisms such as maternal transmission and CI are affected.
We found significantly lower Wolbachia densities relative to wMel controls in 0–2 d-old females emerging from eight of the nine treatments (Fig 2)...

It is also unknown whether the prevalence of wMel-infected A. aegypti and the wMel Wolbachia levels within individual mosquitoes will remain high enough to prevent DENV and ZIKV transmission in all environments.
The wMel Wolbachia strain causes complete CI and has had some success in invading wild A. aegypti populations [28, 33].
453.Rationale: The passage documents that high developmental temperatures can reduce Wolbachia levels and explicitly notes this could be a barrier to spread, and that it is unknown whether prevalence will remain high in all environments. However, it also reports that wMel has had 'some success' invading wild populations and provides evidence of partial recovery in some conditions. The passage does not provide direct field evidence or analyses demonstrating that wMel is 'unlikely to persist' in urban environments with frequent heatwaves, nor does it conclude that control will be rendered ineffective across tropical cities. Thus the categorical, definitive claim in this option extends beyond the data presented and cannot be derived solely from the passage.

454.Option: The high seroprevalence of C. trachomatis and enteric pathogens among children in rural Ethiopia suggests that water and hygiene-related transmission pathways are the primary drivers of disease burden in this population.
Evidence: Diarrhea and trachoma typically afflict the world’s poorest populations and are major contributors to preventable morbidity [1,2]. Diarrhea, caused by parasitic, viral and bacterial infections, and trachoma, caused by repeated Chlamydia trachomatis infections of the eye, share water and hygiene related transmission pathways.
We collected dried blood spots from 2267 children residing in 40 communities between January and March of 2016.

The seroprevalence among 0–9 year-olds was 43.1% (95% CI: 38, 48.4) for C. trachomatis, 27.5% (95% CI: 23.6, 31.6) for S. enterica, 70.3% (95% CI:67.7, 72.8) for E. histolytica, 53.9% (95% CI: 51.8, 56.0) for G. intestinalis, 95.6% (95% CI: 94.4, 96.5) for C. jejuni, 76.3% (95% CI: 74.1, 78.4) for ETEC and 94% (95% CI: 92.8, 94.9) for C. parvum.

There was no indication for trend in community-level seroprevalence by community-level median distance to the nearest water source; however, there was considerable variability on community-level seroprevalence for some pathogens (C. trachomatis, G. intestinalis, E. histolytica and S. enterica (Fig 3)).
454.Rationale: The passage documents high seroprevalence for multiple pathogens and notes that diarrhea and trachoma share water- and hygiene-related transmission pathways (supporting plausibility). However, the data do not demonstrate that water and hygiene pathways are the primary drivers across this population: the authors explicitly state there was no indication of a trend in community-level seroprevalence by median distance to the nearest water source overall. Therefore the stronger causal claim that water/hygiene are the primary drivers is not directly supported by the presented results.

454.Option: The use of multiplex immunoassays allowed for the detection of multiple pathogens simultaneously, which revealed that co-exposure to enteric pathogens and C. trachomatis is common and may be linked to household-level socioeconomic status.
Evidence: With recent advances in microsphere-based multiplex immunoassays, antibodies against multiple antigens can be detected simultaneously from a single blood spot [14].

We used a multiplex bead assay to simultaneously measure IgG antibodies to antigens from Chlamydia trachomatis (Pgp3, CT694), Giardia intestinalis (VSP3, VSP5), Cryptosporidium parvum (Cp17, Cp23), Entamoeba histolytica (LecA), Salmonella enterica (LPS Groups B and D), Campylobacter jejuni (p18, p39), enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC heat labile toxin β subunit) and Vibrio cholerae (CtxB) from blood spots collected during the baseline study visit.
Seropositivity for more than 1 pathogen was common (Fig 2). At age 2 years, the median number of pathogens to which a child was seropositive was 4 (IQR 3–5), increasing to 5 (IQR 4–6) by age 4 years.

In the subset of children with household-level data, point estimates were similar but there was no longer a statistically-significant association between distance to the nearest water source and seroprevalence for S. enterica, ETEC or G. intestinalis in the unadjusted or SES-adjusted analysis largely due to the smaller sample size and wider confidence intervals (Table 2).
454.Rationale: The passage clearly supports the first two clauses: multiplex immunoassays were used to detect multiple pathogens simultaneously, and co-exposure (seropositivity to multiple pathogens) was common. However, the option's final clause—that co-exposure "may be linked to household-level socioeconomic status"—is not supported by the presented results. The subset analysis that included household-level SES did not demonstrate statistically significant associations (and the authors attribute loss of significance to small sample size), so there is no direct evidence in the passage that co-exposure is linked to household SES. Therefore the overall option is not supported by the Passage Body.

454.Option: The absence of a significant association between distance to water and seroprevalence for most pathogens suggests that water quantity is not a meaningful determinant of enteric pathogen transmission in high-transmission settings.
Evidence: We hypothesized that the quantity of water available to the household would have a larger effect on C. trachomatis and enteropathogen transmission compared to water quality, and thus used distance to the nearest water source (improved or unimproved) for the analysis.

There was no indication for trend in community-level seroprevalence by community-level median distance to the nearest water source; however, there was considerable variability on community-level seroprevalence for some pathogens (C. trachomatis, G. intestinalis, E. histolytica and S. enterica (Fig 3)).

Children in the quartile living farthest from any water source had a 12% (95% CI: 2.6, 21.4) higher seroprevalence of S. enterica and a 12.7% (95% CI: 2.9, 22.6) higher seroprevalence of G. intestinalis compared to children living in the nearest quartile (Table 2).
454.Rationale: While the authors hypothesized that distance (as a proxy for water quantity) would influence transmission, the results are mixed: there was no overall community-level trend by median distance, but there were statistically significant higher seroprevalences for S. enterica and G. intestinalis among children living farthest from water. The option asserts a broad negative conclusion (water quantity is not a meaningful determinant) that overgeneralizes beyond the evidence. The data do not uniformly show absence of association for all pathogens, nor do they establish the general statement about high-transmission settings. Thus the claim is not supported by the Passage Body.

454.Option: The decline in G. intestinalis seroprevalence after age two years may be due to waning immunity or reduced exposure in older children, suggesting that interventions targeting younger children would be most effective.
Evidence: Seroprevalence increased with age with marked differences across pathogens. The age-dependent seroprevalence of G. intestinalis declined after age 2. (Fig 1).

Antibody response integrates information over time, offering a longer window to identify exposed individuals. [12]. This advantage is especially desirable for studies with infrequent monitoring and data collection visits. Antibody response enumerates symptomatic, asymptomatic and past infections, revealing a more complete picture of transmission [12].
454.Rationale: The passage documents the observation that G. intestinalis seroprevalence declines after age 2. However, explanations such as waning immunity or reduced exposure are speculative and are not provided or tested in the Passage Body. Likewise, the recommendation that interventions targeting younger children would be most effective is an inference beyond the presented data. The passage does not provide evidence to attribute the decline to specific mechanisms or to evaluate intervention effectiveness by age, so the option cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body.

454.Option: Children living farther from a water source had higher seroprevalence of S. enterica and G. intestinalis compared to children living closest to a water source.
Evidence: Children in the quartile living farthest from any water source had a 12% (95% CI: 2.6, 21.4) higher seroprevalence of S. enterica and a 12.7% (95% CI: 2.9, 22.6) higher seroprevalence of G. intestinalis compared to children living in the nearest quartile (Table 2).
454.Rationale: This option restates a direct result reported in the Passage Body. The passage explicitly reports that children in the farthest distance quartile had statistically significantly higher seroprevalence for both S. enterica and G. intestinalis compared to children in the nearest quartile, so this inference is directly supported by the study findings.

455.Option: Age over 60 years is an independent predictor of platelet recovery within 14 days, as older patients tend to have more robust immune responses and faster recovery during convalescence.
Evidence: Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).
455.Rationale: Although age was among variables identified in univariable analysis, the multivariate analysis — which determines independent predictors after adjustment for other variables — found only three independent predictors: days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT. Age was not retained as an independent predictor in the multivariate model, so the claim that age over 60 is an independent predictor is not supported by the passage.

455.Option: Patients with neurological signs or diarrhea at admission are more likely to achieve platelet recovery within two weeks, indicating that these symptoms may reflect a more robust immune response to SFTSV.
Evidence: Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).
The non-recovery group had a higher incidence of diarrhea (P = 0.007), while no significant differences were noted in symptoms of fever, phlegm, and neurological signs (P > 0.05).
455.Rationale: Although neurological signs and diarrhea were considered in univariable analysis, they were not independent predictors in the multivariate model. Moreover, the passage states that the non-recovery group had a higher incidence of diarrhea (P = 0.007), which suggests diarrhea is associated with poorer, not better, recovery. Neurological signs showed no significant difference between groups. Therefore the claim that these symptoms predict more rapid platelet recovery is not supported by the study data.

455.Option: The presence of bacterial or fungal pneumonia during hospitalization is a strong predictor of rapid platelet recovery, suggesting that secondary infections may stimulate compensatory platelet production.
Evidence: The non-recovery group was associated with a disproportionately higher incidence of the need for mechanical ventilation (25%), bacterial pneumonia (40%), and fungal pneumonia (35%) during the hospitalization course (P < 0.05).
455.Rationale: The passage shows that bacterial and fungal pneumonia were more common in the non-recovery group, indicating an association with poorer platelet recovery rather than rapid recovery. There is no evidence in the passage that secondary infections predict faster platelet recovery or stimulate compensatory platelet production; instead, they are linked to non-recovery.

455.Option: The use of favipiravir and supportive therapies was significantly associated with improved platelet recovery within 14 days, suggesting that antiviral treatment plays a central role in accelerating platelet recovery in SFTS patients.
Evidence: treatment information collected from cases included the application of antivirals (favipiravir/ribavirin), symptomatic and supportive therapies (antibacterial therapy, antifungal therapy, corticosteroid therapy, recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (rhG-CSF) therapy, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT), platelet transfusions [12].

These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).
The non-recovery group had a higher application proportion of antifungal therapy (50%), IVIG (30%), and CRRT (10%) (P < 0.05).
455.Rationale: While antiviral and supportive treatments were recorded, the multivariate analysis identified only days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT as independent predictors of platelet recovery. The passage does not report that use of favipiravir or supportive therapies was independently associated with improved 14-day PLT recovery; in fact, some intensive therapies were more common in the non-recovery group. Thus the claim that antiviral treatment was significantly associated with improved PLT recovery is not supported by the presented results.

455.Option: SFTS patients with baseline platelet count greater than 90 × 10⁹/L, days from onset to admission of less than 6 days, and baseline PT of less than 13 seconds may have their PLT recovered rapidly during convalescence.
Evidence: PLT recovery was considered as the primary positive outcome and defined as a value ≥ 100 × 109/L.

These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).

Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery, and baseline PLT greater than 90 × 109/L (HR: 7.929, 95%CI: 1.066–58.990, P = 0.043) contributed to PLT recovery (Table 2).

patients with a PLT count of less than or equal to 30 × 109/L at baseline had a 14-day probability of achieving a PLT recovery of 20%, compared with 63.0% among those with a PLT count at baseline between 30 × 109/L and 60 × 109/L, 81.0% among those with a baseline PLT count between 60 × 109/L and 90 × 109/L, and 92.0% among those with a PLT count at baseline more than 90 × 109/L (P < 0.001).

The probability of PLT recovery within 14 days, based on the stratification of PT at baseline, with 82% for patients with a baseline PT ≤ 13s and 67% for those with a baseline PT > 13s (P = 0.007) (Fig 2C).

According to the stratification by days from onset to admission, patients admitted within 3 days had a 14-day recovery probability of 80%, compared to 85% for those admitted between 3 and 6 days, and 65% for those admitted after more than 6 days (P = 0.002) (Fig 2D).
455.Rationale: The study's multivariate analysis identified baseline PLT, baseline PT, and days from onset to admission as independent predictors of 14-day PLT recovery. Baseline PLT > 90 × 10^9/L had a strongly positive association with recovery (HR 7.929) and the highest 14-day recovery probability (92%). Prothrombin time ≤ 13s was associated with higher recovery probability (82% vs 67%), and admission within ≤6 days (versus >6 days) was associated with better recovery (65% recovery if admitted >6 days vs 80–85% if admitted ≤6 days). Together, these findings directly support the option that patients with baseline PLT >90 ×10^9/L, baseline PT <13s, and earlier admission (less than or equal to 6 days from onset) are most likely to experience rapid platelet recovery within two weeks.

456.Option: The risk score performed well in discriminating case from control households with an accuracy of 0.78 and a sensitivity and specificity at a point value of 3 of 80% and 60%, respectively, suggesting that it can be used to predict individual-level infection risk with high confidence.
Evidence: Receiver operator curve (ROC) analysis yielded a very good to excellent c statistic of 0.78 (95 percent confidence interval: 0.71–0.84) (Figure 2).
We assessed the discriminative power of the score by using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves of sensitivity and specificity.
Table S2 presents the sensitivity, specificity and the estimated proportion of the case and cohort households for each score level.
456.Rationale: The passage supports that the score discriminates case from control households with a c-statistic (AUC) of 0.78, but it does not provide the specific sensitivity and specificity values claimed for a score value of 3 in the body text (those values are only referenced as being in Table S2). Moreover, the study evaluates risk at the household level, not individual-level prediction; claiming high-confidence individual-level prediction overstates what is directly supported by the presented household-level performance metrics.

456.Option: The presence of un-plastered exterior wall surfaces was significantly associated with case households, and is a direct proxy for rodent infestation, indicating that this feature should be prioritized in rodent control programs.
Evidence: The first multivariate logistic regression model, including variables related to household environment, retained the following characteristics: rodent access to water, domicile built on a slope and un-plastered exterior wall surfaces.
The final model retained four variables: two household environmental variables and two rodent infestation factors.
R. norvegicus fecal droppings had the strongest association with case households in the final model followed by rodent burrows, rodent access to water and un-plastered exterior wall surface.
456.Rationale: While un-plastered exterior wall surfaces were retained as an independent environmental variable in the multivariate model, the passage shows that direct markers of infestation (R. norvegicus fecal droppings and burrows) had stronger associations. The text does not state that un-plastered walls are a direct proxy for infestation nor does it provide evidence that this single feature should be prioritized over direct infestation indicators. Thus the stronger claim that it is a direct proxy and should be prioritized is not supported by the passage.

456.Option: The high household infestation rate, elevated Leptospira prevalence, and long-term carriage of R. norvegicus make it a major reservoir host for leptospires, suggesting that eliminating this species would significantly reduce human transmission in urban slums.
Evidence: In this setting, two rat species, the Norway rat (Rattus norvegicus) and black rat (Rattus rattus), are the main reservoirs for this bacterium and contaminate environments via urinary shedding, providing conditions for transmission to humans [2].

Prior studies in urban areas have shown that Leptospira carriage ranges between 7–82% for R. norvegicus [14], [15] and between 7–34% for R. rattus [16], [17]. However, the Norway rat is far more common within the urban slum environments: nearly 100% of rats trapped in the city of Salvador, Brazil comprised of this species [14], [15], [18].
Leptospira strains isolated from Norway rats were genotypically identical with strains obtained from human patients based on PCR-based typing methods [19].

Rat-control programs are commonly implemented as a control measure for leptospirosis in many cities, such as those in Brazil, but their effectiveness is questionable and has not been systematically explored.
456.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that R. norvegicus is a major reservoir (high prevalence among trapped rats, genotypic match to human strains) and that household infestation rates are substantial. However, it explicitly states that rat-control program effectiveness is questionable and has not been systematically evaluated. Therefore the policy conclusion that eliminating this species would significantly reduce human transmission is not directly supported by this study's data and goes beyond the evidence presented.

456.Option: Although our study focused on a single area within Salvador, it would be useful to assess the utility of our measure to predict risk of leptospiral infection in other slum areas, but this will require some tailoring of the survey instrument to some other locations, as every slum has unique characteristics and significant socioeconomic and environmental heterogeneities.
Evidence: This study was conducted in Pau da Lima, a slum community situated in the periphery of Salvador, a city of 2.7 million inhabitants [23] in Northeast Brazil. This site was selected for epidemiological studies on leptospirosis based on the high annual incidence of severe leptospirosis (35.4 cases per 100,000 pop.) identified in this community by active surveillance during1996 to 2002.

The socioeconomic profile in this site was similar to other slum populations in Brazil: subjects were squatters (85%) who did not complete primary school (77%) and had a median household per capita income of $1.30 per day.
the CDC survey form has not been validated for application in slum areas of developing countries.

we describe an environmental and rodent survey instrument, adapted from CDC guidelines, for use in a tropical slum area. In addition, we developed and evaluated the accuracy of a scoring system to predict Leptospira transmission using data easily produced by this instrument.
456.Rationale: The passage documents a single-site study and adaptation of a survey instrument that has not been validated in slum settings. It also notes similarities and variability in socioeconomic context. From an epidemiologic perspective, these facts support the inference that external assessment in other slum areas would be useful and that some tailoring may be needed because slums can differ in socioeconomic and environmental characteristics.

456.Option: The risk score system we developed, by weighting and combining values for each of these features, accurately classified households into risk groups for Leptospira infection with high precision.
Evidence: To build a risk score, we assigned numerical scores to each of the four independent variables from the final model proportionate to the regression coefficient for each variable (Table 2).
The sum of the number was used to classify each household into ten categories ranging from 0 to 9.
The median risk score for case households was 7, statistically different from the value of 2 for control households (p<0.001).
Receiver operator curve (ROC) analysis yielded a very good to excellent c statistic of 0.78 (95 percent confidence interval: 0.71–0.84) (Figure 2).
456.Rationale: The passage supports that a weighted score was developed and that it discriminates cases from controls with a c-statistic of 0.78 and statistically different median scores. However, the claim of 'high precision' is stronger than the evidence: an AUC of 0.78 indicates good discrimination but not necessarily 'high precision' in absolute terms. The passage does not provide measures of precision (e.g., narrow CIs around predictive values at all score levels) that would justify the categorical phrase 'high precision.' Therefore the strong wording is not fully supported by the presented data.

457.Option: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests that these variants were likely acquired through random chance rather than selection.
Evidence: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift.

The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift.
457.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the presence of the same haplotypes across multiple fetuses and dams suggests positive selection rather than genetic drift or random chance. Therefore the option asserting these variants were likely acquired through random chance contradicts the authors' interpretation and cannot be derived from the passage.

457.Option: The observed reduction in ZIKV diversity in both the fetal bodies and fetal brains at the late stages of ZIKV infection could arise by the reduction in ZIKV diversity first occurring in the fetal periphery prior to neuroinvasion, or by the selection of certain ZIKV variants during entry into the brain.
Evidence: ZIKV RNA levels were consistently higher and detected earlier in fetal bodies compared to fetal brains, confirming that ZIKV first enters and replicates in the fetal periphery prior to entering the brain during ZIKV vertical transmission.

In our model, we found that transmission of ZIKV from the placenta to the fetal periphery began in some fetuses by ~7 dpi, and neuroinvasion from the fetal periphery to the fetal brain happened at ~13 dpi.

Overall, there was a small reduction in ZIKV diversity in the placentas and a sharp reduction of ZIKV diversity in most fetal bodies at this later stage of ZIKV infection. This observation suggests that after viral entry into the fetus from the placenta, selection pressures within the fetus result in the homogenous population.

The increase in frequency from the placenta to the fetal body to the fetal brain suggests that these variants may have been selected for in the fetal tissues, however we cannot definitively make this conclusion.
457.Rationale: The passage documents that infection appears first in the fetal periphery and later in the brain, and reports a sharp reduction in diversity in fetal bodies and brains at late stages. The authors explicitly propose that this reduction could reflect selection pressures within the fetus and note the possibility that variants become enriched between placenta, fetal body, and brain. Thus both mechanisms presented in the option (reduction in periphery prior to neuroinvasion and selection during entry into the brain) are directly supported by the passage.

457.Option: The immunocompromised nature of the AIR mouse model may influence the dynamics of vertical transmission, and the observed ZIKV population patterns may not reflect the selection pressures in immunocompetent humans.
Evidence: To evaluate ZIKV diversity during vertical transmission and entry into the fetal central nervous system (CNS), we used the previously established anti-interferon treated Rag1-/- (AIR) mouse model of ZIKV vertical transmission, which was associated with higher fetal viability than the Ifnar-/- x WT model [16,17].
Rag1-/- mice (Jackson Laboratories), which are deficient in B and T lymphocytes, were maintained on a C57BL/6 background in a breeding colony at RML.

At 8–12 weeks old, Rag1-/- female mice were time-mated with age-matched Rag1-/- males for 3 days. Six days after the initial pairing (embryonic day (E) 3–6), pregnant AIR mice were treated intraperitoneally (i.p.) with 1mg of anti-IFNAR1 clone MAR1-5A3.
457.Rationale: The passage describes the AIR mouse model as Rag1-/- (lacking B and T lymphocytes) and treated with anti-IFNAR1 antibody, demonstrating an immunocompromised experimental system. From an epidemiological/experimental-interpretation perspective, different immune environments can alter viral population dynamics and selection pressures. Although the authors do not explicitly state that results do not generalize to immunocompetent humans, this inference is reasonably derived from the documented immunodeficient features of the model and thus is supported by the passage.

457.Option: The two fetuses with mismatched ZIKV populations in fetal bodies and brains likely represent a failure of vertical transmission, indicating that the virus did not successfully cross the placenta in these cases.
Evidence: For fetus E-3, the variants present at the highest frequency in the body, 3895 (83.9%) and 4823 (74.7%), were only found in the matched fetal brain at 1.4% and 0, respectively.
The dominant sites in the E-3 fetal brain at positions 449, 4319, and 5336 were only detected in the matched fetal body at frequencies of 9.2, 0, and 4.4%, respectively.

promastigote stages of the L. major NTPD1 null mutant exhibited a marked and highly reproducible delay in lesion development. This delay was largely abrogated by complementation of the null mutant by insertion of a full-length ntpd1 gene in the highly-transcribed ribosomal ssu locus [52].

It also emphasizes community engagement and empowerment as key to success. These direct descriptions of system-level shortcomings reasonably support the epidemiological inference that the health system demonstrated substantial unpreparedness for large-scale public health emergencies. The conclusion is drawn from the reported operational and resource failures rather than external assumptions.

460.Option: The 2023/2024 outbreak in Zambia was the largest in the country’s history, with a case fatality rate of 1.8%, indicating that the current case management and treatment protocols are insufficient to prevent severe outcomes in high-burden settings.

The option's conclusion—that current protocols are insufficient to prevent severe outcomes—attributes causality specifically to protocols rather than to access, resource constraints, community-level delays, or patient-level risk factors. suggested the need for booster vaccinations, particularly in high-risk areas, as a possible public health protective strategy.

From these statements it is a reasonable epidemiological inference that predictable geography and seasonality could inform the planning (location, sizing, and timing) of repeat or pre-emptive vaccination campaigns, including timing them before the rainy season. 461.Option: The spotted doves' susceptibility to SFTSV infection and their migratory behavior suggest that they are the primary source of SFTSV introduction into new regions, including Japan and South Korea.

461.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that spotted doves can be parasitized by the tick vector and can be infected, and it states these features give them the potential to contribute to long-distance spread. The latter half of the option (that increased vulnerability makes them less likely to contribute to long-distance transmission) is not supported by any data: the study does not measure effects on migration, transmission to ticks, or population-level impacts. Thus the causal inference about reduced contribution to long-distance transmission cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

The paper also states that vertebrate animals with moderate viremia might serve as amplifying hosts. Therefore, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that JS2014's higher replication in spotted doves confers a greater potential for those birds to act as amplifying hosts (i.e., infect feeding ticks), compared with the Chinese lineage, based on the evidence presented.

461.Option: Spotted doves are likely to serve as reservoir hosts for SFTSV due to their ability to develop persistent viremia and long-term immunity after infection.
Evidence: Our data suggest that the infection of SFTSV in spotted doves was primarily self-limiting and infected birds recovered after a defined period of viremia.
Viremia detected in birds from both 107 PFU challenge groups fell to the threshold of detection (5–6 days) more rapidly than either the 105 or 103 PFU challenge groups (7–8 days).
At 14 dpi, however, SFTSV was no longer detectable by either viral isolation or RT-PCR in any tissues from the three sacrificed birds.
SFTSV specific antibodies were detected in all three inoculated birds at 14 dpi while they were negative by viral isolation.
461.Rationale: A reservoir host would be expected to maintain the pathogen over extended periods (persistent infection) to sustain transmission. The passage demonstrates that spotted doves develop a transient, self-limiting viremia that falls below detection within about 5–8 days and that virus is not detectable in tissues by 14 dpi; they do develop antibodies. These findings indicate clearance rather than persistence, so the data do not support classifying spotted doves as reservoir hosts. They may have short-term infectiousness (potentially amplifying), but not persistent reservoir status based on the presented evidence.

462.Option: The low apparent density of tsetse in Yasa Bonga, despite high g-HAT incidence, suggests that tsetse abundance is not a reliable predictor of disease transmission risk and that alternative factors such as host behavior or vector competence must be prioritized in future control strategies.
Evidence: Across the 1,234 individual trapping sites a total of 837 flies were caught over 4,952 trapping days (0.17 flies/trap/day).

we implemented a large-scale trial of Tiny Targets in Yasa Bonga Health Zone, one of the foci where the addition of vector control to screening activities is predicted to accelerate progress towards the elimination goal [4,6].
462.Rationale: The passage documents a low measured tsetse catch (0.17 flies/trap/day) and that Yasa Bonga is a g-HAT focus where interventions were implemented. However, the passage does not provide data on g-HAT incidence in Yasa Bonga during the study or analyses linking local tsetse abundance to transmission risk. The leap to conclude that tsetse abundance is not a reliable predictor of transmission, and that alternative factors must be prioritized, is not supported by the provided text. Therefore this inference cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body.

462.Option: The habitat suitability maps derived from dry season data can be reliably extrapolated to predict tsetse distribution and intervention effectiveness across all seasons, enabling year-round planning of vector control activities.
Evidence: Due to limitations such as cloud cover, no suitable Sentinel-2 wet season images were available for the targeted time period and a single dry season image was identified for July 2016.
Due to these limitations in our ability to obtain regular images it was not possible to explore the impact of interannual variability in temporally varying environmental factors in this study.
Dry season data were used to develop the habitat suitability maps i.e. data from 332 locations of which 20% (65/332) caught at least one fly during the observation period.
the flies/trap/day over the nine-month wet season (0.12 flies/trap/day) was lower than that of the shorter dry season (0.28 flies/trap/day) with this difference being significant (p<0.0001)
there does not appear to be any interaction between the two i.e. the effect of the intervention on the fly rate is the same both in the wet and dry season.
462.Rationale: The study explicitly used dry season data for habitat mapping because of data limitations and states it could not explore interannual or broad temporal variability. It also documents significant seasonal differences in tsetse catches. Although the intervention effect was observed in both seasons, the passage does not support the strong claim that dry-season-derived maps can be reliably extrapolated across all seasons for year-round planning. Thus this option is not supported by the Passage Body.

462.Option: Tiny Targets exerted their greatest impact in areas close (<500m) to where they were deployed, and this local effect is likely to strengthen public perception of their effectiveness, suggesting potential for community-based implementation with proper support and supervision.
Evidence: In comparing the number of flies caught in pre-intervention traps ... and post-intervention traps (those within 0-500m of a recently deployed target) the number of flies per trap per day reduces from 0.22 to 0.06 (Table 2).

In the GLMM fitted to the fly count data from all traps, with an intervention factor (0-500m, 500m-5km, >5km from the intervention) and trap site-level random effects only, this intervention effect is significant.
Within the wet season the rate reduces from 0.16 to 0.04 (within 500m of the intervention) whereas in the dry season the rate reduces from 0.33 to 0.10 flies/trap/day.

Within 6 months of the implementation of the intervention (monitoring operation T1 and T2) a reduction in fly counts is observed in traps set within 500m from a target, and in the ‘edge’ areas (within 500m: adjusted log-RR = -3.25, 95% CI = [-4.5682, -1.5294]; ‘edge’ areas: adjusted log-RR = -5.90, 95% CI = [-9.8315, -2.5211]).

The deployment of Tiny Targets (Vestergaard, Lausanne, Switzerland) in Yasa Bonga was carried out by teams of local individuals who had no experience of tsetse control prior to the intervention. These teams were recruited and trained by LSTM and the Programme National de Lutte contre la Trypanosomiase Humaine Africaine (PNLTHA) on how to install a Tiny Target ... and how to record the location of all Tiny Targets using GPSs.

They deployed over relatively long distances of up to 40km of river within a one week period. They remained in the field during the deployment, camping along the rivers, which enabled them to cover these distances in a short time period.

Within the study period, Tiny Targets were first deployed in July-August 2015 (4,782 targets deployed) and thereafter in January-February and July-September of 2016 (12,421 targets deployed) and 2017 (18,883 targets deployed) (Fig 1).
462.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear entomological evidence that the intervention produced the largest reductions in fly catches in traps located within 500m of targets, with statistically significant declines shown by GLMM results and adjusted log-RR estimates. The operational description documents that local, previously inexperienced teams were recruited, trained, and successfully deployed large numbers of targets rapidly over long river stretches, demonstrating operational feasibility at scale with appropriate training and supervision. While the passage does not present direct survey data on public perception, the strong, localized impact combined with community involvement reasonably supports the inference that perceived effectiveness and community-based implementation are plausible with proper support—this inference follows from the documented local impact and the described operational model.

462.Option: The seasonal variation in tsetse abundance, with higher catches in the dry season, indicates that Tiny Targets should be deployed only during the dry season to maximize their impact and reduce operational costs.
Evidence: the flies/trap/day over the nine-month wet season (0.12 flies/trap/day) was lower than that of the shorter dry season (0.28 flies/trap/day) with this difference being significant (p<0.0001)

In considering both the effect of season and the intervention on fly rate simultaneously, there does not appear to be any interaction between the two i.e. the effect of the intervention on the fly rate is the same both in the wet and dry season.
Within the study period, Tiny Targets were first deployed in July-August 2015 ... and thereafter in January-February and July-September of 2016 ... and 2017 ...

Time since target deployment on the tsetse population was stratified into three categories: 0–2 months, 3–5 months, >6 months/never). ... A simple negative binomial GLMM with time since deployment as the only fixed effect suggests no difference between catches made 0–2 month after target deployment and 3–5 months after target deployment (p = 0.1170), suggesting that the impact of the targets is rapid and persistent.
462.Rationale: Although the Passage Body documents higher raw tsetse catches in the dry season, it also reports that the intervention effect did not differ between wet and dry seasons (no interaction), and that the impact of targets was rapid and persistent for several months. Additionally, targets were repeatedly deployed at roughly six-month intervals (covering both wet and dry periods). The passage does not provide evidence that limiting deployments solely to the dry season would maintain effectiveness or be advisable; thus the recommendation to deploy only in the dry season is not supported by the data presented.

462.Option: The success of the Tiny Targets intervention in Yasa Bonga demonstrates that community-led vector control can be effective without external supervision, as local teams were able to deploy targets rapidly and efficiently using only minimal training.
Evidence: The deployment of Tiny Targets (Vestergaard, Lausanne, Switzerland) in Yasa Bonga was carried out by teams of local individuals who had no experience of tsetse control prior to the intervention. These teams were recruited and trained by LSTM and the Programme National de Lutte contre la Trypanosomiase Humaine Africaine (PNLTHA) on how to install a Tiny Target on the riverbank to ensure optimal performance and how to record the location of all Tiny Targets using GPSs.
They were recruited and trained by LSTM and the Programme National de Lutte contre la Trypanosomiase Humaine Africaine (PNLTHA) ...

They deployed over relatively long distances of up to 40km of river within a one week period. They remained in the field during the deployment, camping along the rivers, which enabled them to cover these distances in a short time period.
Within the study period, Tiny Targets were first deployed ... (4,782 targets deployed) and thereafter ... (12,421 targets deployed) and 2017 (18,883 targets deployed) (Fig 1).
462.Rationale: While the passage describes that local teams without prior experience were successfully recruited, trained, and carried out large-scale, rapid deployments, it explicitly states that these teams were trained by external partners (LSTM and PNLTHA). The text does not claim deployments occurred without external supervision or support; indeed, training and programmatic coordination were provided. Therefore the stronger claim that community-led control was effective without external supervision is not supported by the Passage Body.

463.Option: The successful reduction of esophageal varices and prevention of re-bleeding in this case series suggests that TIPS should be prioritized as a first-line treatment for hepatosplenic schistosomiasis in all endemic regions.
Evidence: After TIPS or shunt surgery, esophageal varices were distinctively reduced with a median reduction of 2 degrees according to Paquet’s classification.
No variceal bleeding events were seen during this time.

Implementation in HSS, however, is based on limited evidence: One prospective study from 1994 showed unfavorable rates of re-bleeding and hepatic encephalopathy [17], which led to TIPS being discouraged in HSS patients [18].
This case series was compiled from all patients with confirmed HSS treated interventionally or surgically between 2012 and 2020 in four centers in Germany (3), and Italy (1).
463.Rationale: While the passage documents reduction of varices and absence of re-bleeding in this small, selected case series, it also explicitly states that TIPS implementation in HSS is based on limited evidence and was previously discouraged. The data come from a small number of patients treated in specialized centers in Germany and Italy, so extrapolating to recommend TIPS as first-line treatment across all endemic regions is not supported by the passage. The passage lacks broader comparative data, population-level outcomes, and considerations of feasibility in endemic settings that would be necessary to justify prioritizing TIPS everywhere.

463.Option: The transient thrombocytosis observed after splenectomy in this case series suggests that shunt surgery is superior to TIPS in improving platelet counts and reducing bleeding risk in hepatosplenic schistosomiasis.
Evidence: Shunt surgery was conducted in the patient with unsuccessful TIPS insertion and another patient with portal venous thrombosis.

Both patients developed a transient thrombocytosis (max. 967/nl and 831/nl) (Table 5), which may have contributed to the development of an irreversible shunt occlusion in one patient despite anticoagulative therapy with rivaroxaban in combination with ASA for the inhibition of platelet aggregation.
even in this patient, follow-up revealed complete regression of esophageal varicosis (previously III°).
In ten patients TIPS insertion was successful at the first attempt (technical success rate 83%)
463.Rationale: The passage reports transient thrombocytosis after splenectomy in two surgical patients, and notes regression of varices in these cases, but it does not provide comparative data demonstrating superiority of shunt surgery over TIPS for platelet counts or bleeding risk. The surgical cases were only two patients and were selected due to unsuccessful or contraindicated TIPS. Moreover, thrombocytosis was noted as a potential problem (contributing to shunt occlusion), not clearly a beneficial outcome. Therefore, superiority of surgery relative to TIPS cannot be inferred from the presented data.

463.Option: TIPS intervention and shunt surgery require a high level of expertise and equipment, and therefore do not yet represent a broad treatment option for the majority of endemic regions.
Evidence: This case series was compiled from all patients with confirmed HSS treated interventionally or surgically between 2012 and 2020 in four centers in Germany (3), and Italy (1).
For assessment of vascular structure and cardiac function a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen and an echocardiography were performed.

After transjugular catheterization of the right hepatic vein and ultrasound- or CT-guided transhepatic puncture of an intrahepatic branch of the portal vein, a portosystemic stent was inserted and dilated to the desired diameter.
Successful placement and shunting were confirmed by angiography. Intravascular pressures of caval and portal veins were measured invasively during the procedure before and after placing the stent.
In ten patients TIPS insertion was successful at the first attempt (technical success rate 83%) despite significant stiffness of the fibrotic periportal tissue.
Postinterventional inpatient care was continued for about one week.
463.Rationale: The passage describes advanced imaging (contrast CT, angiography), invasive hemodynamic measurements, specialized interventional technique and postprocedural inpatient care, and reports performance only in specialized centers in Germany and Italy. These details indicate substantial infrastructure, equipment and expertise requirements. From an epidemiological and feasibility perspective, these requirements imply that TIPS and complex shunt surgeries are unlikely to be readily scalable as broad interventions across most schistosomiasis-endemic regions, which commonly have limited specialized interventional radiology and surgical capacity. Thus the inference about limited broad applicability in endemic regions is supported by the passage.

463.Option: The high rate of hepatic encephalopathy observed in this study indicates that TIPS is contraindicated in patients with hepatosplenic schistosomiasis due to the risk of irreversible neurological complications.
Evidence: In addition to the three previously mentioned patients with hepatic encephalopathy immediately after TIPS insertion, two more patients experienced de-novo episodes of overt hepatic encephalopathy during later follow-up.
Both subsided upon increasing or restart of the lactulose therapy and addition of rifaximin.
No major periinterventional complications (e.g. major bleeding, hepatic laceration) occurred and no post-TIPS cardiac decompensation was observed.
463.Rationale: Although some patients experienced hepatic encephalopathy after TIPS, the passage reports that these episodes were treatable and subsided with therapy. The series does not characterize the encephalopathy as irreversible, nor does it present rates or severity sufficient to conclude a contraindication. Additionally, the cohort is small and selected, and the passage does not state that encephalopathy rates are unacceptably high compared with benefits. Therefore the strong claim that TIPS is contraindicated due to irreversible neurological risk is not supported by the presented data.

463.Option: The cost of TIPS implantation in endemic regions is likely to be significantly lower than in high-income countries due to reduced personnel and equipment expenses.
Evidence: For assessment of vascular structure and cardiac function a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen and an echocardiography were performed.

After transjugular catheterization of the right hepatic vein and ultrasound- or CT-guided transhepatic puncture of an intrahepatic branch of the portal vein, a portosystemic stent was inserted and dilated to the desired diameter.
Successful placement and shunting were confirmed by angiography. Intravascular pressures of caval and portal veins were measured invasively during the procedure before and after placing the stent.
This case series was compiled from all patients with confirmed HSS treated interventionally or surgically between 2012 and 2020 in four centers in Germany (3), and Italy (1).
463.Rationale: The passage provides detailed descriptions of the imaging, invasive monitoring and specialized interventional techniques required for TIPS, and shows that procedures were performed in high-resource centers. It contains no data or statements about costs in endemic regions or relative personnel/equipment expenses. There is no basis in the passage to infer that TIPS would be significantly cheaper in endemic regions; indeed, the resource-intense procedures described suggest the opposite. Hence the cost claim cannot be derived from the presented material.

464.Option: The community-led response to Ebola in Liberia was primarily driven by external funding and technical support from international organizations, which enabled the development of local infrastructure and surveillance systems.
Evidence: communities were compelled to generate solutions of their own.
Communities are doing things on their own, with or without our support
In the absence of open clinics and hospitals, residents tried to assume responsibility for all aspects of healthcare in their local communities.

They requested government and other organizational support to create community “holding centers” to serve as interim sites for the sick and dead while waiting for Ebola response teams and/or burial teams to arrive.
464.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes that communities initiated and implemented responses themselves because formal public health systems had failed or been insufficient. Phrases such as "communities were compelled to generate solutions of their own" and "Communities are doing things on their own, with or without our support" indicate local initiative rather than being "primarily driven" by external funding and technical support. Although communities requested government and organizational support (quote: "They requested government and other organizational support..."), the text does not state that external funding/technical support primarily drove the development of local infrastructure and surveillance. On the contrary, the evidence indicates community-led, self-reliant action in the absence or delay of external assistance, so the asserted causal claim is not supported by the passage.

464.Option: The gendered division of labor in community responses to Ebola resulted in equal levels of exposure and risk for men and women, despite differences in their roles in surveillance and caregiving.
Evidence: home-based care constituted a zone of risk for both predominantly female caregivers and their dependents, in contrast to community-based surveillance, education, and transport roles, which functioned as a zone of risk for men.

This may have also had unrecognized repercussions on infant and child mortality, and on the now-confirmed explosive chains of Ebola transmission within kinship networks and families during the Ebola crisis [46].

The reported deaths of young children under quarantine paint a challenging picture to communities’ descriptions of providing care (food and water) to local families and children throughout the quarantine period. It also facilitated the impression that women’s greater involvement in the direct support of children’s diet and healthcare and in traditional burial practices was resulting in a gender differential in levels of exposure, although this has not been borne out by the official data [47–48, 22].

although male and female case incidences and fatalities were roughly equal [2], women’s risk of mortality may have been impacted by their greater reluctance to seek early treatment for Ebola out of fear for children’s lives, and due to concern for children’s and dependents’ well-being under the quarantine that would follow their hospitalization at an Ebola treatment unit (ETU) or temporary quarantine at community-care centers (CCCs).
464.Rationale: The passage documents a clear gendered division of roles (women in home-based caregiving; men in external surveillance/transport) and suggests these roles create different zones of risk: "home-based care constituted a zone of risk for both predominantly female caregivers..." and "community-based surveillance... risk for men." It also notes an "impression" of a gender differential in exposure but explicitly states that this has "not been borne out by the official data" and that male and female incidences/fatalities were "roughly equal." Thus the passage does not support the categorical inference that the gendered division resulted in equal levels of exposure and risk; it instead reports role-based differences in exposure potential and indicates that official outcome data did not show a clear differential. Therefore the statement that the division "resulted in equal levels of exposure and risk" is not supported by the passage.

464.Option: The ethnographic evidence suggests that local communities in Liberia were capable of engaging in medical self-surveillance, self-management, and self-triage during the Ebola outbreak, despite the absence of functioning public health infrastructure.
Evidence: communities were compelled to generate solutions of their own.
Communities are doing things on their own, with or without our support
In the absence of open clinics and hospitals, residents tried to assume responsibility for all aspects of healthcare in their local communities.

community leaders described how they proposed to engage in prevention efforts through training and awareness, hygiene, surveillance, and the creation of local infrastructures; how they proposed to conduct treatment and response through a process of isolation, quarantine, and triage;

community leaders’ accounts of optimal community-based Ebola surveillance included a four-tiered system of surveillance that was designed to prevent introduction of the virus into the community, facilitate reporting, disseminate information, sustain house-to-house monitoring, and support whistle blowing when community-members were non-compliant with EVD prevention protocols (Fig 2).
They described a plan for isolating themselves with a sick family member[s] and for providing the best locally available appropriate care they could offer, using available resources (Fig 4).
community-based quarantine and home-based healthcare... community-leaders gave careful thought to how they might best support individuals and families in isolation and quarantine.
464.Rationale: Multiple passages document that, faced with failing formal health systems, communities planned and executed surveillance, quarantine, triage, and home-based care. Statements such as "residents tried to assume responsibility for all aspects of healthcare," the detailed description of a "four-tiered system of surveillance," and community plans for "isolation, quarantine, and triage" directly support the inference that local communities engaged in medical self-surveillance, self-management, and self-triage despite the absence of functioning public health infrastructure. The ethnographic data presented are explicitly about community-led prevention, response, and treatment strategies, making this inference derivable from the passage.

464.Option: The failure of formal health systems in Liberia led to the complete collapse of community-based response efforts, as communities lacked the resources and knowledge to manage the outbreak independently.
Evidence: In the absence of open clinics and hospitals, residents tried to assume responsibility for all aspects of healthcare in their local communities.

This study shows how community-based responses both supported and discouraged formal efforts to manage the epidemic; and how community-based informal responses may have contributed to containing the outbreak.

community leaders described how they proposed to engage in prevention efforts through training and awareness, hygiene, surveillance, and the creation of local infrastructures; how they proposed to conduct treatment and response through a process of isolation, quarantine, and triage;
They described a plan for isolating themselves with a sick family member[s] and for providing the best locally available appropriate care they could offer, using available resources (Fig 4).
464.Rationale: The passage documents active community planning and implementation of surveillance, quarantine, home-based care, holding centers, and other responses. It states that "residents tried to assume responsibility for all aspects of healthcare" and that community-based responses "may have contributed to containing the outbreak." While the text also describes severe resource constraints and tragic consequences (e.g., deaths of children in quarantine due to lack of care), it does not say community efforts completely collapsed or that communities uniformly lacked knowledge or will to respond. Therefore the assertion of a "complete collapse of community-based response efforts" is not supported by the passage.

465.Option: ZIKV blocks stress granule assembly by directly cleaving G3BP1 and TIAR, preventing their recruitment to sites of viral replication.
Evidence: Cleavage of proteins that nucleate SG assembly has been reported to be a strategy employed by viruses to overcome cellular stress response [28].
No alteration was observed in the levels of G3BP-1, TIAR and PABP between mock and infected cells (Fig 1C).
These results indicate that ZIKV infection does not induce changes in the levels of SG-nucleating proteins.

TIAR was still found in both the cytoplasm and nucleus but also concentrated in foci in the perinuclear region (Fig 1A and 1B), colocalizing with the ZIKV RNA (Fig 1A) and viral nonstructural protein, NS1 (Fig 1B).
465.Rationale: The passage explicitly examined whether ZIKV altered levels of SG-nucleating proteins and found no change in the levels of G3BP-1 and TIAR, which argues against direct cleavage. Furthermore, TIAR was redistributed and colocalized with viral RNA/NS1 rather than being absent, inconsistent with cleavage preventing recruitment. Thus the specific mechanism of direct proteolytic cleavage of G3BP1 or TIAR by ZIKV is not supported by the data in the passage.

465.Option: ZIKV blocks stress granule assembly by activating the PERK kinase to promote eIF2α phosphorylation, thereby preventing the formation of stress granules.
Evidence: ZIKV infection blocks SG assembly and phosphorylation of eIF2α triggered by Ars, an HRI activator.

DTT treatment induced SG assembly in 81.8% of the mock-infected cells (Fig 5A and 5B). In contrast, only 28.6% of ZIKV-infected cells presented SG (Fig 5A and 5B). The blockage of SG assembly correlates with lower levels of p-eIF2α upon DTT treated in ZIKV-infected cells (Fig 5C, lane 4) when compared to mock-infected cells (Fig 5C, lane 2).

Interestingly, the activation of PERK in DTT-treated cells, demonstrated by an increased PERK mobility, was similar in mock and ZIKV-infected cells (Fig 5C, compare lanes 2 and 4 ...), suggesting that the reduced levels of p-eIF2α in infected cells are a result of an interference downstream the activation of the eIF2α kinases.
465.Rationale: The passage shows that PERK activation (upon DTT treatment) is similar in mock and infected cells, while phosphorylated eIF2α (p-eIF2α) levels are reduced in ZIKV-infected cells. Thus ZIKV does not promote eIF2α phosphorylation via PERK activation; rather, it impairs eIF2α phosphorylation downstream of kinase activation. Therefore the proposed mechanism (blocking SGs by activating PERK to promote eIF2α phosphorylation) contradicts the experimental findings.

465.Option: ZIKV infection induces the assembly of stress granules in Huh-7 cells, suggesting that the virus's interaction with the host stress response is cell type-specific.
Evidence: Green African monkey kidney (Vero) (ATCC) cells and human osteosarcoma-derived U2OS containing G3BP1-GFP ... were maintained ...

In the absence of stress, mock-infected (blue arrows) and ZIKV-infected cells (red arrows) exhibited SG assembly at a rate of 0.61% and 0%, respectively (Fig 2A, top panels and Fig 2B), indicating that ZIKV infection does not induce the assembly of SG.
Similar results were observed when U2OS cells were used in place of Vero cells (S1 Fig).
465.Rationale: The experiments were performed in Vero and U2OS cells, and the passage reports that ZIKV infection did not induce stress granule assembly in these cell types. The passage contains no data about Huh-7 cells, nor any evidence that ZIKV induces SGs in any cell type. Therefore the specific claim about Huh-7 cells and a cell-type-specific induction of SGs is unsupported by the presented data.

465.Option: ZIKV inhibits stress granule assembly in a phospho-eIF2α-dependent manner, which may reflect a strategy to promote a cellular environment favorable for viral replication.
Evidence: These findings indicate that ZIKV infection blockage of SG assembly is eIF2α-dependent.
These results indicate that ZIKV infection impairs eIF2α phosphorylation triggered by oxidative stress.
ZIKV infection blocks SG assembly and phosphorylation of eIF2α triggered by Ars, an HRI activator.

In the absence of stress, mock-infected ... and ZIKV-infected cells ... exhibited SG assembly at a rate of 0.61% and 0%, respectively ... indicating that ZIKV infection does not induce the assembly of SG.

Pateamine A ... leads to the assembly of SG in an eIF2α-independent manner ... ZIKV infection did not impair PatA-induced SG assembly, as 97.5% of the infected cells presented TIAR and eIF4G puncta (Fig 3A, top panels, and 3B).
Several viruses, including many members of Flaviviridae family, have the ability to modulate SG assembly to keep the cell environment favorable to their own replication [29, 30].
Treatment of cells with salubrinal led to a dose-dependent decrease in the production of infectious particles released to the culture media (Fig 7A, bar graph and 7B).
465.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that ZIKV blocks SG assembly specifically in contexts that require eIF2α phosphorylation (arsenite and ER stress) while not blocking eIF2α-independent SG assembly (PatA, Se). It also shows that ZIKV infection impairs eIF2α phosphorylation and that manipulating the eIF2α dephosphorylation machinery (salubrinal) both restores SG assembly and reduces viral titers. The authors explicitly state the blockage is eIF2α-dependent and place this in the context of viral strategies to keep the cell environment favorable for replication. Thus this inference is directly supported by the data in the passage.

465.Option: The inhibition of ZIKV replication by salubrinal is due to the drug's ability to enhance eIF2α phosphorylation independently of viral infection, suggesting a broad antiviral effect.
Evidence: In cells treated with salubrinal prior to Ars-induced stress, ZIKV-infected cells present higher levels of phospho-eIF2α as compared to mock-infected control (Fig 6C, compare lanes 6 and 8).

The assembly of SG in the distinct conditions was monitored by indirect immunofluorescence. SGs were induced in 23.1±6.5% of ZIKV-infected cells treated with Ars. This value increased to 47.8±7.0% in cells that were treated with salubrinal prior to Ars-induced stress and was not significantly different from mock-infected cells (Fig 6D and 6E). No significant difference was observed between control or salubrinal pre-treated mock-infected cells (Fig 6E).
Treatment of cells with salubrinal led to a dose-dependent decrease in the production of infectious particles released to the culture media (Fig 7A, bar graph and 7B).
465.Rationale: The passage shows that salubrinal (a PP1/GADD34 inhibitor) increases phospho-eIF2α in the context of arsenite-induced stress and ZIKV infection, and that salubrinal treatment reduces ZIKV titers. However, the data do not demonstrate that salubrinal enhances eIF2α phosphorylation independently of viral infection (there is no experiment showing salubrinal alone raises p-eIF2α or has antiviral effects across infections), and the passage even notes no significant difference between control and salubrinal pre-treated mock-infected cells in one assay. Therefore concluding that the antiviral effect is due to an infection-independent enhancement of eIF2α phosphorylation and that it implies a broad antiviral effect is not supported by the presented data.

466.Option: The WHO-recommended single dose of 500 mg mebendazole is more effective than previously believed when accounting for diagnostic error, as the model-based estimates show a higher cure rate than observed data.
Evidence: WHO recommends single doses of 400 mg albendazole and 500 mg mebendazole in PC campaigns against hookworm and other STH infections [1].

The two least efficacious treatments were mebendazole 500 mg and oxantel pamoate (20 mg/kg) with CRs of 14% (95% BCI 11–18%) and 10% (95% BCI 7–15%) and ERRs of 21% (95% BCI -5–41%) and -26% (95% BCI -8–16%), respectively.
Fig 2 shows a comparison of the model estimates of the CRs and ERRs to the observed ones, where the ‘true’ estimates of CR were lower than the observed ones.
466.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the model-based ('true') cure rates were lower than the observed cure rates. It also identifies single-dose 500 mg mebendazole as one of the two least efficacious treatments with a low model-based CR (14%). Therefore the claim that the model shows a higher cure rate for 500 mg mebendazole than observed (i.e., that it is more effective when accounting for diagnostic error) is contradicted by the passage.

466.Option: The combination of albendazole, pyrantel pamoate, and oxantel pamoate is the most effective treatment for hookworm infection, with a cure rate of 66% and an egg reduction rate of 98%, which exceeds the efficacy of all other regimens tested.
Evidence: The treatments that achieved the highest ‘true’ CRs and ERRs were mebendazole (6x100 mg) with a CR of 88% (95% BCI 79–95%) and an ERR of 99.6% (95% BCI 98–100%) and the triple combination treatment (albendazole 400 mg plus oxantel pamoate 20 mg/kg plus pyrantel pamoate 20 mg/kg) with a CR of 66% (95% BCI 59–73%) and an ERR of 98% (95% BCI 97–99%).
466.Rationale: Although the triple combination achieved a CR of 66% and an ERR of 98% according to the model, the passage clearly states that mebendazole given as 6x100 mg had higher effectiveness (CR 88%, ERR 99.6%). Thus the triple combination is not the single most effective regimen among those tested; the multi-dose mebendazole regimen showed superior results.

466.Option: Despite considerably lower cure rates obtained by our modeling framework, our results confirm earlier findings that treatments which consist of a combination of drugs or multiple doses are more efficacious than a single-drug treatment.
Evidence: Recent studies and meta-analyses have shown that other regimens and combination treatment are more efficacious.
Fig 2 shows a comparison of the model estimates of the CRs and ERRs to the observed ones, where the ‘true’ estimates of CR were lower than the observed ones.

The treatments that achieved the highest ‘true’ CRs and ERRs were mebendazole (6x100 mg) with a CR of 88% (95% BCI 79–95%) and an ERR of 99.6% (95% BCI 98–100%) and the triple combination treatment (albendazole 400 mg plus oxantel pamoate 20 mg/kg plus pyrantel pamoate 20 mg/kg) with a CR of 66% (95% BCI 59–73%) and an ERR of 98% (95% BCI 97–99%).

The two least efficacious treatments were mebendazole 500 mg and oxantel pamoate (20 mg/kg) with CRs of 14% (95% BCI 11–18%) and 10% (95% BCI 7–15%) and ERRs of 21% (95% BCI -5–41%) and -26% (95% BCI -8–16%), respectively.
466.Rationale: The passage notes that the model-derived ('true') CRs were lower than observed, and it reports that the most effective regimens were either multiple doses (mebendazole 6x100 mg) or multi-drug combinations (triple combination, tribendimidine plus ivermectin also had high CR/ERR). Conversely, single-dose mebendazole 500 mg and monotherapy with oxantel pamoate were among the least efficacious. Together these statements support the inference that, even after the modeling which reduced estimated CRs, combination regimens or multiple-dose regimens outperform single-drug single-dose treatments.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers of New World alphaviruses by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding, likely through sequestering capsid in the nucleus and disrupting capsid/glycoprotein interaction at the plasma membrane.
Evidence: The three active compounds, KPT-185, KPT-335, and KPT-350, confined capsid to the nucleus (Fig 1A and S3A Fig), yielding a localization pattern similar to that seen with treatment by the well-documented CRM1 inhibitor, Leptomycin B [18].
Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls (Fig 3A), with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi.
This indicates that SINE compounds likely disrupt a late step in the virus lifecycle such as viral assembly or budding.
Capsid was present in the supernatants of DMSO and KPT-301 controls, but not in Leptomycin B or KPT-185 samples.

These data combined with the altered capsid localization and the observed decrease in viral RNA in extracellular supernatants, indicate that SINE compounds can sequester capsid, removing it from the pool of available structural proteins, leading to decrease in virus assembly and/or release of mature virions.
